item.description:
## 一、最反直觉的观察：测试不是"计算机使用"，而是"分布式系统推理"
大多数人把 AI 测试理解为"让模型点对按钮"——这是巨大的误读。Walden 在访谈中暗示了一个被严重低估的事实：**测试的真正难度不在视觉与坐标，而在于"如何让一个跨前后端的修改在一个被精确编排的环境中复现某个特定行为"**。换句话说，测试 AI 实际上在做的是"分布式系统的反向运维"——它必须先理解服务拓扑、再理解 feature flag、再理解权限矩阵、再理解触发路径，最后才轮到点击。
这里有个隐藏的真相：**Devon 已经在事实上把"测试"重新定义为"代码库语境下的因果链推理"**。这是为什么他们说"没有任何一个 frontier model 能独自完成端到端测试"——他们必须 orchestrate 多个模型协作。而这恰恰违背了 Walden 自己写的"不要构建 multi-agent"那篇著名博客。一个有意思的张力：**他在编排层反对 multi-agent，却在认知层默默拥抱了 multi-model 协作。** 这是一种"产品层单 agent、能力层多模型"的微妙双重态——一个尚未被命名

item.title: [Invest Like The Best] 30 Years of Finding Alpha | Dan Loeb
item.dc:creator: Invest Like The Best
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、最反直觉的观察：Loeb 的"进化"不是策略迭代，而是身份重构

大多数投资人谈"进化"，指的是工具箱扩容——多加一个因子、多看一个行业。但读完 Loeb 的轨迹，你会发现他做的根本不是"加法"，而是反复杀死那个让他成功的自己。

  • 1995–2013：他靠 Greenblatt 的事件驱动框架赚钱，明确说"我几乎不考虑商业质量、护城河、ROIC"。
  • 2013 之后：他承认那套打法养出的同行"没能活过来"，于是把整个团队从 generalist 改组为行业专家，把核心信仰换成 Quality Investing。
  • 今天：他甚至说"今天你必须是 tech person"——这是一个从 distressed debt 出身、把 Greenblatt 奉为圣经的人说的话。

真正惊人的不是他的转型本身，而是节奏：他几乎是在每一个"上一代赢家集体阵亡"的临界点前完成切换。这意味着 Loeb 的 alpha 来源不是任何一种风格，而是他对"风格何时会被时代抛弃"的元判断。换句话说，他的核心能力不是选股，是对自己当前打法的剩余寿命有定价能力——这是绝大多数基金经理终生学不会的能力，因为承认你正在做的事即将失效，是一种身份层面的死亡。

二、跨领域连接：Loeb 的投资框架其实是"资本结构里的拓扑学"

Loeb 说他在 XAI 上没买股权、却买了债，在 Twitter 上没参与并购、却抄底了 Morgan Stanley 甩卖的贷款。这听起来像投行话术，但本质上他在做的事和生物学里的"生态位选择"几乎一模一样：

  • 同一家"企业生物体"，在不同的资本层级（股权 / Hold Co / Op Co / 优先股）上有完全不同的捕食者、不同的竞争密度、不同的猎物可得性。
  • 多数基金被"我们是股票基金"或"我们是信用基金"这种物种身份绑死，只能在一个生态位捕食。
  • Loeb 真正在做的，是用对企业的同一份认知，沿着资本结构上下游迁徙，去捕捉那个生态位里被定价错误最严重的猎物（fulcrum security）。

这也解释了为什么 Third Point 是 60% 信用——一个"对冲基金"做到 60% 信用，从 PowerPoint 角度无法解释，但从生态学角度完全合理：这家公司根本不是基金，是一个跨资本结构的食腐者集群。

更妙的连接：他描述 Danaher 的 DBS 系统时强调"发现谁表现不佳是被庆祝的，因为意味着可以修"——这和上面的生态位思维是同构的。真正的高质量系统，对"错误"和"错误定价"都不带情绪，只把它们当作可以被搬运的能量。

三、被你和他自己都忽略的隐藏模式：他的失败案例都是"信任的失败"，不是"分析的失败"

Loeb 谈到的两类痛苦： 1. FTX：分析框架是对的，他甚至能在区块链上验证，但他错信了一个人。 2. 被 AI 颠覆的"高质量公司"：分析框架是对的，但他过度信任了"高质量"这个标签本身——以为护城河是结构性的。

把这两件事放在一起，会发现 Loeb 的盲区不是估值、不是行业，而是"信任迁移成本"：当世界的底层变化速度（他引用 Schmidt 的"logarithmic acceleration"）超过任何一个组织重建信任结构的速度时，"高质量"作为一种持久标签就开始失效，它变成了一种瞬时状态。

这就引出一个让我自己也觉得意外的预测——

Loeb 接下来几年最可能的下一次"杀死自己"，不是从 value 切到 quality，也不是从 equity 切到 credit，而是把"质量"从一个名词重新定义为一个动词：不再问"这家公司是不是 quality business"，而是问"这家公司更新自己的速度，是否快于它所处生态的 entropy 增速"。

如果这个判断成立，那么他对 Anthropic、Nvidia、Elon World 这三家公司的偏爱就有了更深的解释：它们不是质量最高的公司，它们是当前世界上自我更新速度最快的三个组织。Loeb 真正在押注的，可能是"组织的代谢率"这个尚未被命名的因子。

而这件事，又和他在采访结尾对"kindness"的执念奇妙呼应：在一个所有结构化优势都在加速折旧的时代，唯一不会被 AI 套利掉的复利，是你在别人一无所有时建立的人际信任——Carter 让他睡沙发，最后变成了 Third Point 的种子资本。这不是鸡汤，这是他这套投资观里最严肃的一条逻辑：当所有可被结构化的 alpha 都在归零，未被结构化的关系就成了最后的护城河。

📝 转录文本

30 Years of Finding Alpha | Dan Loeb

Invest Like The Best

开场：在一个加速的世界里寻找 Alpha

"There was a time when you could say, 'I'm just going to punt on tech and focus on industrials and consumer and whatever.' I think you have to be a tech person today. It's such a big and growing and compounding part of the economy. It affects everything else. Hold on to your seats — things are only going to accelerate from here. You have to figure out the things that are most important and most relevant. Maybe that's where the human element comes in: to understand and to be able to make those tough trading decisions when fundamentals are going one way and stock prices are going the other way. What an awesome opportunity to be able to incorporate everything that you can possibly know about the world that's relevant."

Dan: We've only been trying to do this for six years.

Host: Yeah, welcome. I've been excited to finally chat with you about all things markets. It's such a crazy time.

一、每天的信息流：如何在信息过载中保持清醒

Host: I was walking in here, I'm like, what am I actually the most curious about as it relates to how Dan runs his life? And one of the questions is the simplest: in this time where there's more information than you could ever read, literally, what does your day look like to stay up to speed on all the investments that you've made, the investments that you could make? What is your — like, today, even just take today where it's the end of the day — how did you decide what to read, who to talk to, how do you stay on top of the fire hose?

Dan: I wish I could say I have a Claude Code that has organized all the information in one place and I go through it all. But, you know, I check the news and see what's relevant for the economy and what's relevant for our positions. I try not to get too obsessed with the minute-to-minute stuff because that will drive you crazy. I try to be a little more tactical than strategic.

People will ask me about macro — what's important? And I think when people think about macro, they think about all the typical stuff that the government reports: growth, unemployment, inflation, rates, currencies, or where was gold, where's crypto? I think all that stuff is trumped right now by two things:

  • Where's oil — and that's going to be dictated by what happens in the war and geopolitics.
  • What's happening with AI — both on the spending front, infrastructure, and what's the impact of that going to be on society and on the economy.

Those are the main things I'm focused on, really trying to deeply understand.

AI 作为思考栈

Host: What is your model of both of those things? Everyone's talking about this all day every day, so obviously these are two big issues. Do you feel like you need to have a clear view, or a differentiated view? How do you process things as big as this where probably no one person can understand the whole thing?

Dan: I'm not natively a tech person, but given where the world is today — I think there was a time when you could say, "I'm just going to punt on tech and focus on industrials and consumer and healthcare or whatever." I think you have to be a tech person today. It's such a big and growing and compounding part of the economy. It affects everything else.

So I try to talk to smart people regularly. Jensen has laid out well, and everybody talks about the AI stack: starting with power and energy at the bottom, then chips and infrastructure, moving up through LLMs and software and applications, and how that plays through. That's a good mental model. And we've played different elements of that through industrials and infrastructure and hyperscalers and things like that.

Look, the SOX is up 40%. I don't think I've ever seen an event like that. In fact, if you just go back a few years, semiconductors were kind of left for dead. They were roadkill in the market. People were just not thinking about them at all. That all changed when Nvidia reported its March results three years ago. You either were there, or it's okay if you weren't — you could quickly play catch-up. That was the big event. And now of course it's thinking about the Nvidia, the Trainium and the TPU ecosystems, and how those play out, and relative strength, and how that plays through the different hyperscalers, and then of course the foundational models.

I try to think in terms of that stack, but I also think about what's going on in probably the three most consequential companies today: Nvidia, Anthropic, and Elon World (all of his companies collectively). There are a lot of ways to look through the prism, but for me, that's been an effective way to think about how things flow through.

二、风格的来历：从 Event-Driven 到 Quality Investing

Host: How would you describe yourself as an investor? You have an early reputation for being this incredibly precise — an ability to go through things with a forensic lens as an activist early in your career. But now Third Point is a much bigger, broader, more diversified collection of, you know, $25 billion or so of assets. How do you think of yourself stylistically?

Dan: The roots of Third Point really come out of my experience as a credit investor and my time at Jefferies. Jefferies was like my laboratory for studying some of the best investors. My clients were people like David Tepper at — who had not yet started Appaloosa but started Appaloosa — guys like Eric Mitch who ran the trading desk at Goldman, firms like Angelo Gordon, Farallon. I was able to watch firsthand how the best, I guess at that point we would call them either distressed debt or event-driven or risk arbitrage investors.

So my natural first lens was to think about credit, but on the equity side to think about it in terms of a hierarchy and a mental model. I completely thought about it through the lens of event-driven investing — so merger arbitrage a little bit off to the side, because that's less directional. The non-merger arb part is really just a mathematical exercise in earning a return and the risk you're taking. But in terms of a fundamental investment lens, it would be best characterized by those types of firms.

The best book, which I think is still relevant today, would be Joel Greenblatt's classic: You Can Be a Stock Market Genius. I think the original title was You Can Be a Stock Market Genius Even If You're Not That Smart.

Host: He cut it down a little bit.

Dan: He took that part out because investors don't like to think of themselves that way. But a brilliant book, and most of the people I know in that world use it as their framework. He talked about things like spin-offs, demutualizations, privatizations, post-reorg equities. That's how I thought about things.

为什么 Spin-off 那么赚钱

I was totally unfocused on business quality, the moat, return on capital. I barely even thought about relative multiples for different kinds of businesses. All I thought about was: am I buying something really cheap that has the following characteristics?

A new security is often created which is priced and valued at a very cheap price because of a lack of liquidity. So when a large company spins off a subsidiary, especially in those days, there's a new stock being born. The existing investors at that point in time — especially mutual fund types or folks who didn't want it for whatever reason, maybe it wasn't in their sector — they just weren't doing the work. So they would just routinely sell it. There'd be this sort of liquidity gap. If you could figure out the value of the business, those tended to be good trades.

That was exacerbated by the fact that the management teams would go out, in the case of a spin-off, and do a road show that presented a very conservative view. Some might even say sandbag the numbers. Why? People are always creatures of incentives. Their incentive package would be set at the time of the spin-off, so they would come out very conservatively.

But there's more. The companies themselves were inefficiently operated within these larger businesses. Margins were lower than they should have been. Sales were probably less. The management team didn't have the incentives to really optimize the businesses.

So that was an incredible business model. And that dynamic applied to spin-offs, privatizations, demutualizations, newly created companies like Visa or Mastercard. It was a beautiful business and it was sort of underappreciated for a long, long time — you could generate really excess returns. Then we would take that basic framework and start applying it to other things like the synergies that come when you combine two companies in a major merger: something like Union Pacific–Norfolk Southern.

From 1995, when we started the fund, probably up until 2013 to 2015, that was our bread and butter.

进化：转向 Quality Investing

Host: What changed in that era, and how would you describe that landscape today? Does Joel Greenblatt's book still feel as valuable? Are there opportunities like that that still exist? Are they too small for someone like you?

Dan: No, they still exist all the time. The real opportunity today is overlaying that understanding with a business-quality lens.

Let's talk about how my business evolved. A lot of the people who have underperformed or haven't survived the last decade are people who were really stuck on the idea of deep value, low multiples, being really stubborn about how they viewed businesses, less flexible about moving into higher-multiple companies or growthier companies. We basically started to look at companies that grew faster, that had better returns on capital, that were quote-unquote quality businesses. That opened up a whole new world for us.

So I would call that the other significant part of what we do: quality investing, thematic investing. That's when we started to organize our team around industry experts and less around generalists and transactions.

A couple of books I'd suggest. Two of the most consequential books I read in that area:

  • The Outsiders — talks about managers who understand capital allocation along with great operations: companies like Danaher, TransDigm and others.
  • Quality Investing by Cunningham — the most influential and eye-opening to me. Really lays out the idea of super high-quality businesses with good moats and high return on capital, that you might want to own for many, many years.

Now, what happened last year was really interesting because a lot of these companies that appeared to be super high quality — it's probably the worst year, going into the beginning of this year, because of the disruption of AI. A lot of these apparently high-quality companies very rapidly became less so.

[赞助内容：Ramp / Felix by Rogo / WorkOS]

三、不断进化：在加速的世界里改变自己

Host: One of the most distinctive things about you and the business is how much changing of your stripes, or evolving of your strategy, you've done relative to others. I'm assuming that's going to have to keep happening. The world's going to keep changing probably at a faster clip than before. How have you done that? What's the key to doing that well across 30 years now?

Dan: I remember in 2013, I was in a room — a dinner in Davos, which I don't go to anymore. It was a Goldman Sachs dinner and Eric Schmidt was there and gave a talk. You go back to around 2013, it was a time — when you look back at it, it was almost quaint in terms of the technological innovation. This was probably around the time the Uber app came out and the iPhone was starting to —

Host: Explode.

Dan: Explode — I say "explode," but it was starting to come up with some interesting apps which were starting to be adopted. The SaaS revolution was at the front end. Companies like Microsoft were starting to find their footings.

Think about the period from the dot-com bubble through the GFC. The '90s were a time of incredible technological innovation, and yes, stuff was happening, but I don't think we felt there was a lot going on. The sexy industries at times were natural resources, energy, financial services.

So Schmidt said to the room: "It will be your natural tendency to think that this increase in technological innovation and disruption and change that we've been experiencing for the last couple of years is an anomaly, and that things are going back to a steadier type of innovation and growth. Hold on to your seats. Things are only going to accelerate from here."

He was really right. You could have said that again in 2017. You could have said it again in 2020. You can really say that right now. It has just continued to logarithmically accelerate. We're going through something now where we're at the front end of AI. We're just going to have to learn to live with this.

I don't know that from an evolutionary standpoint our brains have been able to deal with things like social media and some of the other changes. It's going to take a lot of work for us to prepare ourselves as a species and mentally how to ingest all this information.

Brad Gerstner talks about this book —

Host: Essentialism.

Dan: Essentialism. I think we also have to adopt this idea of essentialism, because you can't do it all. You have to figure out the things that are most important and most relevant to what you do.

资本配置者在 AI 时代的角色

Host: Imagine five years hence, or pick your period of time, when it seems we're destined for a world where no human will be anywhere near as good at ingesting, pattern recognition, synthesis of data, analysis — this sort of stuff. You could argue it's already here if you had the right pipeline set up. What will be the role of capital allocators in that environment? I think about your early letters to some of these companies where you really had to pick your way through datasets and do some gumshoe work, and you could earn a ton of alpha that way. What will be the remainder job of the capital allocator?

Dan: Honestly, I have no idea. I don't even know what it's going to look like in six months or a year. I think we're okay for the next couple of years. Companies will need to raise money. The securities created from that will need to change hands somehow. People want to save. People want to invest. People need to borrow. I think you always need some human interface in the middle of that. But it's highly unlikely there will be a completely AI-managed capital system.

人性永远不变

Host: Is your sense that there is a lot of opportunity to earn great returns still in this environment? The fact that SOX is up 40% this year — we're several years into everyone being aware that this is a big trend, and yet these things were up tremendously in year three or four.

Dan: I talk a lot about investment books. There's so much wisdom in books for investing. One of my favorite books, Reminiscences of a Stock Operator — the guy who wrote it quotes, I think, from Ecclesiastes: "There's nothing new under the sun." Human nature.

The question is: will AI take human nature and the flaws in human emotion out of the investment process? Because there's a lot of — or might the AI even adopt some of that under the name of risk management or managing downside? In theory it could take it out, but if you go back to the principle that there's nothing new under the sun, the thing that doesn't change is hysteria, bubbles, panics, the extremes of human nature both optimistically and pessimistically.

Think about just this year. Why is the SOX up so much? Because all the evidence has pointed to the fundamentals in semiconductors, semi-cap equipment, memory — everything around it being super strong. So what happened? Expectations were too high. In the same way that Nvidia after Q1 three years ago had this monster quarter, people piled on, it kept going up. You had a couple of quarters in a row where it put up solid numbers, then shockingly good numbers, and the stock tanked, and then the whole sector went down. People were scratching their heads: "Why are the numbers so good and stock prices keep going down?"

The same thing happened with Micron. They had a phenomenal quarter, up 80%, way ahead of expectations. The stock went up a little because expectations were too high, then went down. That happens a lot. Happened to Meta a couple of years ago — they put up a good quarter, the stock went up like Wile E. Coyote, there was no one else to buy the stock, it tanked.

That's where the human element comes in: to understand and to make those tough trading decisions when fundamentals are going one way and stock prices are going the other way, and to be able to take the pain of losses in the short run.

量化与基本面的共生

The advantage that someone like me — a fundamental investor who doesn't make trading decisions based on computers — has, is that there are still a lot of market irregularities caused by some very good strategies, but collectively they create anomalies. You have quants and CTAs, you have pods. They have a great strategy for them and their investors, but it causes some unusual behaviors. Fundamental investors believe that, as Warren Buffett would say, if a stock goes down, you celebrate it because it's a chance to buy more at a better price. They have risk metrics which force selling on the way down. They do the opposite of what might be rational for long-term investors, but it's rational for their business model. So a lot of these things will continue to create opportunities for fundamental investors.

Host: It's so interesting to imagine where the source of potential outsized returns are: human behavior, and structural things like these. Absent those two, maybe it would be a less exciting market.

Dan: Yeah. But then you'll have corporate transactions that create opportunities. It feels like there's always something. There's failures, credit cycles, bankruptcies. It's hard to imagine the computers sitting on a creditor's committee and working through the capital structure and being able to transact.

If you think about a continuum of public securities and private equity — AI will not do private equity. You always need people to do deals. And then you have the stuff in between that requires a lot of negotiation and human interaction and high-touch: private credit, working through a restructuring. You'll probably always need human beings to do that part of the investment business.

四、公司治理：从父亲那里继承的兴趣

Host: It seems like we're entering a time where governance becomes incredibly important, especially in companies. We saw this play out with OpenAI in the public — where the governance structure and the people on a board can matter tremendously to outcomes. You've spent a huge amount of time thinking, writing, investing on this. When did you first get interested in it?

Dan: My dad was a securities lawyer who was an expert on corporate governance and wrote books about it. So I always heard about it at home. He's actually the first person I'd ever heard talk about corporate responsibility. He was on the board of Mattel and later Williams-Sonoma. He would go visit these factories where they were sourcing their materials from and wanted to make sure they were ethically sourced and the workers were treated well. He was really ahead of his time on that stuff.

Host: What was he like as a person?

Dan: My dad was incredibly funny, warm, irreverent, really smart. He was the only son of two immigrants from Europe. My grandmother, his mother, came from Poland in 1914. His father came from Romania in like 1898. Incidentally, his mother's youngest sister ended up — she was the first of that family that was born in this country — her younger sister founded Mattel Toys. He was a self-made person. He went to UCLA, did well, went to Harvard Law School, and spent most of his career at one law firm.

好治理与坏治理

Host: Sum up at the highest possible level — what are good and bad governance?

Dan: We have an incredible system. There's something beautiful about the American capitalist system in the aspect that creates boards of directors that ultimately have a role within both capitalism and a democratic system, where the board is responsible to the shareholders, responsible for accountability for management, setting strategy and key financial decisions.

The shortcomings within governance happen when:

  • Board members lose sight of what their duties are as fiduciaries.
  • The composition of the board is such that they aren't really equipped to carry out that duty — lack of deep knowledge, intellectual or talent diversity.
  • They are thinking excessively about things other than their duty to shareholders.

Not to say the board doesn't have other responsibilities, but ultimately they should feed into creating shareholder value. If you go back to both Milton Friedman and Warren Buffett — of course boards care about the communities they serve, the products, the employees, proper conduct. All those things are very important and they are not inconsistent with creating shareholder value. In fact, it's part of creating shareholder value.

A few years ago, the Business Roundtable said: "We're no longer going to say the board's responsibility is primarily to drive shareholder value. It's this thing and that thing and the other thing." I think it was a distraction from what their real duty is, and it didn't recognize how all these things go together.

The bad governance I've seen is when they let their loyalty or relationship to a CEO who's not up to the job overshadow their duty to shareholders. That's probably the main thing — because the CEO, you know, it's very important to understand: boards don't run the companies. The board is strategic, not tactical. They should be focusing on those things. But if the company isn't allocating capital well, or isn't holding the management team responsible, or there are obvious things that should be done differently — that's when we can come in.

Most of the time we've been able to work with existing boards in redirecting them and offering solutions. Sometimes we don't even have to get on the board. You hear about the extreme cases where our presence is needed on the board to actually shake things up.

写作作为激进股东的武器

Host: What have you learned about the power of writing in investing? You're extremely well known for some letters you've written to chairs of the board throughout your entire career. What is great writing to you, and how do you use it?

Dan: All great writing is really about clear thinking and organizing your thoughts, communicating them to people in a clear way to get a desired outcome. You can also use writing to influence — in our case it's been helpful in getting the attention of other shareholders, sometimes the board itself, shaking them up a little bit, getting media attention focused on a board.

Activism — you've got a few different levers:

  • Financial lever — obviously, we can make a bid for the company.
  • Legal levers — proxy contests, litigation, information requests, etc.
  • Social pressure — actually a very effective way. The best way to put social pressure on a company is through writing and PR efforts.

Sotheby's 的故事

Host: Looking across all the different activism you've done, it seems there's an interesting theme of you going activist on places or people or companies that hold themselves out as quite high-status, but they're not living up to it — whether that's Sotheby's, some Japanese conglomerates, or certain CEOs and their family members on the board. There's a status component that would be deserved if being earned on an ongoing basis, but maybe the company wasn't. You saw that gap as an opportunity. Is that a pattern?

Dan: That's interesting. Certainly was the case in Sotheby's.

I think all board members often feel a sense of status for being on these boards. That itself needs to be dispelled — because if you're on a board because you are either getting status or income, and that's your primary reason for doing it, not for representing shareholders, that's where we come in to disintermediate that and take away some of the status, or increase the cost of doing that.

Sotheby's was an interesting company. It was a pretty small target for us. It's a good example because although it was a public company, it wasn't really run for the shareholders. It was run because people felt like it was a high-status business. It had been mismanaged, didn't really recover from an antitrust violation that spilled into criminal charges brought against the company and some of the individuals. The business itself was a good business, but run unbelievably unprofitably. The company had been around since the 1700s and some of the business practices had not really been updated since that time.

We took a position, bought 9.9% of the company. We went after the board but really just wanted them to implement some basic business practices that we thought would be better. The CEO that was there — he didn't have a particularly deep knowledge of art. He came out of the rug division, didn't really have deep relationships with the collectors. We gave him a shot for a year, then the board came to realize he wasn't the right guy. We brought in a guy named Tad Smith, who was terrific, from MSG. Cleaned up the operations, improved the technology, then they sold the company.

Host: Good result.

Dan: Yeah, a good result.

小市值机会：负向选择

Host: Do you think there's a lot of that lurking out there today? If one wanted to start a career and only do that — go find companies where it was mediocre or bad management, where if it was good management, the company would do way better?

Dan: There's probably some of that in the sub-$2 billion market cap space — and not necessarily bad management but maybe B+ management, not optimizing.

What we're finding is that it's almost like a negative selection process. We'd much rather invest in a great company with awesome management that's doing all the right things and cheer them on, than find something that "but for mismanagement" would be worth a lot more. Because what you find is that if the things you've identified are badly run, then there's probably 10 times more things that are badly run. It just gets to be a morass.

五、Third Point 的结构：跨资本结构的"食腐者集群"

Host: One of the interesting things about Third Point is that — if I understand it correctly — it's something like 60% credit, which would surprise a lot of people in terms of total assets. When you started, I think it was no institutional investors — it was individuals, families. You sort of felt your way into the strategy. The strategy as it exists today probably wouldn't go on a clean PowerPoint deck if you were a new firm doing this from scratch. I'd love to talk about that evolution.

Dan: Third Point is a whole collection of businesses. My main focus is on the hedge fund strategy, which started at $3 million. It's now about $9 billion today. That fund itself is about 30% credit in total. The rest of the portfolio — it moves all over the place. It's primarily equities. Our equity book is generically around 110 long by 30 or 40 short, but that can be all over the place. Going into the war, we really dialed back our risk, and for the first time since 2009 we did have more credit exposure than equity, but we very quickly took that back up.

Across the firm we have:

  • A CLO business with about $7 billion.
  • Within Third Point itself, about 30% — close to $3 billion — in structured credit and corporate credit, within the hedge fund.
  • An insurance company that we manage about a billion dollars in credit for.
  • A couple billion dollars in asbestos liabilities that we manage in its own pool.
  • A new private credit business, which is small.
  • A venture capital business.

共通主线：Fulcrum Security

Host: What's the thread that unites all those things — asbestos liability, private credit, corporates? It seems all over the place.

Dan: I've worked in venture capital, risk arbitrage, credit, equities. I think having a view of value and thinking about valuing enterprises — whether they're earlier stage, mid-stage, or mature — and looking to invest in whatever the fulcrum security is in that enterprise. Obviously for an early-stage company the only fulcrum is the equity.

Host: What does that mean — fulcrum? Just define that.

Dan: The one that's going to have the best risk/reward. It's usually used in terms of companies that have debt and equity. It's more in terms of companies going through a restructuring of some sort: do you want to be in the equity? Do you want to be in this junior debt? Do you want to be in the senior debt?

Credit Suisse was going through its troubles and being bought by UBS. You could invest in the preferred shares, in the HoldCo paper, or in the OpCo paper, which was most senior within the capital structure. The fulcrum there was actually the HoldCo paper, which had the most upside. The OpCo paper also did well. The preferreds were wiped out — that was the wrong place to be.

There are always different interesting places within the capital structure to play, and having a comprehensive view of these companies gives you a great vantage point to make alpha-generating investment decisions.

Twitter 和 XAI：两个案例

Let me give you two examples. We had a good enough knowledge of Twitter and XAI to understand the equity value of both businesses. Without making a decision whether we wanted to own equity in XAI, and having not participated in the Twitter deal, there were two financing transactions that came up.

Twitter: The Twitter debt was a resale of the financing debt that was offered when Elon bought the company. Morgan Stanley sat on it for a while. It was deep underwater. When it got close to par, they decided to sell it. Most credit investors were really scared and nervous to buy that, even though it was at 96–97 cents on the dollar yielding around 12%. We were comfortable enough with the underlying value of the business and with the fundamentals that we made that, at that time, our largest credit position.

XAI: When XAI did a debt financing, very few credit people wanted to play in that one because there was no cash flow — $2 billion in revenues and a $20 billion enterprise value. But we were very comfortable that this was a real business. We looked at it as credit investors, but we also were able to bring in the resources of our private investing knowledge.

Host: A fascinating example of the value of seeing across the whole ecosystem and being able to invest however you want.

单一 PM 还是多 PM？

Host: Which brings me to the question — as I understand it, you're sort of still the single portfolio manager that sits on top of all these assets across all these different buckets. Ultimately you have to make the decision to buy or sell something.

Dan: Let me push back on that. I'm the portfolio manager of the hedge fund. But private credit, CLOs, structured credit, the high-yield business — they all have their own PMs. I will come in if there's an interesting opportunity — like we supersized both Twitter and XAI when I got involved — but I'm not even on the investment committees of those businesses.

六、当下的市场：科技股的估值与新一轮 Bubble 的质疑

Host: To zoom in on just the hedge fund where you're the PM — you mentioned before, you could be a great investor and not bother with tech, like Warren Buffett style. Today the market's, what, 70% tech depending on how you measure it. What do you think of that complex of companies today — Amazon, Microsoft, Google, big big technology companies — relative to the last 10 years? What does the setup feel like to you today?

Dan: I think the setup's great. You can still buy Nvidia — maybe the multiple slightly higher right now. There's such a catch-up trade. Nvidia at 15 times '27, 12 times '28, for the most dominant — I don't know if it's the fastest growing, but very fast growing — company at its size.

I looked through our whole semis, semi-cap equipment, hyperscaler portfolio. My instinct was going to be: "Okay, we've got to take profits here." I looked at the valuations, I looked at their growth rates. Unless you are really draconian or negative and you think that somehow the AI world is going to roll over in '31 or '32 — I think it's the most attractive sector right now. It's where the bulk of our capital is invested.

Host: As a person who was born in the value discipline and made a lot of money in that kind of investing, those people who tend to have not evolved as much would point to today and say, "Oh, it's just like another classic example of a giant bubble in the making." Do you feel seeds of that at all? Other aspects of the market that feel euphoric or strange?

Dan: If you don't believe the capex numbers are going to yield a return, then you would have to believe that they're just flushing money down the toilet and they're not going to get a return — because the earnings are really strong and the multiples on that. They might say, "Yes, but the cash flow after capex" — you know, they're scoring. But these are companies that are investing money off their balance sheets and are generating enormous amounts of cash.

It's very different from the dot-com bubble, which we were short going into and had good numbers in those years. You don't have the valuation bubble now on those companies that you had back in those days.

You look at Anthropic's revenue growth and the adoption and the usefulness of its products and the anecdotes you hear about the next generation, the new models and what that's going to do — you can make a good argument we're barely scratching the surface. There are so many layers of corporations that are just getting started. So I'm in the optimist camp.

[赞助内容：Vanta / Ridgeline]

七、海外市场：日本、Sony 与激进股东之路

Host: What about the rest of the world? I'd love you to tell the Sony story — the story of going to Japan and spending so much time there and everything that you learned. It's a colorful, cool story, but it's also an excuse to ask about the rest of the world. I know you'd be willing to invest anywhere, whereas the American companies get basically all the attention in markets these days.

Dan: Israel is an interesting market — a nichier market. We have one of our top investments there that, despite the war, has been one of the best performing stocks in our portfolio. But in terms of the big markets, there's a lot going on in Korea, Taiwan, Japan. I'm probably more bullish on them as a hunting ground to find great companies.

The European markets are tough right now given the regulatory environment. They just have a different attitude about business and capitalism. We're invested in a couple of businesses there — Rolls-Royce, ASML — but the companies in Europe dependent on the local economy are more challenged.

Sony 的故事

Host: Can you tell that Sony investment story and everything you learned through that process?

Dan: At one point we owned 7% of Sony. There's a list of companies that we at one point owned significant stakes in that, had I not sold, would be worth in the mid- to upper-single-digit billions of dollars.

Host: I feel like I hear this all the time.

Dan: Yeah, we all have that. We sort of took two runs at Sony. The first time we invested, it was basically a conglomerate. It had the Sony studios, a semiconductor business, a life insurance business, and all the consumer electronics. We advised them to separate these businesses — at a minimum take out the insurance business, which had no place in it.

We met with the management team. We had a big deck. At the end of the meeting we told them, in the interest of transparency, we shared our investment thesis with the New York Times. Andrew Ross Sorkin wrote the story. They went into a panic when we told them about that, and Andrew agreed to embargo the story until the Japanese market closed.

The story came out and they had pre-arranged for us to go on a tour of their innovation center. Before we went on the tour, when we told him there was going to be a story, the CEO — his name was Kaz Hirai at the time — said, "You told the New York Times?" I said, "Yeah, but just the New York Times, nobody else." He says, "Okay, just the New York Times." It was wild. We were walking around the innovation center, looking at our BlackBerrys, and the story went everywhere.

It ended up being a really good investment. They really pushed back on everything we recommended, but it took them about five years and, one by one, they've done many of the things — they've broken out the semi business, they partially spun out or they plan to spin out their financial services business.

日本激进股东的特殊性

The one thing I learned is that activism in Japan is really hard. But here's what's interesting. On one of our first trips, we met with the prime minister and his right-hand man, Suga-san. I told him I would write a paper explaining why activism was good for Japan as a country.

They had released something called the Three Arrows: fiscal, monetary, and restructuring. My suggestion was that they needed to include corporate governance — in particular a focus on return on invested capital — as part of their three-arrow strategy.

I came back to New York. I met with Larry Lindsey and Niall Ferguson. We wrote a three-person paper. They did most of the writing. We wrote a paper for AEI. It was then picked up as an editorial in the Wall Street Journal, and they then adopted that. It was pretty cool.

The government actually really wants the companies to do this. It's really the management teams that are more entrenched — because the shareholders and the government kind of want it. You've really seen progress since we first went over there. They're breaking up some of these cross-shareholdings. They're penalizing companies that trade at discounts to book value. So they're definitely moving in the right direction.

八、最有教益的一笔投资：Danaher

Host: If you think about all the investments that you've made, is there any that stands out as the one that taught you the most just about how this world works? I know I've asked this question a couple different ways, but I'm always interested in these instructive lessons — learning by doing versus by reading. You can read all these amazing books, The Outsiders and Joel Greenblatt's book, and they're great, but it feels like part of investing is you have to get hit in the face to really imbibe the lessons.

Dan: I think investing in Danaher has been the most instructive, because it really is one of the best-run businesses. It was also one of the first experiences I had investing in a super high-quality business that internalized some of the best practices of creating a corporate operating system.

My partner then, Munib, and I actually went to Danaher and got them to boil down their five-day DBS — Danaher Business System — training into a one-day thing for us. It was really instructive. It was a really good investment for about four years, by observing them, by watching them incrementally improve the business quality of the company — by shedding lower-quality businesses, buying higher return-on-capital, better-quality, higher-margin businesses, to shift from general industrials into healthcare. I learned a ton.

Then it stopped working as an investment. Because of COVID there were all kinds of irregularities — surges in orders, increases in inventory, and then a correction. All the benefits they got from the surge in demand that was a tailwind became a headwind, and they still haven't really come out of it. It'll be interesting to see how they navigate AI in the next few years. We actually sold it and, with the recent selloff, have gotten back in, but in a small way.

But I learned a lot about how really thoughtful businesses think philosophically and deeply about their operating system, about optimizing and motivating and inspiring their team — and even the fact that they have a system in place. It's also been incredibly instructive watching the diaspora of executives.

Host: Larry Culp was there, right?

Dan: Yeah, Larry Culp was there. There have been a lot of others — the guy from Ingersoll Rand, which has been a well-run business.

Host: If you had to sum up that one day, what was it? Just continuous — some system for continuous improvement was the thing that you took away?

Dan: "Cult" is a little bit too strong of a word to describe it, but they had a very strong corporate identity and culture. It's one thing to say "we are a Kaizen company and we're dedicated to continual improvement," but what they have is a whole system of implementing improvement across the organization.

One of my big takeaways from that day: they would hold people very accountable, and individually show when people were underperforming. But the interesting thing was that, because these things were all addressable and fixable, when they found someone underperforming, it was celebrated instead of shamed. "Look, look at all these things you're doing wrong. We can fix those." And they did. Over and over and over again. They do it both in terms of operations and working capital. It was really cool to walk around a place and see everybody on the same wavelength trying to accomplish that.

九、保险公司：被低估的资金来源

Host: Can you talk about the insurance business that you've built, on the same point that everyone talks about in investing — the stuff you're putting money in, far less discussion of where the money's coming from? Apollo has certainly done this with Athene. There's been a lot of cool innovation on the liability side. How have you thought about it?

Dan: We actually started an insurance company de novo. It was 2010. We were backed by myself, Kelso, and Pine Brook, and we started a Bermuda-based reinsurance company.

The thesis was: we have an executive that will do these reinsurance deals, we will invest the float all in Third Point and in treasury — sort of a barbell — then we can defer taxes, get leverage on our capital. Greenlight Re at the time was trading at 140% of book value. I thought this would be the future — we'll just keep raising money for this vehicle.

The problem was that the reinsurance business took a sharp turn for the worse, and we had some good years at Third Point but we were scrambling to offset the losses from the insurance company.

About three years ago, I said: "We had the right idea, but we had the wrong insurance vehicle. We were doing P&C insurance. We really should have just done plain vanilla annuities basically." The annuity business can't invest in the hedge fund — it can only do credit. The good news is we had about five years when Sirius Point stopped investing in my hedge fund, but we started investing in structured credit, corporate credit, and other things that were either rated or more appropriate for an insurance company.

We've done two things since then:

  1. We merged our reinsurance company into a closed-end fund we had in the UK — Third Point Offshore Investors.
  2. We reincorporated that business from Guernsey to Cayman and repurposed it from a closed-end investor in my hedge fund into an insurance company. It still has some investment in the hedge fund, but it now owns our reinsurance company.

That company will be in a position to do more reinsurance deals, issue primary annuities, and then Third Point manages the money in private credit, structured credit, whole-loan mortgages, some direct lending in real estate, some investment-grade corporate debt, and private investment grade. We'll also put the equity of that business in things like the junior tranches of structured financings, and for growth equity investments.

十、最痛的教训：FTX 与对"高质量"的过度信任

Host: What's the hardest investment lesson that you've ever learned?

Dan: I would have to say our investment in FTX. It looked great. The company was growing fast. We could verify it all on the blockchain. We felt we had good company on the cap table with us. We did it. It just turned out it wasn't what we thought it was.

It was painful because — in general, one of the amazing things about our capitalist system is the ability for venture-backed companies to go out and raise capital for interesting ideas. Most people are good actors with good intentions, and we've rarely had any kind of mishap. Doesn't mean you don't do very careful due diligence. Now our due diligence process — we definitely check bank balances and do the most basic due diligence that probably would have turned stuff up on this.

But if left alone, if he hadn't ended up being a crook or very sloppy, the venture investments he made would have made him —

Host: Like the best venture investor of this era.

Dan: Cursor, Anthropic, Solana — everything he did. The guy had a great nose for value. So that was probably the toughest mistake we made.

Within the last two years — we obviously were looking for, and we've made some great short investments in things being disrupted by AI. But where we've made the mistake is that we thought we knew better — that AI wasn't really going to affect this part of the info-services business, or these guys had proprietary information. That's where we've made some mistakes. I still think there could be a shakeout. There will probably be some phoenixes that rise from the ashes. But that's been the investment lesson of the last year or so.

引导团队穿越 AI 的不确定性

Host: How do you guide your team through this? You've got a bunch of extremely smart, ambitious, hungry analysts. What do you tell them going into this crazy uncertainty?

Dan: First of all, we all have to just start using it. The only way to get good at this is just to use it. We have people at different levels. We have experts that we have brought on that are native computer scientists, coming at this as expert AI people, working on specific projects. They're coaching the team. But we're also encouraging everyone to use AI and find as many applications as you can. We do hire system integrators — we have a system integrator working with us. We're starting a new insurance company.

I'm obsessive about continual improvement, both at the individual level and organizationally. Claude really enables you to be an individual self-improver. It makes you very autonomous. Your ability is sort of like — it'll give you back whatever you put into it, if you put a lot of time and energy and effort into it. So I'm encouraging everyone to do that. Collectively, we're all sharing best practices. Some people are running agents overnight, using tons and tons of tokens. Other people are probably more like me, just using it more for queries — but we're all very involved with it.

与同行的差异

Host: Where do you feel like your and the firm's view is the most different from your peers these days? You're close with lots of the great investors of this era. Where do you feel the most out by yourself, or distinctive in how you view the world?

Dan: I think we're probably more optimistic — or you could say less pessimistic — that there's going to be some kind of apocalypse from AI. I'm still pretty optimistic that it will create opportunities and create jobs, create net jobs. Obviously it'll lose in some places, gain in other places.

The differentiator for us is that we can always default into credit investing. When things get — I mean, we haven't been in a scenario where we had a real credit cycle, but I'm very comfortable investing in incredibly stressed times. We really haven't had any since 2020.

Going back to COVID — the thing we got really right, because we had a good year that year, was that it wasn't because we piled into stocks. We piled into IG credit when it was so cheap. Maybe that's answering the question a little bit differently.

Host: Why do you suppose more places don't do that? It seems like an obvious advantage — to have more options for expressing a view.

Dan: It's so different. I grew up in credit. I worked on a trading desk. You don't just electronically trade bonds — you have to have relationships with these firms. Part of the reason for expanding into the CLO business is that we had the high-yield market — a trillion-and-a-half-dollar market — pretty well wired. We were dabbling in the broadly syndicated loan market, which is another trillion and a half. But we also have eyes on a $6 trillion structured credit market. These are not markets that lend themselves to tourism. When opportunities come up, we're already there, with the relationships and the understanding of the companies.

今天什么样的分析师最优秀

Host: What makes a great analyst today, in any way distinctive from what made a great analyst 20 years ago? How has that changed? If you picture the most talented analyst here right now, what are they like?

Dan: The great analyst 20 years ago was someone that could build a model really fast and understand some really complicated restructuring. I'll use myself as the example of that ancient dinosaur of an analyst that used to be useful. When I was at Jefferies, Drexel went bankrupt. There was a thick disclosure statement about three or four inches thick on the bankruptcy of a company called Drexel Burnham. This thing got passed around and nobody could crack the code. I was relatively new and I knew I had to differentiate myself. So I just spent a whole weekend studying this thing. Ultimately, that was one of the best investments ever in the history of bankruptcies — the claims on Drexel — because people didn't understand the complexity of the different value pools and liquidations. The claims were overstated, the assets were understated, but it was super complicated. That was the kind of thing that differentiated the analysts of the '90s, when I did that, or even the 2000s.

Now, I think it's somebody who's like a Gavin Baker type — a junior Gavin Baker. Somebody who understands a company, understands an industry, and understands the nuances of a technology.

Let's get away from technology for a minute. Casey's General Stores — why was this one of the best performing stocks? It looked like a tech stock. It was because they were not a convenience store chain — they were a pizza chain masquerading as convenience stores. Had an analyst who went to Texas and ate pizza and saw — that kind of analyst, today, is what was different.

十一、下一个十年：最兴奋的事与最担心的事

Host: If you think about the next 10 years for yourself — and everything going on in markets — we're all lucky to be alive to watch this era. What excites you the most about this next 10-year period as an investor? You're lucky, you have all these resources, you can invest in anything. And then what worries you the most?

Dan: The thing that worries me the most is just not having the time to do the things that I care about — spending time with my family, or being able to go surfing, and read books that I want to read. I'm not really worried about the business — you have good process, good things with value.

The thing that excites me is just the things that have excited me, that keep me going. What an awesome opportunity to be able to incorporate everything that you can possibly know about the world that's relevant — to study industries, to study technology, to study consumer behavior, to look at the US economy, look at politics, to travel to the Middle East, which I think is probably the most vibrant, interesting part of the world.

Who would have thought, 20 years ago or even three years ago, that Bahrain, the Emirates, Saudi, Morocco, Azerbaijan would be better allies to the US than NATO?

Host: Crazy.

Dan: And who would have thought that their growth rates would way exceed them, or that their embracing of technology — that's the stuff that keeps me going. Being able to incorporate all these different things, and also to form relationships with people doing interesting things — Jeremy O'Brien who founded PsiQuantum, talking about quantum computing, or Elad Raz who started Next Silicon, or the CEOs of the companies we invest in. I've got to know Mitch Rales from Danaher.

十二、收尾：关于善良

Host: It's so cool to finally hear about it directly and do this with you. When I do these, I ask the same traditional closing question of everyone. What's the kindest thing that anyone's ever done for you?

Dan: I thought about that because I knew the question was coming, and I had a lot of different ways to answer it. But before I answer, I do want to talk about the importance of kindness. I know you know it's important because you ask this question every time you interview someone.

It's important on many levels. If you elevate kindness as a characteristic that you want to elevate in your hierarchy of things you want to be — honest, truthful, smart, clever, innovative, whatever — kindness is very important. It goes with forming deep relationships with people. Kindness enables you to be empathetic. That enables you to connect with people, to learn from them, to be better as a human being, and — I hate to sound crass — but ultimately it will benefit your business.

So I will say on the kindness front: be kind to the people not just that will benefit you. Be kind to people you have no idea how it will ever benefit you. Sometimes it will and sometimes it won't. Sometimes you just connect with someone who thinks you're a better person. Good for its own sake.

Carter 的沙发

But anyway, I came down to my friend Carter, who, when I was — let's just say in between jobs — before I started at Jefferies, I had about a six- or nine-month period where I wasn't working. He let me sleep on his couch. Then, when I got my job at Jefferies, I suggested to him a bunch of different distressed debt situations, and he trusted me with a few hundred thousand of his money. That turned into a little over a million dollars. He then rolled that into my fund, and it really enabled me to get my business started. So that was one of the kindest things.

I want to say one other thing on the kindness topic. Gavin actually said this, and I think he was quoting Palmer Luckey:

"The one thing money doesn't buy you is friends that believed in you when you had nothing."

Host: It's a great line. Amen. Shout out to Carter. Dan, thanks so much for your time.

Dan: All right. Thank you very much.

[赞助内容：Ramp / Vanta / Rogo / WorkOS / Ridgeline]

item.description:
## 一、最反直觉的观察：Loeb 的"进化"不是策略迭代，而是身份重构
大多数投资人谈"进化"，指的是工具箱扩容——多加一个因子、多看一个行业。但读完 Loeb 的轨迹，你会发现他做的根本不是"加法"，而是**反复杀死那个让他成功的自己**。
- 1995–2013：他靠 Greenblatt 的事件驱动框架赚钱，明确说"我几乎不考虑商业质量、护城河、ROIC"。 - 2013 之后：他承认那套打法养出的同行"没能活过来"，于是把整个团队从 generalist 改组为行业专家，把核心信仰换成 *Quality Investing*。 - 今天：他甚至说"今天你必须是 tech person"——这是一个从 distressed debt 出身、把 Greenblatt 奉为圣经的人说的话。
**真正惊人的不是他的转型本身，而是节奏**：他几乎是在每一个"上一代赢家集体阵亡"的临界点前完成切换。这意味着 Loeb 的 alpha 来源不是任何一种风格，而是他对"风格何时会被时代抛弃"的元判断。换句话说，他的核心能力不是选股，是**对自己当前打法的剩余寿命有定价能力**——这是绝

item.title: [a16z Deep Dives] Rebuilding Global Banking With Stablecoins and AI | Deep Dives with Jeeves CEO
item.dc:creator: a16z Deep Dives
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、最反直觉的发现：「困难即护城河」其实是一种反向时间套利

Dileep 说"difficulty is defensible"，听起来像创业者的自我安慰，但仔细看 Jeeves 的数据会发现一个隐藏的非对称：他们用 3 年时间建的「难做的基础设施」（自有 ledger、自有 BIN、25 国本地牌照），在 Stablecoin 时代变成了 3 个月就能撬动新市场的杠杆。

这是真正的反直觉之处——Jeeves 看似在 2021–2023 那段时间"走慢路"，实则在偷偷积累一种「可重用的底层抽象」。当 Genius Act 通过、稳定币合法化的那一刻，他们的边际扩张成本从 70 万美元/8 个月骤降到「3 个销售人头」就能开一个秘鲁。

这其实是 AWS 的故事在金融业的重演：亚马逊先为自己建了笨重的基础设施，然后把它变成了对外的商品。Jeeves 的 CMS（中间抽象层）就是它的 AWS——只是没人这么看它。

更出人意料的是：他们之所以能 4 个人做下 30 亿 TPV 的承销，根源不在 AI 模型有多强，而在于他们 3 年前坚持自建 ledger 留下的数据结构是 AI-ready 的。AI 红利不是均等地降临到每个公司头上，它降临到那些"数据本来就干净、结构本来就统一"的公司头上。

二、跨领域连接：Jeeves 正在做的事，本质是"集装箱革命"在金融业的重演

1956 年 Malcolm McLean 发明集装箱时，所有人都觉得这只是个铁盒子。但集装箱真正改变的不是装货方式，而是让港口、卡车、轮船、仓库都被迫围绕一个统一标准重组，最终把全球贸易成本砍掉 90%。

Stablecoin 之于 Jeeves，就是金融业的集装箱： - 以前：墨西哥 peso → 美国 USD 要经过两个 correspondent bank，像 1950 年代的散货船一个个倒货 - 现在：peso 进来立刻打包成 USDC（标准集装箱），任何"港口"（国家、卡组织、银行）都能直接接驳

但真正出乎意料的洞察在这里：Dileep 提到他们卖的时候不提 stablecoin，而是叫"Jeeves Instant Pay"。这正是 McLean 当年的智慧——他从不跟客户讲"集装箱原理"，他卖的是"门到门 3 天送达"。

任何颠覆性基础设施在商业化的第一阶段，都必须把自己伪装成一个产品功能。 等到客户离不开了，再揭示它的本质。这是 Jeeves 与那些"教育市场使用 Web3"的失败者最深的分野。

三、隐藏的系统性真相：Jeeves 的组织结构正在向加密协议趋同

注意这几个看似无关的细节串起来： - 员工从 200 减到 140，营收增 10 倍 - 4 个人做 30 亿 TPV 的承销 - 边际开国成本从 70 万降到 3 个销售人头 - 一个国家的 GM 是「全权本地代理」 - CEO 自己每周开 3 个 stablecoin standup

意外的发现：Jeeves 的组织形态正在从"传统跨国公司"演变成一种类协议结构（protocol-like organization）——CEO 是协议升级提案者，AI 是执行层，本地 GM 是节点验证者，stablecoin 是共识层。

这解释了为什么 Dileep 一再强调"你不能外包 AI 给 Chief AI Officer"。在协议型组织里，节奏（cadence）本身就是核心代码。一个 10x 的 AI 原生公司之所以能碾压 3x 的"传统加 AI"公司，不是因为他们工具更好，而是因为他们的组织时钟频率就是不同的物种。

一个赠予你的额外预言

Dileep 提到的"把应收账款 token 化成链上抵押品换取 60–70% USDC"——这看似只是个融资创新。但如果你把视野拉远：当全球中型企业的现金流都变成可编程的链上对象时，"国家信用"作为商业银行的核心定价权将第一次出现替代品。

Jeeves 不只是在建一家银行，它在无意中成为主权货币让渡商业话语权的早期催化剂之一。这一点连 Dileep 自己在访谈里都没明说，但他说的"money is going to become programmable"已经埋下了这颗种子。

下一个十年最大的金融变革不会发生在华尔街，会发生在阿根廷、巴西、墨西哥这些"被迫提前进入 stablecoin 实战"的市场。这才是 Jeeves 真正押注的东西。

📝 转录文本

Rebuilding Global Banking With Stablecoins and AI | Deep Dives with Jeeves CEO

开场：核心愿景与冲击性数据

The goal with Jeeves is really to build a global business bank that can function in multiple countries.

Stablecoins is a lived experience. It's not something that's a theory. Like in Argentina, 60% of the population use stablecoins. Our revenue has grown 10x. Our volume has grown 8x. This just wouldn't be possible without it. Money is going to become programmable, and hopefully we're at that, you know, nexus of all of this happening.

Often when companies start in financial services, just because there's so much product to build, the tendency is to start with the smaller guys, grow with them, and then eventually earn the credibility to get with the bigger guys. You took the opposite approach. How did you become relevant to relatively large companies fairly quickly in the journey?

Our underwriting team today is four people. That just wouldn't have been possible 2 and 1/2 years ago. You need 15 people just to get that off the ground. That's what's changed. If you are not AI pilled, you're not going to make it.

一、个人背景：从尼日利亚到 Jeeves

Dileep, welcome to the podcast.

Thanks for having me. I'm super excited.

Before we dive into Jeeves, I don't think a lot of listeners know your backstory, which is pretty interesting. You grew up internationally, you've got a technical background, you started another company, sold it for over $100 million before this. Maybe tell us about you and how that led you to starting Jeeves.

Yeah. So I was actually born in Nigeria. I grew up in three or four different continents actually. So we moved from Nigeria to Doha, Qatar. That's where I spent most of my childhood. And this was when Doha was like a one-camel town — like they didn't… I mean, it's funny looking at the airport now which is massive. It used to be an airport like two stalls and plane A or plane B, and you get on one of them and that was the airport. So it's very small.

And then we moved to Florida, outside of Orlando, probably about 25 years ago. What I've kind of noticed is, you know, the company that I'm working on now, Jeeves, is also a global company. So it's almost like a manifestation of my childhood and the fact that I grew up in all of these different places.

I then went to school for engineering. So I have actually two engineering degrees, which now I feel a lot more unleashed with all of the stuff with AI because I can start building. But the funny story is my master's thesis was actually on using genetic algorithms to mimic money markets. And that was basically LLMs — which is basically you'd have an end goal and it would come up with test cases, rank them, and it's just wild. This was in 2006, and it's amazing seeing that full circle 20 years later with everything that's happening with AI.

After that, I started a company that was in the marketing automation space. Right before that, I was at Stanford GSB. That company was PowerInbox, which we sold for about 106 million, and then I started Jeeves. So, you know, basically at this point, if I don't start a company, I'm unemployable.

But yeah, it's been a great experience learning a lot of different things. I think what I love about Jeeves most is it's just such a steep learning curve. Like one day you're doing banking in Brazil, next day you have to be an expert on stablecoin. And if you don't like the learning curve, it can be so exhausting, but if you like it, it's very exciting. That's the biggest thing that I find interesting about the day-to-day.

二、Jeeves 是什么：产品现状

Amazing. So we're going to talk through the backstory, but just to ground us — we're you're 5 years into the company. What is Jeeves today?

So Jeeves is a stablecoin-native financial operating system for global enterprises. We have two core products:

  • First: Expense management. We sell corporate cards that we offer to our companies, and we can do that in 25 countries. Our core markets are Brazil, Colombia, Mexico.
  • Second: Payments. We do full local payments and international payments. Today our international payments — more than 50–60% — settles directly on stablecoin. And that's been powering a lot of the growth that we've seen in the last year.

三、护城河之一：基础设施

One of the most interesting ways to understand Jeeves is through your moats. Now they're moats — I would say they were massive challenges to building the company and getting scale, and now we can classify them in the realm of defensibility. So two big ones: infrastructure and regulation. Start with infrastructure, which is my personal favorite because it looks like unsexy plumbing, but then the decisions there are really what drive the products you're able to offer, specifically the differentiation. And I think where you started in Latin America, an added challenge is, unlike the US, there's no "as a service" for all of these different pieces. So maybe talk us through — what did you splice together, what did you build, and how does that enable you to drive different things?

It's a really good point. Internally we have this saying: difficulty is very defensible — because it's difficult, it's actually defensible. And we've done a lot of difficult things from day one.

Just to give you an example: our core IP — what we consider our core product, so to speak — is not the front-facing product. It's the layer in between. We connect to 50–60 different partners, different vendors, and we abstract all of that information into the central layer. We call it a CMS.

Just to give you an idea of what it can do: - We maintain our own ledger in 25 countries, and that is really hard to do because you have to do that across currencies, across FX. - We issue statements in 25 countries in less than 24 hours. That took us 18 months to figure out. And today we can do it — and that's a core skill set, because then the ledger sits directly with us.

We sit directly on the card stream. A big difference where we play on the card side is we're full principal members, for instance with Mastercard. So when we issue the card, it says "issued by Jeeves" — Jeeves Brazil — there's no bank in between. That obviously requires a certain level of sophistication to be able to run, and we've learned that over time of how to build that. But we try to go very, very close to the infrastructure stack in all of these countries that we play in.

卡发行的四层结构

I'll give one more example on the card side, because I think this might open up how we think about it. When I think about card issuing, I see it as four components:

  1. Top of the funnel — customer acquisition
  2. The processor
  3. The program manager for regulation and compliance
  4. The issuing BIN from Visa or Mastercard that gives you that license to issue the 16 digits

What we do — the first three — sits on our own stack. Even though we have different partners, if we were going from Mexico to Brazil, we only have to change the BIN. If you tomorrow wanted to launch Indonesia, we only have to change the BIN. And now with stablecoin, even the BIN doesn't have to change, because you get one BIN and you carve it up by different countries.

So it's a super exciting time to be building, and part of the reason we're seeing this acceleration on stablecoin is because we did the hard work of building our own infrastructure stack for 3 years.

为什么本地 BIN 重要

Yeah. So for people who are maybe less in the weeds of infrastructure — why would it matter to someone like Nubank that you have a Brazilian BIN versus, you know, a US BIN?

The value is that the customer experience is very seamless. One of the things at Jeeves is: if you log in to Jeeves in Brazil, Mexico, Colombia, it looks the same. You're used to seeing the same cards in the same places. And you can't do that unless you sit directly on the rail.

When we started — which I think you might remember — we actually were shipping US cards.

Remember? Yes.

We were shipping US cards because we didn't have the setup yet. It takes time to get the licenses. It takes time to get the processors. All of those things. Today, in every country we operate in, we have local issuing and local licenses. What that gives us is the experience for companies like a Hotmart, for a Burger King — it's seamless. You don't have to train the team three times for three different logins for three different countries. It looks exactly the same. You can't do that unless you sit on the rail.

Well, and your acceptance is probably higher, you're able to onboard sort of…

Yeah. Our acceptance is super high because we are sitting directly with our own BIN. In Mexico, Colombia, Brazil, we have our own BIN that's just for Jeeves. The second part of that is we can also build in much better card controls — because again, we do the actual authentication when you swipe it, we say yes or no. We also do some of the fraud detection components, obviously with partners like Sardine. But all of that sits directly with us.

四、护城河之二：监管牌照

Okay, infrastructure. The next critical but often tedious topic is regulation. Maybe talk us through — we've been at this for sort of five years at this point. I think you've got a lot of your licenses, you've got another roadmap. What have you built there?

Where we sit, we sell directly to mid-market and enterprise companies. So we do not really sell to the long tail. The reason I bring that up is it's really hard for us to gain the trust of these companies without the right licenses.

  • We are a regulated entity in Mexico. We've filed for our money movement license in Mexico as well.
  • In Brazil, we're actually filing to be a full deposit-taking bank. So it's a very high bar for a license.

If we don't have this license, again, we don't have the full customer flow. We can provide some version of the cards, we can provide some version of the payments. But to really be kind of the global business bank, enterprise bank, financial operating system — you need to own accounts as well. And to own accounts, you need these licenses.

So we have always looked at licenses as a core defensible moat — as in, the product we want to build cannot be built without licenses. The issue with licenses is it takes time. So part of the way I think about building is: how do you get into market quickly, get a lot of market share, and then you just have a team that just every day is building layers and layers of this license. That way the customer experience hopefully is seamless — they see the same thing, but on the back end, the product is a lot better because now the licenses sit directly with us.

牌照与利润率

The second part that is really important with licenses and with infrastructure is it helps us to expand the margin. Our margin has expanded from 40% two years ago to north of 80% right now. You cannot do that unless you own the infrastructure and unless you have the regulatory coverage — because then you get the whole lion's share, and then you pay it out to partners.

So that's the positive side of having all these things. But it's really hard, and you need a team that — that's what they're doing. We go country by country, and even now, I would say we probably need to get four or five more licenses in the regions we're in to get to the stage that we want to get to.

五、为什么直接切入大企业

You mentioned you're a little bit more enterprise versus smaller company, where I think often when companies start in financial services, just because there's so much product to build, the tendency is to start with the smaller guys, grow with them, and then eventually earn the credibility to go with the bigger guys. You took the opposite approach. How did you become relevant to relatively large companies fairly quickly in the journey?

It's funny because we started with a little bit of doing everything for everybody. And then, you know, 2023 was kind of the year that forced focus.

We were realizing that we were just not making that much money in terms of revenue from smaller businesses, and we needed the same support for them. We're a very lean company. We have less people today than we did two years ago, and our revenue has grown about 10x.

What I'm trying to get to is: we're not built to sustain 10,000 small businesses. That's just not our model. What we're built for is these real mid-market enterprise companies that we sell one product to, and then we cross-sell six others, and we increase the retention, we increase the gross profit that we have with these companies.

It was a little bit of: we did everything, and then we made this really hard decision in '23 to say, okay, which is the audience that really resonates with the product, and what's the product we're looking to build? At that point we did have to make the hard call to debank some of the smaller businesses that we had. But I don't think we'd be here today if we didn't make that hard call in 2023.

从卡到支付的产品演进

That started putting the focus on what we are offering. Before '23, we did not have a payments product. When you go sell this to enterprise, payments is a huge, huge part of their business. In fact, card volume is actually much smaller than payments volume — because if you look at share of wallet, AP (accounts payable) is a much bigger box than corporate card.

We started with cards, we started with these versions of credit products. We turned all of that off. We were like, what's our focus? It's enterprise, it's mid-markets. What do they need? They need accounts, they need payments.

Then how do we make sure we only sell into that subset? We have requirements where, if you're below a certain revenue size — and that goes from somewhere between 10 million ARR all the way up to 100 million ARR — those are the two segments we have. We just wouldn't onboard you. We can't onboard you.

That was a hard decision because it also then focuses on companies like Y Combinator, where we came from, and they've been incredibly supportive. We've had to find ways of like, okay, can we squeeze that in, because those are the companies you want to build in, but they tend to be smaller to start.

Yeah, so maybe a special YC exception here.

六、产品打包：从卡到全栈

Customer point — maybe just a good way to understand Jeeves's product suite is to take a company like Nubank, which is Brazil, Mexico, Colombia. You landed with cards. Do you want to just walk through — you land with this and then where does it go?

What's interesting about our product suite is we are global, but we are fully localized. It took us 2 years to figure out how to sell in Brazil — and a big reason we were not selling in Brazil — and Brazil is actually going to be our biggest market this quarter. It really started turning in September of '24, and the reason was: we started localizing the product.

We made it obviously in Portuguese, but it's more than that — it's tying it into the actual way people use the product. Brazil has an incredibly sophisticated financial ecosystem. I would say in some sense even more sophisticated than the US. There's no cash. Everything is digitized. It's instant. In fact, when they come to the US and ACH takes two days, it's almost like a year for sale.

Exactly.

It took us a while to figure out how to localize the product. The reason I think that's important to your question of "how do these companies use us": we usually start with the card and then we sell second and third products.

  • In Mexico and Colombia, that usually moves into Jeeves Pay, which is our payment product.
  • In Brazil, we actually have a very strong travel product.

If you think about Brazil, a big concern there is fraud. It has one of the highest fraud rates in the world. We had customers that would come to us and say, "Hey, if you have cards that are automatically turning themselves off — meaning they're single use — we would use that for all transactions." So we started playing around with: okay, let's build out a cards API that we now provide to these different companies.

When you think about Nubank: you have corporate spend, and then you have travel. Those are the two areas that we work with them on. That travel component is strong in Brazil but might not be as strong in other countries.

So we have this strong primary product, which is usually the card, and then the secondary product changes depending on the region that we're in, and then that tends to be localized.

七、Why Now：稳定币与 AI 的双引擎

We were fortunate enough to lead your Series A. And every investment has a "why now." The "why now" at Jeeves when you started — which I felt pretty strongly — is I had a broad portfolio in Latin America, all who had tens of millions of dollars in their bank accounts and they could not get corporate cards. And if they could, they'd be from incumbents that made US incumbents look like the most tech-forward companies on the planet. So that was a good "why now." I think now you've got two more which are even stronger. AI and stablecoins. And so before we maybe dive into specifically for Jeeves, as you think about the region of LatAm and the countries that you're in, those tailwinds I would argue are even stronger there than in the US.

稳定币：拉美的真实生活方式

Stablecoin — let's start with that one because in Latin America, stablecoins is a lived experience. It's not something that's a theory. Like in Argentina, 60% of the population use stablecoins. That's not something you have to train people how to use.

In the US, it's a little more like, "Okay, why would I use stablecoins? Because I don't quite get it. Because I have a stable US currency. Amazing. That's fantastic." A lot of countries don't have that. So the fact that you have this dollar-denominated coin that you can almost instantly transfer your local currency to is a huge selling point.

What we started realizing — and a big part of this was the Genius Act passing, which started making it more kosher to talk through stablecoin — was that a lot of the infrastructure we had could now be adapted towards stablecoin. Going back to your question earlier: the reason we can do that is because, again, we maintain our own ledger. So we don't have to have two separate ledgers — one for USDC, one for USD. It all feeds into the same thing.

As soon as the Genius Act passed, it was one of those things where I would say it was a little bit founder-mode. I was just like, "This is happening. Like, this is not debatable. We're going to go build this whole thing." In fact, even today — I mean, you can ask our team — we have three standups every week, and I run them directly. Because to me, it's that important that we move everything onto stablecoin. I see that as absolutely the future. And specifically for LatAm, which is one of the highest corridors of stablecoin usage, it just becomes something that people know how to use, and the product becomes a lot better.

卖产品而非卖技术

The second part on stablecoin that I think is very interesting is: when we sell it to these enterprises, we actually don't pitch stablecoin directly. We pitch it as "Jeeves Instant Pay." But the inside is stablecoin.

The reason that's important is: when you talk to a CFO at an enterprise company, they don't care as much on the technology side. They care: can I trust you? Is the money going to show up? Is this something where the cost is better than what I have right now? If they don't think the money is going to show up, it doesn't matter what the technology is — because at the end of the day, they're going to get probably fired.

So we have to win that trust. We almost kind of guarantee it with the Jeeves brand of like, "Hey, you use us for all these other things. Trust us on this, and we'll make sure it shows up." That's how we sell the product in region. That's been a huge driver of the volume we're seeing. Our TPV has increased from about 400 million two years ago to north of 3 billion right now. And we're probably going to touch about 6 billion this year. So it's significantly increased. A big part of that is because we have the stablecoin as the infrastructure in between.

法币轨道 vs 稳定币轨道

So maybe contrast the — and you've got some more innovative products coming out — but to start with the sort of classic cross-border: contrast fiat rails versus stablecoin rails, because you were serving both customers.

If you think about where we were maybe 18 months ago, before we had stablecoin, and you wanted to do a payment between Mexico and the US, you would usually have to have two different correspondent banks in between. You'd send the payment in Mexico, we'd have to make sure that we had a bank partner that could handle it in the US, because in the US you need to know the source of the funds as well.
