So we basically had two different bank partners that had to do a handoff to transfer, let's say, a 1,000 pesos and get it to show up for $50 in the US.

Now what happens is: in Mexico you can send us a 1,000 pesos. We collect it on the ground in Mexico. As soon as we collect it, we release USDC, and that settles instantly. And then we pay out on the US side. The longest leg is actually the US payout — because in Mexico that settles instantly, and the ACH still takes one day, but it's much, much, much faster.

For the end user, we don't sell the stablecoin. We sell accounts payable. We sell: your invoices are reconciled. We sell: put in your tax ID, we'll download your invoices and send international payments. That's what we sell. It just so happens that the infrastructure that it runs on is now a lot better, because we can do that in one hour instead of one day or two days.

We give the end user the choice. If they want to use our SWIFT rails and get the MT103 kind of confirmation, we can do that for you — it will just take longer, but that's your choice. If you want to use a faster version, which we call Jeeves Instant Pay, you can do that in the product. You just have to click a dropdown and select that. So we give you that choice — but a lot of the way we sell it is, it's part of the product.

Yeah — would you like your money in a day or five days?

You'd be surprised how many take one day.

Not surprised. Yeah, exactly. Not surprised at all.

阿根廷：稳定币卡的首发地

Amazing. And then coming back to Argentina, which I think anyone who's been there, lived there, can get a sense of — your money at the end of the year is worth nothing close to what it was at the start of the year. You're about to launch a stablecoin card. Who is that for and how is that going to work?

Taking a step back — and you probably remember this when we first chatted — the goal with Jeeves is really to build an enterprise version of Revolut. A global business bank that can function in multiple countries. To be able to do that, you have to launch multiple countries.

Previously, launching a country is a big undertaking, because you have to first get local entities. You need to get local issuing licenses. You need to be able to collect funds. So it would usually take about 8 months, and that's very fast. We were probably the fastest to be able to do that in 8 months, and that would cost maybe 500–700K to just get spun up.

Now with stablecoin — and Argentina is our first new country launch in 3 years, we're super excited about this — you can do that whole thing much, much faster and much cheaper.

In Argentina, we have our own pay-in rails. So you can pay in on Argentine peso. As soon as it comes in, it converts to USDC. So you instantly get currency volatility protection, which in Argentina, where currency devalues, is a huge, huge selling point.

As soon as it converts to USDC, we can issue corporate cards on it. And now if you think about our core product, it's not necessarily just the stablecoin or the payments. We sell the layer on top. We sell the operating system. So now we can sell budgets. We can sell approval flows. We can sell teams. We can sell card controls. We can sell fraud controls. All of that can now work.

What's really cool about this setup and very unique compared to a lot of other stablecoin cards in the market is: if you swipe that card in Argentina, it does not have an FX fee. The reason that's important is the customers we go after are mid-market and enterprise customers. They have 50 employees. You're not going to get a card for 50 employees if you're going to get charged 2% when they buy a Coke. That's just not going to work.

So it's a really cool version of a product that we've used a lot of different technologies to piece together, but now allows us to sell the full product suite in Argentina.

阿根廷作为蓝图

Tying this back to what Argentina is going to become: it's going to become the blueprint for other countries.

Take Peru, for instance. Nobody's going to launch an expense management suite for Peru. It's too small of a TAM. Now we can. I basically have to staff two salespeople in Peru. They'll sell this product out there. I can onboard 10 companies and it can still be a profitable market.

The cost is completely collapsed in a way that previously no one's going to launch in Peru. Now we can launch in Peru because my cost is three sales headcounts. It's amazing.

八、AI：另一个引擎

So I want to shift gears a little bit to AI, which I think has been the other "oh thank god." Like for instance, I think one of the things we all underestimated was just KYB in, for instance, Mexico — and just the volume of documents you need to collect, what you need to link to languages.

Exactly. So that is an example. But maybe talk through — where are you using it in Jeeves, and then what is the impact that it's had on the business?

It's one of those things where, just like a lot of companies, we've tried to figure out how to incorporate it across the board. I would say some functions are seeing a lot more adoption than other functions.

承销：4 人做 30 亿 TPV

If you think about anything tied to document ingesting, we've seen a lot of success there. So I'll give you an example. Our core product is like a 30-day charge card — like a Ramp or Brex. That still requires us to cover the customer spend for 30 days. So it's not a credit card. You can't keep it open. You have to pay us on day 31. But for 30 days, we are covering your spend. That requires underwriting.

Our underwriting team today is four people, and that team is doing 2–3 billion in TPV. That just wouldn't have been possible 2 and a half years ago. You need 15 people just to get that off the ground. That's what's changed. The models are better. It's self-learning. One person that's really smart can make this work in multiple different regions.

That's one example, but we're seeing that across the board. The only real headcounts we're adding are some components of engineering and product, and then go-to-market. Go-to-market is one area where we haven't had as much success using AI — we're trying different components for enriching data, etc. So we do hire humans there. But on almost all the other functions, we're seeing a significant uplift using AI.

The biggest number is: our headcount today is 140 people, and two years ago it was 200 people. Our revenue has significantly increased, and that's why you see that margin expansion. Basically revenue is going up and costs are the same or going down.

客户服务与本地化细节

Well, and customer service, I think, is another notoriously challenging in multiple different languages, multiple different countries.

Customer service is one where we do have our own internal kind of chatbot. We call it Lenora. It handles most of what people bring up.

I think what's interesting — and it's taken us a little while to learn this — is just the cultural nuances. Everybody's got OCR, but it took us a while to figure out that in Brazil they use the commas and the dots interchangeably. So when you're doing underwriting and you see the commas, the commas are actually the dots, and vice versa. That training is what needs specialization, and that's again specialized to just Jeeves and what we do.

So yeah, it is something where we've seen it across the board: customer service for sure, KYB, document ingestion is another area that we've been using that as well.

九、未来：链上化与可编程货币

Great. And so now I want to fast forward a little bit to the future. You gave us a little bit of a preview about this, right? It's getting easier to launch more countries, things are coming more global. As you think of a global business bank two years from now, five years from now — what does that look like? What might be a little less intuitive to anybody?

I actually think a lot more is going to go on-chain. There are two or three things that we're thinking about.

1. 统一资金池

If you think about a business like ours, we have to still maintain capital pools in different markets. We have to maintain pesos, BRL, Mexican pesos, Colombian pesos, etc. We maintain a lot.

What would be very interesting — which is what we're starting to dabble into — is actually just keeping a single source of USDC or USDT and minting when we need it. That way the cost of capital significantly drops, and we don't have to maintain these pools.

When I think about where this goes from an infrastructure perspective, I actually see a lot of the existing stack moving on-chain.

2. 应收账款的链上抵押

The second part is, going back to that whole 1–2–3 billion TPV that we do: is there a world where we can start collateralizing that on-chain? We basically take the receivables, put it on chain, get 60–70% against that on USDC. It doesn't remove our existing capital sources, but it helps supplement that with on-chain USDC.

When I think about where the infrastructure is going, I see the entire company stack moving onto stablecoin in the next 2–3 years. I think it's actually going to make it a much more efficient company that we can operate.

3. 可编程货币与 Agent

The last one — which we've been dabbling with right now — is basically thinking of programmable money. You have, obviously, the agents that are going to be doing purchasing from MCP, CLI, etc. But how do you make that something that's very usable for these different companies that we service?

Today we do have two agents that we sell. We have a lot more, but those are the ones that resonate the most:

  • Reconciliation agent — it basically looks at the purchases you have and helps you match them one-to-one.
  • GL coding agent — if you think about enterprises, a big function is literally someone's job is to look at every transaction and put a code on it so your ERP can read it. We trained an agent to be able to do that with 99% accuracy or better.

One of the huge advantages we have is the companies that we sell to look like Jeeves. We are operating in 12 countries. We have 60+ bank accounts. We have a sophisticated finance team. We operate in LatAm. So if our finance team can use it and they find it valuable, I'm pretty confident I can sell that product.

As I look at the next 2, 3, 4, 5 years, I see this convergence of stablecoin, I see the convergence of agents. Money is going to become programmable, and hopefully we're at that nexus of all of this happening.

十、创始人个人议题

不断重塑超能力

All right, I want to wrap maybe with a couple more personal questions about you. I think there's going to be more global businesses built. Huge opportunities there, very challenging. You've talked pretty publicly about how you need to almost reinvent your superpower every six to eight months. What do you think your superpower was when you started, and how is that evolving?

I think a lot of founders try to do a lot of different things when they start. The way I see it: your job is to focus on one or two maximum metrics that matter, and then you need to be like a monkey with a stick — how do you move that metric, and everything else doesn't matter. Once those metrics are moving, then you figure out other things to do.

I'll give you an example. When we started, our number one metric was spend. It's not revenue. It's not necessarily "does it have margin." It's: can I get someone to use this card in Latin America? I don't know — I did not grow up in Latin America. Actually, when I first flew to Mexico City, it was kind of dawning on me. I'm like, "I'm going to launch this product in Mexico. That was the second time I was…"

Spanish is a little rusty.

But that was the focus. It was just: can we get people to use the cards? It started there. We were clearly getting acceleration on the spend, and then we figured out all the other things — revenue, margin, etc. A lot of times founders try to do everything in the beginning, and in the beginning you need to just focus on what matters to get you from point A to point B.

70% 全能法则

The second part — which is where I think most founders need to really focus on — is you have to be 70% good at almost every function. And you just have to rotate. That's your job.

Today your job is product. Today I spent a lot of time on product engineering — it's just so important with stablecoin. Two years ago, it was finance, because we were navigating '23 and interest rates had shot up 600%. Every day I was on these calls, and that was a job. Now we have an amazing CFO, amazing finance team. So now I'm like, I don't need to spend that much time.

As a CEO, as a founder, your job is to do whatever job is needed for the company to get to the next stage. A lot of times founders are like, "Oh, I can't do sales." It's like, nobody can do sales. You just learn what you need to do. And then you hire someone better than you — that's also a skill. But you have to be dangerous enough to know what you're looking at. I think that's now even more easier with AI. If people can't do that now, I don't know what to say.

Yep. Even if you don't have an AI thesis, you should be able to lead.

It's amazing.

人才哲学

And then maybe — I think the other biggest challenge building a company anywhere is talent. How do you think about talent? What is Jeeves's philosophy, especially being in the number of countries that you're in?

We need local talent to succeed. That's the first hire we do in any geo. We actually in Argentina just hired this person — they are what we call a local GM. They have to be local. They have to know the market. They have to live there, because I'm not the one selling on the ground. So that's always the first hire.

What we look for — we have this internal bar, we call it relentlessness. There's no real playbook for what we're doing. There's different types of playbooks, but no one's launching this in 25 countries with multiple product stacks, and everyone's like, "Are you guys doing too much, and are you guys doing too little?" But that's what we do. That's what we offer. So you need to have this very high motor that is constantly just going.

The good thing with a company like Jeeves is you'll know within probably a week if this is the right fit. Because it's either going to be like, "Holy s***, I'm learning so much," or it's going to be like, "I don't know what's happening, I feel like I'm drowning every day." A lot of people cycle out fairly quickly, which I think is fine. It might not be the right fit. So we really push that engine part of like — you have to just have this high bar and keep going.

第二代领导团队

The second part here is: one thing we've been able to do is bring in good seasoned leaders for the second cycle of Jeeves. The first cycle is a zero-to-one cycle. This year we should be crossing hopefully 100 million in top-line ARR, and we need a second set of leaders. I think we've done a very good job, at least so far, of getting those seasoned leaders:

  • We brought in a CRO from Brex.
  • We brought in a risk officer from Capital One that joined us last year.
  • Our CFO took Pioneer public.

Getting those people to join has really upped the talent bar in the company as well.

十一、AI 原生 vs AI 改造：CEO 必须 AI-pilled

Very focused on stablecoins. You've also been chief AI officer of Jeeves. I think it is a huge advantage if you started pre-AI, you have the distribution, and then you can use that to really drive it forward. I think the challenge though is getting the company to be AI native. So maybe talk through: how do you make that happen?

It's a good question. Today we are at 140 people. We were at 200 people 2 years ago, and our revenue has grown 10x. Our volume has grown 8x. So clearly it's accelerating, and it's accelerating with less people. This just wouldn't be possible without AI.

So I'll walk through some concrete examples where we use it.

承销 / 客服 / KYB

Underwriting: We do pretty serious underwriting for any company that comes to us — because just like a Ramp or Brex, we have to give them 30 days of spend, and at the end of 30 days, they have to pay us back. So we need to know they're good to pay us back on day 31. Today we have a team of four people that does this underwriting across all these regions. That just simply wouldn't have been possible. Even two years ago, you needed 15 people just to get the underwriting going. Better models, self-learning models, self-training models. We have a lot of data, and now the models are actually keeping up with the data. That's a huge unlock.

Customer service: Same thing. We have maybe three people on the customer service team, and we have to handle different languages. We have to handle people that talked about payments, but then also cards, and also AP. We just are starting to test an AR product. It's a very small team. Wouldn't be possible without training what we have on the customer service side.

KYB and document ingestion: This is an area where we had to build our own version of an OCR machine. Even if you can do Spanish and Portuguese, there are little nuances. Like in Brazil, the commas and the dots are intertwined. If you look at something and it says $2,000, it's actually $200,000 because the comma and the dot is switched. It took us a while to train our models to be able to do that.

必须自有核心基础设施

Just like your question on the infrastructure, we want to own this core infrastructure ourselves. I think without that, we will not be able to succeed.

这件事必须 CEO 自己来

That to me falls on the founder and the CEO. If you are not AI-pilled, you're not going to make it.

Part of what I've been pushing internally is just: look, we are pre-AI, and we are trying to make that jump. If you think you're fast at 3x, there are native AI companies that are moving at 10x. So you're still slow. You need to be 20x just to keep up with the 10x.

Making that pace — if you don't have that pace yourself and you don't set that clock beat, it's not going to happen. You can't outsource this. You can't hire a chief AI person. You can hire that, and there are many very smart people. But that energy, that pace has to come from the CEO.

Thankfully, I have a little bit of a technical background. So I'm one of those maybe annoyingly dangerous CEOs where I'm like, "Oh, I'm good enough to tell you what to do, but then you can go figure out what to do." I feel so unblocked as an ex-engineer now with AI, because I feel like I can actually start building.

As soon as Open Claude came out, I built the first Open Claude for Jeeves — not because I'm some genius, but if I don't do it, everyone's going to say, "Hey, what's the security, etc." There are real security things you need to figure out. But if you don't show that you can build an Open Claude, and someone can tag it in a WhatsApp channel and it's going to give you jokes or whatever you want to do, then people just don't feel like they can do it themselves.

It's so important. Everybody says the same thing: you don't have enough time, etc. You have to make the time. If it's important for the company to succeed, you need AI, and if it's important to do AI, you need to figure out the time.

Candidly, it helps that it's actually very exciting building. It doesn't feel like a chore. I wish I had more time, because there's just so much I can do. Every week, it feels like Anthropic is releasing something else, and I'm like, "Give me one day so I can catch up to this."

收尾

Amazing. Well, thank you very much for joining us. I'm sure the great enterprises of Peru will be very happy to hear you can now economically launch there. Appreciate you coming on.

Thanks for having me. This is fantastic. And yeah, if there are companies out there that are looking to build with stablecoin and AI, we'd love for them to try us and give us feedback. Send an email directly to me, and I'll respond probably pretty quickly.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一、最反直觉的发现：「困难即护城河」其实是一种**反向时间套利**
Dileep 说"difficulty is defensible"，听起来像创业者的自我安慰，但仔细看 Jeeves 的数据会发现一个隐藏的非对称：**他们用 3 年时间建的「难做的基础设施」（自有 ledger、自有 BIN、25 国本地牌照），在 Stablecoin 时代变成了 3 个月就能撬动新市场的杠杆**。
这是真正的反直觉之处——Jeeves 看似在 2021–2023 那段时间"走慢路"，实则在偷偷积累一种「可重用的底层抽象」。当 Genius Act 通过、稳定币合法化的那一刻，他们的边际扩张成本从 70 万美元/8 个月骤降到「3 个销售人头」就能开一个秘鲁。
> **这其实是 AWS 的故事在金融业的重演**：亚马逊先为自己建了笨重的基础设施，然后把它变成了对外的商品。Jeeves 的 CMS（中间抽象层）就是它的 AWS——只是没人这么看它。
更出人意料的是：他们之所以能 4 个人做下 30 亿 TPV 的承销，**根源不在 AI 模型有多强，而在于他们 3 年前坚持自建 led

item.title: [Sequoia Capital] Neuralink's DJ Seo: Inside the Race to Connect Brains and AI
item.dc:creator: Sequoia Capital
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、最让人意外的观察：Neuralink 真正的护城河不是芯片，而是"照护者经济学"

大多数报道聚焦于电极密度、机器人手术、神经解码算法。但 DJ 在被问及"最难忘的患者故事"时，把镜头转向了Nolan 的母亲 Mia、Brad 的妻子 Tiffany、Ken 的妻子 Cheryl——照护者。这不是煽情的偏题，而是一个被严重低估的系统性事实：

BCI 的临床价值函数里，"照护者解放的小时数"可能是比"光标移动精度"更关键的变量。 一个 ALS 患者重新获得 10% 的数字自主权，背后释放出的是一位 24/7 照护者的人生。这意味着 Neuralink 真正在重新分配的，不是带宽，而是家庭内部的时间主权。从这个角度看，BCI 的市场规模算法应该乘以"隐性照护劳动 GDP"（在美国估值约 6000 亿美元/年），而不是仅仅按患者数计算。这是华尔街分析师集体盲视的一个数量级。

二、跨领域连接：Elon 的"全绿灯调度"= 编译器的死代码消除

DJ 描述的"all green light schedule"思维框架——剥离一切人为瓶颈，问"物理极限下到底多快"——和编译器优化里的"死代码消除 + 常量折叠"是同构的。

更深一层的连接：这其实是把物理学家做费米估算的肌肉移植到了项目管理上。费米问题的精髓是"先得到正确的数量级，再讨论修正项"。而大多数组织反着来——先讨论修正项（合规、流程、汇报链），永远到不了数量级。DJ 说"80-90% 的现状只是因为没人从这个视角想过"，这句话和 Carlo Rovelli 描述物理学突破时说的"我们以为是定律的东西，其实只是没人质问的习惯"——惊人地同源。

更出人意料的延伸：这套方法论之所以在 SpaceX、Tesla、Neuralink 都奏效，而在大多数公司翻车，是因为它需要一个前提条件——团队必须有能力区分"物理约束"和"社会约束"。这种能力在受过严格物理/EE 训练的工程师里是默认能力，在 MBA 主导的组织里几乎是绝迹技能。所以"Elon 时间"本质上是一个人才筛选机制，而不是管理工具。

三、隐藏的系统性真相：BCI 的终局不是"打字更快"，而是"语言的废除"

DJ 说了一句几乎被一笑而过、但极度颠覆的话：

"我们当前的系统仍在把神经意图转换成遗留系统——键盘、鼠标、语言。"

注意：他把自然语言和键盘鼠标并列归为"legacy system"。

这是整场对话最激进的论断，没有之一。它意味着：

  • 语言不是思维的载体，而是思维的有损压缩格式。我们用 2000 年前为农业社会优化的串行符号系统，传输高维并行的神经状态——这就像用电报传输 4K 视频。
  • 一旦 BCI 能在"raw intent"层互联，人类将首次面对"后语言交流"。这不是科幻，是带宽问题——人脑内部的信息流速估计在 10^11 bit/s 量级，口语输出约 39 bit/s。我们活在一个8 个数量级的压缩损失里而不自知。

真正的"啊哈时刻"：如果 LLM 是在压缩后的语言语料上训练的"二阶模型"，那么在 BCI 神经基础模型（DJ 提到的 neural foundational model）上训练的 AI 将是一阶模型——直接学习思维的潜流形，而非思维的语言投影。这意味着当前所有基于文本的 AI 对齐研究，可能都是在错误的抽象层上做的。真正的对齐战场会移到神经流形层。

跨领域连接收尾：这件事在哲学史上有一个奇特的回响——维特根斯坦后期放弃"语言图像论"，认为语言无法准确映射思维。一百年后，一位韩裔工程师在沙丘山路上轻描淡写地说"语言是 legacy system"，宣告了维特根斯坦的胜利。

📝 转录文本

开场介绍

Okay, guys, this one needs really no introduction. We've got our brilliant partner Shawn Maguire, president of Neuralink DJ. Come on up, guys. Thank you for coming. Welcome, Neuralink.

[applause]

We're actually going to stay on the side cuz we're going to play a 2-minute video just showing how badass DJ and his team are.

视频片段：Telepathy 与患者故事

We're starting off with reducing human suffering. Our first product is called Telepathy and that enables someone [music] who lost the ability to command their body to be able to communicate with a computer.

I'm thinking [music] and a cursor is moving on a screen. It blew my mind. It's not a matter of if I can do something, but like [music] how. It's been nothing short of miraculous.

Before the implant, I was locked in, non-verbal, quadriplegic. Now I control my computer just by thinking and the rewards have been immense for me.

ALS hit you like a pound of bricks. I am not able to do things that other dads can, but now he thinks it's so [music] cool that I can do things that other dads cannot. It's great to control a computer. That's a huge step.

Convoy 团队与机械臂

The convoy team [music] is the team at Neuralink that's working on assistive robotic devices controlled by the brain implant, physical independence in the real world.

I was moving the different directions, the different axes [music] with — I don't know, it's pretty incredible just to think about. Really good feeling seeing what those things capable of. It was incredible [music] to be able to just gesture with an arm again. Gaining functionality [music] that I thought was gone forever was so incredibly life-changing.

声音的回归

Like most ALS patients, Kenneth was losing his ability to speak. The real value of the brain-computer interface is that it would give Kenneth [music] his voice back.

Hello. There we go. I'm talking to you with my mind. Everyone gets his own color. Will be blue. You're going to do that? Yes please. I'm done with my turn.

Blindsight 与未来展望

Our next product is Blindsight, which will enable those who have total loss of vision — including they've lost their eyes or the optic nerve — to be able to see again.

The future of this technology feels almost unlimited. We are going to continue advancing the quality of the sensors, the number [music] of the sensors. Surgery will advance. Robotics is going to advance. And we are finding ways to apply it across all regions of the brain as we find new ways to refine and enhance its capabilities.

It really feels like the sky's the limit for what we can do to help. How does it feel, Audrey? Life-changing. [music] Life-changing. I could not ask for more. [music] All with my mind.

Mhm. All with my mind.

[applause] [applause]

主持人感言

We were cracking a lot of jokes before that video, but I mean, honestly, that brought tears to my eyes. This is one of the most inspiring projects in the world. It's incredibly difficult what they're doing and I mean, they're truly saving people. They're changing their lives. They're opening up possibilities that had been lost to them. It really is remarkable, DJ.

问答一：2016 年的起点与 AI 的中心位置

So, DJ co-founded Neuralink with Elon Musk and a few other people back in 2016. It's a different era in so many ways. A lot has happened in AI since then. Can you just tell us more, DJ? What made you drop everything in 2016 to build BCIs? And, you know, how were you guys thinking about AI back then?

Uh first of all, thanks for having me. It's a pleasure to be here. I would say AI has really been central to the origin story of Neuralink. You know, really the key insight back then was sort of the the IO bottleneck between the human output and AI capabilities. And, you know, even now talking about sort of bridging that gap seems a little crazy and wild. Although I guess like with every passing week, it seems more real and real. But certainly back in 2016, 10 years ago, it sounded insane.

And, you know, as far as I guess my personal involvement in this journey, I guess even as a kid, I was super fascinated by the brain. I mean, how can you not? It's the most interesting compute that we all carry. It does incredible things. It's the only form of general intelligence that we know to date. And as an engineer, I always wanted to just understand what makes us us and really try to explore the inner cosmos, so to speak.

And academically, I got introduced to brain-computer interface or brain-machine interface in the late 2000s. This is a couple years after first human got implanted with the Utah array, which is this rigid silicon shank with sort of the through-the-skin port.

[snorts]

And just doing something incredible. Someone who was quadriplegic and be able to regain some of that autonomy. And that was very inspirational. At the time I was studying at Caltech. Go Beavers.

Go Beavers, yeah. He was more hardcore though. The undergrads are way smarter than the grad students.

[laughter]

I'd beg to differ, but —

[gasps]

— I was focused around building miniaturized low-power electronics. And went to Berkeley to pursue PhD in that program. Really with the eye towards: can you sort of build a lot of the principles from semiconductor to miniaturize these systems. Take it out of the lab setting, which was kind of where things were, into the real world. And I think when I met Elon and the team towards the tail end of my PhD program, I mean, just the sheer ambitiousness and the scale of this project was something that I could not say no to.

问答二：被低估的 Neuralink

So, something that's actually surprising me — a lot of people when I ask them about Neuralink, a lot of people have never even heard of the company. Even really smart people often times they're not aware of how far along you guys are. We just saw a video, you have 20-plus patients where you've changed their lives. What are some of the things you think smart people most misunderstand or underappreciate about the company?

Yeah, I think often times for those that have heard about Neuralink, they do kind of see these patient stories on X and also it's kind of natural to think about the implanted device that's recording neural intent and then some sort of algorithm — neural decoding — to convert those neural intent to something useful for the patients. So I think that part is fairly well known.

I think the part that's underappreciated — and I think this is really where Elon magic kind of sprinkles in — is, you know, even at the founding and day one of the company, we had scale in mind. We had not just the device, but all of the infrastructure around it to be able to do the surgery, do the deployment, build really, really hard factories for building these devices.

And, you know, I think that sheer scale obviously requires a lot of capital and a lot of boldness. But that formula seems to work really, really well. Vertical integration is something that is really the lifeblood of Neuralink and Elon companies, and what really enables us to have that fast iteration loop from design, develop, deploy, and then just being able to stack literally best talent across the entire tech stack.

And I think the sheer scale of even the robotic surgery that we had in mind — of making it as a LASIK surgery that can be deployed to millions and billions of people eventually — I think it's something that people fully do not appreciate, and there's a lot of effort that goes into building that machine.

Just in case anyone didn't understand it, these guys build surgical robots. And just on the robot side itself, there's unbelievable breakthroughs that make this a very scalable approach and technology. But this is kind of my experience as well — people underappreciate when you build for scale from the beginning, it seems like you're going slow cuz you're building stuff under the waterline. And then once the iceberg pops over the waterline, it just becomes massive so quickly, and I think people are going to be surprised by — once this thing really ramps — how quickly it goes.

Yeah, and the reality also is that these things take time. Like the world of atoms is very unforgiving and I think doing these sort of investments, building out the team, having that muscle, having those hard lessons that you can learn from building physical things is invaluable.

问答三：最难忘的患者故事——以及照护者

So you guys now have over 20 human patients. Is there a particular patient story you can tell us about that inspires you the most or is the most memorable for you?

Yeah, it's really hard to pick one. I think all these patients and participants that [clears throat] we have worked with — they have incredible stories, obviously. And there are very special moments that I do have the privilege of witnessing, especially when they I guess first encounter that digital barrier drop with the Neuralink, and that's always a very special moment for the participants as well as the family.

The thing that I will say though is that oftentimes I think what sometimes becomes underappreciated is the role of the caregivers and their family members — usually their family members. You know, it's people like Nolan's mother, Mia, Brad's wife, Tiffany, and Ken's wife Cheryl, where I think it's a really powerful human story of love, sacrifice, and resilience that I think is really inspiring to me.

一个哲学切线

And if I were to maybe take a slight philosophical tangent — one of my core beliefs and life philosophies is that a lot of our fulfillment comes from helping others. And the reason I believe that's true is, you know, similar to how your present self is very different than your past and future selves. Sometimes maybe not unlike how others are different. And if your goal and fulfillment comes from striving to do best for yourself, I do think that you can apply the same principle to say: if you strive to help others, there's a lot of fulfillment that comes in.

And because I believe that this is something that — I personally as well as many others at Neuralink find extreme fulfillment being able to help those that really cannot help themselves. And I think it's an extremely inspiring and motivating thing to kind of stand at the team-human side and being able to ignite that sort of fire of hope — which is something that we hear from our future or current participants and future patients every day.

问答四：Blindsight 产品

So, in the video we watched in the beginning, it talked about this next product, Blindsight. What can you tell us about Blindsight? What's coming?

Yeah, I guess first of all, Blindsight is a completely different paradigm shift than our motor prosthesis. So this will be for patients that are blind or have total loss of vision from diseases of their eyes or optic nerves, where you would have an external camera that captures the scene and then be able to directly write in the back of your head in the visual cortex — which, through electrical stimulation that changes polarization environment of the neurons, can create these things called phosphenes. And more electrodes you have, more pixels you have essentially, and be able to regain vision.

And this is something that we're very excited about. Where it is right now is that we have currently the next-gen version that we want to go to human with in preclinical testing. So, hopefully — you know, I might eat my word — but hopefully by the —

Do not need to make any predictions that you're uncomfortable making.

— end of this year.

问答五：BCI 与 AI 的交汇

So, everyone in this room is thinking about AI. It's a stacked audience. How do you think things play out at the interface of BCIs and AI? With the simplest thing being just, you know, thinking straight to Grok prompts —

[laughter]

— I'm sure no one has this.

Yeah, I'd be careful what you wish for, I guess. I do think in some time horizon, AI basically becomes an exocortex similar to our neocortex, which is basically a layer above our limbic system. And really that's what kind of drives a lot of the cognitive abilities that we have.

And, you know, it's ultimately all about the bandwidth. It's really all about that interface. And I think if you really think about the ultimate ceiling of this technology, I think it's really enabling direct uncompressed high-fidelity and multimodal transfer of concepts. The "I learned kung fu in the Matrix" — that's one, but even maybe beyond that, right?

You know, this is obviously still far out in the future. Our current system still is converting neural intents into legacy systems like keyboard and mouse control and language. But I think the real breakthrough is going to happen when you can bypass all of that and be able to sort of compute on the raw intent itself. And I think it's actually not super distant in the future.

In many ways, I think we're seeing this with modern AI systems where these transformer architectures are doing incredible things. There's nothing fundamentally that prevents them from, say, training on and learning about the actual latent manifolds of the neural systems. And I think the key component here really is scale.

I think again, we're seeing this with AI revolution where things just seem impossible without scale, but things just become inevitable with scale. And I think similar things are going to happen — and you know, even at our scale of 20 or so participants, we're starting to see some interesting results in building what's called a neural foundational model, where you have these state-of-the-art LLM models or transformer networks that are being fine-tuned with neural data. And there's a lot of really interesting patterns and things that it's learning that's very counterintuitive.

So I think being able to sort of think about — and again, I think this is where scale is key — that's where in the world of atoms it's very unforgiving and there's a lot of work to be done on the ground.

问答六：向 Elon 学到了什么

What is something you've learned from working with Elon? And bonus points if it's something underappreciated by the public.

Yeah, I think I certainly also had a different conception of what Elon time is. The insanely aggressive schedule. What I've learned is that it's not really some arbitrary management tactic, but it's really used as a rigorous engineering and first-principle tool.

All-Green-Light Schedule

You know, again, this is more applicable in the world of atoms. But we have this concept called all-green-light schedule, which is kind of forcing us to think about: if every single light is green, you know, there's no administrative burdens or delays or no shipping delays or risk or no legacy bottlenecks — how fast can you build it?

And when you think from that point of view and vantage point, and literally strip away any man-made bottlenecks, and you ask a question of "how fast can you actually manufacture a chip? How fast can you design a robot and test it?" — it turns out, like, I don't know what the right number is, but at least 80–90% of it is things that you think are the way it is just cuz you don't really think about it from that perspective.

So I think having that ability to frame everything from what is physically possible — first-principle baseline for building something like that — I think it's a really useful framework for thinking.

I should warn people that this is not for everyone, and it obviously comes at a cost in terms of shifting priorities and insane stress sometimes, and cultural ramification of having this sort of system. But when it works — boy, it's something that is very empowering. And I think it's also just like the best thing ever to just see it unfold.

When it works, you get reusable rockets and electric vehicles.

问答七：招聘

I'm going to ask one more question and then open it up to the audience. And this is a layup. You know, part of how I convinced DJ to come — cuz this guy is very hard — was, you know, that maybe we can help him with recruiting. There's all these AI foundation model representatives here where everyone's competing against each other. It's hard to do recruiting. This is something different. They're on an island that's scaling brain-computer interfaces.

The question for DJ is: are there any particular roles that would be very helpful to fill? And for any of you, if you have brilliant neuroscience friends or people doing BCIs at Stanford or Berkeley or anywhere — send them to me and I'll screen them and then send them to DJ. Any roles that are particularly burning?

Yeah, there's a saying — I'll answer that question, but I'll start by saying that there's a saying at Neuralink that you don't have to be a brain surgeon to work at Neuralink. We really don't require any background in neuroscience. Most people — and especially like some of the best people at the company — are just hardcore engineers. So I'll state that we're just in general looking for hardcore engineers, and you can learn the neuroscience, and we'll learn as we go. And a lot of our problems that we're solving are real hardcore engineering challenges in the manufacturing and the robotics and things like that.

I would say in their software and firmware right now is a critical role for us to fill. And as I mentioned, I think we're starting to enter into an era where the scale of the data that we're getting, the quality of the data that we're getting, is extremely interesting to train some of the most state-of-the-art models. And there's also some inherent challenges associated with how do you even label this dataset? Like how do you actually know what the human true intent is? — unless how do you design it in a way such that you can sort of clean up the data, which is obviously a big part of AI.

So any really smart person that doesn't want to work at a frontier lab.

观众问答一：何时深耕，何时扩张

We have 4 minutes for questions. Eunice is bringing you a box.

[snorts]

Question on how you choose when to go deep on a product versus expanding to others — like what you built with telepathy seems like already a transformational product, and yet you're already kind of on to the next thing. Like how do you decide when to expand into another area, and what are the biggest blockers right now to taking even what you've already built and making that more widely available?

Yeah, that's a great question. Our whole strategy is "beachhead and then expand," and then we try to make everything generalizable enough so that you don't just build a very specialized system for motor cortex and visual cortex, auditory cortex, etc.

For us, there is a pretty huge bottleneck that is difficult to overcome, which is biology. And things that are somewhat easier to overcome but still difficult is regulatory and payment and market dynamics. So a lot of our strategy sort of hinges on: what is the optimal time at which we should put some amount of resources so we can pipeline the crap out of things, so that once one of them is approved — which is going to be the first and the hardest one to get approval — to build that clinical evidence of safety, you can then point to it, go through a much faster process that's PMA supplements, 510(k) and things like that. But in order to do that, you want to build up some amount of preclinical and clinical data. So that's kind of how we think about the timing of launching different products.

观众问答二：增强 vs 修复

Dan: So the video was incredibly moving, and I guess a lot of what you talked about — maybe these [clears throat] are not exactly the right words, but are like, their piece for patients that have diseases, and that's incredible. And then Shawn mentioned something about "I know kung fu." I'm wondering — is there a near, or I'm curious what the near and maybe even longer-term view of more things that are optional and for augmentation, as opposed to fixing problems?

Yeah, certainly our focus right now is restoration of lost function, and this is obviously a very serious medical device and serious surgery that you would have to go through. The way I think about all of this is that at the end of the day it's all about benefit-risk. And that's how everything's framed. Obviously for people that are quadriplegic, their risk appetite for potential benefit is much much higher.

I do think that it's still not entirely clear what that transition to non-medical use case would be. At what point do you cross the benefit-risk for someone who's otherwise healthy? Again, I think we have some ideas as to what that benefit side would be, and then obviously figuring out a way to reduce the risk as well.

But one thing I will say is that there's a wonderful thing called off-label use once everything is approved — where if you can find a neurosurgeon, if you can find a way to pay for it yourself, you could get it for someone who's otherwise healthy. So I do think that once we get to market, there may be some handful of people that might walk around with a Neuralink.

快问快答：意识难题

We have 30 seconds to rapid-fire question, then rapid-fire answer, and we're done.

Do you think the hard problem of consciousness is a hard problem, and if you do, is there a pathway to solving it?

Yeah, hard problem of consciousness is an extremely hard problem. I do think that if you are able to inject new senses, there may be ways to quantitatively understand that.

That was very fast. DJ — one of the most inspiring companies in the world. Thank you for being here. Really appreciate it.

[applause]

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一、最让人意外的观察：Neuralink 真正的护城河不是芯片，而是"照护者经济学"
大多数报道聚焦于电极密度、机器人手术、神经解码算法。但 DJ 在被问及"最难忘的患者故事"时，把镜头转向了**Nolan 的母亲 Mia、Brad 的妻子 Tiffany、Ken 的妻子 Cheryl**——照护者。这不是煽情的偏题，而是一个被严重低估的系统性事实：
**BCI 的临床价值函数里，"照护者解放的小时数"可能是比"光标移动精度"更关键的变量。** 一个 ALS 患者重新获得 10% 的数字自主权，背后释放出的是一位 24/7 照护者的人生。这意味着 Neuralink 真正在重新分配的，不是带宽，而是**家庭内部的时间主权**。从这个角度看，BCI 的市场规模算法应该乘以"隐性照护劳动 GDP"（在美国估值约 6000 亿美元/年），而不是仅仅按患者数计算。这是华尔街分析师集体盲视的一个数量级。
## 二、跨领域连接：Elon 的"全绿灯调度"= 编译器的死代码消除
DJ 描述的"all green light schedule"思维框架——剥离一切人为瓶颈，问"物理极限下

item.title: [No Priors: AI, Machine Learning, Tech, & Startups] Building an AI Guardian for Enterprise with Onyx Security CEO Maxim Bar Kogan
item.dc:creator: No Priors: AI, Machine Learning, Tech, & Startups
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、最反直觉的悖论：安全的核心矛盾从"限权"变成了"放权"

传统企业安全的第一性原理是最小权限原则——不管系统多聪明，都只给它"刚好够用"的权限。但 Maxim 在访谈中无意间揭示了一个范式断裂：人们购买 Claude Code 的根本动机，恰恰是希望它拥有"我自己的全部权限"，这样我们才能"交代它做事、去吃个午饭、回来看到事情已经完成"。

这意味着 AI Agent 是历史上第一类用户主动放弃权限边界的软件。Identity Security、Endpoint Security、API Gateway 这套统治了企业安全二十年的体系，在这个时刻全部失效——不是因为它们技术落后，而是因为它们的前提假设（系统的权限应该被限制）被产品的核心价值主张（系统应该拥有人的全部权限）反转了。

Onyx 的真正赌注不是"做一个更好的代理监控工具"，而是赌定整个企业安全栈的范式必须从"权限即边界"切换到"意图即边界"——这是一次和当年从外网防火墙转向零信任同等量级的范式断裂，但极少有人意识到我们正处于这场断裂的第一年。

二、跨领域连接：闪电棋手、麻醉师与 Onyx 的"小模型直觉"

主持人和 Maxim 都谈到象棋——大师 99% 的招式是直觉，只有少数关键时刻才会"停下来长考"。但更深的跨领域映射其实是麻醉学：麻醉师并不需要懂遍所有外科手术细节，他们训练自己的核心能力是识别"什么时候该叫醒主刀医生"——一种"高阶元判断"，比任何一项专科知识都更稀缺、更值钱。

Onyx 训练的不是"更聪明的模型"，而是最廉价、最快、近乎只会一件事的模型——判断'现在是不是该让聪明 Agent 来介入了'。这恰好对应了认知科学里的Kahneman 系统1/系统2 架构：系统1 永远在线、极便宜、几乎不出错地判断"这件事需不需要系统2"。

更令人意外的一步连接：这种结构和人体免疫系统惊人地同构——大部分时间里，廉价的先天免疫（巨噬细胞、补体）做粗糙判断；只有当模式被识别为"可疑"时，才召唤昂贵的适应性免疫（B/T 细胞克隆扩增）。Onyx 实际上在为企业的 AI 层级重建一套数字免疫系统，而这套系统的核心智慧不在"杀伤力"，而在"分流阈值"。把安全公司当作"数字免疫学"公司来看，估值逻辑会完全改变。

三、隐藏的结构性壁垒：Onyx 真正的护城河不是技术，而是"数据隔离权"

这是访谈里最不显眼、但战略意义最巨大的一句话——

"We're allowed to look at a lot of historical data of how these agents have behaved. But enterprises today are not willing to have Anthropic or OpenAI keep that historical data because they know these are very data companies that will want to train on that data."

这句话揭示了一个结构性反直觉事实：OpenAI 和 Anthropic 越成功、越被信任为模型供应商，就越不可能拿到企业 Agent 的行为历史数据。因为它们的 DNA 是"用数据训练更好的模型"，而企业的法务/合规视角恰好把这条 DNA 视为利益冲突。

这是一种"成功的诅咒"——和审计行业里会计师事务所不能同时给同一家公司做咨询和审计的结构完全同构（SOX 法案的核心）。Maxim 隐约押注的，是 AI 行业最终也会出现一种类似 SOX 的强制结构性分离：模型供应商和模型监督者必须是不同实体。

如果这个判断成真，那么 Onyx 的护城河就不是"我们模型更好"，而是"我们因为不训练大模型，所以才被允许看你们的全部 Agent 历史"。这是一种以"放弃野心"换取"独家数据访问"的反向护城河——和 Cloudflare 早期坚决不做内容业务、Stripe 坚决不做银行业务、Snowflake 坚决不做应用层是同一种战略形状。

最让我惊讶的推论是：Onyx 必须永远不做一件事——训练通用前沿模型。一旦他们开始训练通用模型，他们今天最大的、看似不起眼的优势（被允许保留客户的 Agent 行为历史）就会瞬间蒸发。这是一种自我设限即护城河的罕见战略形态。

📝 转录文本

开场：Agent 失控的现实风险

"As you're exponentially doing more things with the AIs, you're going to start having really bad actions happen. And we've seen some of that happen lately with agents accidentally publishing code and tokens that they weren't supposed to. Like definitely enterprises are starting to realize that that risk is grown exponentially and that they don't have any way to stop the adoption. They just now have to do something to reduce the chance of these agent actions being illegitimate or incorrect. But we're allowed to look at a lot of historical data of how these agents have behaved. But enterprises today are not willing to have Anthropic or OpenAI keep that historical data because they know these are very data companies that will want to train on that data."

嘉宾介绍

Hi listeners, welcome back to No Priors. Today I'm here with Maxim Bar Kogan, the co-founder and CEO of Onyx Security, an Israeli-based startup of researchers, mathematicians, and engineers building agents to watch the AI agents. We talk about specialized model training, Mythos, alignment research, and the Israeli ecosystem in security and now AI. Welcome, Maxim. Thanks so much for doing this.

Thank you. Pleasure to be here.

一、为什么两年前就押注 Agent 安全？

Everyone is much more concerned about security and the impact of AI on security than they were certainly a few months ago. The consensus risk story two years ago when you started the company was basically like DLP for chatbots — like what are employees putting into ChatGPT. Now we have clearly something that is not quite panic but close to market-wide panic. How did you decide to bet on agent actions when you started?

Look, I think for us the pivotal point was AutoGPT. I think AutoGPT kind of let everyone's imagination — including ours — run wild because it was a…

Can you remind listeners what that was?

Sure. So AutoGPT — and I'm sorry if I don't know the guy behind it, but a huge huge fan — they created the first, as far as I know, first really autonomous agent running on LLMs. Right? So an agent that would let an LLM not generate text but decide what to do, and then give that agent an API access to do that thing, a tool to do it, and then would do that in a loop. So it basically in theory could let agents do very complicated things — anything a person could do on a computer.

Now, granted, it didn't work that well. It was too early. The models were not good enough. GPT-4 was not good enough. But I think it did give everyone a glimpse into the future of: what if the models were good enough, and then basically using that same structure we could have very capable agents doing stuff for us? I think that in many ways Claude Code today is not dissimilar to AutoGPT back then. I think they were a bit early on, again, before the models were ready, but the concept was right.

And the thought that stuck with me was — I was very AGI-pilled even back then. So I was thinking: oh my god, models are going to be way smarter than us. When that happens, how do we oversee these very smart agents that are, you know, smarter than us? They're very capable. How are we going to feel easy about them doing stuff for us, especially when they start managing really important stuff? You know, then one day they're managing your water supply and your electricity, your power grid. Right? How do you control them? And that was like the thing I was kind of obsessed about.

I was also too early. So I think at the time enterprises were not using any agents. There were hardly any agents out there, and talking with a lot of security buds at the time they were like, "Oh dude, you're way too early, like this is not something that's going to happen."

As you question, I said: "Is anyone going to do this before you run out of money?" And I think there was a good chance that I would have run out of money before — because I think you were right, like there was an element of chance here. But then the market did happen. So we had suddenly reasoning models that could do long-horizon tasks. We had Claude Code, which became like the really first widely used autonomous agent, and then we had Cursor and OpenClaw.

And I think we're starting to see now that these types of agents that are very autonomous — even though everyone was afraid to build them, so everyone started building these low-code platforms that were much more limited, much more based on connectors. Those platforms ended up being quite limited, so we didn't get the productivity gains from those limited platforms. But when we started getting the crazy benefits from these very unleashed agents that could do everything, that had much less controls baked into them — even very large enterprises decided they're going to adopt it. You know, Anthropic's revenue is coming from enterprises that are paying for Claude Code to do a lot of the work that developers used to do.

That was a bit about how we started, and we definitely were in luck that very autonomous agents appeared before it was too late.

二、Onyx 的产品定位：监督 Agent 的 Agent

So can you describe a little bit — because I think it's both close to impossible and then very useful in this period of AI to think about what is deployment right now, and then what's changing about capability. What's the one-liner on what the Onyx product does today, and then how you think about long-term vision?

Today, Onyx really does two things. Number one is we train models and build agents that can oversee other agents. And the goal of that is to say: okay, we need someone to be able to tell that all of these actions that are now happening by these AIs that we're adopting are legitimate. Because the number of these actions is going up exponentially. And so things that we thought might be useful in the past — like a human in the loop — now that you're going to have 100x, a thousand-x, a million-x of these actions, that's not going to work.

And then we take that capability and we basically productize it in a product that we call the control plane, or the secure control plane, where we come to the prospect and say: "Hey, let's find all of your AIs and autonomous agents and hook them up to Onyx, to this system where we can oversee what your AIs are doing." So that you don't run into the risk of — as you're exponentially doing more things with the AIs — you're going to start having really bad actions happen.

And we've seen some of that happen lately: downtimes that were caused by AI just doing the wrong thing; agents accidentally publishing code and tokens that they weren't supposed to; and so on. So definitely enterprises are starting to realize that that risk is growing exponentially, and they don't have any way to stop the adoption. So they just now have to do something to reduce the chance of these agent actions being illegitimate or incorrect.

三、为代码而生的双刃剑

Yeah. One of the core reasons obviously the foundation model labs are going after code is because it is very powerful in general and can do, in theory, all things software can over time. The flip side of that is it can do all things software can — right? And so, I joyously am already in the camp of having been over-permissive with my agents such that it deleted data permanently and caused rework. So I'm like, oh okay, I think I see — I need some guardian spirits around it.

Given your deployments today and talking to large enterprises, what is the state of deployment? How much do you see that's within these more scoped, Studio-L–like platforms versus freewheeling coding agents? How much are you actually seeing in large enterprises in different sectors?

Yeah, so I think right now in our typical enterprise we're going to see — if we break it down to three categories:

  1. Various SaaS platforms that are typically more low-code, where people build agents in this drag-and-drop way. They're not really autonomous agents — they're kind of automations.
  2. First-party agents people are building in their cloud, potentially because it's an application they want inside the company or even a product they're planning to release to customers, that is agentic.
  3. Very autonomous coding agents and assistants.

Of these categories, I would say roughly at this point over 50% is the autonomous coding agents and assistants in the average enterprise. Then probably 45% is those low-code automations. And the last 2% are really the first-party ones that they're building themselves, because obviously it's much harder to build effective agents — it's much easier to adopt agents off the shelf or build them with low-code.

And that's what we're seeing. And we're inducing that the autonomous ones are also the fastest growing category. So it used to be that only developers — and we would see Claude Code growing like fire in our customer base — and now we're seeing Claude Code growing even faster. We're starting to see, to our own surprise actually, people adopting OpenClaw as a legitimate sanctioned tool in the company because the CEO is very driven to adopt AI. So I think that today autonomous agents are by far the fastest growing category, and today typically comes without any controls.

四、为什么现有安全栈失灵了？

So, enterprises already buy let's say a hundred billion dollars of security today. They have lots of different protections at the endpoint and network and cloud and identity domains. What's relevant here for securing agents — or is none of it? How do you think about the existing protection set?

Security is always a space where you have some overlap between different tooling, but in this — and you have the concept of defensive depth as well. So you want to have defenses at different levels of your technology stack to solve the problem.

That said, I think in this space a lot of enterprises are kind of helpless. I'll take an example: the identity approach. Traditionally, if we have a software system that's running in our company, our first and most important control will be to limit what permission it has — because then, no matter what, even if it goes wrong, even if it's compromised, it can't typically do stuff that was originally allowed to do.

But with these autonomous AIs, with these assistants, with these coding agents, we kind of want them to have our permissions — because we want to tell Claude Code to do something, or Claude Co-worker to do something, and we want to then go have lunch and we want to come back and see that it's done. And we want to give it so many diverse tasks as well that we kind of can't find the right set of permissions to do so. Suddenly our identity security software is not very useful.

Then if you think about endpoint security, or API security — if we tell our Claude Code that we want to recreate a database and it should delete it and recreate it, that's great, that's going to save our DevOps team and our platform teams a lot of time. It's a great benefit of Claude Code. But if Claude Code is working on an unrelated task and suddenly thinks that maybe the right thing to do is to delete our database and recreate it, maybe we don't want that to happen. And unfortunately our endpoint providers or API security tools — they don't know what Claude was thinking. Why is it doing what it's doing?

A lot of these existing tools, they don't have the context to understand what these very flexible, unpredictable systems are doing. If you're not building some kind of controls that are built for these systems, then you're either going to end up limiting them a lot — making them much less useful to the enterprise — or you're going to miss a lot of pretty dangerous things that they might be doing.

五、为什么"代理 + 策略引擎"不够用？

As somebody who has worked in security for a long time, my first very traditional instinct on a problem like this is: that sounds like a problem for a proxy with a policy engine. We make some rules, we make the rules smarter. Why doesn't that work? Or did you try it?

There are a few things. I mean, proxy is an integration method, I would say. So there are some AI systems where you would want to integrate with a proxy if that's the easiest way to do it. But number one, there are a lot of systems where that's just not viable technically because AI today runs on the cloud, on someone else's infrastructure, on your endpoint — and proxy is not always an option.

And the second thing is the question: okay great, you're proxying, so you're seeing the data. But that's not the hard problem. The hard problem is understanding what I should do now. It turns out that in the case of AI systems, that is the hard question. What is the engine that needs to underwrite these different actions and say if they're okay or not? Because we need to be able to understand what another system is thinking, what is it planning to do, and then have our own opinion on that.

And consider: we're trying to understand if some of the smartest models in the world are doing the right thing. So who for we to do it? How are we going to do it correctly? Right? And so that turns out to be a really difficult technical question.

六、为什么 Onyx 自己训练模型？

Part of the solution for Onyx has been training its own models. What can you say about that?

If you tried today — let's say you were trying to build a solution to oversee and kind of control how other agents are operating — maybe the first thing a lot of our listeners might think is: "Well, I'll just ask Claude Code to do it." And in a sense they would be right, because Claude Code is great, and maybe we can ask it to spawn a version of itself for every agent that we have and kind of keep monitoring everything that agent is starting to do, and if you think that there's a problem, intervene.

That approach — it has, obviously, it's pretty naive and there are some ways in which it totally fails we could talk about — but it has some merit to it. So it does seem intuitive that it's a good idea to have capable agents reviewing what other agents are doing, same as we have capable humans reviewing what other humans are doing, right?

But then the problems that you're going to run into is: how do I make this work from a cost, latency, and reliability perspective? Because if I need to run an agent for every agent you're running, as your security vendor, you're going to be paying me more than you're paying for your AI. Right? So that's pretty much a deal-breaker. And also it's going to be so slow — you're not going to be happy with whatever latency you're going to get.

And so the challenge then becomes: how do I know what are the times where I need to interject with these smart agents to look at what's happening? And that's when actually what you want to do is — you want to try to train very smart models that are… actually, let me correct, said very not smart models, but models that are just good at one thing. They're very small. They almost can't do anything else other than be able to say, "Should I have a smarter agent look at this?"

And if you manage to bake in that intuition into those small models — well, in the sense that they don't miss a lot of stuff and they don't call that other agent too much — then you can get to a really good balance of: we're very performant, we have smart agents overseeing things when needed, but our costs are low and our latency is low.

And then that becomes the challenge, because you need to make sure that as the frontier models get smarter and the harnesses become more evolved, you need to have models that are on your side that are small and effective at continuously being able to say: "Now is the time, this is the action where I think someone should take a closer look." And that's why Onyx trains models for this purpose — and you know, most of the hard things that we're doing are in this space.

七、Blitz 国际象棋的类比：直觉与长考

Yeah. You and I actually both love to play blitz chess, and I look at Guardian as a system that's a little bit analogous. Like, it's not clear either of us is going to be competitive with Magnus in a real game. But if you play enough times with the right data, and all you have to do is make intuitive decisions under time pressure very quickly, it's actually a different game, right? Do you think that makes sense, or am I reaching here?

Yeah, I actually didn't think about it, but yeah, there's a lot of analogies. Because I think if you look at top chess players in the world, most of the moves that they make are intuitive. They don't calculate forward. They've seen so many games and they've played so many games that they already have a good sense of what is the right move, and that they're not taking too much risk here by taking this move without calculating.

And then if you look at those games, every once in a while they do stop for suddenly a really long period of time to just calculate forward a lot of options — because they know this is a critical move in the game. There's risk. You need to think through what you're doing, and you need to decide correctly.

I think that's very similar. It's the efficient way to run computation, right? You don't want to spend too much intelligence where you don't have to, and you want to spend a lot of intelligence — overwhelmingly a lot — in situations where there's high risk.

八、Onyx 的以色列 DNA 与可解释性研究

You guys are a team mostly based in Israel today. I think the world has accepted that there is a cohort of amazing Israeli security talent that comes out of, you know, the military and offensive security, and then repeat entrepreneurs like you guys. I think the DNA at Onyx is a little bit different here. Your co-founder Gil came out of building synthetic data and working at Nvidia. How would you characterize the talent at Onyx as particularly good at? And then, are people actually training interesting frontier models in Israel now?

So first of all, I think Israel started maybe a bit late in the game but is catching up quickly. There's now amazing companies in Israel building world models, building AI infrastructure that's top of its class, building chips. So I think Israel in general is becoming very strong in AI, and we're proud to be a part of that movement.

And I think you're right — our company has a very mixed DNA between cyber and AI, which kind of reflects mine and Gil's backgrounds. Most of the people in our company, most of our research engineering, come from a unit in the Israeli intelligence where we actually deal with math and cyber and the intersection thereof. And so I think it is also reflected in the type of talent that we bring in.

I think it's important for a few reasons. The first and foremost is that we want to be more than just a security company long-term. We think that to solve this problem well it's going to require deep AI expertise — but then the problem is not just cybersecurity. The problem is: how do we control advanced AI long-term? And that problem — even if you just forget about enterprise security and the different gaps in various controls that they currently have — first principles, that problem just sounds very important to me.

So I think it will be crucially important if you have AI companies that are $10 trillion companies. We think you want a company that is not the vendor of the AI itself to oversee and help you control what AI is doing. And we think that's an opening that's a hundred-billion-plus opening for a really important company.

And then if you think about what is going to take to control advanced AI long-term — then we're just scratching the surface, because long-term you're going to have to also understand much better what models are thinking, what's happening on the internals of these models as they're operating. And that's also a lot of where our research is focused.

九、机制可解释性：模型监督模型

So the industry is quite divided on this issue — I mean, amongst the people who think about whether mechanistic interpretability or research into better understanding models is possible. Like, that's a question. So it's something you believe in.

We believe that there's been a lot of strong progress in that direction. We believe that understanding the internal weights and activations — what is the internal structure, the mathematical structure of these systems — is going to be at least part of the solution.

And in many ways we think that — and this is maybe, you know, we'll only know when we get there — but we think that for our level of intelligence it's kind of difficult to understand very quickly what is the internal structure of a large language model.

You mean —

Our level — like human intelligence — or our level of your model? Okay, human intelligence.

Oh yeah, yeah. I think as humans, it might still be very difficult to understand what weights and activations mean, and maybe mechanistic interpretability — it seems like, oh, maybe that's too hard or shouldn't be possible. But as we're starting to have models that are much smarter than us, at least in some important ways, we think that we'll be able to start cracking mechanistic interpretability much more effectively.

And I think it's going to be extremely rewarding, by the way, long-term — for understanding intelligence in general. Like, not just overseeing, but just understanding what intelligence is, how it works, what's the difference between the smarter model and the less smart model.

十、为什么 Fortune 100 会信任一家不到 100 人的初创公司？

I completely agree that the opportunity to understand and trust and secure and govern these superintelligent AIs is a very large opportunity. If we just scroll back to today, the security person in me says: well, then I have to give you all the permissions and understanding that I have to give these companies too. Like, how do you get customers — or the Fortune 100s you're working with already, or, you know, tech-natives — everybody cares about their own security and business — to trust you now as a less-than-100-people company?

Right. I think it's one of those things that should not be possible. So in theory, there's no reason why a Fortune 10 or 20 company would work with us, because, you know, who are we? We're a 2-year-old company, like a few people from Panmatic Cyber.

But I think it's an opening that only happens when the pain is very strong. So their pain is so strong that they're going to say: "Oh my god, I just saw this company come out of stealth, but it's a problem that I have daily, so I'll give them a call." And suddenly you get inbound from these large customers, which is of course the best thing you could hope for as an entrepreneur.

And I think it reflects, in my opinion, their understanding that a lot of the startups in this space are still small and new — but there's going to be a huge company here, and we want to find the right horse to bet on. So we're going to take a look at these companies. And number two, that if we don't do anything, then in a very short time this will disable our business.

At the end of the day, security people are in the business of revenue preservation. They understand that this is between the tourists. They want to partner with someone that's promising and early rather than not doing anything.

十一、漏洞发现的暴跌成本与 "Mythos 级别"模型

The other thing besides agent actions across their surface area that every CISO I know is freaking out about — and every engineering leader is freaking out about — is what I would just describe as the plummeting cost of vulnerability finding with these coding tools.

Yes.

And that has caused a number of issues for vendors that are being compromised. How do you think people should react to this other issue?

I think Mythos is really like — if you took me 10 years ago, automated vulnerability research looked like a dream that would take 20–50 years to happen. Maybe it's because we were doing a lot of that in the Israeli intelligence, and we like to pat ourselves on the shoulder of how difficult the job is that we're doing — but it did look really far. And suddenly it's coming all at once.

So I think, first of all, the market is not overreacting. I think this is a huge change in what this means for security teams. If you're a pragmatic security person today, you understand that you need to move very quickly. Your strategy might look something like:

  • I need to do the fastest quick fixes I can to mitigate the immediate risk. So maybe I'll invest in whatever the vulnerabilities that have been found — let's try to mitigate for them, whether it is through patching or through mitigating controls.
  • But then the real solution — and every security leader at a large enterprise knows it — is that we need to have the foundational pieces in place to avoid those risks.

And the foundational pieces are: we need to have identity locked down, we need to have a firewall, we need to have endpoint detection. For different asset classes in your enterprise, for different parts of your stack, there's a different foundational security mechanism that you need in place. For the AI attack surface that you now have, or for the AIs in your company, you also need a foundational security — and that's kind of the role we play in that space.

So as part of your preparation for Mythos-level models and beyond, you're going to need a lot of foundational security tools to fortify your different parts of the enterprise, and we're playing that part in the AI space.

十二、Glass Swing、Daybreak 与受控发布

Do you have a point of view on the phased rollout or controlled rollout with Glass Swing and Daybreak from Anthropic and OpenAI in this area?

I don't have a strong opinion, but I think — on the one hand, if we knew that there's not going to be anyone who's going to release a Mythos-level model soon, I think that would be great, because it gives enough time to prepare — to build the know-how, to build the playbooks, to share that around in the community, and to make sure that we're not starting to see airlines go down and power plants go down, and really like disastrous effects that could happen.

The problem is that if anyone gets to a Mythos-level model earlier, then in retrospect it would look like a huge mistake, because we could have at least given companies the choice to start moving very quickly and give more companies access to Mythos. Now they're all vulnerable because there's a Chinese model that's Mythos level and there's nothing they can do about it.

So I think hopefully we'll manage to do the gradual rollout correctly. I would really encourage that we expand the amount of companies that get access to this and make it much easier for people to get. I would advise everyone to assume that these models are coming anyway. The only thing you can do right now is to invest in these foundational controls that will stop the downstream effects of these vulnerabilities that are going to be found in their systems.

十三、企业的"拒绝派"还剩多少？

Do you see in large enterprises any holdouts? I would say I actually haven't spent a bunch of time talking to people about this recently, but I remember a year and a half, two years ago, there were large companies that just said, "We're going to ban all of this stuff until it's safe."

Yeah, I hardly see it anymore. I think in the financial sector, there's some companies that are more opinionated on what they allow. They still allow agents, but they're maybe more granular — like, maybe we're only going to allow these two tools.

I personally think that the companies that are going to do well are the companies that are going to allow a lot of different tools, because the landscape is changing so quickly. If you bet on OpenAI a year ago, that would have been the safest bet in the world. But suddenly Anthropic has much better models and better tools, and potentially a year from now there's someone else who has much better tools. So there's a price to pay.

But I think if you're a large company, your risk profile is — and should be — different. You know, when you're a startup, you want to have your agents do everything for you because you have everything to gain and you have nothing to lose. When you're large, when you're JP Morgan, you have so much to lose and you can maybe take a bit more time to gain what you can gain from AI. And by the way, JP Morgan is adopting AI very quickly. I think it is okay for companies to have a nuanced view, the bigger they are, on how they're adopting AI.

十四、Onyx 自身的风险姿态

How do you think about that question for yourself? Like, risk profile, pace? The environment is changing very quickly, and then you see a lot of problems. Growing the scope of the product and the research thesis here is already quite large.

We are kind of in luck in the AI security space, because yes, there are a lot of vendors, there's a lot of new technologies that are coming up, but the two core pillars of how 2026 AI works have not changed in the last few years.

So we're still using largely LLM foundation models that are not entirely dissimilar to how they were a few years back. And we're still building agents in pretty much the same way, where we have an LLM decide what are the tool calls that we're going to make and generate those.

And so that does allow a company today like us to skate to a lot of different applications that are utilizing these two primitives, while still keeping the core technology that we're developing fairly lean and focused. Now, of course, there's always a risk that tomorrow there's a completely new LLM paradigm that could happen, or a completely new agent paradigm that could happen.

And that's why we do try to — we have strong opinions, loosely held, about what does that look like in 2027. We maybe have a good picture for 2026, but for 2027 we're very open-minded, and we think that's the right stance to be for the next two years until we see what does AGI / ASI look like.

十五、为什么这事儿不会被大模型实验室自己干掉？

Do you see the set of problems you're addressing — trust in the models and governance of them — as something that the labs could ever do, or do you think it's a structural thing? I ask because the number one question amongst the startup ecosystem in the Bay Area today is: if you assume capability improves, or when the labs just get hungrier from their already currently ambitious stance — why wouldn't they do this too? And so I ask you the same question.

Today, if you're a private person or if you're a security buyer, there are some places where you don't want to trust the same person that you're buying it from. So, you know, maybe if you're buying a car, you're not going to have the same guy that you're buying it from certify that the car is good, right? You're maybe going to have someone else do it.

And if you're a security team, you're not going to trust the vendor of a product to tell you that this product is not going to mess your environment. You're going to want to have an independent party whose whole business depends on telling you that this thing is correct and being right, this thing is legitimate and being right. So there's the buyer psychology in the space that I think really goes in our favor.

And then I think there's the core problems: why are models even making mistakes? Why are agents even making mistakes? I would broadly categorize it into two things:

  1. Jagged intelligence of these models. There's like sometimes very silly mistakes that they make. I think that problem will go away. I think we're heading for much smarter models that make less silly mistakes, and our role is not going to be to prevent silly mistakes. That will be taken care of by the model vendors because they're very incentivized to do it.

  2. The other fast growing category of things that we're seeing models do wrong: places where they're actually not making a thing that is like a silly mistake, but more — I would say — have an independent, you would even say semi-aware or semi-conscious perspective on what should happen. And that perspective might not always align with your perspective. And I think that is a problem that we've seen grow hand-in-hand with models getting smarter. Maybe just the way it is — that as you get smarter you have more independent thoughts, and you're more conscious. And I think that problem is actually seemingly very hard to tackle today, even for the large vendors.

And one of the key things that are making it easier for us to understand and detect these things versus the other vendors is that we're allowed to do certain things that they're not. So for example, we're allowed to look at a lot of historical data of how these agents have behaved. But enterprises today are not willing to have Anthropic or OpenAI give that historical data, because they know these are very data companies that will want to train on that data.

And so there are some ways in which we're given more context and more latitude to know if something is happening that is wrong — compared to the past, compared to how these agents typically behave, and so on — that the vendors don't have, and is really important in solving this problem.

And the last thing I'll say is that you're not dealing with one vendor. So we're heading for a world where there's a multitude of different vendors, for many reasons. You're going to have, for cost reasons, open-source models that people are going to use because it's cheaper. And you're going to have models that are better at different tasks and at different cost profiles. And so it is going to be unrealistic to expect all the vendors to provide the same level of security, and to assume that as you're trying to adopt technology very quickly, especially coming from new vendors that obviously have not yet built out all of that.

So I think that these are the reasons why I think it would be very difficult for this problem to be just completely solved by the large labs.

十六、给硅谷的告别建议：去爱那些安全团队

Just to close, and also thinking about what people in Silicon Valley or outside of security may not know — you're building this from Tel Aviv, right?
