Well, there are five new markets: - We serve individuals. - We serve insurance companies. - We serve the debt and equity bucket of institutions. - We serve traditional asset managers. - And we serve 401(k).

All of these other five markets want nothing to do with a drawdown fund. They live in a public world. And so the notion that they are going to somehow conform to us is just hubris. We are going to have to conform to them if we want to serve them, if we want to exist in their world. But we have to also do it in a way that does not bastardize our products, that does not create unacceptable mismatches between risk and reward.

And so we're starting with our investment grade private suite of products and we will be daily-estimated value by June 30. Value alone is not enough. We are standardized information, standardized CUSIPs or ICE IDs, standardized data warehouses, market-making, regular disclosure of prices, other dealers. This is about creating an ecosystem. And by the end of September, this will be across the entirety of our credit business.

And I believe this is the direction of travel. I've never seen a market in the world where you have transparency and price discovery that is not 10 times its size. And change like everything else, it may be uncomfortable for people, but it's coming. And five other markets want it. And will it be perfect the first day? It will not. Will it get better every day? It will get better every day. And one day soon, maybe it'll even come for equity. But that's not this year's business.

重新定义 Private Credit

You know, I know you've also talked a lot about the press's sort of very narrow definition of private credit being kind of direct lending and BDCs. But from your perspective, how do you describe the broader private credit ecosystem? And what separates the winners from the rest of the market as this ecosystem matures?

So I do think it starts with a skill set of managing a credit book, because at the end of the day, managing credit is different than managing equity. In credit, you only get your principle and interest. You should not be around risk-taking as a rule. You should be fully diversified. In the equity business, you actually get paid for risk-taking.

And so that mindset difference, perhaps is obvious, but it has not been obvious in people's actual performance and how they've constructed portfolios.

The second is you need a low cost of capital or you need a variety of costs of capital. And so one of the reasons I think we've been so successful at this is we are willing to match low-cost retirement liabilities with safe long-term yield assets. Not risky long-term yield assets — that does not belong in a regulated balance sheet.

But if you think of the largest issuers of private investment grade, it's Intel, it's Air France, it's EDF, it's AT&T, it's Meta, it's BP Energy, and so on and so on. And you're hearing lots of public companies. And so public companies and public companies' CFOs and CEOs now understand that there are three markets for financing:

  1. The bank market. The bank market is the best source of financing on a short-term basis anywhere in the world. A bank borrows short deposits and lends short. A bank is the best short-term lender. It is not a good long-term lender.
  2. The public market.
  3. Private capital.

The public market and private capital are both good long-term lenders. The public market does something very standard. If you want anything other than plain vanilla, you need to come to the private market.

And if you think again about the world we're in today, when we're building a data center that is marrying energy and chips and offtake — it is anything but simple. It can be creditworthy, but it is not simple. It is not a 10-year bond underwritten by a single issuer.

And so the ability and willingness to take brain power and apply it to investment grade — which itself is not a great asset management business, but we need the asset. In addition, once we originate an asset, it feeds our third-party credit business. Because if we, as a large retirement services insurance company, need the asset, other insurance companies need the asset. Pension funds need the asset. Endowments need the asset. And individuals likely will want this asset as well.

And that's what we've seen. Our underwriting risk as principal shows people the alignment that they need to get comfortable with these investment grade underwritings.

Apollo × a16z：机会出现在领域的交叉处

You know, one of my favorite lines and it's a bit of a metaphor maybe for my career, but also an investment philosophy is that opportunities live between fields of expertise. I like kind of living at the intersections of things. I'm curious, maybe we'll transition the conversation to some of the opportunities you see at the intersection of Apollo and a16z.

Mark, our Mark — you know, Andre wrote this piece over a decade ago that software is eating the world, and that feels more true than ever as AI proliferates all parts of the economy. As a result, we're finding ourselves funding more capital-intensive businesses, right? In areas like defense and energy and robotics and manufacturing and public safety. Ultimately, I think most of these businesses will need to graduate at some point beyond venture equity and likely become — and already are — clients or customers of yours. What do you see as the opportunities between our two firms?

Immense. There's — it's only about time in the day right now — but I'm going to first delve into this notion of intersections, because this intersection notion is actually what creates value in our business.

So if you think about how institutions allocate capital, they allocate it into buckets. Some of those buckets they advise themselves if they have good investment teams. Some of those buckets they outsource to consultants or advisers, but they're still in buckets.

The traditional buckets are: - Equity — what's in the equity bucket? Public equities. - Fixed income — what's there? Public fixed income. - Then there's sometimes a liquidity bucket, a real assets bucket, and then there's this thing called alternatives.

That's been most of the world for 40 years.

What do you do with the equity that is private, safe, but doesn't have a high enough return for alternatives? It doesn't have a home. It's neither. It's not public. It doesn't go into the public bucket. It's not an alternative. It's not high enough rate of return, but its risk-reward is the best risk-reward. We call that hybrid. For us, that's our fastest growing business.

Again, this notion of private investment grade — most things that are in institutions' fixed income bucket are public. Therefore, they're not a source of capital. We have been able to originate and earn excess return because private but investment grade is not a bucket.

Now, as we get bigger and bigger, we are changing the world and we've seen institutions adopt this notion of total portfolio approach. We've seen family offices and we see a general migration. So in-between is almost always the best asset class because there is poor capital formation. And there's no one who is assigned every day as their day job for the risk.

This is exactly what's happening between our two firms. Because you have an entire ecosystem of which your firm is a major player that has never been capital intensive. And for the first time, not only is it capital intensive, but it is going to be capital intensive on a scale that is unimaginable — because the amount of money that's going to be put into data centers, into chips, into robotics, into manufacturing, into defense is, as I suggested, every dollar since the invention of fire.

That is not going to be financed with equity entirely, because that is not efficient and the scale of it is not achievable. It is going to have to be parceled out into various risks, and that's what we're seeing happen right now.

So if I look at the drivers of our business for this year, it is data centers. It is massive amounts of chip financing. And what we're doing is we're parceling out the risks. On the venture side, on the equity side, there is the fundamental business underwrite of this company or that company. And then on the infrastructure side, things that are reusable, things that have hard asset value, are being offloaded into the credit markets at the appropriate rate of return and at the appropriate risk rating.

But I believe we're approaching a really interesting time. We've never really talked about the quantum of money. 2025 was just proof of concept that data centers and chips and energy were all needed. 2026, the market is starting to recognize that if this continues — 800 billion of capex from just four public companies this year, not to mention the private — that everyone who is an investor is going to be concentrated in certain names and we're actually going to hit concentration limits.

We're starting to see this across the board. I think spreads are going to widen. I think really good entrepreneurs are going to end up in partnership with entrepreneurs of another type — those who are financial entrepreneurs who help to democratize credit assets and hybrid equity and other types of things.

And I don't think the imagination is going to stop at chips and data and energy. The visits I've had out to the Bay Area and to Seattle and to elsewhere — robotics is a whole another thing.

The notion that once the world was able to solve the Waymo problem — which was a real problem: how do you solve a situation for self-driving in a constantly changing environment where safety is paramount and where you can't stop? Well, therefore, the equation of doing this for construction equipment should not be as difficult as doing Waymo. The equation for doing this for other types of robotics should not be as difficult. And we're just getting there.

Why should that all be financed with equity? I mean, we have a whole market for equipment rental — that is a much lower cost of capital than venture capital, and a much greater scale of capital gives us and people like us diversification, gives you the appropriate amount of leverage and the understanding of how to parcel out those risks. The trust of how to partner on these risks I think is going to make sure that we spend a lot more time in your hometown. And vice versa.

And it's part of our physical strategy. I mean, yes, we love being in New York. New York has certain constraints. This is not the podcast for that. We could go off in the political direction. But we are committed to a second headquarters. We want access to a second talent pool. And something tells me that second talent pool is going to be much more focused on change, on challenger business models, and on making sure we partner with the growth ecosystem.

The US is the envy of the world and we want to stay that way.

Totally. It's nice to hear you see that. People ask: "Are you Silicon Valley or are you Wall Street? Are you an entrepreneur, investor, and operator?" I think the answer should just be yes. If it's too easily bucketed, it's too legible — and again, the opportunity lives at this intersection.

Look, for our industry, from 1990, our founding, until 2008, the firm was slightly larger, but it kind of did the same thing. Almost all the firms in our industry that you know were 40 billion in 2008 and now are a trillion dollars. Others are slightly larger, slightly smaller. And this is not good management. I'd like to think there was some of that, but that is not the primary driver.

The answer is we're shaped by outside forces. And those outside forces — coming out of the great financial crisis, moving into COVID, moving into the change in rates, moving into the product proliferation. Okay, what are the outside forces now shaping our industry? Well, they're primarily coming from this shift in the economy.

We operate under the assumption that every job is going to be replaced or enhanced. Every single job. And I think that's what is going to happen. I mean, a world where GDP grows, where profit margins grow, where wages grow, but where employment does not — maybe is okay. Maybe that's the consequence of having an older workforce or not having as many workers per retiree or not having as much immigration. How we balance this as a country, how we balance this as a world, how we balance this as a city, I think is going to be the interesting challenge.

创业者如何与 Apollo 合作

A lot of our audience are entrepreneurs in the tech ecosystem. Many of whom maybe haven't had experience working with Apollo yet. How should entrepreneurs listening to this think about when and how to engage with you?

Early, and partner. I mean, this is who we are. The ability and willingness to focus on any one transaction for an entrepreneur — it's just about the two resources we have, time and money. And of those two, time is the one that is short, in shortest supply right now.

And so the ability to engage us and to paint a picture of not just where you are, but where you're going and how we can win together, I think is where the world is going. And this is happening in places that require specified knowledge. Defense, where we're spending an awful lot of time. You do not get to come out and just show up in defense. You have to know a lot about the ecosystem and the environment and everything else.

But it's also showing up in the whole notion of capital being limited. Great entrepreneurs who have created things of value have had a choice historically, which is wait to the public markets as their exit. Now the world is changing so fast. Maybe what they want to do is to have an interim private liquidity event where they then get to recycle their capital back into the much higher rate of return and participate with the private capital event going forward — and eventually getting to a public exit or getting to a full monetization.

We're seeing all different manner of this take place across our ecosystem. The number of partnerships I believe that are going to sprout up — whether it is the OpenAI ecosystem that they're building to be able to democratize their LLM, or it is the Anthropic ecosystem that is being built to democratize their way of doing things — I think it's the beginning of the proliferation of growth and finance partnerships.

AI 对企业软件的冲击

Awesome. I'm curious to dig into maybe the value or residual value that you see in this world of AI. There's been kind of the "SaaS apocalypse." You've been saying for months that a lot of the real problem in direct lending has been overexposure in enterprise software, and that ultimately AI is going to keep hurting that book of business. Where are we now and where do you think we go from here?

I think there's no going back. I mean, and this is our bias and this is not exhaustive across the board. It does not apply to every company. But the notion that we woke up 8-12 weeks ago and figured out that AI was going to impact enterprise software — really? Like how could we as responsible credit people do this, or as responsible investors?

And so the focus so far has been on credit — that's the most visible, that's where the press has focused. And if credit is problematic, that means the equity is really problematic. And 30% of the private equity industry over the past decade has been devoted to enterprise software. I personally expect the returns from private equity in the ground to be disastrous because so much of exposure is to enterprise software.

And this does not mean that enterprise software companies are going out of business — far from it. It means that the prospects of on-selling it either to the public markets or to someone else are now simply reduced, just because the prices that they paid were too high. And the price they paid reflected a future that did not have AI in it, and now there's AI in it. So there's a competitor.

And again, it doesn't apply to every company. It doesn't apply to every situation. But the scale of change is just off the charts.

And if people who are not as tech-focused are seeing this in their business day-to-day — you know, I think about how we run the business here. Everyone at Apollo can envision how the job they do currently can change with the benefit of AI. A handful of people can actually envision when data and software become free, how the business should exist versus how it does exist. Well, there's still yet another part of this, which is: how do you envision how you start new businesses, the cost of starting a new business, the velocity of starting a new business.

We've seen more business startups than ever — because challengers now can start from a much different place.

This is why we're tired as well. We have to be established companies, including companies that are successful like ours, and we have to be really paranoid about replacement risk.

But change is taking place faster in places where there is a right answer. So why do we see coding in software? Because at the end of the day, the AI can check whether the AI is right. So the rate of change is a vertical line.

On the other hand, if you want an answer to "what is the best Shakespeare essay," we're seeing improvement, but someone has to inform. There's no right answer. What is the best Shakespeare essay? And so we're going to see change, but not that same rate of change.

This describes our business world perfectly. In some things that have a right answer — accounting, trade ops, a number of other applications — we are going to see replacement. On the other hand, in things that require judgment and know-how, we're going to see augmentation or enhancement.

Is this a permanent state of events? No. Because none of us know how this ends or how good the judgment will eventually be. So we need to continue to be paranoid, to continue to be change-focused. But for the near term, I'm very bullish on businesses that adopt change and have a change mentality. I'm actually very bullish on wages. And I think we will see a cycling in employment.

What I've said previously is I think we're going to see a little bit of blue-collar ascendancy and white-collar decline. And I think that's going to be a difficult spot for politics, which has not operated with that notion historically. It's going to be a difficult thing for blue cities where much of this white-collar employment is focused. But the faster we get on with this and create the new businesses, the new industries — which historically has always been the case — the better off everyone is going to be.

借贷的本质：变化是常态

I guess even kind of stepping back, as a lender, how has AI shifted your perspective of the type of collateral you're willing to lend against, or what sort of predictable cash flows look like, given things are changing so quickly?

So with a lender's hat on, we've always had that mentality, which is: change is a constant.

If I go back and I think about the year 2000 as a dividing line, people were worried as to whether the entire digital infrastructure of the US would fall down on Y2K. All right, we survived.

But you go from there. In 2000, the market was still lending against something called Yellow Pages. How could a Yellow Pages be replaced? After all, it was free. It was granular. It was ingrained in culture. In this year's associate class, I use the term Yellow Pages and people raise their hand. They want to know what it was.

And so — but it doesn't stop there. The value: TV stations and radio stations were once thought to be massive franchises. They're diminished. Other forms of content have come along and replaced them. They haven't disappeared, but they're diminished. Cable television was in part the successor. That's now been replaced. Satellite television has been replaced. Mobile telephony has been replaced. Fiber's been…

You just look at the cycle of change. As a lender, you know this. You are diversified. You are senior where you perceive risk. You look for hard collateral. And you accept that you can't make a decision for 20 or 30 years. You can make a decision for three or five or seven years in what we're doing.

There are good lenders, of which I think we're one. There are bad lenders. There are good banks. There are bad banks. There are good insurance companies. There are bad insurance companies. Credit is a skill, and is not a skill that everyone possesses.

道德领导力：从反犹问题到 DEI

So I want to kind of shift the conversation in a slightly different direction — really to moral leadership. One of the things that I've admired about you is just how passionate you've been about fighting anti-Semitism. Obviously this came to a head after October 7th, and at your alma mater. I'm curious how you thought about being so vocal with university leadership in that moment.

Well, if I had thought about it more, I might not have done it. But I am a passionate person, and I will say the whole thing struck me as just incredibly unfair and incredibly ill-advised. What we were watching was not free speech. We were watching favorite speech, preferred speech.

And in the initial foray with the university that I had ahead of their Palestine Rights Conference, I wrote to the president of the university and I said: "I'm a free speech absolutist. I believe this conference should go forward. But the university, as a 300-year-old moral institution, is funding it, promoting it, requiring students who are Jewish to attend it during Jewish high holidays, and you've outsourced the ownership of this conference, which had gone on for many years, to a known Hamas sympathizer and terrorist sympathizer. Other than that, I was fine with it."

And the inability to reflect on the role of a university in society. What is the role of a university in society? And too many times the question was amorphous. I don't think our president at the time knew what the role of a university in society was.

Is it academic excellence and research, or is it social change? And if it's social change, whose social change? Is it her social change? Is it what the trustees decided? Is it what the faculty decided? Was there a vote? No.

What had happened in our university is we had had this us-versus-them, this anti-Americanism, anti-capitalism, anti-merit on steroids. Israel-Palestine is not fully an anti-Semitic issue. And that's not what I saw at these universities. I saw it as an anti-American, anti-system issue.

And so when it persisted, I believe that we should not support those things that violate fundamental moral principles. And I said so. Turns out a lot of other people felt the same way, even if they weren't saying it. Vast majority of donors decided to give the university a dollar per year versus whatever their donation was. We got the university's attention.

Ultimately, the public vu in testimony in DC, where the inability to actually call terrorism, you know, murder — and not just reprehensible — was too much for the public to bear and for the powers that be to bear. And ultimately the chair and the president of the university resigned.

But we're seeing that in a lot of places in society. We saw it in our business community. We saw people fall on the DEI sword in some instances or on the climate sword. People became absolutists.

And when I took over in 2021, one of the things I said is, I want to be able to say the same thing in Texas as I say in California. It's too hard to remember a story. I would rather just be who I am.

And so if you look at what we did in climate, one rule: make it better, not worse. That's it. That didn't work for some absolutists. Okay, we are who we are. We're going to do what we're going to do. We're going to make it better, not worse.

On the employment side, the notion that we would either admit people to an institution of higher learning or bring them into the workforce based on immutable characteristics sounds as anti-American as I could possibly imagine. And it never made any sense. And yet the business community adopted it, not us.

We've stuck with the same formula. We hire for merit adjusted for distance traveled. And distance traveled is not about your immutable characteristics. It is about you as an individual, not your class, not your group. Show me the kid. Show me the individual who's had to overcome something and still achieved. That's who I want. That's who we should want in our universities. That's who we should want in our companies. That's who we should want as our entrepreneurs. That's who we should back.

This is not about concentrating power in an elite. This is about democratizing that. But also not destroying the notion of what I think makes this country great — which is everyone has a shot at it. Not based on your skin color, not based on your religion, your sexual orientation, your country of origin, or any other immutable characteristic. We don't treat you as a group. We treat you as an individual. Apparently, that's controversial, by the way. Shouldn't be. Apparently, it is.

But I think that whether the team here agreed with what I did at UPenn or not, whether the team agreed with the climate or not, whether they agreed with what we did on employment or not — a resounding amount of positive feedback on moral leadership. And it's one of the principles that we have at Apollo, which is we do right over easy.

It was easy to have said "no carbon," or said nothing. It was hard to have said, "We're going to make it better, not worse. But if that includes financing hydrocarbons, we're going to finance hydrocarbons." And it was easy to have said, "Yes, we're going to sign up for this metric or that metric." And it was harder to have said "merit plus distance traveled."

But I hope that this is now ingrained in our company, and it hasn't been cost-free, but I don't think it should be cost-free. But on balance, I would do it all again in exactly the same way. I think it does set us apart, and we're not the only company who does it. There are others out there who do it. But anytime you raise your head above the parapet, there's a cost to doing it.

But how many of us get to do this? It's something I said to Marc when I visited him: we are the luckiest people in the world. We've achieved all we've ever wanted to achieve. It's not about money. It's now going not from success to success, but from success to significance.

What can we do to change the world? We've been given an opportunity. We can play golf. I don't know. It's not going to work for me. Or we can actually lead.

Apollo 的文化：Play to Win

Totally. I think it's a very natural segue into culture here at Apollo. How do you view Apollo's culture? "Playing to win" is kind of one of the core ethoses. And how do you sort of maintain that entrepreneurial culture, which you've described throughout this conversation, as the firm continues to scale?

It's the best question of the day, and it actually is what occupies most of my time. So the greatest amount of effort of any project we've done in the past year has been on culture. And it's been to answer a simple question: what makes Apollo Apollo?

And the good news or bad news, it's been a six-month negotiation. Because if you think about what's happened — when we were a small firm, the culture was one culture. Everyone was brought up. Everyone was onboarded in the same way. It was all visible. It was pretty straightforward.

At 4,000 people in asset management and 2,000 people in retirement services, we now have to be really deliberate. Plus, I'm going to do this for a long time, but I'm not going to do this forever. As a founder, I get some amount of leeway on culture. But I want to make sure that we are intentional about what we're doing.

And so, if we hire you as a young person, we teach you the business and we teach you the culture and we do it really well. But if you are the 15-year person from another firm coming in to help augment our skill set, and there are now 500 of you, how do we teach you our culture? We know how to make you successful commercially. And if you work for me, you learn the culture one way. If you work for John Zito, another way. Jim Zelter, a third way. Scott Kleinman, and so on and so on.

And so we've had a six-month negotiation over what makes Apollo Apollo. And that negotiation is reflected in a work product. That work product is now on our website under employment. It asks the question of what makes Apollo Apollo. It is really controversial and it is really honest. And it's meant to be that way. It is: if you're thinking about coming to work here, make sure this is for you. And if you're already here and trying to figure out what our cultural norms are, this is for you.

And now it's up to us to hire this way, review this way, promote this way, onboard this way, and do it.

But while there are six principles, it does come back to playing to win. And you see this now, I'm sure, in all the growth companies. How do you keep a company that's been really successful hungry and playing to win? Most companies hit an arc, and then they decline to mediocrity. Some actually descend into chaos. I saw your graphic of, you know, Blockbuster and like Mason.

Look, those are the obvious ones. But most companies, the leadership, the senior team — you know, in the Steve Jobs world, they say they mistake the product for the process. They think the process is what got them there.

In our world, the desire to win starts becoming overwhelmed by the fear of losing. People are afraid to make mistakes. And so, how do we teach this? Well, we basically say — even for me, I'm right 60% of the time, max. I fail quickly and fix it quickly.

You do not get fired here for making a bad decision. You get fired here for not recognizing it, or not owning it, and not fixing it.

We have a wall of shame. Every senior professional here has lost money for the firm. If you haven't, you're just not doing enough. You're not taking enough risk, or you haven't done as much for the firm.

And so we've normalized the notion of winning as a team and losing as a team. We keep moving people around. We have a culture that is clean sheet thinking. We have a culture that supports you through the moments in your life. But there's no one answer. You have to live it every day.

And what's interesting is, internally, this is really well known. And people come here and they're like, "Oh my God, this is amazing. We're so happy to be here." Externally, it's really hard, because we've gone from a world that was very media-resourced where they really understood the companies and they lived with us for 18 years as a private equity firm. And we're said, "You know, you have sharp elbows, you do this, you do that, you do the other thing." Well, okay, that may have been true. You might want to revisit the firm 20 years later and actually do it, and they are without resources to do it.

So part of it is to overcome the stereotypes that fit with our industry. Private equity is an amazing business. It's now 100 billion of a trillion-fifty, which in five years from now, it'll be 100 billion of a trillion-four, a trillion-five, whatever the number is. It's still going to be the most important business from a generation point of view, from an idea point of view, from a change agent, from an impact. But boy, there are a lot of other things going on here, and maybe it's worth a look.

This culture project — incredibly fun, too. And now it's about bringing it to life.

建造一家金融机构，而不是运营一只基金

No. And I, as I was reading the document, I saw a lot of similarities between our two firms. We kind of approach the world from very different places, but — and I think it's one of the reasons why I've always admired Apollo — is just this notion of building a firm more than running a fund. Something I sort of draw as a contrast often as well.

It's what's happening. We are building a financial institution. If I characterize our industry, we all started as private equity firms and then we decided to do some real estate, but it was really real estate private equity, right? And then we did infrastructure private equity, right? And then credit private equity, right? And almost every firm in our industry has stopped there because the amount of wealth that's been amassed is off the charts. It's been a really good life. And if you're not building something to change, why aggravate yourself?

The next group of companies has aggravated themselves somewhat by saying, "Oh, let's serve the retail marketplace," and has developed strategies and infrastructure and technology to do that. And most firms are stopping there.

For us, we're looking at the world and saying: the world is short retirement income. The world is going to need more retirement income. The world is going to need a better source of financing for this global industrial renaissance. Let's build the structure, the products, the infrastructure to do it. It's what drives us to enterprise to daily pricing. It's what drives us to market making. And what's what drives us to innovation.

I don't think the next five years are going to be passive. I think the firms are going to look more different five years from now than the last five years, which has been a tremendous amount of change. And so a culture that not only has the characteristics we've already talked about, but a culture that adapts, a culture that knows that change is coming, that accepts that change is coming, where people move around and do different things just the way you have and others have in your organization. That's what we strive for.

未来 40 年：什么必须保留？

And I guess, maybe you've answered this, but if you look out 25 years from now — you're going to be doing this — and maybe I'll say 40 years from now because you're going to be doing this for a long time — what's the part of Apollo's culture that needs to survive you? What is the core piece of the business that you want to remain the same?

Look, there's a bunch of elements to this.

  • Clean sheet thinking. Let's not try simply to improve. Let's ask the question of what the right answer is.
  • Informality. Informality in terms of intellectual insubordination, which I contrast with real insubordination. An environment where the right answer wins, where we can treat each other as humans.
  • Moments that matter. This is a lifetime job for people. The business ultimately runs on experience. And that only lasts if your partners stay with you for their entire career. If they're going to stay with you for their entire career, we have to recognize they're going to have outside their careers a bunch of happy things that happen to them and a bunch of sad things for them. How we deal with people in these moments that matter — at 4,000 people, at 6,000 people, 2,000 — it's really important. It actually is almost more impactful than anything else we do.

And so, yeah, let's be really intellectually engaged. Let's have a challenged culture. Let's be really informal. Let's get to the right answer. Let's accept that the power hierarchy is at risk with technology especially. But let's also be human. Let's deal with people as people. And how you balance those two and how you put them together, I think is the magic.

And the team that's around me, I wouldn't trade for the team anywhere. Maybe it's just because they — we know each other — but I really do think they're exceptional.

It's awesome, Mark. Thank you so much for joining us.

Absolute pleasure. Thank you so much. Great.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一、最反常识的观察：Apollo 卖的根本不是"资本"，而是"稀缺性"
绝大多数人听到"万亿美元资管"会本能地认为它的核心资产是"钱"。但 Rowan 在采访中暴露了一个颠覆性事实——**Apollo 的瓶颈不是资本，而是"原创资产的产能"**。"If you give us any amount of money, we will not invest it." 这一句话把整个传统资管的"AUM 崇拜"打翻在地。
这意味着 Apollo 实际上是一家**"资产制造业公司"**——它不是把钱搬来搬去的金融机构，而是像台积电那样的"代工厂"，只不过它代工的是"投资级私募信贷"这种新物种。一旦你接受这个视角，Apollo 收购 Athene（永久负债端）、推动每日定价（流通性）、与 a16z 合作（上游订单流）的所有动作，就突然变得像一家**半导体公司在垂直整合产业链**：负债是晶圆厂的电力合同、原始资产是光刻机、二级流动性是封测厂。
跨领域连接——这与 OpenAI 的算力瓶颈、与新能源的电网瓶颈、与生物医药的临床产能瓶颈**完全同构**。整个 2026 年的世界，赢家不是钱

item.title: [Mercatus Center] The Hayekian Triangle: The Wealth of Nations at 250
item.dc:creator: Mercatus Center
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一个被忽视的悖论：Smith被"经典化"的代价

这场对话中藏着一个令人不安的递归：三位经济学家用了近两个小时讨论"为什么还要读Smith"，而他们给出的最有力的辩护——Boulding那句"After Samuelson, Who Needs Smith?"——本身就是一个失败的预言。Samuelson式的现代经济学之所以"丢失"了Smith，恰恰是因为它把Smith"封圣"成了教科书第一页的引用对象。被神化的思想家，往往是被埋葬最深的思想家。Peter Boettke说Mises写过一篇"糟糕的"Wealth of Nations导读，本质上是在说：连最虔诚的追随者都不得不通过贬低Smith来保护他。这是思想史上一个普遍现象——救Smith的方式，就是先杀死那个被供奉的Smith。

跨领域的意外连接：Smith、爵士乐与"开源代码"

Boettke提到"Smith不是权威，而是evolutionary adjacent input"——这其实是软件工程里fork和分支的逻辑，也是爵士乐里standards（标准曲目）的逻辑。爵士乐手不"演奏Coltrane"，而是把Coltrane当作可以分叉、改写、对话的基础结构。Wealth of Nations的真正价值不在于它"说对了什么"，而在于它的API设计足够好——开放接口、模块化论点、允许后人在任何抽象层级上接入。这就解释了为什么Hutchinson和Hume虽然在某些点上更深刻，却没成为"Smith"：他们写的是封闭的、自洽的成品，而Smith写的是可被无限分叉的开源仓库。一本书能存活250年，不是因为它正确，而是因为它可被继续编写。

隐藏的系统性真相：被你们三人都回避的那个矛盾

整场对话有一个未被言明的紧张关系：你们花了大量时间证明Smith反对殖民主义、反对奴隶制、强调底层尊严——同时又承认他的"savage talk"和文化等级。Virgil敏锐地指出"如果Smith底色是种族主义，那么整个自由方案就值得怀疑"。但更深一层的洞察是：Smith项目的内在张力恰恰是它持久的引擎。一个完全自洽的、没有道德污点的Smith，反而会失去演化潜力。Wealth of Nations之所以250年后仍被讨论，部分原因正是它内部装着一个"未解决的Smith"——pin工厂里变得"as stupid as possible"的工人，与扩展秩序中获得尊严的普通人，是同一支笔写出的两个矛盾命题。

最让我意外的观察是：你们三人讨论Smith时使用的"对话式、谦逊式、互相打断式"的风格本身，就是Wealth of Nations最深层的政治哲学的实践展演。Boettke说Smith写作有"graciousness and humility"，Chris说要重视"clarity in writing"——但你们没意识到，这场Podcast的形式（三个朋友互相说"我有点不舒服"、"也许吧"、"你说得对"）才是Smith真正传下来的东西。Smith最大的遗产不是分工理论，也不是看不见的手，而是一种允许同行之间在公开场合保持柔软的认知姿态。这种姿态在Marx、Mises、Keynes那里都没有。这或许才是"the system of natural liberty"在智识层面的真正样貌——它首先是一种对话伦理。

📝 转录文本

开场介绍

You're listening to the Hayek Program podcast. This podcast includes audio from lectures, interviews, and discussions from scholars and visitors of the F.A. Hayek Program for Advanced Study in Philosophy, Politics, and Economics at the Mercatus Center at George Mason University. To learn more about the Hayek Program, visit hayek.mercatus.org. To learn about graduate student fellowship opportunities with the Mercatus Center at George Mason University for students at Mason as well as at universities across the globe, please visit students.mercatus.org.

Welcome. My name is Virgil Stohr. I'm thrilled to be here with two great friends, colleagues, co-authors, Chris Coyne and Peter Boettke. Today, I want to talk about Adam Smith's The Wealth of Nations. This is meant to be the first in a series of conversations that we're going to have about political economy and social philosophy. Adam Smith is a figure that I know that the three of us were there. I know that we assign him to our students. I know that we talk about him often.

为什么还要读一本250年前的书？

Yes, the question that I want to begin, and I'll put it over to Pete first, is that why read any book that was written 250 years ago? And why still read this particular book?

Thank you for having us have this conversation, Virgil. It's a great opportunity for us. I had a great teacher in graduate school. His name was Kenneth Boulding, and he actually answered this question. He wrote a wonderful piece, which I hope everyone out there listening goes and reads. It was published in 1971, and it's called After Samuelson, Who Needs Smith?

Because a standard way to think about the evolution of ideas is that all that is good in the ancients is embodied in the moderns. And so then why would we ever read old books? The only reason why we'd ever read old books is because of antiquarian interests. You know, we're like, we're book dealers and we run around, we want to collect first editions or whatever. But what we wouldn't do is we wouldn't use them for science. You know, physicists don't need to go back and read Newton kind of thing, right?

And what Boulding argues is that that's not quite true, because there are ideas in Adam Smith that have evolutionary potential for science today that are not fully captured in Samuelson's embodiment of economics. And so then the question is, what are those things?

And I think that's kind of what, you know, so yes, it's true that Adam Smith still talks to us today about the human condition, about the social dilemmas we face, about the trap of violence that is possible, and also the possibility of peace and what would make that possible. And then finally, I think the science and art of political economy. So he gives us really sort of a lot to chew on in the book that we don't get when we just read standard modern economics.

科学作为对迷信与偏见的解毒剂

And, you know, to make a broader point, because hopefully we'll go dig down deeper in mechanics in a second, but Smith himself said science is the great antidote to superstition and prejudices. And he described science as going through three phases: a phase of wonder, a phase of surprise, and then a phase of appreciation.

In economic terms, we wonder at the mystery of the mundane. How is it that the dinner ends up on your table? How is it that the common woolen coat ends up on the back of the day laborer? None of us know how to produce those things independently of all these individuals. And then Smith surprises us by showing us that the governing dynamics of how that wonder comes about is through the channeling of self-interest within particular sets of institutional constraints. And so we are surprised by that result. And then we grow in appreciation of those institutional constraints precisely because we were so surprised.

And so to me, I think when we understand Adam Smith correctly, The Wealth of Nations is both a great example of this scientific endeavor to march us through wonder, surprise and appreciation, but it also is a great antidote to the superstitions and prejudices that have plagued humanity from antiquity to modernity, which includes things like military adventurism, colonialism, imperialism, protectionism, all of these kind of ideas that he dispels the myths of throughout the arguments in the book.

And so I think that's why we read Adam Smith. He still speaks to us today. We can learn a lot more from him than we can learn sometimes from standard journal articles or textbooks. And I'll end with one other quote that was constant throughout my graduate educational career, which was from one of my other teachers, Jim Buchanan. And he used to say to us all the time, "What more do we know about markets today than what Adam Smith taught us in 1776?" And he'd pause. And then he would say, "If we can't have an answer that has a huge magnitude, perhaps the emperor has no clothes."

Yeah. You say, perhaps the emperor has no clothes. And so I've been wrestling with this question that you've given to me ever since I was in my young twenties. And I still am fascinated in reading Adam Smith and I learned new things from him each time, you know, we read them in our, in our seminars and everything like that. So, yeah.

世界已经变了：Smith还能告诉我们什么？

Yeah. So Chris, I saw, so I, I both love that answer, Pete. And I'm, I think, deeply uncomfortable with that answer. Let me ask Chris a follow-up in this way. I mostly want to ask you if you agree, but I'll put two factors there.

When Smith was writing, there was less than a billion people, I think, in the world. Now there's 10 times of that, that when he was writing, there's way fewer countries, that the world was way less complex and what have you. And so the world seems like it's changed quite a bit since 1776. And so this person writing about the world then sort of unclear what it would mean about the world now. So it's sort of one question, the one factor.

The second one is since Smith, a bunch of folks, including us, have devoted a lot of time trying to understand political economy. And presumably we've done meaningful stuff since he's written. And if we haven't, then doesn't that say something about what we've been up to for the 250 years. And so I'm sort of just curious about how you respond to Pete or how you respond to me or what have you. Or both.

You know, my initial reaction to your first question is not as sophisticated as Pete's, which is that you should read it if nothing else, just because everyone else is doing it. The very fact that there's celebrations across the world and gatherings and academic meetings, proceedings in both written and public forms suggest that there's something to the book itself. And so if you want to be one of the cool kids, you might want to check it out. That's one reason just to read it in and of itself, know what's going on.

But beyond that, I think Pete largely hit the nail on the head. And just to build on it, if you think about, you know, Virgil, the kind of literature in economics and economic development when you and I were coming up through the ranks, late 90s, early 2000s in graduate school. It was all the stuff on development institutions. And what did almost every paper start with in those debates? Adam Smith.

And the interesting thing is economists could pick a quote and they could say, "Adam Smith said geography is important. See, geography is important for development." And then other people could say like, "oh, Smith says institutions are important." And people could say, you know, "that trade is important." And then it was all based around that. That, again, suggested in and of itself that the questions are relevant.

Then you kind of fast forward to the more recent stuff. And what's all the stuff now? Just the system of natural liberty under various different names. You can call it the narrow corridor. You can call it kind of doorstep conditions to this, that or the other thing, whatever you want to dress it up as. People seem to realize that the kind of system of natural liberty, which we can return to later that Smith talked about, seems to be quite important.

In terms of the question you asked, here's something to think about. We can talk about this more. Maybe it's that we haven't, it's not that we haven't learned anything. It's that the principles that Smith points us to is that there's no uniform recipe for getting development because it's not something you just get like you get a cup of coffee. The title itself that you are inquiring into the nature and causes suggests that those are things that need to be explored and understood. And while there might be kind of general principles that we can follow, which I think there are, and which shed light on things, the way they manifest themselves in practice varies across time and space, although the principles themselves do not. And once we introduce and understand that point, then your point about changes in population, changes in the number of nations, the constitution of individual nations and societies kind of makes sense, but also gets interesting because now we have a whole host of research questions to think about and talk about.

人们真的理解这本书吗？关于"自由放任"的常见误读

No, I like that. So I guess I'm curious, too, about if you think, if you both think people actually really understand the book, right? You mentioned, Chris, that there are all these celebrations of the 250th anniversary of the book that people continue to read it. People continue to write about it for years. People were with the whole literatures that were "Adam Smith said X" and sometimes "X" and "not X" were both things that people thought Adam Smith said.

And so I'm sort of, I'm, so I'm going to, what I'm going to do now, I think is raise a couple of common takes about the book and ask you to react to them, and they're not there for either of you. And so you should sort of please just jump in and sort of respond if you have something that you want to add.

But one claim that gets made is that, you know, Adam Smith is revered as this champion of capitalism, this sort of pro free market type person, but that The Wealth of Nations has all these exceptions to free markets and laissez faire. And so it's not really a defense of laissez-faire. So I'm curious about what you think of that claim.

I think both the laissez-faire being either a defense or not a defense is grounded in a serious set of misconceptions. So one of them, let me just give you an example of why people say it's not a defense, is that they'll point to this letter that Smith wrote after the book was published, in which he says the following thing: that The Wealth of Nations is, quote unquote, "an aggressive assault on all of British commercial life." People read that and then they say, "ah, see, Smith is against, you know, capitalism," but he wasn't writing that against capitalism. He's writing that against mercantilism. The swapping of coins for things does not make a market, right?

And the other thing is, is that people think laissez-faire, as this is what Thomas Carlyle later on, century later, described as "anarchy with a constable." All right. So the question is, these guys are anarchy with a constable. And they somehow think that somehow, you know, Smith is going to say something like, you know, the people who are critics of it, they say, "oh, you think Smith says greed works." Right. And therefore greed under any set of circumstances will channel private interest into the publicly desirable outcomes. See, Smith didn't believe that. Therefore, you must be foolish.

So part of the issue is that the people who appropriated Adam Smith in order to defend their position about laissez-faire did it in a really bad job. And none of them, those defenders of quote unquote laissez-faire actually understood what laissez-faire was. They totally misunderstood. They tried to do laissez-faire without law, without politics, without society. And as a result, they gave a bad definition of what the classical liberals were all up to.

On the other hand, the critics did the same thing. And so they misunderstood. So in both cases, they're both wrong, in my opinion. And that we would be much better off if we just tried to say, "everyone, chill out, you're wrong." And let's actually go to the text. It's like when I was a kid, there's a sportscaster on the TV back in New York named Warren Wolf. And he always used to say when there was debates about what went on, he'd say, "let's go to the videotape," right? And what we should do is we should actually go to the text, contextualize the text, and then go through all of those things about what are those general principles. And like what Chris said before, what are the general principles? And then what are the particularities of the application of those principles?

And that's like, you know, to me, I think, I think a large part, one of the main problems with the debate was the idea that you could do in 20th century social science without institutions, social science without taking into account norms, conventions, and mores, all the things that are a part of what Adam Smith is writing about.

Smith文本的复杂性与"什么都能读出来"的代价

Of course, Pete, one of the things we need to think about is that people do go to the text and they quote passages and they come to a slightly different interpretation than you do.

Yeah, they're wrong. Yeah. Perhaps.

But to go to Virgil's question about it's kind of prompted the people understand the book. In some sense, it's like, who knows? I mean, the nice thing about Smith is that there's, this goes back to why I read it, there's like something for everyone. The potential downside of that, not downside, but like thing we need to think about is there's something for everyone, which is why people can pick and choose.

Now, again, there's like, there are right and wrong facts. I'm not trying to say like, oh, the words on the paper mean, even though some Smith scholars believe that too, like, look, I can read every eighth word and there's the, you know, secret decoder, like in Christmas Story with the Ovaltine. But the, you know what I mean, where it's like, look, the, I think the point you raised is a good one, which is that part of it's what you mean by laissez-faire. If you mean like you can do whatever you want when you want to do it, then that's not what Smith meant, I don't think, at least based on my reading.

But he also clearly recognizes that there are issues, potential issues with markets that we can delve into. And some of those are due to politics. Some are due to purely endogenous forces in markets. So we have like the division of labor on the one hand, but then like the discussion, well, you know, if people are outside just mowing a lawn every day, for instance, they might become like dullards. And then that runs into issues with like, civics and everything like that.

The cool part, I think, about Smith is the richness and the complexity of the story, because he does highlight that everything's kind of wrapped up and entangled. This was your point about politics, society. We need to appreciate all those things.

And again, part of the criticism, the way I understand it of people of this, is that the way I get it... but not every time people invoke Adam Smith, are they going to give a full in-depth reading? It's like Milton Friedman talks about the invisible hand, and he uses it to justify markets. I like that. I think there's something to that. It doesn't capture all of Smith.

But the final thing I just want to say, and then Virgil, I want to hear your thoughts on this too, is it's cool to talk about what Smith thought, but in my own view, we shouldn't get too caught up like Smith is the authority that either justifies or dispels markets. It's like, all right, he pointed out some cool things, but turning to him as kind of the ultimate arbiter of the desirability of markets perhaps isn't the way to go in and of itself. But even that's a point of contention.

工人与雇主：合作还是对立？

Yeah. So I think so. So Smith is interesting here because I do think that if you take about what was Smith arguing about — he does say for someone who we hold up as a champion of markets, some really peculiar sort of things that I, you know, given my views on markets, find kind of odd.

That one he sets up, you know, so chapter eight, he talks about the wages of labor, but in there he sets up that there's a necessary battle between the worker and the employer. And that in that battle, he says, well, the worker's necessarily going to lose. And that the employer's goal is going to be to drive down worker income to as low as possible.

And so I get the logic of that, but it sort of strikes me as a sort of odd way to frame the nature of the relationship between employer and employee, right? That one could think about it as, "oh, that's actually a cooperative relationship between the worker and the employee." The employer needs the workers in order to be able to achieve some end and some goal. The workers need the employer in order to be able to earn a living. Those aren't against one another. Those are working in concert with one another. For Smith to highlight that strikes me as being for someone who maybe has even a more favorable view of markets than Smith, it strikes me as an odd sort of thing.

And so it is... I want to maybe step back because I take your point, Chris, to be... okay, well, Smith, 250 years ago, said some interesting things about the nature of economic life and maybe the potential of markets to lead to wealth. That doesn't mean that everything he says is exactly right and has to be taken as exactly right. And so I think that's, I take that to be true.

It also though is the case though that I do think it's useful to like try to get at like what in fact did he mean precisely because he said some profound and interesting things about the nature of market. And it seems to have been more right about that than most. And so it sort of makes it harder for me to dismiss some of the things that he did say.

一千页书与论证的"主导倾向"

Let me ask another one of these common takes on The Wealth of Nations.

Before you ask that question, can I just say one thing about what I think with both of your answers, just very quickly? We're wrong.

Is that what you're going to say, Pete?

No, not wrong. I'm just going to say that The Wealth of Nations is a thousand page book, right? It's a thousand page book. And so the question I always ask on these when people bring up certain passes, what's the preponderance of the argument that he gives?

Now, he doesn't give, I'm not saying that Adam Smith is like the pinnacle of libertarian thinking or whatever, right? But in many ways, he was made evolutionary, he's an evolutionary adjacent character. Without Smith making the arguments, they don't get made later on. And part of the problem with the answer to my first question is that science, after Mill got derailed and it lost its Smithian evolution. And so that meant that that had to be rediscovered later on, which is part of why it is that we can still learn so much from him. But it's not like you're treating him as an authority. You're treating the text and the arguments as evolutionary adjacent inputs. So they're part of what Boulding calls our "extended present," right?

And I want to use the example that you just raised, Virgil, because I think that there's different in that chapter on wages of labor. There's a different way to think about that. So what we know now, or what we would like, I would take the perspective is that workers compete with workers and employers compete with employers, right? It's not that workers compete with employers. That's not the case.

But when we're talking about what, you know, Smith is bringing this up. He's not explaining the balance of power and, in a kind of monopsonistic way. I think you just have to recognize that when you have a buyer's market, that's different than when you have a seller's market. And at the early stages of any market, the first person that's opening up is going to have a buyer's market.

关于Walter Williams的轶事

And so that's what's going to, you know, all of us, you know, I, I, I'll shut up after this, but I'm sure that you guys have all heard this, but my dad loved Walter Williams. Yeah. My dad loved Walter Williams. And so I had opportunities to go elsewhere when I came here or stay where I was. And my dad said to me, "when a man like Walter Williams calls you to duty, you show up." That's what he said to me.

So after I was here, I told Walter that story. And Walter's comment to me wasn't like, "oh, that's great" or anything like that, which I expected him to say. He says, "damn, I wish I knew that. I could have got you for a lot less." Yeah. Right.

And so I think that, you know, we all would like to get that kind of idea, but that's not necessarily this exploitive relationship, which a lot of people read into that story. Anyway, maybe I'm torturing the text here, but you have a thousand page book. What is the preponderance of the nature of the argument is what I would always like to stress. And it's going to affect the way we talk about these other questions. That's why I wanted to bring it up.

Yeah, not for nothing though, Pete, that Marx in his philosophical, economic philosophical manuscript makes exactly the same point that you made just now about, well, buyer's markets, it looks different. Seller's markets looks different. It's all about... I don't know. I mean, yeah, but but you know, it's interesting.

So let's say let's go back to Virgil's initial kind of summary of the wage stuff and let's say let's just take it what it is. It's like, all right, so not every market's the same. So let's say that's one scenario that can happen, but it's certainly not the only scenario that can happen. But now we start introducing other stuff, which hopefully we'll get to. I mean, again, this goes to your laissez-faire question, Virgil, which is the thing Smith recognizes. He talks about collusion, of course, but he also talks that we can get into mercantilism and regulation. So then it makes no sense to talk about labor markets without labor regulations, not just labor regulations, but market regulations, which restrict competition, which then gets us into the early parts of the book with division of labor specialization, increasing the extent of the market. So maybe we can get to that eventually. But all these things are bound up, I think.

自利、贪婪与制度环境

Yeah, I think that's fair. Let me raise another common take on the book. And Pete mentioned it briefly, but maybe it's an opportunity to say a little bit more about it. And it's that the book is all about self-interest, greed, egoism.

So first of all, if you look at The Theory of Moral Sentiments, the very first sentence of The Theory of Moral Sentiments says — however, I'm going to get the quote wrong, but I'm going to come back to that in a second — "However selfish may man may so appear," right? There's evidence of his benevolence. And it's very interesting that in the famous chapter on the, "not from the benevolence of the butcher, the baker, the brewer," he says it's with regard to their self-love.

One ways that you could think about this is with regard to self-interest rightly understood, not necessarily narrow, egotistical, the way we've modeled it later on. So that in Smith, it's not what Veblen later referred to as, you know, this "lightning calculator of pleasure and pain" and blah, blah, blah, and all this stuff. And so that's one aspect of it. I think Smith has a richer notion of what is behind mankind's motivations.

But the second one is that he is going to see that this self-love can only be channeled in productive directions within a certain specific set of institutions. And so one of the examples that I always like to stress is Smith talking about professors in Oxford and professors in Glasgow. And he doesn't say that the professors in Glasgow are just better professors than the professors in Oxford. You can assume that the professors are professors. But what happens is the different reward mechanism in Oxford is different than the reward mechanism in Glasgow. And as a result, the professors do a better job in Glasgow than they do in Oxford.

And so I think that that's kind of the style of invisible hand explanations that is underlying Smith's work. And so it's not the Gordon Gekko, "greed works," you know, kind of interpretation of Smith. It's a much more complicated issue having to do with individuals who find themselves within various institutional environments who are responding, trying to improve their circumstances as best as they can, given their limitations and everything else.

So we can't even trust the professor motivated by a calling to educate young minds to actually show up and do the job that we're too are slaves to the way the incentives sort of push us hither and thither. We have no moral bearing. We have no commitment. We have no true love.

That's a different question though, right? Because that's a question that doesn't need an explanation. That's a question that just is a description. Good professors do a good job.

But it does get to what the book's about though, right? Is the book about self-love or is the book about human in their fullness with the stuff that you mentioned from TMS?

Or both. Why not both? Yeah. Let me stake out the sane middle ground. That's what I like to call it. That's the scientific name.

Pete, just to support your point on the passage you read, even in Wealth of Nations, which we'll get back to this later maybe, but in the famous passage about the man of the system and the man of public spirit, the man of public spirit is motivated by benevolence and a sense of humanity. So Smith certainly recognized space for that.

One of the interesting things I think with Smith is, and Pete, you've written papers on the robust political economy issue that you link back to Smith and Hayek and others, and we can revisit that later. But the reason I raise that is even in a situation where people are purely motivated by some kind of narrow self-interest, the mechanisms and the kind of causal processes that Smith points us to allow us to explore that as well and at least have a understanding of what that might look like and how it might generate beneficial outcomes. And so when I say the middle ground, it seems like there's space for both, that Smith doesn't want us to have the Gordon Gekko view of everyone in markets, but that doesn't mean there won't be those people out there. There certainly will be. And then how does that play out in the system? And this is your point about different institutional environments and what that means.

Smith与Mandeville的区别

As an intellectual history point, though, Virgil, I think it is, you know, the evidence siding on your side for this more complex notion of a human is the fact that Smith viewed himself as different from Mandeville, right? So his project is different from Mandeville's project. And he's very explicit about that kind of idea.

And so while I agree with Chris that the institutions in some sense can work together, or in fact do work even under very strict assumptions about, you know, the Mandeville type thing, the vices or whatever. The bigger issue for Smith is that there's virtue and prudence that's involved in the individuals. And there's a kind of like a moral framing and learning and all of that stuff that's involved.

And so, I mean, I think it was a great discussion of Smith and humanity in Martha Nussbaum's book on cosmopolitanism, the failed ideal or whatever. She has two chapters on Smith. And one of her conclusions is that Smith requires us to be more stoic than what we are built to be in some sense. But that's the part of the developing of our moral character and our ability to see outside of ourselves and all these other things that are like the kind of underpinnings of the nature of the market, I would see.

And I think, you know, going back to Chris's response to the first question, this is one of the things that we've seen happen in economics. So if you tell my narrative and after Mill economics gets derailed, but yet there is some very valuable scientific ideas in the Marginalist Revolution, part of the post 1950s movement to bring institutions and bring humanity back into economics is to actually recapture aspects of what Smith and to John Stuart Mill were doing, but now sift them through the marginalist principles. So we have scientific advance and that's kind of what we've inherited.

So no, Smith, you can't just give — you know, one of our favorite economists, Ludwig von Mises wrote a introduction to a shortened abridged version of The Wealth of Nations. You know, that was one of his things when he came to America. And it's kind of a crappy introduction. That's hard for me to say about Mises, because he kind of trashes Smith in a weird way, but not because Smith didn't write a great book. He was just trying to warn modern audiences. You can't just go to Adam Smith and learn economics. In some sense, he was saying, you can only do that by reading me. So you have to read my book. But Adam Smith can't be just read alone. And so I think there's something about that which is actually true. But you have to say it in a more subtle way than the way Mises did, I think.

两本书的关系：道德情操论与国富论

I wonder, can Smith be read alone question? Your answer made me, and your invoking of The Theory of Moral Sentiments, made me sort of wonder about, can you read or even make sense of The Wealth of Nations without The Theory of Moral Sentiments? Might be a question that you might ask. And that people have, you know, this has been a long debate within the literature. I think it's probably resolved in one direction now. But I'm curious about, as people who have both read both of Smith's famous books a ton, one take that they are still very different books, I think comes out, even if people see less of attention. So I'm curious about if you think of them as very different books.

Do I think of, yes, I think of them as different books. Do I think they're related? I certainly do. Can you understand one without the other? I think, of course you can. The same way I could read Virgil, your book on pirates and then your book, later book on culture of markets or on the morality of markets. I could, I, well, I have read them. I could read them again. Multiple times I've read them, Virgil. Thank you. On my end table at home.

In any case, you could read those in isolation and understand what you're saying. But I think if actually you read the whole series together, you get a more comprehensive understanding of how markets operate. And I think it's the same thing with Smith. You know, again, to my own way of thinking, which is just that, driving the wedge between the two and being like, well, there's, you know, the Adam Smith problem of this great, you know, break between the two or wedge between the two. I mean, people can hold that view if they want. An alternative view is that they're related. And we just kind of got at that with our discussion of the kind of select quotes from Wealth of Nations, where at least Smith seems to recognize that, you know, there are human motivations and features of individual human nature and characteristics beyond narrow opportunism.

So, you know, if you want to go down this path, one interesting kind of thought experiment is to read them as distinct books. Another is to read them as connected and think through what that means. Like, what if you read Wealth of Nations by taking Theory of Moral Sentiments is kind of given and is a background condition in Smith's mind. Of course, we can never know for sure, but that's kind of an interesting thought experiment. And so that's how I've always thought about it, partially because I was a student of Pete's from a young age. So that's always how I've heard him talk about it. And I think that's right now that I've spent years reading them.

There's also a history of thought point that he kept reviving The Theory of Moral Sentiments long after he published The Wealth of Nations. So he's probably not himself off. It's not like he changed his mind and he had an opportunity to sink them, even if they didn't begin that way.

Yep. It's also, you know, been argued that the notebooks from which he wrote The Wealth of Nations were derived from his efforts at revising The Theory of Moral Sentiments. So there's a — that's obviously, you know, none of us can go and talk to Adam Smith and say, but this is what a lot of people did. I'm disappointed that I know seances in 2026 where we get. And none of us have a, you know, a phone line to God to double check it. Right.

发展经济学的觉醒史

So we're all. But I think that maybe one of the things about contextualizing to see this issue is actually what you and Chris were talking about development economics at the time when you were graduate students. Because if you go back a decade before that, the nature of development economics would have been taught totally different. That's before communism collapsed.

Once communism collapsed, people went through a kind of a three-stage wake up, right? The first one was, "oh, communism, when we reform it, they had bad prices. So we just got to get the prices right. So let's just, you know, they had a shortage economy. Let's like, you know, we're going to price liberalize. We're going to macro stabilize and we'll be able to fix it." That didn't work out, right?

So then people were like, "huh, you know, so maybe we can't just have prices without institutions. So we have to have these institutions and blah, blah, blah," you know. And so that led to property rule of law. And, you know, people ran all these, you know, studies, you know, trying to isolate what institutions are the key ones. Are they contracting institutions? Are they, you know, and all this kind of stuff.

And then, you know, people then started to talk and this has taken a variety of forms and you know, having to do with, say, for example, the evolutionary anthropology work of like Heinrich or others. So, or for example, just more recognition that we have to recognize that it's states, markets and moral community. So we have to pay that attention to that. And so what you see is this kind of continuing effort in the most cutting edge of the science, because it couldn't explain the lack of convergence of in the growth rates, and it couldn't explain the collapse of communism. Both of those things hit people in the head and they're like, "okay, I got to explain this." And so that set off this motion or this whole evolution of modern economic thought.

And if you take someone like McCloskey, what she's trying to do is take the two books and smush them together, right? That you can't explain the wealth of and poverty of nations, unless you explain the sort of moral virtues and the way that we interact with each other, the way we talk to one another, the way we give dignity to one another.

自然平等与街头搬运工

And so this is going to relate to some of your other questions, but, you know, Adam Smith, if you read him in this light, right? One of the things that stands out is that he wants to give dignity and respect to the people that are in the world that he's studying. He wants to give agency to them — rather than the agency to the expert from afar. That's Chris mentioned the public spirit versus the man of systems kind of idea. And part of that is, is there's a certain sense of humanity that he's recognizing.

And this is, I would argue is what's behind, and this relates to your professor point, at least in my head, it does. My mind works in weird ways, I guess, but in the street porter and a philosopher line, because I think when he's really the way I would read that passage is, Smith is criticizing any notion of the natural hierarchy of mankind, which was a very common doctrine at the time. And he's criticizing it and saying, "no, we're all natural equals."

Now, does that mean that we all are actual equals? No. Sometimes professors are good professors. Sometimes professors are bad professors, even under the same incentives. So, you know, one of my teachers at undergrad school, very, very nice man, worst professor I ever had. He would come into class with a textbook, right? There's 15 chapters in the textbook. He would read the chapter for the two lectures, you know, so chapter one, and he would read it out loud to us. That's how he taught. It was horrible. It was like, you know, and so like one of the lessons I learned when I was going to go teach is do not be like that, right? Told me like that. But he faced the same incentives as the professor that changed my life. but he was just a bad professor.

But I don't really need a theory to explain him. He was just a bad professor. The other one was a good professor. But that doesn't, what I want to do is I want to treat people analytically equal and then try to see that the differences in the patterns, as Smith says, are not so much from their nature, but instead, and I would put it, the context within which they've been nurtured. And so it's not to deny nature, but it's to put a big emphasis on the idea that it's the contextual environment within which we act that leads to the differences.

Smith与种族主义指控

So I like what you said. And there's also a bit of what you said that makes me a bit uncomfortable. And so let me try to—

That's twice that you've said that.

Yeah, I know. I know it. Only a bit though. Only a bit. We're still okay, guys. Yep. This is the nature of our relationship that I spend — since I met you, it's been 90% inspiration and 10% uh-oh. And so I'm trying to figure out how to reconcile that.

And so Smith has this line. I'll read the full quote, "where the difference between the most dissimilar characters, between a philosopher and a common street porter, seems to arise not so much from nature as from habit, custom, and education."

And I get that line, and I think a couple of things. One, I think almost identical to what you said. Look, that Smith is saying that at core, there's a kind of equality between human beings. At core, there's a kind of even potential that all human beings enjoy, that what differentiates them isn't something about their sort of like the DNA or what have you that would differentiate them in some sense, the opportunities that they've gotten or something like that.

So you might read it like that, but then you sort of drill down on like, what is he getting at with the sort of this habit talk and this custom talk? And that is he is in getting to this habit talk and this custom talk, is he then moving from a kind of naturalist critique to a kind of, you know, sort of cultural criticism that, you know, "no, the problem with the quote-unquote savages in this part of West Africa isn't that they're black, it's that they're savages, they're..." you know, so like I don't... and so does that — that actually gets to another take on the book that some people will challenge it on. And I want, I'm curious as to what, you know, maybe Chris first and then you, Pete, think about this, but the sort of notion that there's a kind of underlying, maybe even racism in the book, right? There's savage talk all over the place. There's certainly a sense that he values certain kinds of cultures more than he values sort of others. He does seem to think that there's a kind of, you know, maybe superiority to the, you know, certainly the sort of Western, maybe even Scottish type sort of context and as opposed to the other parts of the world. You see this maybe in how he talks about Native Americans. You see this in maybe how he talks about savages or whatever. And so how do you think about that particular take?

Yeah, well, you know, there's I think there's a couple of different ways to engage that point. One could point out, for instance, that for many of these things — colonialism, slavery — Smith argued that these were oftentimes, if not always, a bad deal for Europeans, certainly. So the European colonial project was a net cost, as he argued. Free labor was more productive than slave labor.

Now, of course, the response to that is that those are economic or kind of consequentialist type arguments rather than arguments based on first principles on issues of justice, on issues of human dignity, like Pete was raising before. And so from that standpoint, you know, I've just always thought of Smith as someone who is writing at a certain period of time. And again, I don't have any insider knowledge into his mind of where he really stood on these issues.

You know, with all thinkers, we need to contextualize them, but also think about if we want to hold them to today's standards. Certainly relative to people writing at the time Smith is writing, the fact that he was critical of these things, even if purely on purely economic or consequentialist grounds is in and of itself a significant difference, not for all people, but for many, he was quite different. And so from that standpoint, you know, I think we can give him credit, but also recognize that there are things that he perhaps didn't argue for, whether that's because of his own personal biases or flaws or because he just didn't choose to do it. I don't know.

But again, this is where I think we can connect the different readings from across the books. So what happens if we take the kind of issues that we're thinking about earlier about human dignity and all the other stuff, Pete's points about how we treat people, how we frame them, how we engage them, and then we extend that to these other areas — from to my way of thinking, that offers a richer picture. Whether Smith subscribed to that or not, I can't say for sure, but I also think it's irrelevant, irrelevant for understanding the world today, certainly.

Smith对奴隶贩子的强烈谴责

So I agree very much with that last statement because I think that one of the problems, again, Virgil, in the conversation is whether or not we're doing Smithian exegesis or we're looking at what is a Smithian argument that we can leverage for our understanding of the world today.
