So am I viewing Smith as an input into science or am I viewing Smith as an exercise in intellectual history? And one of the key questions that like someone like me holds is that intellectual history done in a certain way, that is what I refer to, you know, as opportunistic readings of individuals can be an input into science today. as opposed to faithful readings of Smith, which I'm all in favor of doing. I just don't normally think in those terms because I'm trying to leverage the arguments.

But just let me say something about Smith in this. He refers to the slavers, right, as "the refuse of the jails of Europe." That's how he describes them. He refers to the poor souls who have now been enslaved as heroes, right, "the heroes from these places." So at the time, that's like, you know, if you put him in context at a time, that sure is a lot stronger indictment than what you get from Hume, right? It was his friend or whatever, right? Let alone even Mill later on, you know, right?

So we know that he is a critic of colonialism, imperialism, militarism, right? In the fifth book of Wealth of Nations, when he's talking about why it is that countries, you know, go bankrupt, he refers to it as "a juggling trick" that governments engage in. They run deficits, accumulate debt, and then debase the currency. But what motivates these government spending? He says military adventurism. They go off to fight. It's what Chris refers to as going out to find monsters to destroy all over the world. And he's against that.

And so I think one of the things to go back to Chris's point is now this is the intellectual history of me, which is I think Smith is very interesting because he's writing in a time in which rights and utility or rights and consequences are not in conflict with each other. So he's writing before utilitarianism. And so to him, if it's right for a fish to swim in water, it's good for a fish to swim in water. All right. If it's right for human beings to exist under a system of natural liberty, it's also going to be good for them to exist under a system of natural liberty.

So there's kind of a mixing up in a way that we're not used to because we tend to think of, am I making a consequentialist argument? Am I making an act utilitarian argument, a rule utilitarian argument? Or am I making this natural rights argument, right? And we don't necessarily see the natural rights necessarily generate the consequences kind of idea. But I think to Smith that that's not what he's working with. And so I would see more weight in his moral indictment of things like slavery when he's also making the argument that they are not economically viable, right? Or they're economically costly.

Now, Virgil can point to, or any writer can point to, several passages which Smith defends militarism, that Smith makes some other things like that. But I think at the preponderance of evidence, is he hated the East India Company, right? And to me, I think you can derive a lot of his political economy by just tracing that out. Because what is it that he doesn't like about the East India Company? What does that say about today when we think about, let's say, you know, specially privileged businesses today or whatever, right?

And so I think that, you know, Smith is a precursor. And again, I can't expect a full-blown political economy of today to be in Adam Smith in 1776. But the question is, was what Smith gave us made it possible for us to now think in these alternative ways in the evolution of our ideas? And I think that's how I would do it. But to me, I think Smith stressing the issue of the agency and dignity of these individuals and the consent that's required for them rather than the imposition of from outside is a very important point.

为什么"Smith在道德上站对边"很重要

I guess I was going to say, I think that that way you both began and ended that answer is, I think, a thing that we don't maybe say enough, right? That I think there's sort of one, there's one defense of Smith on these grounds that is maybe fine enough, but I don't love. And it's the, "well, I'm just trying to get what's useful out of Smith. I'm sort of leaving aside all the non-useful bits," right?

Because the reason for that is if it turned out that the whole thing was rooted in some kind of sort of racism or elitism or whatever like that, that might actually undermine the potential of any of the good stuff that you could get out of it. I think it actually matters that Smith is on the right side of these kinds of questions, right? That he's anti-slavery at a time when that's not the dominant view, that he's sort of anti-colonialism at a time when that's not the sort of the direction that the state is going in, that he's, you know, sort of chiding the slavers and celebrating the enslaved. I think at a time when that's not, I think that matters because that actually talks about the sort of freedom potential of the entire system that he developed. That if that wasn't in there, then I think it might make us, maybe it might force us to be a little bit more suspicious about the freedom potential of the project. You might say, "well, it seemed like it's compatible with all this ick, but it turns out for him, it wasn't," right? And I think that that's a thing that it maybe matters that we say.

Yeah, it's a good point, Virgil. I actually like that a lot, to be honest with you. I think that's a, it's a, it's critical, right? Because there's fruit from the poisonous tree kind of argument on the other hand.

And yeah, I mean, it's interesting. You contrast, Pete, you brought up the imperialism point. You think of like Smith and like Mill even, right? I mean, in my reading of Mill is he's much more open to militarism abroad in the name of raising people out of development than Smith is. I don't mean that as an indictment of Mill. I like Mill too. And I think there's value in Mill, but Smith is unique in this set of critiques, again, even if it's not in today's language. And as Pete put it, which I listen to Pete about kind of the intellectual history point about the convergence of what today we'd kind of break out as kind of moral arguments and consequentialist arguments makes it even stronger, I think, given the time he's writing.

And also, Chris, along your lines, this is something I mentioned Boulding earlier, but this is also something Boulding stress in his AEA presidential address, as you know, right? So it's a key aspect. So Boulding was trying to bring back that aspect of Smith to modern economics. And just so people know, Boulding's address is, this is economics and moral science, right? Is the paper you're talking about?

Yeah. Yeah. And he's trying to make the argument that economics is a moral science on two levels, because on the one level, it's the agents in the economy who we're studying are imbued with moral agency, right? But we as scientists are also moral agents in the way that we do it. So we can't ever escape this idea that economics is a moral science. But our entire 20th century was built on the premise that we could have an economics that was completely, you know, amoral.

At both levels, Pete, right? At both levels. It's like, you know, we are scientists that step outside the system. So we don't impose our, you know, there's no — that's why, you know, chapter one of principles, normative, positive, we're only positive scientists. But then, you know, we've been talking about kind of contemporary development economics and like reinventing the wheel. We'll think about like Rajan stuff on like the third pillar, which is like, "hey, everyone, you know, communities and family and that might be an important thing we should think about." And in some sense, you might, one response to that is like, "that's kind of weird that you're recognizing that right now that that's important." But it's because economists didn't think about that. It's like markets, state, all this other stuff is we don't think about it.

I think this contrast with Smith and Mill is quite fascinating because Mill, we normally think of as the, you know, one of the strongest defenders of the liberal vision. And in many ways he is, but he also has a kind of a paternalism that he's pushing against because Mill again was, was, anti-slavery and things like that. But he also has this idea that, you know, some people need to be nurtured until they're ready for freedom. It's Virgil's culture point. I take it to be Virgil's point about culture from back when, right? Several minutes ago now. But that's his argument. Some people are not capable of self-governance. And so the harm principle, we'll come back to that later when you're more mature.

But I don't think Smith has that.

No, no. That's why I brought up the contrast as a point.

国富论里"糟糕的经济学"还剩什么？

Yeah. We've been sort of positive about Smith. There's another, I guess, a couple more takes on Smith, on The Wealth of Nations in particular that I want to raise. I think it's important, particularly since we're economists. One is that it's just actually bad economics in various places of The Wealth of Nations. It's got a labor theory of value, maybe at its core, that it seems to be all about at some sort of fundamental level. And so I guess I'm curious about what's the economics that survives from The Wealth of Nations and what's the economics that we should jettison?

You won't be surprised about this, my answer on this. So again, I think that Smith's puzzle that he thinks that we should answer is how do we have peaceful social cooperation under the division of labor. And so that is our explanandum. And our explanans is some kind of notion of what we would call today rational choice institutionalism. And so that research project, I think, is not only good economics, it's at the core of what economics is about.

One of the problems, I think, is that Smith didn't have the analytical tools that we later developed to be able to make that argument tighter. Now, it's also an interesting thing because in order to make that argument tighter, the tools were abused in the 20th century. So we ended up by making it so tight that we lost sight of some of the things that Smith was already telling us about.

So we have to do a kind of a two-step, which is on the one hand, we have to tighten up how it is I go from the... I derive social cooperation under the division of labor from the exchange behavior of individuals, right, and explain how that comes about. I could do that by making a very tight mathematical model about the first and second welfare theorems. But in the process of doing that, I lose the very mechanisms by which real human beings are ever come to solve social dilemmas among each other.

So then I wake up one day and I go, "oh crap, I got to start paying attention to something else." And I start introducing all these things. So I'm going to introduce, you know, irrationality. I'm going to introduce imperfect, you know, institutional structures. I'm going to introduce, you know, blah, blah, these various different things. But because I don't have the whole system working out each time the introduction comes in, it blows up my ability to get the social cooperation.

So the evolution of modern economics is all of us trying to figure out how Smith made his argument in the most unlikely of circumstances. Now, Smith doesn't do that, right? Smith just tells us that there's this cool thing, social cooperation and division of labor. And it begins from man's propensity to truck barter and exchange. So we start with truck barter and exchanges and we end up with social cooperation. And we have devoted 250 years of trying to tighten that argument. And after we've tightened it so tight, we lost it. So we needed to recapture it.

为什么奥地利学派重要

And this is why I think that, and this is, you know, obviously, again, listeners and everyone are going to, you know, they're going to accuse me of it. But this is why Austrian economics matters, right? Austrian economists were the ones that didn't fall into the trap of tighten it so close that you lose it. They tightened it and improved our understanding of it. And that's because they dealt with man, with his foibles, his alluring hopes and his haunting fears, the institutions within which we're involved, property, contract and consent, and the way in which the exchange, the buying and abstaining from buying behavior of individuals steer us in a way so that we can actually end up by engage in productive social cooperation with one another.

And so therefore you got Menger and Böhm-Bawerk and Mises and Hayek, and they're the ones, and then I would throw in Jim Buchanan as well, because I think he really is an Austrian, Israel Kirzner. And it's that research program, not Rajan's program, not Acemoglu's narrow corridor, not North's doorsteps. It's that research program, but that research program that then can find in our sense, you, me, and Virgil and others, like a way to talk to those modern developments. Right. bringing our ideas to them.

But it's that idea of going from the propensity to truck, barter, and exchange to productive specialization and peaceful social cooperation that is behind the wealth and poverty of nations. That step, those steps along that way, that's what we as economists have to lay out. And that's an enduring puzzle that Smith gave us, and that's good economics. The way he went about trying to explain it needs to be improved.

Smith的独特性：相比Hutchinson、Hume，为什么是他被保留下来？

So I like that. Let me ask one more take, I guess I want to get us all to respond to maybe. And it maybe gets back to the question I started us off with, which is like, why are we the wealth of nations after 250 years? Like, why are we still reading this particular book?

And it's that you will hear this charge sometimes that there's actually nothing original in Adam Smith, that he's, you know, Hutchinson had the division of labor and the critique of mercantilism. Hume did all the social philosophy stuff way better than Smith did anyway, so we don't really even need him for that. It's sort of like a peculiarity that he's the one that we've saved and what have you. And so I'm sort of curious about when you look out to the sort of the authors that came before, why does Smith and why does maybe The Wealth of Nations show up as a book you pull out and not these other figures?

Yeah. I mean, it's a good point. I mean, there's a broader point, too, of just why some thinkers are emphasized and others are not. And there's a intellectual history to that explanation of that. But, you know, if I was going to my first response is that one difference is Pete's point about peaceful social cooperation, but framed slightly differently, which is that Smith offers a more systematic, synthesized treatment of all the different factors you just listed in others, in a more linear type manner than those other thinkers did, recognizing the flaws, some of which we've talked about already.

And I think this goes back to your prior question, which Pete addressed about the labor theory of value. I've seen some Smith scholars kind of push back on that point. They'll call it like the laborish theory of value that, you know, he wasn't a full-blown in here at — again, my own view, I mean, it's chapter five, I think I was just trying to find it where he talks about the price of commodities. He says it like in the opening lines of that chapter that it's the amount of labor that's invested. But then the question is, even in the strong form of that, what do you lose in the book? And I think something compared to, as Pete was explaining, what we know now about value and the derivation of value. But there's lots of things we don't lose. And those are really important things. It doesn't affect the division of labor specialization.

I mean, the other thing we need to remember talking about Smith being an outlier is the very nature of wealth itself, which is that wealth is not just a function of natural resources. Of course, much of the argument for war was that it was viewed as the source of wealth. You need to accumulate stuff by taking it from people. From that standpoint, another contribution of Smith, whether I'm not necessarily he was the first person in humanity to recognize it, but emphasized by Smith is that a key part of wealth creation is creation, that people with agency through coordination need to figure out how to do stuff. And that is how wealth is created. It's not just a function of stocks of things that are out there without the mixing of the human element.

And so, again, for the other thinkers you've listed and others as well, Pantaleoni and others, it's great to read them too. And so maybe they are underestimated thinkers or under-read thinkers. But to my own thinking, that doesn't in any way kind of undermine Smith's value and what he's trying to do across the two books.

Smith的笔锋与"谁讲述你的故事"

I was going to say that Virgil's question is a great question. I really like your first answer to that, Chris, which is about the systemic synthesis of all these earlier writings. But I think we also have to recognize that Smith had a gift with the pen, right? He just wrote better than a lot of other people. Hume was a great writer too, but he was snarky. So, you know, I've seen people refer to David Hume as the first internet troll, right? In the way that he, you know, wrote or whatever. And Smith writes with a kind of a graciousness and humility in which his snarkiness is only directed at those who think of themselves as your betters. Right. And so that's where it comes out is sort of sharpness of his wit.

But I then think finally, so you guys know this story, but during COVID, when we all moved to the online teaching system, I was trying to find ways to be more entertaining than just staring into the black screen. And so I was teaching this course and I started with playing the song from Hamilton, "Who Tells Your Story," which is at the end of Hamilton. And I was stupid because I thought people really far away from me because they're in the computer and I can't see them. So I had it on full blast and it was before I had headphones or anything like that. So my whole house was filled with the song and Rosemary came in and she's like, "what the hell are you doing?" And I'm like trying to tell her that I'm on the, the, you know, the class. Yeah, I'm teaching.

But I think that that's that particular song in that musical has a very, very important aspect to it. And I think that Smith was very fortunate in who got to tell his story and the motivation and dedication that they all had. I just retweeted an old article that Richard Ebeling points to, which is a discussion of the celebration of Adam Smith at 100 years. So not 250 years, but what actually went on 100 years. And Jean-Baptiste Say's grandson was there to represent his father because J.B. Say, who was the leading French economist, was — many people say is basically translated Adam Smith into French and then published the book, again, a systematic treatise. And Say tells the story of how Say made a sojourn to Glasgow or whatever and sat where the seat that Adam Smith sat in. And he said that his grandfather was told the story that he sat there and then he like prayed that he would absorb all the brilliance of Adam Smith to be able to bring it back to France.

When you have followers, quote unquote, followers or people that are willing to do that, you know, all of us are involved in the ideas business. We had a brilliant sociologist come when we had our first markets in society. Her name is Viviana Zelizer. And we went to dinner with her. And one of the things, I don't know if you remember this, Virgil, that she said was she was talking about how surprising it is to her how quickly intellectuals disappear, scholars disappear from the conversation. So they were the top scholar in 1990. And now in 2020, no one even remembers who they are. Right.

And so to have somebody that wrote in 1776 and we still know who they are meant that there was a lot of people along the way that made that possible. And that's part of what we study in reception studies. And how it is that they interpreted them, how it is they maintain them, how they reprinted the books, you know, and all of that stuff. And so why is it that Adam Smith got picked? A large part of it is he just, it's the book itself reads better than I think the books otherwise. But also it's the case that he just had good fortune to have good followers and people that made his ideas alive for the next generation and the next generation and the next generation after that. I don't know if we have a systematic answer to that.

Yeah, no, so I think I largely agree with all of you. I'm more, I sort of maybe lean a lot into the it's a laborish theory of value in the sense that I think he certainly says it, but I don't think he makes much use of it throughout. It's sort of unclear how core it really is to some extent. And so I'm not sort of sure how much one would want to dismiss of his work because it turned out that that's wrong. That it, that, that Marx who I've, I've, you know, read a lot more about, made much of it. And so you rarely see it being sort of essential in quarter to Marx's framework. And so if the labor theory of value is wrong, then much of Marx is certainly wrong, that it's unclear that if the labor theory of value is wrong, that much of Adam Smith is actually wrong, that you sort of could take out those passages and what have you.

But I do think even there when he's talking about that, but certainly sort of elsewhere, To Pete's point, he's an amazing writer. And so you're sort of stuck with some of his lines and passages sort of saying, you know, like from TMS, if you read him talk about, you know, the poor man's son or something like that, that that is actually like actually, you know, good literature, something like to want to think about it. It sort of inspires that kind of thing.

分工的阴暗面：工人的"愚钝化"

One of the things I wanted to do sort of before we conclude is actually pull out a passage from Smith that I wanted to get Pete in the first instance and then Chris in the second instance to respond to. Because I think that they, one, I think they were very well written passages in Smith, but I think they're also provocative.

And so the one for Pete is in book five, towards the end, that Smith, who had been talking favorably about the division of labor for the entire book, offers the following passage. Chris alluded to this, I think, in an earlier answer, but he says:

"In the progress of the division of labor, the employment of the far greater part of those who live by labor, that is, of the great body of people, comes to be confined to a very simple operations, frequently to one or two. The man whose whole life is spent performing a few simple operations of which the effects are perhaps always the same, very nearly the same, has no occasion to exert his understanding or to exercise his invention in finding out expedience for moving difficulties which never occur. He naturally loses, therefore, the habit of such exertion and generally becomes as stupid and ignorant as it is possible for a human creature to become."

It sort of goes on, right? And the story gets worse. The human being actually becomes more of a moral monster as a result of this. And so the framing there at least in that passage, is you've got the division of labor, this thing that's the font of all the productivity gains and the wealth of nations, and then the impact that that has on the individual worker, which is that he becomes as stupid and ignorant as is possible for a human being to become, at least is the way. Like, how do you understand that claim? And isn't this a point of view that we'd more often associate with Marx? That this sounds much more like alienation than it sounds like what we think sort of someone who was liked markets or was positive markets would say?

规模经济 vs. 范围经济

I mean, first of all, it is very nicely written and very challenging, right? The second thing is that we as observers of the world should not try to do Jedi mind tricks, right? And that is to, you know, wave a hand and say, there is no misery or monotony in like working in the factory division of labor kind of idea. I've actually worked on an assembly line, so I kind of know what that's like. And I've seen the people that work on the assembly line. And I tried to get out of that job as fast as I possibly could, right, kind of thing. And I understand that kind of idea.

I do think technically Smith's benefits of the division of labor relate to economies of scope. That's going to be why the division of labor is limited by the extent of the market kind of idea. And so that's about the, whereas what you're talking about here is like the guy in the pin factory learning to straighten out the pin, right? And then we're just going to, now there are efficiency gains in that, but those efficiency gains are not enough to explain the wealth of nations, right? This is a McCloskey point. Those are labor efficiency gains. What matters is the tremendous increase in specialization and exchange that's made possible as we grow the market. So we get economies of scope, increasing returns to this growing of the scope, as opposed to the returns that are, you know, increasing returns, you know, diminishing returns kind of thing from any one exercise within the labor market.

And so I'm not saying, by the way, Smith was making that distinction. A whole boatload of economics needs to be addressed later on. But I think if you read in Smith, these notions of economies of scope versus economies of scale, that would be the way that I would tackle it.

But at the same time, I think it's very valuable. One of the great things about Adam Smith, Chris alluded to it earlier, which is that many people can read many different things in to him. I think that's one benefit that a lot of authors benefit from. That's part of the reason why people say Keynes became so important because he was confusing. And so I had no idea what was going on. So then it creates a cottage industry. I don't think Smith is that, but I understand many people can read many things in.

But I think what Smith is, is he's an astute observer of a complex reality. And so we as students of civilization have to understand that the world is not smooth and continuous and twice differentiable. And so as a result, it has all kinds of jagged, sharp edges to it. And one of the issues is how do we have social cooperation in spite of the fact that we have all these problematic situations?

So if you think about it, I think it's about, you know, how is it that we have, for example, in the passage prior to the butcher, baker and brewer, right? If you go back to it, he says, "we require a great cooperation of a great multitude, but scarce in our life do we have the opportunity to make but a few close personal friends." How is it that we're able to achieve social cooperation when we don't have the ability to rely on the benevolence of our friends? That puts the puzzle in a very stark way.

I think this is another way in which, you know, there's a puzzle. Yes, we increase by straightening out the pin and another guy putting the pin, you know, on the head and, you know, another one, you know, fixing the fine point of the point of the pin. And then we get more pins produced. But there's a downside to that if that's all we do. But the benefit of this economies of scope is that the number of jobs is infinite. And as each market grows, we have alternative ways to do so. We graduate from doing the pin to something else. And so if we're just getting the economies of scale, we have a reason why we would have declining aspects to it as it continues to increase. But if we have these economies of scope, we keep on seeing new and new opportunities. And so the machinery then ends up by freeing us up to do other things. And then we do that. And then we're freed up to do these other things. And that's the way I would sort of try to tackle it. But I'm not as an astute observer of reality as Adam Smith. But that would be the way that I would try to tackle it.

Smith错了吗？关于异化的反驳

Yeah. As you were talking in the beginning, I was like, well, if it turns out that I'm not famous by the end of my career, I'll blame it on my being too clear a writer.

Yeah, you got to be more confusing in life. I got to be more confusing so I could up my situation. People don't know what you're saying and they can get whatever they want out of your stuff. That's the key. This is ideal.

But I wonder why we don't say the following thing in response to that. Smith was just wrong here. The Smith at the beginning of the book who's talked about the fact that the person in that boring task is going to be attuned to opportunities to innovate and to get to escape that task is the right version of Smith. And the one who's talking about this alienation stuff, he just got it wrong. He was a professor who couldn't imagine anything worse than working on a sort of an assembly line of the assembly line of his day. And so like sort of over, it is true that, you know, that particular task is boring. But one of the things that's cool about a boring task is you then get to chat with your friends next to you, or you get to sort of go on flights of imagination in your head and think about the next thing or what have you. Like, there's not just this kind of it makes you dumb. It seems like there's also this, it creates these other opportunities. It's what have you.

Maybe, Virgil, how would you respond to this, though? And again, this is me. It's speculative, but Look, Pete raises his own experience, but we all know people, maybe ourselves included, as in Pete's case, but who hate their job. And they either hate their job or they don't view it as fulfilling. It's a means to some and mainly for them to feed themselves and house themselves and so on. And some people are able to move on from that. But I know a lot of people, I'm sure you do too, spend their whole life in that setting.

And I think that's what people maybe have in mind. It's like, even now it's not flattening the pin. It's going to a cube and clocking in and there's FaceTime, corporate Face, not FaceTime, the app FaceTime. You know, I have to be here so my boss can see me. I run report monotonous reports all day or do some job like that. And then I go home and I'm tired and I do wake up and do it all over again. Sure. There's I can daydream and go hang out on the weekends, but 85% of my time is spent in a crappy job I don't like and I have no way out. I think that's what people are thinking of perhaps when they're thinking of that.

Maybe. So I'm not saying that people don't have crappy jobs or people don't have aspects of their jobs that are crappy, right? That's certainly not what I'm pushing back against. I'm pushing back on the moral and social consequences of working in a crappy job. And that's where I take Smith to be going in that passage, that he's not just saying, well, people have jobs that are sort of unsatisfactory or unfulfilling. I take him to be saying that because of those kinds of jobs, they turn into a certain kind of person. And that certain kind of person is weak of mind and weak of morals. And I just don't believe that is the social consequence of those kinds of jobs. That I think that it's perfectly possible to work a job like that or a job or to do the aspects of our jobs that we hate and come out the other side still, you know, intellectually intact and still morally intact. And that's the way I want to say, like, we should, on stuff like this, I think we can just be comfortable saying, we've got this piece wrong.

国防作为贸易的例外

I want to actually raise, this is the sort of final quote that I want to on the table and it's, I want you to respond to it. First Chris, which is another place where maybe he gets it wrong. And so I'm sort of curious as to your take there. He said, he says, you know, he spends the whole book, much of the book talking about the value of trade. And then he says:

"This seems however, to be two cases in which it will generally be advantageous to lay some burden upon foreign for the encouragement of domestic industry. The first is... when some particular sort of industry is necessary for the defense of the country."

And so I'm curious what he's arguing here. Maybe useful to frame on like why he thinks trade is generally good. Unfortunately, we have to make that case. We have to sort of lay out that argument in 2026. But people, but sort of like, why does he think trade is generally good? And then why does he think defense is an exception to it? And then do you think he's correct?

So the case for trade is straightforward from an economic standpoint, which is it's positive sum exchange that allows you to take advantage of the division of labor specialization. It's an increase in the extent of the market. There's more nuance below that, but that's the gist of it in a sentence, I think.

The national security exception is that there are certain goods that are crucial to the security of the nation such that if they were either blocked from occurring due to a policy choice by foreign government or due to war making itself, that the sustainability of the society would be threatened. That's how I understand it to be. And of course, this is very common and people invoke Smith all the time on this.

So then the question is, well, is he right or wrong? And it depends on one's view, of course. I mean, look, Smith did leave space for in his kind of list of expenses and functions of the sovereign to provide for the public defense. And so from that standpoint, you can read him as being entirely consistent, where he views there being free trade or the importance of free trade. But given the sovereign's role in providing defense, this is one of those, there's a series of kind of edge cases where trade's going to have to be restricted. So you can certainly see the argument for that. It doesn't strike me as crazy.

The issue, of course, becomes whether you expect that to happen in practice. And so the tension I see in Smith is something like this. You pointed out that he's highlighted trade throughout the book and then pointed out these exceptions. Here's something else he's pointed out: collusion between merchants and government and how they manipulate government policy and foreign policy in the role of colonies for the of the merchants who feather their own nests at the expense of the general populace. Yet when it comes to national security exceptions for trade, that kind of discussion perhaps is lacking. But that doesn't mean we can't have it.

And so one response would be to use Smith on his discussion of colonies, on his discussion of the manipulation of markets by merchants and the interplay between politicians and merchants, and then to apply it to that exception. So then you can still have the exception on paper, but the number of cases which meet the bar of overcoming the Smithian cronyism might be so small that it's non-existent. And I think there's many good empirical cases of that throughout history. When I say empirical cases of that, I mean of people who utilizing the rhetoric of national security to restrict trade, which actually has nothing at all to do with national security once you actually analyze it. And meaning that it doesn't make people safer and it's just special interest groups manipulating things to line their own pockets.

贸易能否避免战争？

I guess I'm curious. Let me push back on this a little bit. Because one of the things that always struck me as peculiar about this is if you build my ships and I need them, then and I... I buy your ships and you need to sell them. I think the likelihood of us going to war with each other is pretty low. And so it seems like the, if we move to a world where I build all the ships because I think I need them for defense, I actually make it more likely that I'm going to have people who are enemies. And so like, how, like, is he missing something critical there? Am I like imagining something?

No, no. I think you raise a good point. I, you know, I, the way I understand your point is trade makes it less likely we go to war. So that's one take. That's the kind of capitalist or market peace hypothesis. Of course, there's various forms to that argument. There's stronger forms that war will never take place. I do think we see cases where trade happens and countries still go to war. So it's not an absolute type argument.

But I still think another response might be something like this. If that's your concern, that's more of an argument even to diversify your trading partners even more. It's like diversifying your investment portfolio. It hedges against any one point of failure. And so your point still holds that isolating and self-sufficiency, which is usually the response to that, we need to produce it here, in some sense makes you even more vulnerable, certainly on many margins, if not all of them.

总结：每个人希望读者带走的教训

So I want to thank you both for what I thought was a really great conversation about Smith. I have one more question for each of you. It's more reflective in some sense, but if, and it's, what's one lesson or insight that you hope that readers will take away from Smith, The Wealth of Nations?

Pete：智慧、洞察与谦逊

You know, it's kind of an interesting time because just a few years ago, we celebrated the 300th birthday of Adam Smith. And so there was a lot of celebrations of Adam Smith at that time, as well as now the 250th anniversary of The Wealth of Nations are ironically very close to one another in terms of the celebrations that have been going around and the discussions.

And when it was his 300th birthday, I was asked to give a talk at Moscow State University and I wrote a paper. I was asked to address this question, why read Adam Smith today? And I argued, or in the end, I argued that it was because of his wisdom, his insight and his guidance. And I can't go through all of those here, but I wanted to, what I think about all of those, what's the wisdom that he has, what's the insight, what's the guidance.

I'm going to sort of suggest it here for our conversation that what I think is the most important thing is humility. I think Adam Smith wrote with humility and a graciousness that is not always common, especially in older writers who wrote a little bit more bombastically. Think about Marx or think about Böhm-Bawerk. You're right. They wrote bombastically. They didn't write with grace and humility. And I think Smith had that.

And I think that one of the ways to think about what Smith is getting us to do is that we practice political economy from the inside out, not from the outside in. So the priority is on the creativity, cleverness, and resourcefulness of the human actors that we are studying who go about solving very complex and difficult social dilemmas and not the theorist who envisions how they might solve it and therefore then provides advice to the all-powerful state to do it.

Political economy in this sense is a tool for the curious. And it provides social understanding, but not effective tools for social control. So I choose to read in Smith his emphasis on agency, dignity, respect for ordinary people, who he argues, if they are free, will achieve extraordinary things. For example, modern economic growth. We do not need extraordinary people, experts, with great power entrusted in their hands to achieve the extraordinary. In fact, more often than not, the granting of tremendous powers to a select few produces deprivation and tyranny. So instead, what is required is Smith's liberal plan for liberty, equality, and justice.

And I think that argument is true in 1776, and it's true in 2026. And I think it's a pretty phenomenal achievement by Adam Smith to have established that argument. And it's one that has left us continually trying to tease it out and wrestle with it in the 250 years since he fully articulated it. And I think that is why the book is considered one of the great achievements of the human mind. And I think that, yeah, we should learn from that. But it's the graciousness and humility, I think, that he writes with. That's such a major issue.

Chris：清晰写作与跨学科

Yeah. Well, you know, Pete's answer was — I have to follow up on a great answer, which is always hard. And you could point to many of his individual topics or concepts. But I'll highlight just something we already touched upon in our earlier conversation, which is, you know, one thing to take away is the importance of clarity in writing. And as we talked about, this is part of the reason that it had the readership it did. And so that seems more important than ever in terms of being able to engage in a conversation that can be understood and reach people.

But then the other part is just what it means to study society in terms of what disciplines are siloed oftentimes. And what Smith, I think, reminds us is the importance not just of economics in a narrow sense or political science in a narrow sense, but social philosophy, moral philosophy, insights from economics, insights from these other disciplines and how they all fit together. And to my way of thinking, that's quite a valuable kind of framing for thinking about things and to keep in mind as we read the book, but also think about doing our own work.

Virgil：人们能通过市场改善生活

For me, I guess it gets to what I take to be maybe certainly our core message, maybe the core message of The Wealth of Nations. My takeaway is that people can improve their lives in the markets. Why is it that the average worker today is better off than the richest person 100 years ago? And it's that we've expanded the scope of markets. And it's that expansion of the scope of markets that is what leads to the continued improvement of the human condition and the continued ability of we humans to improve our condition. That was true 250 years ago when Smith made us aware of it. And I take that to still be true today. And so that to me is the one lesson I hope people take away from the book.

Thank you both for what is, I think, a great, was a great conversation. Hopefully the first of many that we do in this format. And I hope folks enjoy it.

结束语

Thank you for listening to the Hayek Program podcast. To learn more about the research, scholars, and work of the Hayek program, visit hayek.mercatus.org. For more information about graduate student fellowship opportunities for students at Mason as well as at universities across the globe, please visit students.mercatus.org. We hope you recommend students to our programs or consider applying yourself.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一个被忽视的悖论：Smith被"经典化"的代价
这场对话中藏着一个令人不安的递归：三位经济学家用了近两个小时讨论"为什么还要读Smith"，而他们给出的最有力的辩护——Boulding那句"After Samuelson, Who Needs Smith?"——本身就是一个失败的预言。Samuelson式的现代经济学之所以"丢失"了Smith，恰恰是因为它把Smith"封圣"成了教科书第一页的引用对象。**被神化的思想家，往往是被埋葬最深的思想家**。Peter Boettke说Mises写过一篇"糟糕的"Wealth of Nations导读，本质上是在说：连最虔诚的追随者都不得不通过贬低Smith来保护他。这是思想史上一个普遍现象——救Smith的方式，就是先杀死那个被供奉的Smith。
## 跨领域的意外连接：Smith、爵士乐与"开源代码"
Boettke提到"Smith不是权威，而是evolutionary adjacent input"——这其实是软件工程里**fork和分支**的逻辑，也是爵士乐里**standards**（标准曲目）的逻辑。爵士乐手不"演奏C

item.title: [投资TALK君] ✨【投资TALK君1435期】MU涨疯了！会突破周期魔咒吗？SpaceX的估值✨20260527#CPI #nvda #美股 #投资 #英伟达 #ai #特斯拉
item.dc:creator: 投资TALK君
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一个让人意外的镜像：UBS的看涨报告，恰恰证明了空头是对的

最反直觉的发现藏在UBS这份"翻倍目标价"的研报里——它给出的EPS预测路径（27年155 → 28年167 → 29年117）本身就是周期股的标准心电图。一份要论证"美光应该脱离周期魔咒、估值正常化"的报告，却在自己的数字里画出了一个完美的周期波峰。这就像一个证人在法庭上反复说"我没杀人"，但每说一句话都在描述案发现场的细节。市场之所以给美光只有8-9倍PE，不是市场没看到UBS看到的东西，而是市场比UBS更诚实地解读了UBS自己的数字。

跨领域连接：Anthropic给你限流，和美光涨价是同一件事

视频里有个细节值得放大——主播说"今天我都没和Anthropic交流，它直接就给我限流了"。这看似闲笔，实则是整条AI硬件叙事的应力测试现场。它揭示了一条隐藏的传导链：

矿工抛比特币 → 电力转去算力 → Hyperscale花钱 → 美光涨价 → 大模型成本上升 → Anthropic限流你

这条链每一节都在涨价、每一节都在转嫁，唯独最末端的"你"承担了所有溢价却没有议价权——你只能选择付费或退出。这是一个典型的"管道经济学"现象：在管道中，压力总是在最窄的那一节释放。当用户开始大规模流失或拒付，这条链就会从末端反向坍缩。所以判断这轮AI硬件周期见顶的最早信号，可能不在SemiAnalysis的报告里，而在C端用户的退订率和Token价格的弹性曲线里。这是个被严重低估的领先指标。

隐藏的系统性真相：这一轮通胀不是政府撒的，是英伟达撒的

主播提到"这轮和2021年类似，但撒钱主体从政府变成了大科技"——这个观察可以再往前推一步：我们正在见证一种新型的"准货币政策"。当七家公司一年合计资本开支超过万亿美元，且这笔钱直接进入硬件供应链并产生涨价传导时，它们事实上扮演了影子央行的角色——只不过传导路径不是利率，而是GPU、内存、电力。

更隐秘的二阶效应是主播点到但没展开的：财富效应的"自我引用循环"。大科技花钱 → 推高硬件公司股价 → 推高指数 → 用户账户变富 → 服务消费上涨 → PCE抬升 → 美联储无法降息 → 但企业资本开支不依赖利率（用现金流）→ 大科技继续花钱。这是一个绕开了利率传导机制的通胀引擎，传统货币政策工具对它几乎失效。这也解释了为什么主播判断"明年可能重新打开加息可能性"——这不是经济过热，这是货币政策对一个新形态资本循环的失灵。

如果这个判断成立，那么真正值得担心的不是美光估值能不能给15倍，而是当美联储意识到自己手里的工具拧不动这台机器时，会做出什么过激反应——这才是2026年最大的尾部风险。

美光MU深度分析：UBS研报背后的逻辑

然后我们就来聊一下美光，主要是从UBS这家机构的一个视角去触发。

UBS上调了美光的这个目标价格，从500多块钱上升到了1625，翻倍的这个上涨。你可以理解为机构也在追涨嘛。

机构追涨杀跌的行业默认

因为机构他们都是这样，无论是对于个股标的的一些追踪，还是对于指数的一些追踪，基本上机构这些他们也是追涨杀跌的。就是你目标价格包括标普500是一个非常好的例子。

当标普500在涨的时候，你会看到所有机构都出来上调标普500的这个目标价。因为你如果不上调的话，会让人看你的目标价，会让你的客户看你的目标价觉得你非常的蠢，对吧。明明标普500已经到7500了，然后你还把标普500的目标价设在7200、7000甚至于6500，你会觉得非常的蠢。所以你一定会跟着同行之间一起把目标价往上提，越涨越提，越跌越往下调，就是这样。

3月份的时候也是这样，越跌的话，它越往下调。很少有机构会出现一个"哎，你越跌，然后我越往上调"，不大会，因为这背后有一个什么叫做career risk。你没有必要做出特立独群的那个人，大家一起错没有关系，但是大家都对了，你错了，你就有问题了，对吧。所以这背后其实有很多的一些哲学在背后，或者有很多行业里面的一些默认在背后。

长期订单锁定与EPS确定性

但是我主要是看它背后上调它目标价格的一个理由，这个理由我认为是成立的。

因为它主要是聊到，其实现在整个芯片行业都是这样，特别是硬件这一块，很多家公司硬件也好、芯片也好，然后你去看一些电力公司也好，基本上产能是全部被定满。26年根本不用想，26年、27年基本上全部是被订满，然后28年已经开始在预定了。

那所以这就是为什么2026年，包括2027年的上半年这些公司这些标的，他们的EPS的一个体量都非常的确定，因为这些订单都已经确定了。所以这篇研报的一个核心思路是：美光他们下面接下去的这个订单，现在长期的这个订单已经占到了30%以上，而这个长期的一个订单就能够保证他们从27财年至29财年能够确定，或者能够使得他们EPS这个体量本身得到非常大的这个确定性。

所以从这边我们可以看到30%的1个DDR的1个volume现在已经完全被locking。所以30%被locking，所以他们认为26、27年到28年EPS这个体量完全可以非常舒适的在每股100美金以上。所以他们认为市场会开始对于美光本身开始对于它进行一个re-rate。

周期股估值之争

因为美光现在的一个远期PE就是在8倍左右、9倍左右。为什么会那么的低？是因为市场仍然认为美光、整个存储这一块就是一个周期板块。因为以前历史告诉我们，它是一个周期板块。那么市场对于它的定价，其实现在已经有一点点远离周期板块了，那市场对于远期PE的一个定价，就是认为它是一个周期板块，所以不会给出一个什么22倍25倍的这种PE，不会。那所以远期的PE现在只是在8倍、在9倍左右。

那UBS他们这边的一个观点是：因为那么未来3年EPS都可以保持在100美金以上，所以市场将会对于美国未来的这个估值进行normal multiple normalize，就是会给他一个正常的估值。那给多少呢？他们其实也并没有非常的激进啊，他们给了15倍的这个PE。

他们认为未来EPS：27、28、29 三年的话是155、167和117。那根据29年的这一个每股收益，然后给他15倍的这个multiple，给他15倍的这个PE，得出了他们1625的这个目标价格。

彭博的滞后预期

然后大家看一下彭博对于美国的这个预期。彭博对于美国的预期其实比较落后的。你看一下他们对于二期财年的预期是104美金EPS体量，104，28财年的话是98，然后29的话是105。那明显是低估了。

他们彭博现在平均的这个预期——彭博上面也是所有的分析员，然后拿了出来取了一个中位数嘛。在彭博上面所有的分析员取中位数的话，基本上认为美光27、28、29 3个年EPS是不动的，基本上就是保持在100至105之间。

但是UBS现在是作为一个所有分析员里面的一个领军者嘛，他们上调了27年和28年：27年来到了155，28年来到了167，29年来到了117。

致命的逻辑漏洞

但是呢，他们这边我认为他们的预测是OK的。非常非常有可能27年他们预测EPS体量在155是非常有可能会达到的。但是在他们的这个预测里边、预期里边嘛，我认为有一个非常大的一个问题。

这个问题在哪？这个问题就是在——首先如果他们的预期是正确的话，从27年到28年155到167，他这一年的EPS增速只有10%都不到，对吧。你155 10%的话要170了。所以如果从27年到28年，市场意识到27年是没有问题嘛，因为27年155比上26年的85的话，你要将近要翻倍不到一些，100%不到，也是90、95%左右。

问题是：如果28年只是167的话，那27到28你EPS的增速只有10%不到的话，市场绝对不会给出现在的这个multiple，能够理解吗？

那最大的一个问题是在哪？最大的问题是在：如果从28年到29年的话，你的EPS从167跌到117的话，你等于要削减30%的话，这完全是恰恰证明了市场现在交易的一个逻辑线——存储板块它是一个周期板块。

所谓的非周期板块一定是你每一年的EPS增速起码都保持着15%的这个增速，就每一年你都要比上一年要高，而并不会出现这种什么29年会突然之间比28年就低了30%，不可能出现这种情况，对吧。

软件板块的对比

我们去想一下，现在市场去杀跌软件板块的这背后的一个逻辑：首先很多软件板块的公司，你根本就没有看到——软件板块已经杀了一年多了，很多软件板块跌了两年多了。在过去两年里面，很多的软件板块你是完全没有看到他们EPS增速放缓，你看到他们营收的增速、EPS增速完全没有任何的放缓，相反，有些公司的EPS增速和营收增速比起上两年的话还在加速。

但是市场在却对于他们在re-rate，市场在却对于他们杀他们远期的这个估值，杀他们远期的这个PE——原本是30倍的PE，现在可能只有20倍；原本是30倍40倍的PE现在只有20倍；原本是30多倍的PE，现在只有十几倍。就基本上价格的这个腰斩全部是来自于对于PE上的一个杀跌。杀什么？杀的是他们最终的这个估值，杀的是他们4年和5年之后他们EPS这个增速，市场再杀他们4年5年之后EPS有可能会出现一个负增长，或者说起码出现一个增速放缓。

那么所以市场在杀跌这些软件板块的时候，都没有看到他们EPS增速出现放缓，也没有对他们未来的EPS增速的这个预期出现这个放缓的这个预期，什么东西都没有看到，直接先从估值先开始杀起。

而相反，如果UBS这个预测是正确的话，其实恰恰证明了市场认为它是一个周期板块的观点是正确的。

27年的确定性 vs 28、29年的不确定性

所以我相信他们对于27年EPS体量155这个体量的一个预测，我认为这个确定性非常的高。因为现在长期订单的这个比例占比越来越高之后，就会使得这些硬件、芯片、硬件，包括很多存储板块、包括很多电力板块，他们未来26年、27年的EPS的增速非常确定的。

所以市场其实对于这些板块都已经在交易28年了。不确定性就是28年。当然29年也说三年半之后、三年之后吧，谁知道三年之后会发生什么事情。

那所以准确的说，你只能认为OK对于27年的预估有一个把握的话，那实际上是走一步看一步。

关键的领先指标：SemiAnalysis 与 Fab On AI

市场里边现在做调研这一块，两家领军的公司：Fab On AI，然后还有一个SemiAnalysis。但凡他们有一天释放出任何一些前瞻性的一些信号，证明存储板块或者证明AI的这个需求有任何的放缓，那整个行业，我认为所有和硬件、所有受到hyperscale花钱获益的这些硬件板块都会受到影响。

涨价链的传导逻辑

这背后的一个逻辑线是：那之所以现在那么无论是存储板块也好，包括硬件也好，很多现在开始在出现涨价。因为从供给商这个角度，他们有能力出现涨价的话，其实很简单——首先英伟达能够拿的价格非常的低，英伟达能够把这个钱递交到谁身上？能够把这个钱转移到NeoCloud身上、转移到Oracle身上、转移到所有的hyperscale，那么这些大科技身上。

那大科技他们为什么能够花这个溢价去购买那么贵的这个存储芯片？他们为什么愿意去买？是因为他们能够把这个溢价传递到他们的消费、他们的客户身上。他们的客户是谁？那么除了下面的一些中小企业以外，他们现在比较大的一些客户仍然是这些大模型。

所以我们就会看到了这些大模型中其实是一步一步的，现在token的价格上涨。大模型我们也看到了Anthropic它现在限流。那么我今天都没有和Anthropic交流成本，他直接就给我限流了，他说你几点之后5小时的练麦已经到了。

所以现在就是问题是，现在的token越来越贵，那大模型的需求也非常高，就大模型有落地，他们的营收在增加。那高的一个价格、高的AI的这个价格，其实对于AI的这个落地并不是一件好事。

暂时来说，这一条逻辑线就是整个的这条逻辑线还是可以走通，是因为大模型这一块他们是有落地的，就他们即使现在那么高的这个token的价格，即使他对我们限流，我还是用它，我还是给他付钱，还是有更多的人愿意给他付钱。这才是最终的根本逻辑。如果大模型这一块说，我需要这个价格下来，那我需要这个价格下来，这个需求就会传递到大科技这边，那大科技自然就会把这个信号传递到他们的上游，那么所谓的硬件啊等等。

何时见顶？

所以如果你要问我，你说哎，在存储这一块，它的一个需求到底什么时候鉴定？说实话我也不知道。

我只是知道，如果两个月之前去看，我记得三秒的那个CEO在接受采访的时候，他说市场可能低估了，那么26年存储这一块价格的一个上涨和上年同期对比的话，还是会出现非常大幅度的这个涨价或者非常大幅度的这个上涨。

那如果有一天，那么SemiAnalysis的Dylan Patel他说，我看到存储这个价格可能没有上涨这个空间了，那我们可能就需要小心了。我前面说就这两家机构Fab On AI、SemiAnalysis，他们对于这个行业领先的这些指标观察的非常的仔细，所以我建议大家可以去关注他们的一些文章。如在他们文章里面看到有这些类似一些信号，那代表接下去整个行业的一个涨价周期可能会见顶。但是他们如果没有释放这些信号之前的话，整个行业可能还会持续涨价。但是这个持续涨价最终一定是有终点的，好么。

美光估值能给到多少倍？

所以回到美光这边，如果你问我，你说美光的这个估值能不能回到forward PE，就比如说现在27年的一个forward PE是在8.6倍，然后28年是在9.1倍，29年是在8.5倍。

你问我29年的1个PE能不能——首先UBS是15倍嘛，你说29年的这个PE能不能给到20倍？我不知道，我完全不知道。我不认为市场现在如果情绪面非常的高，也许能够交易到20倍，但是我并不是认为这种情况会发生。为什么？是因为这个不确定性太大了，市场不可能把三年之后他的一个PE都买到20倍。

我认为如果出现这种情况的话，那我认为市场可能是非常的乐观。那么就像英伟达，你去看一下英伟达现在的forward PE在3年之后，也就是在17倍18倍左右吧，给英伟达的这个PE也其实也非常的低，说明什么？说明三年之后，市场其实没有非常大的这个把握。

那么存储这一块也是一样的。如果什么美光都能够买到20倍，或者像UBS他们的一个预期说给15倍的话，首先我认为他们15倍的估值，它背后的逻辑是不支持的。就是你不可能存在前三年是155、167，然后第三年是117，然后你直给117是15倍——不可能。如果是117的话，你最多只能给到10倍，甚至于10倍以下。因为你从167跌到117，恰恰证明了它是一个周期板块，它并不是一个secular trend，它并不是一个长期的一个、就打破了原来这个周期板块的一个走势。你这个预期完全是没有办法证实你这个结果的。

所以从这个角度去翻，你看市场对于英伟达也是一样的，就是三年之后是7倍是8倍，就是这个不确定性在那边的。市场最多只能看到一年一年半之后啊。

所以你问我，我没有这个答案。所以我还是建议大家，而且我也没有行业里面的一些领先指标，我自己更多的也是依赖于行业里面的一些领先的一些做research做调研的这些机构，依赖于他们所给出的一些信号。所以总体来说，只能是走一步看一步。那么如果他们告诉我整个美光的整个存储这一块还能够继续涨价的话，那整个这个舞蹈可能还能够继续跳下去。

SpaceX估值与解禁分析

今天我上传的最新的一期油管视频，主要是对SpaceX进行了一个估值。

现在市场给它的一个估值是在1.5万亿至2万亿左右，那我给了我自己的一个区间，我认为是在1.6万亿左右。具体为什么会给出1.6万亿，主要是把他们的业务拆为3块，然后每一块去给他一个估值，而且给的都是比较乐观的。大家感兴趣的话可以考虑去看一下油管里边最新的一期会员视频。

阶梯式解禁

那其中里面也有聊到，因为他们这一次SpaceX的一个解禁，一般来说上市的公司解禁是在6个月之后就出现解禁，但是这一次SpaceX的一个解禁，它是阶梯性的，而且最早的这个解禁的时间点并不是在6个月之后，是在第二季度财报之前——也就是在今年的6月12日上市，在7月份、8月份之前，就有可能提前出现一部分的一个解禁的这个体量了。

为何提前解禁？指数纳入逻辑

那为什么会提前出现这个解禁？其实某种程度也是需要满足，我说也是需要让SpaceX进入一些指数，比如说进入QQ，那进入纳斯达克100，或者说今天我看到这条新闻，他说罗素这边富时罗素（FTSE Russell）罗素指数里面他们也更改了自己的这个规则，然后使得像SpaceX这一类一级市场非常大的一个标的非常快速的就进入这个指数。所以他们更改了自己的这个标准，然后可以让SpaceX进入这个指数。

那对于SpaceX来说是一件好事。其实他们本身为什么要把SpaceX加进来？是因为他是想要散户去买啊，想让大家去买。那么今天我有这个指数，我这个指数里面有SpaceX，然后你买我这个指数就相当于买了SpaceX，其实是一样的一个道理。所以他们更改了自己的这一个标准，然后让SpaceX更加早的进入。

那SpaceX更加早的进入之后，其实对于SpaceX的抛压来说是一件好事。因为有这些指入进来之后，它会有一个被动的一个买盘在那边。那虽然解禁完之后，很多一级市场，我相信一级市场里边有很多希望套现的。

因为我认为SpaceX现在是非常贵的，就起码现在市场给它的这个估值是非常贵的。如果你去看面上这些数据的话，现在在2.4，我最新得到2.4万亿、2.5万亿，这是相当我认为是相当贵的一个市值。就按SpaceX现在的一个三战业务来看的话，相当贵的一个市值。

那如果是2.4、2.5的话，对于一级市场的投资者来说，我认为他们是有非常大的这个意愿去套现的。所以有非常大的一个抛压在背后。但是如果这些指数更改自己的这个规则，然后去加入SpaceX的话，那对于抛压来说，一定程度是有一个支撑的一个作用在那边了。

宏观分析：大科技撒钱与通胀

好吧，那最后聊一下宏观。

我其实我特地去看了一下，在6月份的第一周里面，其实有一个宏观数据是比较重要的——是第一周里边周五那一天会有大非农数据。那那一天我还在vacation嘛，所以没有办法和大家分析大非农。但如果我看到数据的话，我可以稍微在X上做一些点评。

个人电脑通胀：被忽视的传导

那主要最近这段时间，其实我在思考的是所有硬件这一块的一些涨价，然后包括苹果产品的一些涨价，包括现在memory存储这一块的一些涨价。其实你可以看到，对于personal electronics、个人的电脑、笔电，其实都出现了价格的一个上涨。

彭博他们追踪的这个数据，你可以看到其实对于笔电、对于personal computer、CPI里面的personal computer这一块的一个增速，其实已经造成了同比15%以上的这个增长。然后主要就是来自于台电和笔电，那就是台式机和笔电啊，分别是里边紫色和蓝色这一块。

所以接下去我认为这个趋势应该还会出现上涨，可能20甚至25%我们都可以看到。所以大家如果要去买电脑的话可能还是趁早，对吧。你如果今年买的越晚的话，你这个电脑那个涨价幅度同样的一个性能，涨价的幅度可能就会非常的高，或者就会更高。

对PCE的影响测算

那对于PCE的这个影响：整个个人的电脑这一块，PC的一个占比是在1%左右。所以如果上涨20%的话，那对于PCE的这个影响就是在0.2%。你说它完全没有影响嘛，其实这个影响还是挺大的，因为你想，整个的PCE你就是百分、你整个PCE就是在3.4%至4%之间。然后如果因为personal computer的这个上涨再加上0.2的话，你原本是3.5，现在就变成3.7了。所以有没有影响？其实还是有一定的一个影响。但是这个影响呢又没有到非常的这个夸张。

2021年的镜像：政府撒钱 vs 大科技撒钱

那我说我最近这段时间在思考的是：其实我在思考就是我们现在是不是和2021年非常的类似。

2021年（2020年）是政府这一块的大撒钱，那美联储它只是进行1个QE，但是政府这一块的大撒钱是政府财政斥资，然后把钱发到企业、发到每一个人的手里，然后大家去开始进行投资。

但这一轮的这个周期其实和21年有一点类似——他不是政府撒钱。当然川普这边也是通过这个退税的政策在撒钱。它更多的是大科技的撒钱。大科技的撒钱一年要撒a hundred billion，一年要撒8000亿、1万亿。那这一个撒钱去到了哪里呢？全部去到了这些硬件公司。

那这些硬件公司它通过涨价是去——确实没有对于通胀造成非常直接的影响，但是间接的你可以看到，通过personal computer这一块也造成了一部分的一些影响。对于PCE的这个影响可能在0.2%，那么现在只是personal computer。如果对于其他有些影响的话，可能是0.2至0.4的这个影响，还没有算上服务。

服务通胀与财富效应

那服务通胀这一块，你想现在这段时间股市走的非常的好，那PCE里边有一个财富管理的一个通胀在里边，那这个财富管理的通胀也会非常的高。

所以其实这一波的一个上涨，对于PCE来说——我只是看PCE，因为美联储衡量通胀的指标就是PCE——所以这一波大科技的这个撒钱、买大科技资本开支这个砸钱，对于PCE有没有影响？其实是有影响的。

那服务这一块，它直接最直接的这个影响就是比值的这个影响，就是通过股市这边的上涨，然后财富管理的这个体量上涨之后，服务通胀这一块造成上涨。

另外一块是财富效应。财富效应是间接的，不能够直接看到的。就比如说股票账户里边，你账户上涨了，那会导致我在个别服务这一块，我可以去承担更高的这个涨价，那么我可以会花更多，我花更多的话就造成服务通胀又会起来。

所以有一些直接的影响，有些间接的影响。我认为直接的影响可能在0.3%至0.5%左右，同比增速0.3至0.5左右。间接的这个影响现在暂时还没有办法去衡量。

下半年通胀展望

所以其实这一波虽然和21年的政府大砸钱是不一样的，但是大科技这一块的撒钱对于通胀的影响，我认为是有的。

所以今年的下半年，可能美联储不会看到他们所预期之内的这个通胀的一个增速放缓。因为上一年因为关税政策所导致这个通胀，今年是有一个基数效应在那边——基数效应嘛，有个基数效应在那边，理论上来说应该增速放缓。但是因为这一次大科技的这个砸钱，对吧，personal computer这一块的一个上涨、股市这一块的上涨，可能今年下半年的通胀还是持续保持在这个高位，那有可能会为明年打开这个加息的可能性。

Seeking Alpha 推广

最后是今天Seeking Alpha他们的这个活动。

那Seeking Alpha这个平台大家应该不陌生了，那么起码在老外的这一个社区里面的话是非常非常活跃的一个平台。那现在他有一个他们的一个量化选股策略，标普500上涨了8%，他们的这个策略上涨了32%。里面调了一些标的，他们基本上每两周会推一个标的，会对为大家做更新的减仓或者加仓或者建仓，他都会有。

那基本上在过去几个月里边所推的一些标的吧，还是走的不错的，基本上都跑赢了标普500。所以大家如果对于Seeking Alpha感兴趣的话，现在这个量化的一个选股策略，原本是499美金一年，现在是10%的这个折扣是449美金一年。

那包括他们还有另外一个订阅，叫做Seeking Alpha的一个premium内档订阅，大家如果感兴趣的话，现在也是10%的这个折扣，加上7天的一个免费使用，不喜欢的话随时可以取消，7天之内是免费的。或者说对于这两个服务都感兴趣的话，有一个combo的一个价格折扣会更高。然我把这三个的这个订阅链接都会放在置顶的留言里面。

结语

好，那今天这期视频就到这边了。下一次和大家见面，可能要10天之后了。那今天这期内容就到这边，我们下期见。

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一个让人意外的镜像：UBS的看涨报告，恰恰证明了空头是对的
最反直觉的发现藏在UBS这份"翻倍目标价"的研报里——它给出的EPS预测路径（27年155 → 28年167 → 29年117）**本身就是周期股的标准心电图**。一份要论证"美光应该脱离周期魔咒、估值正常化"的报告，却在自己的数字里画出了一个完美的周期波峰。这就像一个证人在法庭上反复说"我没杀人"，但每说一句话都在描述案发现场的细节。市场之所以给美光只有8-9倍PE，不是市场没看到UBS看到的东西，而是市场**比UBS更诚实地解读了UBS自己的数字**。
## 跨领域连接：Anthropic给你限流，和美光涨价是同一件事
视频里有个细节值得放大——主播说"今天我都没和Anthropic交流，它直接就给我限流了"。这看似闲笔，实则是整条AI硬件叙事的**应力测试现场**。它揭示了一条隐藏的传导链：
**矿工抛比特币 → 电力转去算力 → Hyperscale花钱 → 美光涨价 → 大模型成本上升 → Anthropic限流你**
这条链每一节都在涨价、每一节都在转嫁，**唯独最末端的"你"承担了所有溢价却没有议

item.title: [Zhang Xiaojun Podcast] 142. 雨森的创投观察第2集：Harness、下一个字节、2026大机会和Stanley Druckenmiller
item.dc:creator: Zhang Xiaojun Podcast
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、雨森自己就是他正在分析的那个"Harness"

最让我意外的观察是：雨森本人正在以"Harness"的方式重构他作为投资人的认知系统——而他自己并没有完全意识到这一点。

注意这个隐藏对称： - 他描述 Cloud Code 的核心价值在于"data flywheel"——通过用户使用产生高质量反馈信号，回流到模型训练。 - 他描述自己录播客的价值在于"市场不会对你说好话，如果市场说你错了就是错了"——通过公开表达产生高质量负面反馈信号，回流到自己的判断系统。

他在节目里说自己"被博客绑架"、不敢加仓——这恰恰证明了他正在经历一个未经设计的强化学习循环：公开表达 → 仓位约束 → 真实金钱损益 → 反馈修正。这比任何一个 AI agent 的 RLHF 都更残酷、更真实。换句话说，他这档节目不是内容产品，是他自己的 RLHF 训练管道。而他选择继续录制，就是选择继续把自己暴露在 OOD（out-of-distribution）的市场反馈里。

二、"返"的真正陷阱：把"输入→产出→结果"误读成"输入→产出"

雨森拆出来的三段式（投入 → 产出 → 结果）看起来朴素，但它其实是这一轮 AI 周期里最被忽略的会计学错误。

跨领域连接一下：这与 19 世纪末电气革命中"电气化生产率悖论"（Productivity Paradox）几乎一模一样——工厂换了电机，但生产率十年没动，因为工厂建筑、流水线、管理制度还停留在蒸汽机时代的"主轴-皮带"思维里。雨森自己在节目尾声讲了这个例子，但他没把它跟自己前面"返"的分析完全连起来：

Anthropic 的收入 ≠ 投资的回报，而是更深一层的投入。 真正决定 capex 能不能闭环的，不是 Anthropic 卖了多少 token，而是买 token 的人有没有学会用 token 设计新组织。这就意味着 AI 周期的破灭点不会出现在模型公司，而会出现在那批最早大规模 burn token 但组织形态没变的中型企业——他们的 ROI 报表会在 2026 下半年到 2027 年集中暴雷。这是一个比"模型估值过高"更隐蔽、更系统性的风险点。

三、Agent 网络效应的真正涌现点：不是技术，是"责任的不可外包性"

雨森提出的"我的 agent 雇佣张小珺的 agent"是个有趣构想，但他自己埋了一个更深的洞察没有展开——"组织能背锅的能力是有限的"。

这句话表面上是在讲为什么 AI 不能直接取代员工，但它其实揭示了一个反直觉的真相：AI 越强，"背锅权"越稀缺，越值钱。

跨领域连接：这与法律史上"法人格"（legal personhood）的演变惊人相似。中世纪后期欧洲商业之所以能爆炸，不是因为生产力突变，而是因为"有限责任公司"这一发明把"承担风险的主体"从个人剥离出来，让资本可以流动。而今天 AI 带来的不是劳动力扩张，而是"无主体行为"的大规模产生——AI 生成的报告、代码、决策没有责任主体。

所以未来真正稀缺、真正有定价权的角色，不是会用 AI 的人，而是愿意为 AI 输出签字背书的人。投资人、医生、律师、工程师中能"签字"的那一部分人，他们的工资不会被 AI 压低，反而会因为 AI 产能膨胀而上涨——因为每个 AI 产出都需要一个人类的"责任签名"才能进入经济循环。

这就解释了一个反常识的现象：为什么 AI 时代最先涨薪的会是律师和合规岗，而不是程序员。雨森其实在节目里碰到了这个边缘，但没能把它推到底——他作为投资人，他自己就是那个"签字的人"，他的工作不可被 agent 取代，恰恰因为他承担了 LP 的信托责任。

四、一个彩蛋式预测

如果按雨森自己的三步推演继续往下推：当 Harness 变成操作系统、模型变成处理器，那么下一个被遗忘但即将重现的角色是——驱动程序开发者。也就是说，2026 下半年到 2027 年，会出现一批专门"翻译"特定模型怪癖、特定行业 context、特定组织结构到通用 Harness 的中间层公司。它们既不是模型公司，也不是应用公司，而是"AI 时代的 Realtek"。这批公司在 VC 雷达上现在几乎不可见，因为他们看起来太"工程"、太"无聊"——这恰恰是雨森自己讲的"too low"陷阱的标准特征。

二十四、移动互联网的老经验为何失效

What are some of the very correct practices in the past or mobile Internet era, but not working today?

I think there are many. Overall, mobile Internet has a lot of things that were in the past. I think it will change now. This is actually the formula of the mobile Internet era. But there is a bug in this formula. There are only so many people in the world. Everyone has 24 hours. You will find that their transmission has reached its limit. So only the depth of transmission is increased. So there are high-transmission products such as reward live broadcasts.

But in the age of agents, the market of users looking at the screen is no longer the most critical indicator. Maybe the more critical indicator is the METR Institute. He said that an agent can continue to complete the key indicators of valuable tasks. How long can you run this task? So how to do a harness at this time to complete the model for a long time This becomes more important. How to release the attention of users and make this AH work very important.

So this is a complete change of index. From attention is all you need to attention is not all you need.

From the perspective of the core, there have been many changes. For example, when we look at mobile Internet, many of these super applications actually put users in their own apps. They often don't let users go out, and let users form a wall in it. But I think there is a big problem in this. If the user's agent becomes very powerful, but the user's agent cannot access the information in your closed app.

For example, before, the user's agent was difficult to access the information in WeChat because WeChat was not open. The user may go to access the information in the flyer, because the flyer is open. 二十六、AI Native 产品的三个阶段

Where is the product of AI Native?

I'm thinking that AI is a bit like an alien. It came to the human world. There are several stages in this process.

The first stage is to allow humans to have more and better agents. For example, three years ago, there were no agents in human society. Now there are some agents, but there are not enough agents. So how can we let more people have good agents? I think no matter it's Cloud Code, Codex, Open Cloud, Manage, Hermes, they are all doing this. Installation is getting easier and easier. Then you can manage it more and more easily. I think this is the first step, which is to let humans have more and better agents.

The second wave is to make the agents in the human world better adapted to the human digital world. Because the human digital world is basically designed for humans. For example, GUI, verification code, credit card, etc. AI is usually the target of the host. It's supposed to stop bots. Now we have a bunch of good bots. AI can effectively use human software and technology to make it more important.

For example, we all find payment is a problem. So, like Stripe or Coinbase, they want to send human credit cards to AI. For example, Cloudflare used to block AI. They blocked AI outside. They recently launched a service that allows agents to register and use various services equally with people. Then, for example, various computer use, various sandbox, and sandbox, in fact, AI can use human-made software. It's like a foreigner coming to China. He needs to have a SIM card. You need to get him a WeChat account. You need to get him a Alipay. This is a way for agents to stay and use human-made facilities.

But when the number of agents is increasing, when the number of agents is becoming more important, there will be a special digital world tool and its infrastructure. For example, humans use credit cards, but human payment has some characteristics. Human payment is relatively low-value, it is difficult for you to pay 10,000 times a day. Human payment is usually point-to-point, I pay this merchant, but human payment is often relatively large, I rarely pay 0.0001 yuan. So, human cards are a behavior that meets the characteristics of human payment.

But the agent may be very high-level. For example, if my agent helps me write a report, he may need to check the data in 100 databases. He may have to pay 100 times, maybe in the future. At that time, it will be very high-level. But at the same time, it may be very small. At the same time, it may be a one-to-one payment.

So, sending cards to agents is just to let agents adapt to the system of people. 让agent用人类的GUI也是让AI吸引人。 最后如果我们认为AI非常强大, 我们认为AI无处不在, 那AI应该有完全自己内部的 native [agent-native infrastructure].

The third stage is to build a powerful Agent. It's the most suitable digital world for it. Because each of these is actually a product opportunity.

What kind of opportunities will there be if the coding ability is unlimited? In fact, there are many places where humans are not enough because of the programming. For example, if you have a lot of programmers serving you, what kind of software can you use?

I think the first one is that we can use better software. For example, If you have a smart home, smart curtains, smart hot water pot, you will find that many software is still because the person who wrote it is not the top programmer. The top programmer may write the promotion method in their own way. But if you make a smart hot water pot, a floor machine, your programmer is just an ordinary programmer. So we often feel that many of these apps are quite difficult to use. But in fact, if each software is written by the top programmer, its performance, application, and safety have all become very good. It may be a big improvement to our existing applications.

Secondly, I think it's a personalized software. This is something that can be improved. For example, I am a senior user, so I may need to have a more efficient way of managing contacts, or group communication, or management of voice. But I may not need any functions that allow me to "blah blah blah". But my grandma may have used another thing.

Now, actually everyone, different people, use the same WeChat. Maybe in the future, my wonderful dream is that such an application will provide you with a base. But you can change the many functions on it. You can meet your personal needs. For example, for you, you can do an interview or a blog on WeChat. This is a more personalized software.

So when you have infinite programmers at a very low price, what can you do? I think there are actually a lot of new needs to be born. But this new need to be born is a gradual process. Because it needs someone to imagine. It also needs a good base to carry out this kind of personalization. For example, we just said that we want to reform WeChat. But WeChat has to give me a new interface. So in this, it's not a process that comes and goes.

二十七、AI Native 公司的组织形态

We just talked about AI Native entrepreneurs and the new opportunities. Do you have any observations about the organizational form of AI Native entrepreneurs? Do you have any good examples that you have seen that are different from the previous generation?

I think the first one is that it has indeed become smaller. Indeed, for entrepreneurs, there are often people who are missing. Because if you have a lot of land to push, you have a lot of business negotiations, you may still need people. But if you get PMF on the matter of code writing, the company's number will be smaller.

And in the past, in fact, many times software development is a waterfall-like division. There is this structure, there is this back end, front end, test, UI, operation. But now it may become a full stop more and more. Because I'm a commanding AI agent anyway, I can do all these things. At this time, it may be from the process of pool division to a small independent operating organization. These few people are in charge of a product function module. From its underlying design to its previous online testing to operation, they are all done by the same batch of people.

Now, in fact, many companies are trying to use agents to improve their own efficiency. But for a company that has been operating for 10 years, the biggest problem is that your context, your data is not in AI. It's actually very difficult to get these contacts in.

So we also see some new companies. From the first day, their contacts are all visible to AI. So AI knows very well what they want to do. For example, we invested in a company called Slack. It's actually a platform for people and agents to cooperate. It's a bit like Slack for people and agents to work together. Their entire company's operation, task management, and programming are all on their own platform. So they build the Slack using Slack. In fact, Cloud Code and Codex have shared that Cloud Code is written in Cloud Code. Codex is also written in Codex.

It's actually a new product, a new company. It can start from the first day, and then AI participates in the management and guidance of most of their companies' software development. This is very obvious to improve efficiency.

What about the structure? The structure will change. There will be a structure. I think the result of the organization I just mentioned is that the division of labor will usually become blurred. Because there are many divisions based on the context of people. Why is there a front-end and a back-end UI? It's because one person's skills are limited, one person's context is limited, and one person cannot do front-end and back-end UI at the same time. But now AI is okay. So your division of labor may become more blurred. There will be more full-time people, not more people who are clearly organized and divided into departments.

So this will bring a conclusion. That is, if a company has a very clear department wall, it is actually difficult for AI to adapt. So that's why many big companies push AI. It's not simple to say that everyone just installs a cloud code and that's it. It actually requires organizational change. This is also why I think it's not simple. You have a good model, you will reduce the cost-effectiveness. In fact, many times it has to solve people's problems. This is quite difficult.

It's all about organizing the change of AI.

Yes, I want to say more about organizational change. There was an article that was very good. I forgot who wrote it. It says that during the industrial revolution, there was a process from steam engines to electric engines. The steam engine factory was built around a main shaft. Because steam engines have a shaft that rotates, so it has a very long shaft. Then the shaft is pulled off a lot of belt, and the main shaft's power is transmitted to each machine. So at that time, the factory was a narrow-gauge type. The whole factory was built around this shaft.

But when the electric engine came out, because it was a wire, the wire didn't need that main shaft. So the factory in the era of electric engines could be made bigger and flatter. After this kind of bigger and flatter factory came out, a invention called the flow line was brought. It was done by Ford at that time. The emergence of this flow line can only be done when the company can build a very large and flat factory building.

If all the forces you mentioned come from one axis, then it is actually very difficult to make a large flow line factory building. The birth of the flow line brings a significant increase in the production rate.

So what this story is about is that from steam engines to electric engines, it does not naturally bring about a growth rate improvement. You need to have physical organizational changes in the factory and how to organize production. From doing a lot of things in the original position to doing one thing in the position, the flow line is pushed. This thing will bring such changes.

I think the technology is the result of the end. It is a process of gradual penetration. The change of organization required in this way is actually a difficult place. Because organizational changes are all human changes. Human changes don't change in a minute. It may take ten years to change. Even if the old organization is replaced by the new one, or the new organization is replaced by the old organization, it will only happen.
