item.description:
## 一、最反直觉的发现：**合法性才是真正的"核武器"**
整段对话最颠覆常识的洞见，不是"中国会倒下"或"伊朗会倒下"这种宏大预言，而是嘉宾反复点出的一个底层公式：**独裁同时极强又极弱**——强在控制国家机器，弱在缺乏合法性。
我想推得更远一步：**习近平每天早上醒来都在思考"今天要不要除掉某个人"**——这不是政治新闻，这是一种**热力学现象**。独裁政权内部的"信任"是一种被持续抽空的能量，必须靠监控、清洗、恐惧来不断补充。它本质上是一个**熵增系统**：维持低熵（秩序）的代价是不断输入更多的暴力能量。而美国体系的"低信任成本"，是民主结构自带的负熵机制。
换句话说，习近平的真正对手不是五角大楼，而是**热力学第二定律**。
## 二、被忽视的跨领域连接：**亚马逊的"七年计划"与哈马斯的"二十年计划"是同一个算法**
嘉宾顺口提到贝索斯说"如果你有七年计划，你就赢了，因为大多数人会放弃"，然后立刻接到哈马斯在费城酒店里设计的**20 年大学渗透计划**。
这两件事被并置，但他没说出最锋利的那层意思：**长期主义本身是中性的武器**。硅谷推崇的"耐心资本""愿景型

item.title: [Dwarkesh Clips] How Did Italy Cope With the Fall of Rome? – Ada Palmer
item.dc:creator: Dwarkesh Clips
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、被忽视的物质前提：共和制其实是"农业富余"的副产品

我们习惯把共和制看作思想成就——西塞罗、人文主义、公民德性。但 Palmer 揭示的真相令人不安：共和制首先是一种"吃得饱"的政体。能不能开元老院、能不能让贵族家族围坐议事，取决于城墙外那块地够不够肥。土壤贫瘠的城镇直接跳过了"政治哲学"这一环，被饥饿推回君主制怀抱。

这里有个反直觉的推论：意大利文艺复兴的"共和精神"，其本质是波河流域和托斯卡纳的小麦产量在说话。我们一直把佛罗伦萨的辉煌归功于美第奇的金融创新，但真正的底层逻辑是——那片土地养得起一个 10 万人不必跪下的城市。

二、"村庄"一词的语义考古：一个被遗忘的政治迁徙

最让我意外的，是 Palmer 对 "village" 词源的拆解——village = villa + 周边。这不是词源学的小聪明，而是一段被埋葬的政治史化石：

罗马崩塌后，人不是"住进了村庄"，而是"逃进了贵族的保护圈"。每一个今天田园诗里的"小村庄"，在公元 6 世纪都是一次微型的"政治放弃"——一群人用人身自由换取了不被强盗杀死的概率。

跨领域连接：这与今天硅谷大厂园区惊人地同构。当公共基础设施（政府服务、社区安全感、医疗）退化时，员工迁入 Google/Apple 的"villa"——那里有班车、有食堂、有保安、有医疗。我们正在经历一场静悄悄的"再封建化"，只是 villa 改名叫 campus，goons 改名叫 corporate security。Palmer 讲的是 500 年的过去，但她其实在描述未来。

三、城市规模的"政治悖论"：为什么大到独立、小到必死、中间最危险

这里藏着一个被讲座一笔带过的系统性洞见：意大利城邦的悲剧在于它们卡在了一个"规模死亡区间"——

  • 人口 10–15 万：足够富，富到引来雇佣兵觊觎；
  • 但又不够大：凑不出能对抗职业军队的常备军。

财富吸引掠夺者的速度，超过了制度演化出防御能力的速度。 这就是 Palmer 所说"共和国一个接一个被吞噬"的力学根源。

跨学科类比令人毛骨悚然：这与生物学上的"中等体型陷阱"完全同构——太小（老鼠）繁殖快躲得过捕食，太大（大象）无人能敌，中等体型（鹿、羊）注定被狩猎。也与今天中型科技公司的处境惊人相似：年收入几亿到几十亿美元的公司，富到被巨头盯上，却小到无法独立对抗——要么被收购，要么被复制致死。

更深一层的"啊哈时刻"：罗密欧与朱丽叶不是爱情故事，而是一份政治制度失败的尸检报告。两个家族火拼的浪漫外衣下，是一个共和国正在被内部寡头吞噬的实时记录。莎士比亚写的其实是"共和制如何死亡"——他只是用了一对情人当镜头。

📝 转录文本

罗马陷落后：城市必须自治

When the Roman Empire dissolved in the west, individual cities then needed to self-govern, and this is true all across Europe, right? And those individual cities could no longer get the centralized Roman government to oversee supply routes, keep the roads free of bandits. You could no longer import and export goods at scale. You could no longer rely on central infrastructure. You had to support things yourself.

财富与农业：决定政体的底层条件

Larger wealthier towns were able to make this transition because they could support themselves from the local resources and the farms attached to them. So the larger wealthier towns surrounded by good agricultural land were more successful at converting over to: "Okay, let's have a senate like the old Roman Senate. Let's have our top families form a council. They will rule. We'll set up a republic."

A weaker town that can't support itself as well is much more prone to one wealthy family realizing that they can get goons and take over and declare themselves the monarch of the area.

"Village" 的诞生：从城镇逃向贵族庄园

Or worse, this town cannot self-sustain. It doesn't have enough. People there can't get food. They are scared. They're afraid of being robbed by people who are desperate. But outside of town, there is a wealthy villa that belongs to a noble family, and they have bodyguards.

"Hey noble family, if I move next to your villa and work for you, will you protect me with your bodyguards?"

So towns emptied out, and villages — as in villa and its environs — developed as a result. And a village was a monarchal structure in this sense: that was the migration of people out of a town into the protection zone of a local lordling, right? And then those villages grew to different scales, some of them cities, some not.

意大利的优势：肥沃的土地撑起共和

So Italy had great agriculture and great agricultural land. So more of Italy's cities were able to sustain themselves as towns and be republics than in other regions.

一千年后的难题：共和还能维持吗？

But then part two is: okay, you're a republic as of 500 AD. Are you still a republic a thousand years later? And that's trickier.

And one thing that Italy experiences over time is, one by one, republics get taken over and turned into monarchies. Because when you have a city-state — that means you, a large wealthy town — many of the largest cities in all of Europe are in Italy. Almost all of them; Paris is the exception, and Constantinople.

You have a large urban population, you have the neighboring agriculture, but your overall population may be 100,000, 150,000 at the most. You cannot raise a large army out of that. Certainly, you can't press an army of a scale large enough to fight off a professional mercenary army — if somebody decides, "Hey, I want to be duke of that town," right? Whether an outsider or an insider.

君主制吞噬共和：罗密欧与朱丽叶的真相

So, one by one, these republics are threatened by monarchal takeover, sometimes from within when one powerful family wants to dominate the other. This is where Romeo and Juliet is really helpful for us to understand, right?

The Montagues and the Capulets are fighting each other. Eventually, one family will wipe out the other family and declare themselves ruler of a city. That happens over and over. Or let's say the Montagues kick the Capulets out, and the Capulets can no longer live in the city. They might go take over the city next door, if no one in the city next door is wealthy enough to raise as many goons as they do.

制度的恐惧：防止僭主的精巧设计

So all of the Italian city republics are terrified constantly of monarchal takeover, and working on developing complex institutions and balances of power to try to prevent monarchal takeover.

If you enjoyed this clip, you can watch the full episode here and subscribe for more clips. Thanks.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一、被忽视的物质前提：共和制其实是"农业富余"的副产品
我们习惯把共和制看作思想成就——西塞罗、人文主义、公民德性。但 Palmer 揭示的真相令人不安：**共和制首先是一种"吃得饱"的政体**。能不能开元老院、能不能让贵族家族围坐议事，取决于城墙外那块地够不够肥。土壤贫瘠的城镇直接跳过了"政治哲学"这一环，被饥饿推回君主制怀抱。
这里有个反直觉的推论：**意大利文艺复兴的"共和精神"，其本质是波河流域和托斯卡纳的小麦产量在说话**。我们一直把佛罗伦萨的辉煌归功于美第奇的金融创新，但真正的底层逻辑是——那片土地养得起一个 10 万人不必跪下的城市。
## 二、"村庄"一词的语义考古：一个被遗忘的政治迁徙
最让我意外的，是 Palmer 对 "village" 词源的拆解——**village = villa + 周边**。这不是词源学的小聪明，而是一段被埋葬的政治史化石：
罗马崩塌后，人不是"住进了村庄"，而是**"逃进了贵族的保护圈"**。每一个今天田园诗里的"小村庄"，在公元 6 世纪都是一次微型的"政治放弃"——一群人用人身自由换取了不被强盗杀死的概率。
跨领域

item.title: [邦妮區塊鏈 Bonnie Blockchain] 金融市場最大的秘密：散戶的錢，到底去哪了？Krista Lynch【邦妮區塊鏈】
item.dc:creator: 邦妮區塊鏈 Bonnie Blockchain
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、"10点抛压"的真相：阴谋论背后是时区与定价机制的"地壳运动"

散户把每天10:00 AM的Bitcoin下跌解读成"机构联合收割"，但Krista揭示的真相更冷峻、也更有诗意——这不是阴谋，是金融基础设施的"潮汐"。伦敦期货的NAV strike时间、美国4PM的净值定价点，构成了加密世界看不见的"引力场"。

这里有个让人脊背发凉的跨领域类比：这和海洋潮汐由月球引力决定一模一样。渔民世代相传"涨潮抓鱼"的经验，本质是物理规律；而散户在Twitter上传播的"10点抛压"，也是同样的因果倒置——他们看到的是结果（价格波动），却误以为是某个"主谋"的意图。真正的"庄家"，是时区本身。

更深一层的悖论：当ETF成为"避险工具"之后，做空Bitcoin不再意味着看空Bitcoin。这彻底颠覆了散户的二元思维——市场上最大的Bitcoin卖压，可能来自最坚定的Bitcoin多头在对冲其他风险敞口。

二、"鲸鱼回流ETF"——加密原教旨主义的最大反讽

整段访谈最让我震撼的，是Krista那句轻描淡写的"this surprised us"——鲸鱼们正在主动把链上代币装进ETF的"传统金融笼子"里。

这是一个完美的历史性反转。Bitcoin诞生于2008年金融危机的废墟，白皮书里写满了对中心化机构的不信任。可十七年后，最早一批信徒中的"鲸鱼"们，发现要做税务规划、遗产规划、用代币做保证金贷款，必须借助他们曾经鄙视的Wall Street管道。

跨领域连接：这像极了60年代美国嬉皮士运动的"长征"——那些喊着"反体制"的年轻人，到了40岁要给孩子买学区房、做401(k)退休金规划时，最终走回了他们曾经焚烧国旗反对的体系。意识形态可以反叛，但生命周期的财务需求不会。Bitcoin的"机构化"不是被Wall Street征服，而是被"人到中年"征服。

三、隐藏的系统性真相：ETF不是"包装纸"，而是"语言翻译器"

大多数人把ETF理解成一个"容器"——把Bitcoin装进去，让传统投资者能买。但Krista反复用一个词："wrapper（包装）"，并把ETF、ADR、代币并列。

这里有一个被严重低估的洞见：所有wrapper本质上都是"金融语言的翻译器"，让不同法域、不同基础设施、不同交易时段的资产能够"互通"。台积电的ADR能在美股开盘时"代表"亚洲休市的台股表达观点——这不是简单的复制，而是一种跨时空的金融"同声传译"。

最颠覆性的延伸：如果wrapper是"翻译器"，那么tokenization就是金融世界的"Unicode标准化"。Krista说"先现金、再股票、最后房地产"的渐进路径，对应的正是从ASCII（最简单字符）到Unicode（容纳全球文字）的演进——每一次wrapper的进化，都是在扩大金融语言的"可表达性"。

而你提到的小提琴例子背后的产权悖论——"100个代币持有者谁是真正的所有者"——其实在ETF领域早已存在三十年（标普500持有者无法对苹果公司投票）。Web3的"新问题"，往往是传统金融早已解决但答案让人不满意的"老问题"。这意味着：未来十年tokenization的最大战场，不在技术，而在重新定义"所有权"这个被工业时代僵化的概念。

一个留给你的"啊哈"问题：如果ETF鲸鱼回流、Fed未来可能买入Bitcoin ETF、tokenization让所有资产24/7交易——那么"散户"和"机构"的边界，是否正在被ETF本身反向消解？也许十年后，最大的Bitcoin持有者，会是那些今天还在喊"机构收割散户"的人开的退休金账户。

📝 转录文本

开场：10 AM Dump 之谜

主持人：the 10 a.m. dump.

Krista：Some of the futures for Bitcoin and other digital assets have a very important time at which their value is struck. At London's, I believe it's 10:00 a.m. here or 11:00 a.m. Another important timeline for these products is 4 p.m. in the US. That's when NAV is struck. And you see a lot of trading activity between 3:00 and 4:00. For that reason, as the ETFs become a financial instrument, they're used for institutional investment. They're used for hedging. You can go long, you can go short. And going short doesn't even necessarily mean that you think Bitcoin is done.

主持人：Would you see why retail investors would think that is so-called unfair?

预告片段：全球货币、TSMC、鲸鱼与美联储

"In the most progressive of worlds, we would have one global currency."

"TSMC has been the biggest stock in Taiwan. And they're also trading in the US. So TSM, if I own TSM, do I own a piece of the company, too? Sometimes they trade very differently."

"Markets are open during different times, and there can be extended holidays in some Asian countries. My guess is that this happens during times when those markets are closed and the US product becomes a way to continue to express your view."

"And this surprised us. We've recently seen that whales have increasingly been interested in putting their tokens into the ETF. Hype and BNB are two that I'm keeping my eye on."

"Would you see a scenario in which a future Federal Reserve could buy Bitcoin ETFs during a distressed period?"

"I think it's a conversation you had about Taiwan exchange." "Yes."

正式访谈开始

谈论机构采用与代币化的未来，嘉宾是 Grayscale Investments ETF 资本市场高级副总裁 Krista Lynch。

主持人：Welcome to the show, Krista. Good to see you.

Krista：Thanks so much for having me.

一、"10点抛压"到底是怎么回事？

主持人：I am super excited to talk to you. Could you comment on the earlier event of the 10 a.m. dump? So people are speculating that every day at 10:00 a.m. Bitcoin would have this 2 to 3% sell off and then retails would pour in. What is that about?

Krista：Yeah, I know that there have been some theories. Twitter or X of course have latched on to this. I don't necessarily know what exactly drives it, but to me there's a couple things that are indicative or significant. And that has to do with certain NAV strikes and certain other financial instruments that have important times around that time.

As an example, some of the futures for Bitcoin and other digital assets have a very important time at which their value is struck at London's. I believe it's 10:00 a.m. here or 11:00 a.m. here. So that kind of correlates with the timeline you're mentioning. Now another important timeline for these products is 4 p.m. in the US. That's when NAV is struck and you see a lot of trading activity between 3:00 and 4:00 for that reason.

So we do a lot of debunking of some of the theories out there, especially for Grayscale when people see tokens moving in or out of wallets and they're speculating we're buying or selling. We're just facilitating a settlement. And my guess is that this is something similar where those are important times for determining value that may inspire a lot of trading but there's not a lot of deeper meaning behind it.

二、ETF 流入流出 ≠ 机构看多看空

主持人：And there are people talking about the data. Retail investors look at the inflows and outflows of Bitcoin spot ETFs and they're like "oh institutions are buying or selling", but there are a lot of ways to hedge against that. Could you comment on that?

Krista：Yeah exactly. So when we have a settlement for our Bitcoin or other digital asset ETF, you can see movements of Bitcoin coming into and out of our wallets. People often assume that Grayscale is actively making a decision to buy or sell. We are really beholden to the end investor who seeks to buy shares and ultimately that makes its way to an authorized participant who may create or redeem.

When they do that creation or redemption, my team actually will go out and buy Bitcoin or sell Bitcoin commensurate to the number of shares created or redeemed. We're not taking an active management approach, it's really just driven off of the needs of the market.

Now, you also mentioned hedging, and that's an important point too. I think that as the ETFs become a financial instrument, basically a bellwether representation of the asset class, they're not just used for speculative investment. They're used for institutional investment. They're used for hedging. You can go long, you can go short. And going short doesn't even necessarily mean that you think Bitcoin is done. It's just that you're taking an investment thesis and you're applying it through the Bitcoin ETF.

三、GBTC 时代的折溢价之谜

主持人：I remember when I first started covering the space before Bitcoin ETFs first rolled out, the Grayscale GBTC was the gold standard for institutional adoption of securitized products. At the time people used discount or premium to NAV as a really close indicator to sentiment. Can you, before we comment on that aspect, just comment on why premiums or discounts happen?

Krista：Yeah. So ETFs trade in two markets. You or I would trade on the secondary market — that's going into our Fidelity or Schwab accounts and buying shares. But ETFs also trade on an institutional level on the primary market. That's when something called an authorized participant goes directly to the ETF market maker and creates new shares or removes existing shares from the market.

Premiums and discounts happen when that secondary market price does not equal the NAV, which is the primary market price. Typically the authorized participant — really an ETF market maker who's working with an authorized participant — will keep those two very, very tight.

Now, in cases of GBTC, before it was an ETF, it did not have the primary market function. ETF market makers could only access one side of that market, and therefore they couldn't keep it really tight with the share price and the NAV.

Why was that something that people tracked so heavily? Because the premium and discount almost became an indicator of market sentiment about probability of us winning our lawsuit and being able to convert it into an ETF. They knew that if we could convert it, that would allow this mechanism to really work as it's designed to work and share price should become NAV. So every time there was a significant piece of news, you can see the premium or discount abruptly drop or abruptly increase as the market would digest what they thought it meant for the probability that these products would be approved for ETF form.

主持人：But even at this time, Bitcoin was a very liquid market. Why didn't arbitrageurs completely eliminate this discount or premium all the time?

Krista：Yeah, so they do now. If you see the Bitcoin ETFs, the premiums and discounts are in the single-digit BIPS, which is very very small. At the time before GBTC was able to uplist or turn into an ETF, that premium or discount could be like 10–20%, which is really unheard of for ETFs. The reason was because arbitrageurs could not access the primary market — they could only trade on the secondary market. So there was a surplus of shares in market that really had no outlet to be reduced.

四、中心化交易所在 ETF 生态中的角色

主持人：How does centralized exchange play in this whole scene?

Krista：They are very important as an outlet to trade. Like I said, when we get a creation or redemption, we have to go buy Bitcoin or sell Bitcoin. For Grayscale, we work directly bilaterally with certain trading institutions. But there are some issuers that rely on some of the exchanges as another venue for accessing that liquidity. Ultimately, we call them liquidity providers.

The traders that we work with are also tied into that ecosystem. So it's very symbiotic — whether you're trading directly with the liquidity provider, they're probably sourcing some token on these exchanges, or they're providing their own liquidity on exchange. And really all of that funnels into the very tight pricing that these ETFs are able to command.

五、买一份 Bitcoin Spot ETF，幕后发生了什么？

主持人：Could you walk us through the sequence? What happens when I buy a share of Bitcoin spot ETF? What happens behind the scenes?

Krista：Yeah, I love this question. This is what I spend most of my time on.

You and other investors are going to go out and buy Bitcoin ETFs. Eventually there's enough market demand for an ETF market maker who is behind the scenes providing you with those shares that they are becoming short. So they're selling you the shares — they might not necessarily have them though. In order to cover that, they need to create new shares.

So they're going to work with an authorized participant, which is an institution that has certain licenses to engage directly with an issuer like Grayscale. They're going to bundle that short and come to Grayscale and say, "I need to create shares to cover this short that I've established."

My team is going to say, "Okay, how many shares do you need?" Translate that into a quantity of Bitcoin and then go purchase that amount of Bitcoin in the market to actually back those shares with a commensurate amount of Bitcoin. When that all settles, the ETF market maker through their authorized participant is going to get those shares — and you've already gotten your shares. You're good to go.

That will really complete the life cycle when the authorized participant delivers shares to the ETF market maker and covers their short. They're doing this day in, day out. We see creations and redemptions on an ongoing continuous basis throughout the day.

主持人：And that process takes a day?

Krista：It usually takes about a day to settle, but the trade itself is very quick. It might take 5–10 minutes. That's by design. We want to make sure that markets are not moving while the authorized participant is seeking their pricing — as you know, very liquid markets, a piece of news can move a market a lot. So we intentionally move very quickly through this process.

六、AP 与做市商重合——是否"不公平"？

主持人：Just a quick follow-up. The argument was that there are APs that are also liquidity providers.

Krista：This is true. To elaborate a little bit more — the authorized participant role is technically a US broker dealer. A lot of people get confused that the authorized participant is the one pulling the strings and making the decision to create or redeem, but they're really more of an administrative function. The brains behind the operation is an ETF market maker.

Now, a lot of ETF market makers also within their broader company have crypto trading enterprises. So we do typically see a lot of pairing off between ETF market makers sending in orders through their authorized participant. When I go out and do that trade to buy Bitcoin, we often see their crypto desk winning that trade. That's very much by design by that ETF market maker because they can collapse their risk that way. If you have the ETF shares, you probably also want to match the Bitcoin exposure — and they can collapse their risk by winning the Bitcoin trade.

主持人：But would you see why retail investors would think that is so-called unfair? It's like you guys are the smart money and we can't beat that.

Krista：I think potentially, but how I would refute that is the end investor benefits from it by getting very tight spreads in market. When you or I go to buy a Bitcoin ETF, the spread — the bid and the ask — is typically one cent. That's as tight as it can possibly get. That's single-digit BIPS, and that's very, very good pricing.

That's possible because the ETF market makers are able to have these efficiencies. You or I can't access the primary market, and I do think that's where a lot of these theories come from. But we ultimately benefit from it because authorized participants have to engage in huge blocks of shares that we're probably not interested in trading that much. We benefit from their very tight spread. So it is very much an ecosystem where everyone is aligned to have the best outcome possible. I can see where some of these theories come from, but hopefully I've debunked it.

七、Q1/Q2 跌势 vs Tokenization 大涨 245%

主持人：Grayscale Research reported that across six crypto sectors, including financials and ETFs, we have negative returns throughout quarter 1 and 2. At the same time, tokenization volume increased by 245%. Tell us about these counter trends.

Krista：Recently I have seen the biggest themes of my conversations being more about infrastructure build than price appreciation. I think that's indicative of the maturation of the industry that we're seeing. Instead of talking about a token that might 10x in the next few weeks or months, we're talking about what is the actual infrastructure that we're building on the blockchain.

We're talking about banks potentially using stablecoins. We're talking about tokenizing assets so we can trade on a 24/7 basis and bring some of the principles of digital asset investing to the mainstream US markets. So instead of it being about price appreciation — and as you noted, it can actually be prices going down in some cases — I think there's so much progress being made behind the scenes to really implement the true spirit of blockchain technology.

八、看好哪个板块？她说："我是 token agnostic"

主持人：Of the six crypto sectors researched — currencies, smart contract platforms, financials, consumers, artificial intelligence and utilities and services — which still have the most growth potential?

Krista：I am more token agnostic than some might be. I personally think that picking stocks or picking tokens is very, very challenging to do. So I gravitate towards index products that encapsulate all of them. We do have one product that's the top five tokens by market cap excluding memecoins and stablecoins, and we've measured that that can actually capture 90% of the returns of the crypto market.

So while some people are more into picking the right token or the right sector, I don't even do this with stocks. I buy the S&P 500. To me, that's the analog of the S&P 500 and how I approach the investment.

九、回应 Larry Fink 的"万物代币化"愿景

主持人：I'd like to get you to react to your former boss Larry Fink's comments on tokenization.

"I do believe we're just at the beginning of the tokenization of all assets — from real estate to equities to bonds across the board."

BlackRock has a $10 trillion tokenization vision. Why do we need the tokenization of all assets?

Krista：I don't disagree with him, especially his point that we're at the very early innings. A theme that's come up at this conference is the convergence of TradFi and DeFi. We're here in suits — I never thought that would happen at a Consensus. Tokenization is kind of the continuation of that.

I see stablecoins as kind of tokenized cash. That's the first place we'll see banks and other really traditional finance institutions getting their foothold. That can next progress to tokenizing something basic like a US equity. There you gain the benefits of digital asset features like 24/7 trading, instantaneous settlement, and borderless ability to invest. If I'm in another country but I want to buy a US equity, tokenization can really enable that.

There are naysayers who say this isn't that novel. So you bring up real estate — I think things like that will be a very different application. It's going to be years before we see mainstream adoption of that. But I do think eventually that's where we're going. But it's baby steps. We're starting with cash. We're going to get to equities, and then we'll move on to more advanced underliers.

十、Tokenization 与国家货币主权

主持人：How does tokenization change the way nations protect their currency flow?

Krista：I had a great conversation with someone working on a payments company last night about this. I think it could threaten them in some ways. You do see with tokenization rules about who can invest in what. Even in the US, we can't necessarily invest in all the tokenization representations of US equities. There are protections that countries will want to put in place to keep their currencies a little bit more centralized.

But you could also say that Bitcoin has broken down those barriers because Bitcoin is a global currency. So regulation is needed in order to understand what can and cannot be built in certain regions. That will help practitioners really move forward with confidence.

主持人：In, let's say, 20 years, we look back, what would change?

Krista：That's going to come down to geopolitics, honestly. What are countries going to be comfortable with and what are they going to allow? In the most progressive of worlds, we would have one global currency, but I don't think we'll get there anytime soon. There are real reasons why central banks want to control and protect their monetary system.

I don't necessarily see us moving to a place where countries are comfortable with widespread adoption of any currency. But I do think we'll see more FX trading of some of these assets. We already see it with different Bitcoin–USD, Bitcoin–other currency pairs. I could see us moving to a world where we have a more globalized currency, and then there is this need for FX trading to convert to those countries that are the holdouts.

十一、代币化股票——所有权悖论

主持人：A lot of tokenized real assets have been sold as promises that the individual investor retains the ownership right. Say I like music — a Stradivari violin that is tokenized. Many people who own tokenized shares will have a say in how the violin is performed. How does ownership work for stocks? For sake of argument, Krista Lynch Co. has 100 shares outstanding. Bonnie owns 100 shares. She's the largest shareholder. There are 100 tokenized shares on each share — I own the tokenized shares. So who owns your company now?

Krista：There's a lot of analogues here with ETFs as well. That goes with the ownership rights and voting rights. For example, ETFs have had to grapple with this. If I own shares of an ETF, do I get to vote to say that I want one of the shares within my S&P 500 of 500 shares? Do I get to make the managerial decision? This is very reminiscent of that quandary that ETFs have faced.

There's another piece that goes a step further — in the tokenized representation, do you even own the underlying stock, or do you own an IOU? These are some of the regulatory issues that we're really grappling with right now.

An analog we see is the difference between a US ETF and some of the European ETNs or ETPs. In some cases in a European product, you actually own shares or own rights to the issuer, not the underlying assets. That's an analog I study to see how that's been dealt with in other regions for traditional assets. We spend a lot of our time looking at traditional analogues to guide what we think the way should be for digital assets. I wouldn't be surprised if there's the need for roundtables and other thought leadership to look at these real world examples.

主持人：What is your personal view?

Krista：That's a good one. I haven't really applied enough time researching it to have a stance, because it also has to do with the mechanics of how the product works. If you're going direct on-chain, then there's probably more of an argument for you to be able to make that decision. Versus if you're using a system where maybe the shares are still in custody at a big financial institution and you just own the IOU, then I think that's more like the ETF model — the institution tokenizing it and giving you that IOU should retain some more discretion just for administrative purposes.

十二、TSMC、ADR 与"Wrapper"的本质

主持人：TSMC has been the biggest stock in Taiwan and they're also trading in the US. So TSM — if I own TSM, do I own a piece of the company too?

Krista：It depends on how it's gotten to the US. There's something called an ADR — a popular way to get US equities to other countries and vice versa. It really depends on the structuring. I'd have to look into the specifics, but my guess is that you don't actually own the underlying share. It is possible that you do. You always have to read the prospectus — the devil's in the details.

This is another example of a wrapper. ETFs are a wrapper. Tokens are a wrapper. ADRs are a wrapper. They're another way that we study how these things are actually working today in US financial systems and abroad — and how we can digitize it.

主持人：Do wrappers track the underlying asset? Sometimes they trade very differently. Sometimes the ADR in US would be up 15% but in Taiwan only 10%. I own both.

Krista：If the wrapper is working the way it's supposed to, it should be perfect one-to-one matching. That goes back to the GBTC example. The premium or discount existed because it was not working — it could not access the primary market.

In this case, since those markets are open during different times and there can be extended holidays in some Asian countries, my guess is that this happens during times when those markets are closed and the US product becomes a way to continue to express your view even though markets in Asia are closed.

十三、Grayscale 如何逆势吸金

主持人：In early April, US spot Bitcoin ETFs recorded $173.7 million in net outflows as of April 1st. Grayscale's lower-cost mini trust BTC attracted $10.25 million in inflows. How did Grayscale buck the trend?

Krista：We love it. We have studied this a lot. We believe that because we are the lowest cost vehicle in market, we have a stickier investor base. Instead of being a lot of speculative investors who are just in it for a quick expression of inflow/outflow, we think we get a lot of people who really have conviction on the asset class and want to be in it for the long haul. They're less sensitive to news that might be short-lived. They stay in the product. Even when our competitors are seeing outflows, oftentimes we see our investors sticking with us.

主持人：Are the investor profiles different?

Krista：I think it could be — more long-hold "I have conviction in digital assets, I have conviction in Bitcoin" type investor, as opposed to more speculative risk-on/risk-off where you're trying to make a quick call. Those flows can be very volatile — they're not necessarily sticky investors. The low management fee really commands the investor type that is looking to be in it for the long haul.

十四、ETH ETF 与质押收益

主持人：Ether ETFs interestingly closed Q1 with a $769 million quarterly outflow — their worst three-month stretch since launching. However, Grayscale's ETHE product attracted capital despite a higher 2.5% fee. What does that tell you about institutional appetite?

Krista：One thing that differentiates our Ethereum products, especially at that time, was that we implemented staking. Our Ethereum products actually yield — they have value that they provide to investors for staking. Staking is basically when you put your assets to work and you're paid in kind.

The thing that was hard to solve for was: while you stake your assets, they're not liquid. If you need to honor a redemption — me needing to sell Ethereum to provide cash to our authorized participant — I can't necessarily do that with staked assets. So we had to develop a very robust framework for how we could measure what is enough liquidity in the fund, how much can we stake, and monitor that on an ongoing basis. We went to the SEC and showed them our model. It was a long time coming — we ultimately implemented it very late last year in October.

It is a differentiator for our products. Our ETH product actually pays out a distribution — investors get a cash distribution. Our ETHE product reinvests those rewards, so it has some accruing nature to it. I think that could be a reason why investors stuck with us during a period of outflows.

十五、稳定币收益与 Covered Call 策略

主持人：On yield — there's a big debate on Wall Street about how banks can tolerate stablecoins issuing yield. Can Grayscale or any of your peers design a derivative product of a stablecoin that has a native yield embedded?

Krista：One thing we've done to give yield to our investors is our covered call strategies. We're using options overlays in order to build yield on top of Bitcoin positions and Ethereum positions. Aside from staking, which we can do on a number of protocols we deliver in ETF form, we can also provide yield through these synthetic products that are using options overlays.

主持人：Can you comment on which of the tokens on Grayscale's expanded watch list gets an ETF wrapper this year?

Krista：We have a couple active filings right now. I won't elaborate further beyond the protocols, but Hype and BNB are two that I'm keeping my eye on.

十六、如何决定推哪个币种？

主持人：How do you decide which coin?

Krista：There was a period when it was really dictated by regulation. We had Bitcoin available, then Ethereum, and then there was an explosion in the number of filings issuers were putting in front of the SEC. That led them to crank through so many filings — I think they had 90 at one point — that they developed something called the generic listing standards. This is the framework by which issuers understand if a token can be ETP eligible.

That has encapsulated about 15 different tokens at this point, but it's a growing list. It's led us to this crossroads where you have to be selective now. Instead of "okay, we can, so we will" — we have to apply more discretion and be more selective about what assets we bring to market in ETF form. We now offer eight tokens in ETF form, soon to be several more.

We have a research team that does a lot of diligence on protocols, gets to meet the team. They spend years doing this. They really come up with their investment thesis and cultivate relationships. They overlay on top of the regulatory "can I" the "should I." We work in lockstep with our research team to really develop our investment thesis and push forward particular protocols.

十七、鲸鱼回流 ETF——一个反直觉的趋势

主持人：You mentioned BNB and Hype. Hype does not have VC. Is that a consideration? BNB also is very distributed. Do you prefer not to pick those tokens with large whales?

Krista：Not necessarily. The whale point is very interesting for the ETFs because we've recently seen — and this surprised us — that whales have increasingly been interested in putting their tokens into the ETF. We thought that as a crypto native, that would be kind of repugnant to get on the US rails via the ETF.

But the reason it's compelling is because if you move your token into shares format, you can oftentimes get tax planning, estate planning — the benefits that these US brokerage accounts allow. You can generally also use your shares for margin and collateral, which can be also quite compelling. So we've had recent progress with actually whales wanting the ETF.

主持人：Could you share how does that work?

Krista：Behind the scenes, of course there's a lot going on, but yes — my team will actually work directly with whales. We will engage with our authorized participant partners or other third-party intermediaries to help them in-kind tokens into the product.

We talked earlier about the cash creation/redemption process. Last summer the SEC decided that it would be okay to do in-kind creation/redemption, and that really opened the doors for this type of investor. My team, instead of going out and trading, will hold the hands of all the third parties necessary to move token from a whale's account into our trust and issue shares to them in response.

主持人：So if you buy a share of spot ETF, you would have had to go on the market and buy a share — but now I can go to you and give you my token.

Krista：Exactly.

主持人：And that changed last year?

Krista：Yes. Now you would actually be accessing the primary market. Primary markets are almost never available to individual investors because of this authorized participant requirement. But we have some very clever authorized participants who have opened the doors for individual investors to partner with them to do this.

十八、ETF 流量 vs 现货价格的因果关系

主持人：Now that we have two years of data for Bitcoin spot ETFs, have you noticed a correlation between outflows/inflows and the subsequent price movement? What comes first — the ETF flows or the underlying price movement?

Krista：This is a great question and something we studied for our staking model, because we did need to explain to the SEC: if you have an abrupt price change, especially in a volatile asset, is it going to lead to chaos with redemptions?

We found that of course there's some correlation, but it's not necessarily indicative of the other. ETFs are a really good and efficient way to express a view on risk very quickly. You can see it go inflows quickly to outflows the next day, and that's because of the efficiency of the vehicle.

When the price is going up, you typically see creation demand. When the price is going down, you typically see redemption demand. So they do generally align, but I would say one is not a leading indicator of the other. If anything, it might skew a little bit towards the price being what you see — a delayed reaction with the ETFs — but it's not like it's going to happen because you're seeing one versus the other.

十九、Bitcoin 2100 万上限会"枯竭"吗？

主持人：If more and more Bitcoins get bought up by treasury companies, would that skew the creation/redemption process? Would there be less liquidity?

Krista：We get this question a lot, especially with the cap on Bitcoin of 21 million. People say, "Oh no, are we going to run out of Bitcoin?" Really it should just push up the price of Bitcoin with basic supply/demand dynamics. It will not hinder the creation/redemption process. It just means that buying Bitcoin might cost more — but that manifests in a higher price of Bitcoin and the NAV moves alongside that. The actual execution costs I would not expect to be higher.

二十、代币化的"真实性"验证难题

主持人：Back to tokenization. The debate last year was — how do you make sure that a token represents, let's say, 1% of that real estate? Is it a country that has to come out and verify this? What has to happen?

Krista：This is one of those ambiguities that has to be solved before there can be success. There needs to be rules and regulations or guidelines about how you determine the value.

But this is not that dissimilar from traditional finance analogues. Whenever there's ambiguity about a price, an index will have a very robust set of rules by which you determine a waterfall of pricing. There can be instances when you challenge that price — for example, "I typically price this using a Bloomberg index but today I don't agree with that because it's this percentage away from the last trade price." Those will actually go to disputes, and we do see that system working as it's supposed to.

There can be frictions — but of course healthy frictions. I think this is where we'll ultimately have to go with something like tokenization as well. There has to be some sort of either golden source of truth or board that can come to a consensus. Like I said — baby steps. We start with cash, we go to equities, ultimately we'll go to real world assets.

主持人：Is there a timeline?

Krista：I think it'll be years for real world assets. We already see adoption for cash right now in stablecoins. My guess is many banks are using them behind the scenes whether they're vocal about it or not. With equities, there are a number of US institutions like NYSE, NASDAQ, the DTC that have come out with announcements about projects. We'll see progress on that in the coming months. But how far it goes — it'll be a build that takes a couple years.

二十一、美联储会购买 Bitcoin ETF 吗？

主持人：The Federal Reserve has had a history of buying ETFs, especially during emergencies like COVID — more so on the corporate bond side. Would you see a scenario in which a future Federal Reserve could buy Bitcoin ETFs during a distressed period?

Krista：I think it's possible. Don't say anything's impossible. But it seems to me like they'll still need more time to get comfortable with the asset class. I think it's going to take a little bit more time.

But you see pensions, endowments starting to do it, and I think that's the first sign. Bitcoin is becoming so much more institutional. We talked to a lot of people at Grayscale who are just beginning their crypto journey — they're new to it. But here, of course, we're like — everyone should have it, everyone has it. There is quite a continuum of investors out there, but I do think this is going to be something that eventually everyone has in their portfolio.

二十二、Bitcoin 变得"无聊"了吗？

主持人：Is Bitcoin becoming more and more institutional and less exciting? I hate to say less exciting, but —

Krista：I do think it is becoming more institutional. Just looking around the crowd today at Consensus — it's a lot more traditional banks, a lot more people in suits. That's exciting in and of itself because it means there's maturity of the asset class. Ultimately, that's going to bring more investors as we open the doors for people that were sitting on the sidelines.

The ETFs were a huge part of that for people that didn't know how to open a wallet — institutions that didn't have the risk infrastructure to manage a wallet or didn't want to build that out. So while it is less exciting in some ways, it's very exciting in the sense that it is getting more adoption and more institutionalized.

主持人：How do you react to all these Bitcoin OGs' predictions on Bitcoin going to a million dollars or some crazy high number?

Krista：I hope they're right. There are supply/demand dynamics that could make that a reality. A million dollars is very high — it's going to take some time to get there. But if we look at things like the 21 million cap, DATs and ETFs that are buying these, new investors that have come into the fold because of the ETF — those are all positive tailwinds that should push the price up.

主持人：I think the solution to making this exciting is to institutionalize memecoins. There's a big PEPE stand over there with the frogs. Maybe there's a partnership.

二十三、Bitcoin vs Ethereum：年底展望

主持人：The future of Bitcoin and ETH this year — where do you think they go?

Krista：To me Bitcoin represents the "all of the above" for blockchain, for Bitcoin, for other digital assets. We work with a lot of people that are new to the digital asset world, and a lot of them are just learning about Bitcoin — that's what they gravitate to. They might have heard of Bitcoin and aren't necessarily able to go down the token spectrum yet. Bitcoin is very compelling because so many investors gravitate to it for their first experience.

Ethereum I get really excited about because I think the value is in these smart contract type builds. Being able to use blockchain technology to improve our processes — if you think about a fixed income bond, codifying some of these back office functions, making things more efficient — that's really exciting. So Bitcoin and Ethereum both have very bright futures, but for slightly different reasons.

二十四、Staked ETF：给妈妈听得懂的解释

主持人：Earlier you mentioned staked ETF. Could you elaborate — explain to my mom — how do you stake ETH but also get the yield?

Krista：We work with third-party validators. A validator is basically who you are pledging to — pledging is not the right terminology but we'll use it here to simplify things. You're contributing your token to make the overall protocol more safe and secure.

As you have it pledged, it is locked up — it's not liquid. That's the conundrum we face in implementing this for a very liquid product. You're pledging your assets to support the greater good of the ecosystem, and in turn you're paid in kind. You'll actually get a deposit in your wallet of that same asset, in this case Ethereum.

For our ETHE product, we actually monetize that — I'll go out and trade that, raise cash, and pay that out to our investors. In the case of ETH, we will then stake that Ethereum that we've received so it compounds over time.

How do we deal with the illiquidity aspect? Through some very intense mathematical modeling, we've determined what we think should remain unstaked in the fund and we dynamically calibrate that based on market conditions. We additionally have implemented something called delayed settlement with some of our liquidity providers, where they're able to give us cash on a T+1 basis and we will settle token to them once available. Of course they'll charge a little bit of a spread, but it makes the product more efficient and allows us to get yield. Ultimately, the overall economics benefit the investor.

主持人：So if everybody wants to pull out of that ETF, you're still safe.

Krista：Exactly. We keep a portion of the fund unstaked so it's immediately liquid, and then we work with our liquidity providers on any additional top-up needs. They would provide us with that cash in the normal course of business. You as an investor would not experience any disruptions — we would be dealing with it on the back end.

二十五、对零售投资者的建议

主持人：The institutional appeal of your products is obvious, but how would you convince a retail crowd to look into your Bitcoin/ETH products?

Krista：We're actually doing an event down the road today called Crypto Connect, geared towards advisers and end clients. Our BTC and ETH products are typically a really big point of focus. They're very low-cost ways to get into Bitcoin and Ethereum.

If you look at some of the trading costs that you might face on an exchange to buy the spot representation — it can be much higher than the spreads and management fees of these products. Sometimes you can pay as much as 1.5% in order to buy or sell a token on an exchange versus a penny-wide spread to buy or sell the ETF. They also have very low management fees.

主持人：And it's also a way to have access to staked ETH without doing the staking yourself.

Krista：Exactly. To me, that's very compelling. I'm not going to go out and stake my fractional Ethereum token, but Grayscale is able to do that and pass that on to the investor.

二十六、量子计算与 AI 对 Bitcoin 的威胁

主持人：I would love to know your due diligence process on how quantum computers will affect Bitcoin and how AI will break smart contracts.

Krista：This is something that's been coming up in a lot of cocktail conversations here. I've heard competing viewpoints — some think that's going to be the biggest threat to Bitcoin. Others say these things are already happening — AI is already running, we already have these models in place. It's something that should be paid a lot of attention to. I don't necessarily have a thesis on if it's going to break Bitcoin or not. I hope we get ahead of that. It's something we need to be vigilant for.

主持人：How is the company dealing with it? What's the internal conversation?

Krista：We're definitely monitoring it. Something we pay a lot of attention to is also how it could affect our service providers. We not only need to view how it affects our immediate workforce, but how does it also affect some of our service providers like custodians, authorized participants — could they suffer a hack? We're getting in the weeds not only with ourselves but also with all of our touch points.

二十七、台湾交易所的对话

主持人：Earlier we were talking about a conversation you had about Taipei, the Taiwan exchange.

Krista：Yes — that was so interesting for me. I went to a roundtable that Bloomberg sponsored, and it was a way for US issuers to learn about some of the incentives to bring our products overseas. One such incentive was an innovation kind of stipulation. It seems that there's an appetite for some of these covered call products that we talked about. Digital asset covered calls might not necessarily be the flavor that the government is looking for, but it sounds like there's a lot of encouragement for innovation — something that maybe we could look into pushing forward.

结束语

主持人：Okay. Well, thank you, Krista. That is all the time we have.

Krista：Thanks for your time. Appreciate you having me. Insightful. Thank you.

主持人：Thanks so much. Thank you. And do check out Grayscale Investments. Link below.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一、"10点抛压"的真相：阴谋论背后是时区与定价机制的"地壳运动"
散户把每天10:00 AM的Bitcoin下跌解读成"机构联合收割"，但Krista揭示的真相更冷峻、也更有诗意——**这不是阴谋，是金融基础设施的"潮汐"**。伦敦期货的NAV strike时间、美国4PM的净值定价点，构成了加密世界看不见的"引力场"。
这里有个让人脊背发凉的跨领域类比：**这和海洋潮汐由月球引力决定一模一样**。渔民世代相传"涨潮抓鱼"的经验，本质是物理规律；而散户在Twitter上传播的"10点抛压"，也是同样的因果倒置——他们看到的是结果（价格波动），却误以为是某个"主谋"的意图。**真正的"庄家"，是时区本身**。
更深一层的悖论：当ETF成为"避险工具"之后，做空Bitcoin不再意味着看空Bitcoin。这彻底颠覆了散户的二元思维——市场上最大的Bitcoin卖压，可能来自最坚定的Bitcoin多头在对冲其他风险敞口。
## 二、"鲸鱼回流ETF"——加密原教旨主义的最大反讽
整段访谈最让我震撼的，是Krista那句轻描淡写的"this surprised us"——**

item.title: [Business of Sport] How Smaller Clubs are Outsmarting Football’s Elite
item.dc:creator: Business of Sport
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、最反直觉的发现：花得多反而是一种"诅咒"

整段讨论里最颠覆的不是"小球队聪明"，而是节目最后那句几乎被一笔带过的话——"Spurs 真正的问题不是花得太少，而是花得太多。" 这是一个被主流叙事彻底掩盖的悖论：在足球转会市场，预算超过某条临界线之后，钱本身会反过来塑造一种懒惰的认知结构。当你能买到"已验证"的球员时，你的球探部门、数据团队、青训接口都会逐渐萎缩，因为它们的边际价值看起来不如直接掏钱。结果就是——大俱乐部不是"钱不够聪明"，而是钱让它们失去了变聪明的必要性。Brighton、Brentford、Bodø/Glimt 之所以厉害，不是因为他们更聪明，而是因为他们没有退路去不聪明。

这其实和经济学里的"资源诅咒"（Resource Curse）是同构的：石油国家往往制造业孱弱，因为不需要工业也能富裕。足球的"转会预算诅咒"是同一个机制在另一个领域的镜像。

二、跨领域连接：这不是足球故事，是一个"中等规模物种"的进化生态学

你仔细看这些俱乐部的共同特征——预算 4M 到 70M 之间的"中型球队"正在全欧洲集体崛起——这其实是生态学里一个非常成熟的模型："中型捕食者释放"（Mesopredator Release）。当顶级捕食者（豪门）被某种结构性力量（FFP、球迷预期、媒体压力、即时成功的"政治正确"）束缚住时，生态位中段的物种会突然获得不成比例的繁殖优势。

更妙的是，这个机制还嵌套着第二层跨领域映射——Clayton Christensen 的"颠覆性创新"理论。Brighton 用 Ecuador 的 Caicedo 颠覆 Chelsea，和当年丰田用 Corolla 颠覆底特律是同一个剧本：在位者无法服务低端市场（无法签未成形球员、无法接受输球过渡期），新进入者从被忽视的市场段切入，逐步上探。Christensen 当年说："在位者失败不是因为做错了事，而是因为做对了所有事。" 这句话原封不动地套在 Tottenham 身上，毫无违和感。

三、被所有人忽视的隐藏结构："球迷耐心"才是这个时代最稀缺的战略资源

节目里反复出现一个词——"placation"（安抚球迷）——但没人把它当成核心变量。我想指出：在 2025 年的欧洲足球里，真正的护城河不是数据、不是球探、不是教练，而是"球迷允许你输球的时间窗口"。

Bodø/Glimt 的董事会可以连续三年坚持同一个教练、同一个 4-3-3、同一批 20-22 岁的球员；而 Chelsea 的董事会三个月就要换一次方向，因为 Stamford Bridge 的看台不允许"过渡赛季"这个概念存在。这意味着——所有"小球队成功学"的真正基础设施，是一种被低估的、几乎不可交易的资产：低期望值。

这也带来一个极其残酷的悖论：任何一个小球队一旦成功，它的"低期望资产"就会立刻被消耗掉。Brighton 现在已经回不去 5 年前那个"输了也没事"的状态了；Aston Villa 在欧冠出线后球迷开始要求争四了；Tun 一旦真的进入欧冠淘汰赛，赞助商、媒体、球迷的期望会同步通胀，最终把那个让他们成功的"宽松环境"亲手毁掉。

这就解释了一个之前节目里没有点破的现象——为什么"小球队奇迹"是周期性的、而不是积累性的。这不是管理问题，而是期望值的热力学第二定律：成功必然增加期望熵，而期望熵的增加必然侵蚀产生成功的初始条件。

啊哈时刻：小球队的真正对手不是大球队，是"未来那个被自己成功喂大的自己"。

📝 转录文本

开场：来自听众 Robert Kramer 的话题

Our final topic, we're heading back to um the sport of football. This topic derives from a detailed observation made by one of our listeners, Robert Kramer.

Um now, unlike um Mr. Yansen earlier on who made some certain assumptions which turned out to be —

Yansen, sorry, Fransen. Fran 1016. You use his full name, please. 1016 in there as well. Unlike Mr. Francon who made certain assumptions which turned out to be um incorrect. This was an absolute belter of a post for all the right reasons which is some stuff that we've been discussing a bit on and off here or there but really adding detail and context to it and then serving it up to us and saying guys we I want to hear you discuss this.

Do you know why is I think it's because it's very easy to overlook. We get caught up so much in looking at the successes — the big successes in football and the big failures. Who won this trophy? Who who won that trophy? who's uh who's having an absolute disaster, who's been fired, which players been amazing.

And you know, in a lot of sports conversations, that's that's what drives it. In our sports conversations, we can't just get sucked into this. And some of these stories across Europe this year, as we'll just touch on now, are absolutely incredible of small teams outperforming some of the bigger ones.

FC Thun 的奇迹：4 百万欧元预算重返欧冠

And we're not just talking about, you know, okay, look, Aston Villa doing really well, but there's still, you know, 378 million in revenue with the sixth sixth biggest wage from European governor from — Yeah, exactly.

What we're talking about here is FC Tun in Switzerland getting promoted last year, a budget of €4 million, pretty much bankrupt 3 years ago, being saved by a businessman, a local CEO, injecting 3 million into the club. They then get promoted and they've now they've now won the Swiss League and will be back in the Champions League.

I say back in it because they were actually in it 20 years ago playing the likes of Arsenal, uh, Dennard Burkamp, 90th minute winner in that in 2005. But basically from that moment they got about 20 million in revenue, Champions League revenue and they just couldn't handle it and it was a disaster for the club and they've had a real challenge ever since.

Now they've come back into the league and it's the first ever team — even Leicester couldn't didn't do this. The first team is Switzerland but only Kaiser Schlton and Nottingham Forest I think had done it before where they got promoted and won the league the year after.

Bodø/Glimt 与挪威小镇奇迹

These stories are actually cropping up a lot more. We've had Bodo Glimps doing unbelievably well in the Champions League, the Norwegian team who play very far north in Norway on a on a like 4G pitch. I hadn't made the link with your Norwegian background Charles.

No, none of my family in there cuz they're all footballers. Mine are Oh, because it probably is a ski jumping part of Norway, isn't it? It's very snowy. Norway is very snowy. I wonder if it's difficult for them at academy level to try and work out which their kids are going to be better footballers and which of them are going to be ski jumpers. Very easy as we know. But look, as I said, story for another day. I actually know somebody who used to be the technical director at Berlin. So, so I'm going to ask him that question. I do. Yeah. Ask ask him on the selection process.

Either way, it was a very small town. I think I haven't got that data in front of me directly. I think it's about 50,000 grounds 8,000 make it to the last 16 of the Champions League.

英超中下游的崛起：Bournemouth、Brighton、Brentford、Sunderland

Yeah. You know, the these stories have been far more frequent and in the Premier League, you know, there there are still incredible moments this year where you've looked at Bournemouth and Brighton and Brenford all not moments. What's the current EPL table below the top five? I haven't got it in front of me. I'm going to go Bournemouth. Yeah. Brenford. Keep going. Brighton. Keep going. Sunderland. By the way, that's pretty good. Charles, Bournemouth Brighton Brenford Sunderland with the exception of getting Brighton and Brenford the wrong way round. You were spot on there. Well done. And then Chelsea. Um Chelsea bottom half. Yeah, I'm 10th. Oh, so still top half. Okay, let's see what happens.

Well, after tonight obviously. So Bournemouth, Brighton, Brenford and Sunderland this year in the EPLL have outperformed Chelsea and Spurs. And that's quite interesting because in the case of Bournemouth, Brighton and Brenford, that's not a one-off. That's that's they have consistently been pushing towards the top end, the top half of the Premier League.

And these are all clubs that in the relatively recent past have been in the bottom two leagues in England and and and not there on an occasional basis. They're on a like semi-permanent basis. I mean, when I was young, Bournemouth were always in the bottom division. They almost went out the whole league. Brenford were a sort of perennial league one, so tier three type team. And Brighton, well, I remember seeing them in the bottom division as well. But they were

they were I mean, they were literally a game or a half from going out the football league, weren't they? I thought it was Bournemouth, but anyhow, but maybe Brighton as well. Maybe Brighton.

欧洲全景扫描：丹麦、瑞典、奥地利、瑞士、意大利、西班牙、荷兰、法国

Anyway, look, but I actually want to get on to let's let's talk about the other countries.

So, in Denmark, um AGK Arus um are the champions. um not one of the very smallest clubs but absolutely not FC Copenhagen or Bronby right so not not one of those really famous big clubs you regularly see in European football kind of

area measure second and they're also a small club fundamentally um Viking in Norway coming after Bod Glint winning four of the last five seasons Viking this season in Sweden they're the Swedish champions for 2025 again a small to mediumsiz clubs in Scotland obviously just failed to beat we didn't mention that did we I mean how did we not I devastated. Yeah, unfortunate. That would require an entire episode.

Um, Lask in Austria again, you know, got some big big clubs in Austria. Domestic double.

Um, there, you know, again, we're talking about some much more famous clubs. Rapid Vienna and Saltzburg and these types of clubs. Ton in Switzerland, you've mentioned um, you know, again, Grasshoppers of Zurich and Basel and these types of clubs. Young Boys, again, forget it. Ton with their 5 million euro budget.

Ko in Italy, fifth in Serie

ahead of Juventus. Ahead of Juventus with a budget of 35 million. Do you know even Vill I looked at Vel ahead of Atletico Madrid. Third place in V Royale is also on my list. Third in La Liga would very much be regarded in their own region as being the minnows compared to Valencia let alone compared to Atletico Madrid and they're third in La Liga. NEC third I think Ajax having a terrible year. uh you know I think their wage bill is €8.75 million

and in France their whole wage bill and in France we've had a slightly closer fight for the title this year it does look like PSG have done okay they have they have actually done it right against LOS who they've been fighting toeto- toe with all year now I just had a just a look at this cuz we discussed Ton being on four or five million and that being sort of you know maybe 20% L's budget is €30 million right so to give you That's sort of 26 million pounds. That's sort of roughly what a mid-table championship side in England would spend. They're up against PSG, arguably the best and you know, one of the most expensive club sides in the world, €200 million, right? So PSG are spending seven times what lawns are spending and yet at domestic level they're almost on a almost on a level pegging.

Um, so and then we got even in Italy, we got Atlanta who have been outstanding the last few seasons.

They're having a good year this year. I know it's not a good year this year, but they I think sometimes with these clubs having to also play European football can end up catching up with them after a little bit of while just the sheer number of games and the required squad depth becomes a bit of a problem. Well, the sorry which again has happened a bit to glimpse as well this year.

You talked about squad depth and how PSG have actually come out on top and how difficult it is to compete. They have pretty much, if you look at the statistics this year for their players, they have been so comfortable in this league over the last years that Dembele started nine games in the league this year. Marinosh is something similar. They've just been after the Champions League. They've been and and the strength of this team is that they can rest their players to that extent and still win the league.

But I think it's it's nonetheless worth pointing out that Lance have been able to come second in a league where even if you take PSG out of it, you've got clubs like Leon and Marles who would be spending much more money than Lance would be spending. Le as well, which would be a more local competitor to Lance.

Um, so it's part of a pattern of I'm not say small clubs except maybe for Tun. Um, but I'm going to say kind of like ambitious midsize clubs. It appears in the last 5 to 10 years have gained an ability to be able to be if they are very well-run — and we'll come on to what that means in a moment — to be highly competitive at the top of European football even with budgets that would you would think and certainly most fans would think you wouldn't be able to achieve that with.

为什么？五大主题的提出

So yeah, and the the why right is the is the interesting question. I was just trying to I was trying to put it thematically and I wanted I didn't want to tell you beforehand but as someone that again has operated in these environments and knows how this works I wanted to see your thoughts on on thinking about it thematically.

So just to lay out a few of them that I put in, you know, some of them will be obvious: - Data and analytics and the leveraging of that. - Um identity over individuals, - sell smart, reinvest smarter, - financial discipline as a strategic advantage most importantly. - And finally, coaching clarity.

But I thought the coaching clarity in there was one I'd like to throw in because I know of your opinions of of coaching.

Well, I think it's very important. I want to ask about the first one there which is uh what did I say? I said data and analytics. You said something to me yesterday which really like interested me when we always hold Brighton and Brenford up as this incredible case study of how to find a model that beats the established. You then turn around to me and you said, "Yeah, but you know, kind of necessity is the mother of invention. are not going to be making the money that Manchester City and Arsenal and Liverpool are in the same ways, i.e. through massive match day revenue, through big commercial deals. So, they have to go and find different ways to do it. And player trading is the way that they've decided to elevate their position and they've done it incredibly well." What what's the influence of that in these stories?

数据分析的真正含义

Okay. So I think um the phrase "data analytics" has become a bit of a sort of a bit of a thing right in that a lot of people say it and it's become popular parliament even amongst the fan bases and and the football media

but when I ask a lot of people who talk about it have you actually ever seen what it what this looks like what it actually means right and all it basically is is like sort of you know the gathering of evidence and then effectively putting it through rather than just saying well here are some facts which is relatively easy to do is saying, well, how can we then put those through various sort of analytical models which then makes sense of those things and and ends up with calls to action, decisions which a club can then make which are smarter than the decision they might have made if they hadn't been able to use that evidence in that way.

What does this mean? What this means is is that prior to um the the availability of electronic data about players in lots of different parts of the world for Brighton to start recruiting players from Ecuador, they would need to have a massive global scouting operation, right? Which again is very expensive and where they will get disintermediated by the very biggest clubs who have the ability to employ more scouts effectively. Okay? and they would then have to sort of use work out from that scouting operation. Okay, we we found this really interesting player in Ecuador. But how does he compare to another player we found in Cameroon? I have no idea.

Now what's happened for instance is the use of data is that the ability to compare different leagues around the world has very significantly improved. So in other words, all the players who get transferred out of all the various different leagues and what their subsequent performances go on to demonstrate gets fed into the mix and it gets spat out that there's a sort of ranking list of leagues. So you can say, okay, if a player is performing to this level in the Ecuadorian top division at this age, that roughly equates to over here. Okay.

What that then means is once that player has been highlighted in Ecuador, Brighton can then put a scout on a plane to go to Ecuador to watch Kaisedo play, right? And then the whole thing can work from there. So it's it's cut out an area which previously would have been unbelievably expensive and difficult for a medium-sized club to compete against a larger club and enabled them a smarter, cheaper, better way to access this talent from around the world.

FIFA 全球发展、签证体系与"未成熟天才"市场

Now the next thing is is that the various different geographies of the rest of the world have become much more accessible for all sorts of different reasons but chiefly of which is that the football development patterns in these other countries have improved. So whereas previously quite often even if there was significant quote talent in a particular country by the time that a player of that talent had reached the age of 181 19 they were so far behind technically

that actually getting them to translate from there into the English top division or the French top division or the German top division was just just a really really big jump. They pretty much had to be a genius right to be able to bridge that gap in technical and physical preparation.

FIFA over the last 20 and 30 years has been investing large amounts of money in all of these geographies. This is how FIFA presidents get reelected. What mostly happens is they put a lot of money into development systems and facilities in all of these parts of the world. And I've known this through working the Caribbean, right? FIFA is plowing a lot of money into football facilities in the Caribbean to enable young Caribbean talent to be competitive, more competitive, so they don't get left too far behind. at the age of 17 18 they've still got a chance of making on the global stage.

So you've got a higher underlying level of football development. Then digitally you've got the ability to assess at an early stage where that talent is and then through a much more developed understanding of how the visa systems work through the various different geographies. You've now got people at these football clubs working out how are we actually going to move that player within the existing visa rules from here to here to here to here.

为什么豪门做不到？大俱乐部的结构性困境

So why why aren't all the top clubs just

you've already answered it — necessity is the mother of invention right which is that once you know that you cannot sign a fully matured super elite talent once you know that then you come to well we're going to have to find one who is which the market has not yet worked out is that but we have we think he will be in two or three years time and therefore we're going to get two advantages from this signing we believe one of which is we're going to get the benefit of his playing services for the next year or two and then at the end of it one of these very biggest clubs is going to buy him fully formed off us there. So interesting.

So if you look at Chelsea just take your club as an example of why I think it's it's easier said than done for the very biggest clubs to do this right which is that what you need is for that player who is being signed as a development prospect to then be given consistent game time because the only way in which they are realistically going to end up being what they need to be is if they play.

The problem with the very biggest clubs is that the expectation is on a week-to-eek basis is they compete at the very top of their leagues. And this produces a sort of unfortunate vortex because it basically means that you need readymade finished products to come in — that's very expensive on the one hand — makes it very difficult to make a profit out of them on the other hand because they're already fully priced. So unless they do something really spectacular with you then probably going to be worth less rather than more at the end of their contract cuz they're going to be older.

These midsize clubs are sourcing these players aged 19, 20, 21, 22. They're then getting a couple of their best years out of them. They're then selling them at the peak of their powers for the massive amounts of money to the very biggest clubs who then get maybe the last couple of years of their peak years out of them before they start to set off and they get sold at a at a loss.

So it it's quite a big call I think for one of the very biggest clubs to say right even though we can afford to buy these fully formed players actually it's bad practice we don't want to do it

right so what we what we're going to do instead is we're going to bite the bullet of explaining to our fan base that even though we could afford to buy 60 million pound players we're actually going to start buying a whole bunch of 15 million pound players. Yeah that's not good. Well, it's kind of what Chelsea's done, right? And I I know they're not all 15 million. They're more like 20, 30 million and Well, yeah, but they do drip feed it in with the 100 million and Fernando Sky, which I'm saying as a fan like pates you. So,

球迷安抚（Placation）：中型球队的隐形特权

right, this is the point. That plecation is the point, right? Which is all the clubs we're looking at here have no need of placation because their fans are not expecting to win the league. Their fans are happy to see their clubs just be competitive. And even if they have a season where they're not that competitive, oh well, we're not that big a club, so doesn't matter. That takes the pressure off the executives and the board, which means they can pursue a medium-term strategy without constantly having to go out of the stadium with the coats over their heads trying to make sure they don't get, isn't it?

If we look at the Premier League, the six then that you have at the top, they're all playing the same game. It's highly likely that a couple of them may screw that up. That leaves an opportunity for the clubs operating like Brighton, Brenford, Bournemouth to come up and compete at a high level with a really successful execution.

I mean, I was looking at Bodo Glimps. They've averaged kind of a transfer budget about €6 million. The squad value total is 53.4 million euros or has been now they'll go no the Champions League uh run has done an amazing job for them. Some of those players will go for a significantly more than that. But that squad value alone — I just had a look on transfer market — there are 122 individual players in European football valued higher than 53.4 million euros, and that's their total squad value playing in a Champions League last 16.

Identity over Individuals：球队是拼图，不是糖果店

So this so this speaks to another point you mentioned right at the start right which we need to come back to. Right. As we we mentioned data analytics and the way in which it's provided — obviously it's I'm not saying it's easy the development of these systems to do it at the very highest level which is what Brighton and Brenford owners have done is extremely difficult otherwise everyone would do it right so just just to make that clear and there are other clubs within this group in Europe who are also

quite far forward in in the use of those systems but the next bit which is actually a more common theme without this group is the extent to which football is a team game and I think this is the bit which is often underestimated ated by fans and by new owners.

Okay? So, if you think about Chelsea's new owners strategy of garing 30, 40, 50, 60 top young players and hoping that somehow if you throw all these ingredients into the mix that a quality team will emerge from out of that mix. What you're looking at a lot of these other clubs is realistic playing squads which are more like 20 21 players in total who have got any chance of playing and where every single spot in that squad is very carefully chosen for what which bit of the jigsaw is this player solving for.

This is why I turn identity over individual. Yeah. Yeah. And that's super important.

教练连续性：5–10% 的关键边际

Um now the consistency of the coaching position is very important to that because what you're doing is every transfer window you're working out which little bit of the jigsaw isn't quite right. you're removing that piece and replacing it with one piece or two pieces. It's small tweaks, small changes.

And then this is another thing which I think that midsize clubs find it easier to maintain a consistent coaching setup cuz again the pressure on instant success is lower. So you don't get those sackings which happen almost not by mistake but happen almost as a sacrificial lamb to pate the fan base because we've been through a bad patch. are the midsize clubs. You're more able to say, "Hey, yeah, we've been through a bit of a tough patch, but I think we're going to come through it. Hey, everyone, it's fine. We've got this type thing." And again, you're not having to grow a fake mustache and try and flee the country if you say that. You can still walk around the town in one piece.

As you know, my view on the difference that an individual head coach or manager makes, it's five or 10% here or there, but by the way, 5 or 10% is quite a big difference because it's often probably that final five or 10%.

Absolutely it is.

My argument is not that five or 10% is a small number. I think it's quite a big number. It's just not 50% which is what a lot of fans think, right? A lot of fans think our players basically are really good. If we chuck out the current clown in the dugout and replace them with a genius, then everything will be fine. I don't think that really works.

核心结论：钱与成功的关系被严重高估

But yeah, so just looking through these these different clubs and the the current sort of levels of achievement. I don't think it's as simple as saying if you are the owner or on the board of a midsize club, we're going to do the same as TUN. We want to have a budget of 4.5 million. there will be all sorts of particularities to their particular situation and the Swiss League as a whole and the immigration system operating within Switzerland.

But I think what is interesting is one conclusion that can be drawn is that the amount of money being spent as a cause of success is significantly overestimated. I think we can say that right. I think the level of success across this number of leagues with massive differences compared to what you would expect if it was causation rather than correlation. There's clearly correlation but not causation I think because if you look at for instance Bournemouth Brighton Brenford Sunland what would you say their wage budgets are?

I think it's like between 40 and 60 isn't it? 70 about 70 million. What is Spurs playing budget? Uh we don't 240 160 and and they're accused of under spending. Yeah, they're accused of unspending by only spending 160.

"糖果店"与"糖果工厂"的隐喻

So if you were to say to a Spurs fan, do you know what your problem actually is? You're spending too much because effectively even though you're comparing your your spending levels against another club who's spending 180 and you're going, we should be spending an extra 20 million. Actually, the issue here is is that your culture is not to be smart. Your culture is simply say, we got a lot of money to spend. We'll go out in the sweet shop.

all the other big boys will be in the sweet shop and we'll try and get the same sweeties as them but we'll spend a little bit less than them so we'll get slightly less good sweeties whereas these other clubs are saying we can't afford to go in the sweet shop we have to go and find the sweet factory we have to go and find these sweets before they get in the sweet shop so if you're a club like Spurs and you're caught in between neither able to sign the really marquee player who is absolutely proven and who absolutely is going to deliver nor thinking smartly about well yeah but how are we going find the the one that hasn't been discovered yet. They end up somewhere in between ending up signing very expensive players who are actually probably no more proven than some of these other

that is what Chelsea have been doing. That's not me being critical. That's that is exactly where they've been, right? They've been signing these players for 100 — I mean Enzo Fernandez played six months at Benfica before joining Chelsea and he had a great World Cup. That's not proven, right? He's had a good period of a short amount of time.

总结与对听众的开放邀请

So that's so so really what you're saying is if we go back to that necessity mother invention through the way that football has developed some of these smaller clubs have been forced to think differently the way that they've been able to identify value is looking at a lot of the playing side of the game by identifying where the top players can come from that has helped them massively on the pitch shock horror right still about playing but what it's been able to do is create a really exciting group of players across all of these different squads capable of competing with the top ones who may lack some of those core things that we've talked about, whether it's the cultural fit to the organization, they're just a great brand or great global name, those things are actually not as important as it is having 21 to 26 players in there specifically put together for a reason.

Very nicely put, Charlie. So, Mr. Kramer, thank you. Mr. Kramer, thank you for that. And actually, this is the type of topic which I'd be really interested in readers and listeners views on it because look, we don't have a monopoly on wisdom. A lot of what we're saying there is obser observations based on some facts with some presumptions and some assumptions that are made alongside that. But there will be individual instances within these clubs which we are not necessarily aware of.

Now I'm not aware of what Laskin Austria's particular strategies been when they winning the domestic double. You know I am somewhat aware of what's happening in Ko. There'll be others who are more more aware than me. So any thoughts and details that people have out there? I'd be really interested to hear on that on those topics.

Absolutely. And um you know, thank you for for putting that in. I also like the fact that it's kind of a positive take. I think it's very easy to get caught up in the clubs that are doing things wrong or or getting all negative. So looking at these clubs that are doing great things is um is quite refreshing.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一、最反直觉的发现：花得多反而是一种"诅咒"
整段讨论里最颠覆的不是"小球队聪明"，而是节目最后那句几乎被一笔带过的话——**"Spurs 真正的问题不是花得太少，而是花得太多。"** 这是一个被主流叙事彻底掩盖的悖论：在足球转会市场，预算超过某条临界线之后，钱本身会反过来塑造一种**懒惰的认知结构**。当你能买到"已验证"的球员时，你的球探部门、数据团队、青训接口都会逐渐萎缩，因为它们的边际价值看起来不如直接掏钱。结果就是——大俱乐部不是"钱不够聪明"，而是**钱让它们失去了变聪明的必要性**。Brighton、Brentford、Bodø/Glimt 之所以厉害，不是因为他们更聪明，而是因为他们**没有退路去不聪明**。
这其实和经济学里的"资源诅咒"（Resource Curse）是同构的：石油国家往往制造业孱弱，因为不需要工业也能富裕。足球的"转会预算诅咒"是同一个机制在另一个领域的镜像。
## 二、跨领域连接：这不是足球故事，是一个"中等规模物种"的进化生态学
你仔细看这些俱乐部的共同特征——**预算 4M 到 70M 之间的"中型球队"**正在全欧洲集体崛起—

item.title: [Bits + Bips] Kraken's Co-CEO on Building a Bank While Washington Burns
item.dc:creator: Bits + Bips
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、被忽视的悖论：Kraken 正在用"反加密"的方式赢得加密

最反直觉的观察是：Arjun 描述的 Payward 战略本质上是用 TradFi 的方式打败 TradFi——堆叠牌照、并购、银行牌照、清算所、FCM/DCM/DCO 三件套。但加密的原教旨叙事一直是"消灭中间人"。这里隐藏着一个深层悖论：真正能扩张到全球的"去中心化"基础设施，恰恰需要一个高度中心化、垂直整合的实体在每一个司法管辖区做"中心化的合规中介"。

换句话说，Payward 不是在做加密公司，而是在做"监管套利的索引基金"——把全球碎片化的监管缝隙打包成一个统一的产品栈。这跟 Pope Leo 在节目后半段呼吁的"反集中"形成了一个令人不安的镜像：去中心化的协议层之上，正在长出一个新的、远比传统银行更巨型的"超国家金融寡头"。

二、跨领域连接：从"Bund Future 时刻"到"教会—印刷术"的同构

Chris 用 Eurex 抢走 Bund Future 的故事来类比 Hyperliquid 对 TradFi 的威胁，这是一个非常精准的市场结构隐喻。但更深一层的同构在节目里其实被擦肩而过——Chris 自己提到的"教会与古登堡印刷术"的关系。

这两个故事讲的是同一个模式： - 印刷术先被教会拥抱（"我们能传播教义了"），再被新教徒"分叉"使用，最终瓦解了垄断； - Eurex 用电子化"分叉"了 LIFFE 的流动性，再也没还回去； - 今天 Citadel/JPM/DTCC 正处于"教会拥抱印刷术"的阶段——他们想用 tokenization 强化自己的分销，但他们不知道哪一个 Hyperliquid 会是新教徒。

令人意外的预测：真正的"Bund Future 时刻"不会发生在股票 perps 上，而会发生在信贷市场——尤其是新兴市场的中小企业信贷。因为信贷市场的流动性形成最依赖"信息不对称"，而链上原生身份 + AI 风控会一次性把这个护城河抽空。Reap 三倍营收增长真正预示的不是支付革命，是信贷分销权的地缘转移。

三、最反直觉的洞察：Trump Token 才是真正让华尔街崩溃的事件

Chris 顺口提到一个被严重低估的观察："让机构真正吓尿的不是 Hyperliquid，是 Trump Token 在周六凌晨 2 点从 0 到 150 亿市值。"

请把这个事件放回资本形成史的坐标系： - 1602 年荷兰东印度公司用了几个月组织 IPO； - 2004 年 Google 用了几周做荷兰式拍卖； - 2021 年 Coinbase 用了几天完成直接上市； - 2025 年 Trump Token 用了几小时完成了一次价值发现。

时间维度的指数级压缩，意味着资本形成的"门槛函数"正在崩溃。这背后的隐藏真相是：Sarbanes-Oxley 和 IPO 银行家的整套基础设施，其实在补偿一个不再存在的瓶颈——信息传播速度。当全球分销 + 即时清算 + 永续流动性都成立后，"承销"这个职业类别本身会像"报纸排字工"一样被结构性消灭。

这就解释了为什么 Pope Leo 的演讲值得被加密圈认真对待——他无意中触碰到了一个深层议题：当资本形成的物理摩擦消失后，剩下的唯一摩擦就是道德和制度摩擦。Catholic Social Teaching 中的 subsidiarity 原则（Arjun 反复念错的那个词）将不再是哲学装饰，而会成为下一轮 AI + 加密监管的实际语法。谁先把这套语法写进协议层（而不是写进 ToS），谁就拿到下一个十年的合法性溢价。
