## 一个被忽视的镜像：围棋规则即"对齐问题"的微缩模型
最让我意外的是——这段看似在教 Dwarkesh 下围棋的对话，实际上是一场关于 **AI 对齐（alignment）** 的隐喻演练，只是说话人自己可能没意识到。
注意那个关键时刻：人类围棋用"两人都觉得游戏结束了"来判定胜负——这是一种**共识机制下的价值函数对齐**。而 Tromp-Taylor 规则却把这种模糊性砍掉，用算法可判定的方式重新定义"领地"，结果是：**白棋在人类眼中明明已死，在计算机眼中却还能拿三目**。
这不是规则差异，这是两种本体论的冲突——人类用"未来可能性的塌缩"来评估当下，机器用"当下可枚举的状态"来评估当下。同一块棋盘，两种真理。这恰好是 LLM evaluation 里所有争论的本质：基准分数 vs. 实际能力。
## 跨领域连接：围棋规则演化 ≈ 法律体系的「形式化 vs. 普通法」之争
人类围棋规则像**普通法（common law）**——依赖参与者的共同直觉、惯例和事后判例（"我觉得结束了，你也觉得结束了"）。 Tromp-Taylor 规则像**大陆法系/形式化代码**——

item.title: [Latent Space] ⚡️ Google's Open AI Strategy — Omar Sanseviero, Google DeepMind
item.dc:creator: Latent Space
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一个反常识的观察：Gemma 4 不是"更小的模型"，而是"重新发明了内存层级"

大多数人把 Gemma 4 的"effective parameters"理解为一种压缩技巧，但这其实是一次架构级的认知反叛。传统 Transformer 把所有参数视为同等公民，必须全部装进 GPU 的"短期记忆"。而 Gemma 4 的 per-layer embedding 本质上是把模型的一部分"知识"重新归类为可查表的、冷的、可外置的——这与人脑的工作方式惊人一致：你不会把童年记忆放在工作记忆里随时调用，但需要时能瞬时检索。

更耐人寻味的是：Omar 暗示这个技巧"不能 scale"到大模型。为什么？因为当智能足够稠密时，你无法再把知识从计算中干净地剥离出来——这反过来揭示了一个深刻的命题：智能与知识的可分离性，本身就是模型规模的一个相变指标。

跨领域连接：Reddit 上的 Frankenmerges 与 1970 年代的家酿计算机俱乐部

Omar 顺口提到一个被严重低估的现象：2023-2024 年 Reddit/Discord 上的业余模型合并者，比研究实验室提前 1-2 年发现了某些技术，而后来的论文"重新发明"了这些技巧却没人 cite。

这与 1970 年代 Homebrew Computer Club 的剧情完全同构——当时 Woz 和那群业余爱好者在车库里搞出的东西，几年后被 IBM 和 DEC"正式发现"。两者的共同结构是：当工具的边际成本降到俱乐部级别时，创新的源头会从机构转移到社区，但学术 / 工业的"合法性管道"还没建好引用通路。

这意味着：现在 GitHub 上某个不知名的 14 岁少年在 SAE 上做的实验，可能是 2027 年某篇 NeurIPS oral 的真正源头。论文引用网络作为知识产权追溯系统，已经系统性失灵。

一个被遮蔽的战略真相：Google 的开源策略不是"防御"，而是"操作系统级渗透"

表面上看，Gemma 是 Google 对 Llama 的回应。但仔细听 Omar 的话：Gemini Nano 建立在 Gemma 之上，预装在 Pixel 和高端 Samsung 里，Android Studio 集成 Gemma 4 做离线编码，Med Gemma 渗透医疗，140 种语言的 tokenizer 让 Gemma 在小语种 fine-tune 上结构性优于所有竞品。

这不是开源策略，这是重演 Android 对诺基亚的战役：当所有人都在比拼 flagship 模型时，Google 在悄悄让 Gemma 成为"on-device AI 的 Linux"。一旦每部手机、每个 app、每个开发者环境的离线智能默认都跑在 Gemma 上，所谓"模型层的护城河"就变成了生态层的引力井。

最反直觉的一点：Omar 说"我不认为 Gemma 会蚕食 Gemini"。但从系统论看，这恰恰是最危险的自我安慰——因为当 on-device 模型在 1-2 年后达到"今日 Gemini Pro"级别时，蚕食不会发生在产品层，而会发生在用户对"AI 该在哪里运行"的心理默认值上。一旦这个默认值翻转，云端模型的商业模式就要被迫重写。

一个隐藏的产品哲学冲突：Apple 的 LoRA 多任务 vs Google 的参数卸载

Omar 提到一个工程师才会注意的细节：Apple 的 on-device 策略是"一个基模型 + 多个 LoRA"，而 Google 选择了"参数卸载到 CPU/磁盘"。这看起来是技术选择，实际上是两种世界观的对撞：

  • Apple 的 LoRA 模式 = 应用是主体，模型是配件（每个 app 带自己的人格）
  • Google 的卸载模式 = 模型是主体，硬件是临时容器（模型自由跨越内存边界）

Omar 自己也承认 LoRA 多任务面临"基模型升级时所有 LoRA 全要重训"的协调灾难——这其实是康威定律在 AI 时代的复现：你的模型架构会反映你公司的组织结构。Apple 是 app-centric 的封闭花园，所以走 LoRA；Google 是 model-centric 的研究院文化，所以走卸载。

预测：未来 3 年最大的产品战争不在模型质量上，而在"谁能解决 on-device 模型的版本协调问题"——这是一个被严重低估的、纯产品工程的问题，但它将决定下一代 AI 应用的形态。

📝 转录文本

开场

[music]

主持人：We got so much — Gemma 4, Gemma 3.1, Gemma scope, Med Gemma. Give us the TLDR.

Omar：Yeah, so yeah, Gemma 4 is just out. This is the most capable open model we've released so far. We really tried to compact as much intelligence per parameter as we could. Bring all of these multimodal capabilities. So yeah, that's Gemma 4.

Effective Parameters 架构解析

主持人：So one interesting thing, you have this thing with effective parameters, not active parameters. Can you explain what it is?

Omar：Yeah, so pretty much in the traditional transformer architecture you have like this big embedding layer, right? And this new architecture is more of a small change in the transformer architecture, in the transformer block. Pretty much we add a per-layer embedding. So at every layer we add an embedding table. What is exciting is that you don't need to do like the full matrix multiplication. This is pretty much a lookup table.

So the Gemma 4 model is an E2B. That means that it effectively has 2 billion parameters loaded into the GPU. It actually has almost 5 billion parameters, but those 3 billion parameters can be in the CPU, they can be in the disk, which means that you can do inference extremely quickly. This is just a lookup table.

主持人：And what's the con? Why don't we always do this? Can it scale? Is it open research? Like, you know, it seems very… Okay, if I can just offload half the parameters to CPUs.

Omar：Yeah, so pretty much here we did lots of quality experimentation and this is really optimized and designed for on-device. And when I say on-device I mean like running in a phone, Android, Raspberry Pi, and so on, right? When you go larger you usually want to compact more — you want to have more like dense architectures or MoEs. So these research decisions were very helpful for these small use cases.

中国 Super Apps 与 On-device 模型分发

主持人：Yeah, something I learned from the run that you organized this morning. For our listeners, I think it's the first ever like official run club at AIE — 6:30 a.m. Very rough, but at least I woke up for it. I met Cormac and he was telling me that apparently in China the super apps are shipping models in the app bundle for inference and just use among all their super app assistants. And I don't know, is that like a target use case for you guys?

Omar：Yeah, so actually if you install — if you buy a Pixel phone or a high-end Samsung, they come with a Gemini Nano. And Gemini Nano is baked into the operating system, and Gemini Nano is really built on top of Gemma. So last year we released Gemma 3N, which was this architecture really designed for phone use cases. And they use a Gemma 3N with some additional training, some additional adaptations to make the model good for traditional on-device use cases, right? So pretty much when you buy these high-end phones, you can already use a Gemini out of the box.

主持人：Yeah, we actually covered the 3N paper in our paper club, and this idea of parameter offloading or download-on-demand is very cool. Is it exactly the same in the Gemma 4 stuff?

Omar：Yep.

主持人：Okay. For the smaller models.

Omar：Yeah, yeah, yeah.

主持人：And does it scale? Is there a potential — so for reference, Gemma 4 is a 29B and a 31B; only one's dense — but have you scaled it? Have you pushed it up?

Omar：We are doing lots of experiments. Stay tuned.

发布一个主线模型背后的工作

主持人：What goes into shipping a mainline model like this? What's the behind the scenes?

Omar：It's complex. The Gemma team is actually relatively small. We have two or three PMs, we have one marketing person, and then there is our engineers and researchers working on shipping this. Of course there's the full training part — how do we do the post-training, distillation, post-training techniques, and so on.

What is quite exciting is that once we have the model, then we collaborate with a bunch of open source partners, right? So for example, we work with llama.cpp, Ollama, MLX, Hugging Face, vLLM, Nvidia, AMD. So we have almost 50 external partners for the Gemma 4 launch, which has been the most complex launch.

And also internally, we collaborate with a bunch of different teams. So think of Google Cloud, Vertex, Vertex models-as-a-service, ADK, and then Android as well, right? So we work, for example, with Android team. And with the launch of Gemma 4, we released an integration with Android Studio. So in Android Studio, there is this agent mode where you can have a model helping you write code and do things within Android Studio. And they ship this integration with offline models using llama.cpp or vLLM or any OpenAI-compatible endpoint. So now you can use Gemma 4 to also write Android applications in Android Studio.

何时用 Gemma vs Gemini

主持人：What's the difference? When would someone want to do that versus just using Gemini? Outside of, of course, the obvious — you're offline or you want the privacy.

Omar：Planes a lot or something. I did. Okay, I will say, on my long 10-hour flight to London, I did use Gemini as…

主持人：Yeah, I was on Gemma 4 though. Sorry, Gemma, Gemma.

Omar：Yeah, yeah, it's mostly offline use cases. Or if you — yeah — offline or privacy. Like if you want to have all of your development set up locally and you don't want to send any code to any API, you would use that.

主持人：Do you see a future where, you know, small models get good enough? Like, does it cannibalize? It's an interesting position. You have big Gemini, you have Gemma, both get exponentially better over time. Current Gemma is much better than what we had closed-source a few years ago, right?

Omar：Yeah, for me, it's quite exciting. I mean, if you look at Gemma — compare to how we were 1 year ago — I would say Gemma 4 is matching state-of-the-art from 1, 1.5 years ago for most things. With local models or models that you can run in your own hardware, you can get capabilities — so you can get agentic capabilities, function calling, system instructions, conversational, and that kind of stuff.

Knowledge is much trickier. So for knowledge, you do need a larger model, right? That's why if you compare Gemini to Gemma, Gemini has much better knowledge of the world — facts, information, and so on. So it really depends. I do think we are heading towards a future in 1 to 2 years where — imagine you can run a Gemini 3 Pro powerful model directly in your phone, right? And I think once we get there, things will be quite exciting.

From a product integration, from which experiences we can enable to users. I wouldn't say it cannibalizes — still two very different things. If you want flagship capabilities, this super complex, long-running task, you would do with Gemini if you need factuality and so on. But I do think for many of these agentic things, we'll get to a point in which we can do very powerful things directly on device.

多模态能力

主持人：Can we talk about the multimodality side? Any advances there that you want to highlight or you've been getting good feedback on?

Omar：Yeah, so Gemma 4 was built on the same research as Gemini 3, which pretty much means that we benefited from all the improvements that happened with Gemini 3. Multimodal-wise, the smaller models can understand audio, images, and short videos — so 30- to 60-second videos and audios. For us, actually quite long. And even the on-device, like the 2B, 4B, can do very good multimodal.

For audio, we have speech recognition, we have speech-to-translated text, and then a bit of speech understanding. So you can ask questions about an audio file and so on. Use cases that are very optimized for on-device phone use cases.

And then on the vision side, we also improved things quite a bit. So we have object detection, pointing, captioning. We do not have image segmentation, which we know is one thing that many people have been asking us. But otherwise, for many things, we do support that.

The other thing we do not support yet is video with audio. So we can understand video input or audio input separately, but if you want to pass in the same prompt both a visual part and the audio part, we still need to do some improvements around that.

主持人：And that's just a matter of more data?

Omar：Probably some additional fine-tuning could yield some very good baseline models for this.

主持人：What about audio out?

Omar：We are exploring some things here. Nothing I can share at the moment.

主持人：[laughter] I think everyone's excited about — like, when do you have native speech-to-speech, right?

Omar：Yeah. But as far as I see, people always get excited and then the pipelines always win. [laughter]

Tokenizer 与多语言

Omar：Gemma is quite important for the multilingual aspect as well. So Gemma supports 140 languages. You did a lot of work on the multilingual — on the tokenizer. So the tokenizer has been pretty much based on the Gemini tokenizer. It's extremely good. So independently of the Gemma capabilities, if you just pick a base Gemma model and you fine-tune for an additional language, it actually works extremely well.

主持人：What are some — so I didn't read that part — what are some insights on the tokenization?

Omar：This comes from Gemma 3 — this has been done already for over a year — but the tokenizer is pretty much the same as Gemini, which means that the tokenizer lends itself to capture the right tokens for different languages. It's a very good multilingual tokenizer. Which means that if you compare Gemma 3 — so I'm going to the previous generation — if you compare Gemma 3 to other models from back then, maybe the other models were better than Gemma 3 as a general model, but if you train all of these models for, I don't know, a specific Southeast Asian language — Vietnamese, let's say — Gemma would yield better results, even if the other base models were potentially better.

主持人：Yeah. I mean, I think there's some limit at which you basically have tonic representation, right? Like you understand the core concept and it translates to whatever language you want.

Omar：Yes. [laughter]

把研究员带到 AI Engineer 大会

主持人：I guess, you know, you also have purview over all of Google developer experience. And you brought the team here for the first time. What was that like?

Omar：It's quite exciting, to be honest. We have already participated in previous AI Engineer conferences — Philip or other team members have been in some of these in the past. This is London. This is DeepMind's home. You have to.

We brought a bunch of researchers from the team to share about different things that we're working on. We brought other teams from Google, not just from DeepMind, that are also using AI in one way or another. So we brought people that are doing on-device machine learning, people that are doing optimizations to run models directly in phones or in the browser. We brought people from the Android team. We brought people that are working all over Google from robotics to research to Android. So yeah, it's quite exciting to come here and really show all of the things that the company's building — not just come and share the things that our team is doing, but really all of the overarching AI story.

主持人：I think you are — I mean, it is the lab with the biggest scope. Like you do everything including dolphins. And it's very impressive. So you brought Sander. We talk a little bit about the researchers that you brought.

Omar：We brought researchers in a couple of different topics. So we brought one of the researchers that worked in the development of Gemma 4. We brought a researcher that works in diffusion models as well — diffusion transformer models — or diffusion as text generation, not image generation.

Diffusion Text 模型

主持人：Which was announced but not released.

Omar：Exactly. We did the Gemini Diffusion last year at I/O, which is very cool because you can generate code extremely quickly, right? Like, it's stupid.

主持人：So the main pitch is speed, but other than speed, is there a secondary — you know, what can we do with a diffusion model that we cannot do with autoregressive?

Omar：It's mostly speed.

主持人：Okay. Yeah. I feel like in terms of code structure there may be some things where you're like, "Okay, I want the brackets here." And then you fill in the blanks, right? So fill-in-the-middle is a common code problem, but this is extended fill-in-the-middle. Or like extended — "oh, help me upscale or put a LoRA, translate the image analogy to text."

Omar：I think in the past fill-in-the-middle was this task that many companies were trying to tackle as an additional generation task. And now people are just assuming that the model can do fill-in-the-middle with a general — exactly.

主持人：No tricks about special tokenization or anything like that.

Omar：Exactly. It used to be a masked language model that you trained to predict fill-in-the-middle. You had to rearrange your data set as well in order to do FIM. It was a bit tricky. People were always getting the prompt and the tokens wrong. If you deviated in any way from the training format, it didn't yield good results. Now we have very good out-of-the-box capabilities for that.

主持人：What's the idea about investing in text diffusion? Is there a world in which this overtakes autoregressive?

Omar：Yeah, that's a good question. I think at the moment it's still very experimental. I think we'll really be seeing us sharing a bit more research of the things that we have been doing around diffusion text generation models in that space. I would say it's still very early stage. Especially the model quality is still a bit worse from what you would get from a normal autoregressive model.

主持人：Yeah, a lot of what you were mentioning earlier — for fill in this code block, this stuff — it seems different how we're building agents these days of sequential tool calling, this, that. I guess if it's just speed, it's speed. But I could see a world where there's like system one, system two. System one is the diffusion one, system two is autoregressive. System one is the planner, system two is the executor. I don't know.

Omar：Could be. Maybe it's too hypothetical at this point, right? Diffusion transformer models are difficult to fine-tune as well. So there's also a point in which — how much flexibility?

主持人：Yeah, I could see a world in which you just have a very strong agent manager kind of setup, and then you have executors like diffusion-based executors that do specific coding.

微调生态的转变

主持人：Are people fine-tuning outside of — you know, we see a few big companies do okay, like Cursor has a really good consistent — or there's a few that have done fine-tuning, but it seems like it's not picking up as you know…

Omar：Yeah, so there was this period in 2024 — I think, maybe 2023 — where there were all of these fine-tuning communities. And I think it's been changing quite a bit over the last two years because models are getting very good out of the box. So as I was saying, for Gemma 4 we had 50 to 60 partners, and some of them were like, "Oh yeah, we're going to try and fine-tune the 27B model for this vision task," and then they were like, "Oh actually, the model works too well out of the box, we don't need to fine-tune it."

Yeah, we saw lots of those things. So I'm seeing less excitement around fine-tuning nowadays as general conversational models. There is still quite a bit of excitement around fine-tuning for specific domains like finance, healthcare, specific types of data that the model didn't see. But as general conversational — like just changing how the model behaves — you can do most of that via prompting nowadays. And in terms of capabilities, the models are very good out of the box.

So it's been changing quite a bit. There's still the Unsloth people — I don't know if you know Daniel Khan and Astro Michael.

主持人：Workshop to just talk about…

Omar：Yeah, they are the GOATs. They still do amazing tools for the community to fine-tune and the community uses those tools, but I'm seeing some changes in the trends. I think people are not fine-tuning that much anymore.

Med Gemma 与多 LoRA 设备困境

主持人：And you guys put out a version of your own Med Gemma. Is that a fine-tune of…?

Omar：Yeah, so Med Gemma — the last Med Gemma, which we released three months ago, Med Gemma 1.5 — it's based on Gemma 3. So it's pretty much Gemma 3 and then additional training with some of our medical data sets.

主持人：How do you see — if I'm not mistaken, Apple Foundation models on-device were a bunch of LoRAs for different tasks. And when you're constrained or running on-device small efficient models, you guys did offload, so you're caring about efficiency. But do you see a world of multi-LoRAs for tasks? Should people be fine-tuning the small one?

Omar：I think this is a big challenge in general in the whole developer ecosystem. Because let's say that you want to have 20 apps in your phone, right? And let's say that each of those apps comes with its own LoRA, right? What happens when you update the base model? You also need to update all of these LoRAs.

So from a developer point of view, I think it will be very tricky. Because you don't want to have 20 different base models in the phone of the users — the battery will just die. You also don't want to have to update 20 LoRAs every time you update the base model, right? So the release cycles in the Android world and in the iOS world are very different. So yeah, I think it's more of a general industry challenge that we need to figure out — how do we think that people should build on-device phone-powered experiences? It's more of a product and developer experience kind of challenge.

Dense vs MoE 的取舍

主持人：I have a question about the bigger Gemma models. So you have two models that are pretty similar size — one is dense, one is an MoE. Can you talk a bit about — okay, say you have a 27B you're putting out, how do you think about: should I build an MoE outside of inference and using it? How do you think about when to do MoE versus dense? What are the trade-offs? Like, what else is there besides inference? But then there's two at the same size — no, yeah, but I mean one is 31B which is dense, right? And that's the most raw intelligence. And then you have the 27B with 4 billion activated parameters. But why not a 31B 5B active, for example?

Omar：Yeah, I mean, you can just fit more in dense. We did quite a bit of experimentation and research as to which would be the best sizes that would be friendly to developers, and we made decisions along that, right? The 31B is really the largest model size that quantized would fit in a consumer GPU. The 27B is more like an extremely fast inference within those constraints.

MoEs are challenging to fine-tune. I don't know if we've talked about that in the past, but MoEs in general are an extremely good AI architecture. They work great for inference, but when people fine-tune them, they struggle a bit. They are not as easy to fine-tune for instruction following. The standard recipes and hyperparameters that you have may not work out of the box for MoEs.

主持人：The intuition is the routing kills the backprop, or…?

Omar：I think so. I don't have a very strong intuition on it either, to be honest. People always say this, but I'm trying to say why, right? Because if you can train it, you can fine-tune it. Fine-tuning is just training.

I think it's a mix of the routing and just having different distributions. The distribution may affect the routing in a different way than a dense model where you just change the things. That's kind of my intuition, but also I think there are many different variables here — like how many experts do you trigger, etc. There are a bunch of different parameters that you can move. Whether you freeze or you don't freeze the router, a bunch of things that you need to think about.

主持人：To me, the most important asymptotes that I'm looking for are: what is the minimum sparsity level that we can reach, and then what is the most — let's call it — ELO per byte.

Omar：Yeah, that's the thing. I mean, plays quite a bit. What's the intelligence per parameter, right? How do we maximize this intelligence per parameter? Because then we can stop, right? [laughter] Because if you compare — I mean Gemma — we have done the same size, right? Almost 30 billion parameters for Gemma 2, 3, and 4. And the intelligence is much higher, right? We have not increased the model size.

主持人：It was an easier number when everything was dense. Then you have to add in sparsity. Now you have offloading.

Omar：Yeah. So you cannot compare MoE students models. There are some napkin calculations you can do to compare, but it's not apples to apples.

That's a good question — like, I don't know where we'll be in 3 years from now. I would assume a 30B parameter model could be extremely powerful. I still think there are limitations in terms of knowledge. So maybe the model will be able to do super wild gigantic stuff, but it will not know who was the president of X country 25 — I mean, maybe yes, but very niche knowledge probably the models will not have.

主持人：This is just information theory, right? You're using the model as a database. So of course there's going to be limits. The other thing is also — I always think about when we talk about this topic — superposition, right? Anthropic has this concept of superposition where you can store information in the smaller weights because it compounds with the other weights as well. And so not that much research on it since then, but maybe this is my segue into Mech Interp.

GemmaScope 与可解释性

主持人：GemmaScope.

Omar：[laughter] Yeah, so last year in December we released GemmaScope. So GemmaScope pretty much allows you to analyze the activations across different layers based on the tokens in those layers. And yeah, the team released — I don't know if it was a couple of terabytes, maybe even up to one petabyte of data that we had to store — because we did that for every single layer across all of the Gemma 3 models. So it's very complete.

主持人：And Llama as well?

Omar：We did it just for Gemma 3.

主持人：Oh, okay. I think Neuronpedia had some others.

Omar：Could be, could be. There's a little teams — I was like, wow, that was a very, very cool cross-lab partnership. There are a couple of open source tools as well that you can just create your own activation networks.

It's a niche field. I think it's a good opportunity. I think we were talking about this earlier, right? It's an area where you don't need lots of compute to get started. That allows you to understand how a model works. You can experiment. You can get a sense of how all transformer architectures work. So it's a good area.

为什么把研究员带到工程师大会

主持人：Okay. The context of this is really, like, why bring researchers to AI Engineer, which is an engineering applied AI conference. One — to me, it is actually very important that you bring the researchers because engineers want to learn about how the models that they use were trained, even if they never ever trained it themselves. Right? Because I think they just feel more trusting of the model if you peel back the curtains a little bit. And also I think there's some prestige that people want to feel — like they can go home and talk about it intelligently even if they don't actually know how to train it.

The other thing is, I do think that research and engineering are closer than people think. There's research engineers. And Mech Interp is probably the single easiest way that engineers can get into research if they want to.

Omar：Yeah, I think in big part — so many researchers are doing ablations, right? They are just moving the pieces around and seeing what works and what doesn't work. Of course there's a branch within research that is much more like architecture design and much deeper. But there's lots of very empirical experimentation and seeing what works, what doesn't work, moving things around — which for me is much more engineering rather than research, unless we are writing new activation functions maybe.

主持人：Yeah, I think this maybe is a change in your career as well. It used to be a joke like, "haha, researchers are terrible at coding," and then they throw it across the wall to some engineer that will clean up the code. [laughter] But now everyone has their own personal research engineer, right?

Omar：Yeah. And something that is cool that is happening is also how researchers begin to adopt some of the cool agentic tools now. So for example, within the team we are building skills to do experiments and ablations and evaluations, and how the research team can use all of these agentic tools as part of their research process is also quite interesting.

业余社区与 Auto Research

主持人：I had ETA on my podcast who led the post-training for the IMO gold model. I think it was DeepMind. And he was notably an AI researcher that doesn't use AI. It's gone even further — people making novel math research, like some of the Erdős problems. They're engineers, not researchers, with no background in math, just using coding agents among the math guys. Just, you know, solving some of the most unsolved problems.

Omar：Even in the model architecture side of things — two years ago when all of these people started to fine-tune models and to do experiments and do model merging, there was quite a bit of research that was happening in GitHub and in Reddit and in local labs. People were actually inventing new things, and then there were papers — Frankenmerges, like all of the franken merge stuff, all of the Axolotl library, all of these tools — and there were papers published by different companies and research labs 1 or 2 years later that were rediscovering what was already done by the Reddit or Discord people without anyone noticing.

主持人：Do you have a take on auto research? Every AI wave has an auto-ML wave, and this is the auto-ML wave of this wave. Always been a bit skeptic — I mean, auto-ML a few years ago was mostly just parameter search.

Omar：Yeah, pretty much like grid search in that hyperparameter space. I don't know — with Karpathy experiments it's been quite interesting to see how things are evolving. What's your take on this?

主持人：Things are just cooler when he does it. I do think some part of this is you're just speed-running experiments agentically, right? The coding agent is more autonomous; you can actually go to sleep and it will do the things that you would have done anyway. So you're just kind of automating things that you would have done.

I see it differently. I think it will be a very exciting time if we have a "Move 37" from auto research — if you make an impactful discovery that someone wouldn't have thought of, right? So there's a side of "okay, I have these ideas, go run them in the background" — that's fine. But the other side is actually when you're shooting off not just paths that you wouldn't have thought about, but trajectories that people wouldn't think about, and they work, and you make new discoveries. That's the very exciting thing. I think when you have more approach to just token spend and send off, hopefully that becomes possible.

Omar：Yeah, I do think the next generation of fine-tuners will be people that are not coding at all, right? Like 1 year ago we had to write our own code with transformers or Unsloth or whichever library of your choice. I do think as we keep evolving, most people will be fine-tuning with a couple of skills, right? Hugging Face has skills, all of these libraries have skills. They will just prompt the agent to kick off some experiments and see what works, what doesn't work.

And honestly, it's a good middle ground right now. All the tools you've mentioned, they let you fine-tune in minutes. You don't need to know what's happening in the order. So I think that's where the direction will be — people that just want to do fine-tunes to improve their capabilities for a certain domain or add some new behavior. They will not be coding the fine-tuning code. But of course, if you want to do deeper research in the architecture, my hunch is that most likely this will not be automatable, at least in the next 1 or 2 years.

团队成长与全球招聘

主持人：Okay, we got to wrap up soon. I just wanted to end a little bit on your growth in your team. You know, Paige is here, Logan is over in SF, and you've been hiring all my friends — Thor and Ivan and all these. What does the team look like? Where are you looking to grow?

Omar：It's been quite exciting. We are hiring lots of very high-agency people. I think maybe 3-4 years ago we did have a nice interview about how I was growing DevRel at Hugging Face and how we were thinking DevRel should look like. DevRel at Hugging Face is also interesting. It's redefining what DevRel should be in a very AI-centric organization at the frontier. It's a research lab at the end of the day, and we are in this AI era. So it's also rethinking what DevRel world should look like here in 2026.

We are pretty much hiring high-agency people excited to build things, to engage with the community, and so on. Right now, we're growing in Singapore, so we're looking to hire someone in Singapore.

主持人：Come to the AI in Singapore!

Omar：They're coming. Yeah, and to hire someone in India. So those are two locations with huge…

主持人：Why is Singapore so important?

Omar：So Singapore is interesting. Singapore has a relatively small but very dense high-talent community. Now we have a proper DeepMind office as well — it's small, but it's growing quite quickly as well. Mostly because he doesn't want to move. [laughter]

But there's a huge win for Singapore. We don't have research in Singapore usually. So we're trying to grow the team in places colocated to people doing traditional DeepMind research activities. We don't want to have people that are in a single town that is not connected to anyone in person from DeepMind. So ideally, if they go to the office, they can talk with researchers doing their own thing. If it benefits a different project, they can be part of the more DeepMind-y side of things. So we have people in Paris, in London, in Zurich, in SF, New York. So all of these DeepMind hubs, and Singapore now is becoming a very small but very exciting as well.

Kaggle 加入 DeepMind

主持人：So this is all the DevRel, DevEx team in DeepMind, right? DeepMind has also expanded a lot here — like a few weeks ago, Kaggle joined DeepMind. How's the org in general, actually? DeepMind in the past didn't do that much product. And yeah, now we have like — even the Gemini API. Now, Kaggle. But Kaggle is part of the team.

Omar：Actually, Kaggle is also here. There are two team members here talking about evaluations. Last week they released a new system for agent evaluation. It's a very experimental initial benchmark, but pretty much allowing agents to take an exam and compete in a leaderboard, which is always fun.

With Kaggle joining us, I think there are a couple of things. There's a whole Kaggle community hackathon things that enables the community to build hackathons. But there is also the Kaggle benchmarks. And I think Kaggle benchmarks can connect very well with how we think about Gemini and the capabilities. And if you're in the eval space, then you know many benchmarks can be — many people are gaming the benchmarks — and we want to identify which are these capabilities that maybe we are not aware that we have, or that maybe we could improve, and bring all of that feedback from the benchmarks created by the community in an organic way and bring all of that feedback back to the model itself.

The way we are doing Gemma, Gemini, and all of our tools is really based on the feedback from the startups, the community, the developers. So that's why you see Logan, Paige, everyone in the team talking with the community in social media, in forums, in events, and really understanding what people are building with our tools and bringing all of that feedback to the modeling things, which is very cool as being part of the team.

结尾

主持人：Well, you guys are doing amazing work. Thank you so much for joining us here. Can't wait to see what's next.

Omar：Yeah, thank you for having us here.

[music]

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一个反常识的观察：Gemma 4 不是"更小的模型"，而是"重新发明了内存层级"
大多数人把 Gemma 4 的"effective parameters"理解为一种压缩技巧，但这其实是一次**架构级的认知反叛**。传统 Transformer 把所有参数视为同等公民，必须全部装进 GPU 的"短期记忆"。而 Gemma 4 的 per-layer embedding 本质上是把模型的一部分"知识"重新归类为**可查表的、冷的、可外置的**——这与人脑的工作方式惊人一致：你不会把童年记忆放在工作记忆里随时调用，但需要时能瞬时检索。
更耐人寻味的是：Omar 暗示这个技巧"不能 scale"到大模型。为什么？因为**当智能足够稠密时，你无法再把知识从计算中干净地剥离出来**——这反过来揭示了一个深刻的命题：智能与知识的可分离性，本身就是模型规模的一个相变指标。
## 跨领域连接：Reddit 上的 Frankenmerges 与 1970 年代的家酿计算机俱乐部
Omar 顺口提到一个被严重低估的现象：2023-2024 年 Reddit/Discord 上的业余模型合并者

item.title: [Latent Space] ⚡️ Why you should build Science Fiction — Sunil Pai, Cloudflare
item.dc:creator: Latent Space
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一个让我意外的观察："slop fork" 其实是 LLM 时代的版本控制革命

Sunil 表面上在讲一个尴尬的社交事件（用 Opus 生成 5000 行代码 fork 了 Vercel 的 Just Bash），但藏在底下的是一个软件分发史的回归。他自己说漏嘴了：在 npm 之前，软件是通过 Usenet、邮件传播的——你拿到代码、改它、再分享出去。中间这几十年我们把 fork 妖魔化了——它变成"不尊重原作者"、"碎片化生态"。但 LLM 让 fork 的成本从"读懂 5000 行别人的代码"降到了"出去吃顿饭再回来"。

真正的洞察是：我们正在见证一种新的依赖管理哲学诞生——"vendor everything via slop fork"。Sunil 提到的"你不再有 left-pad 问题、不再有 left-pad 问题"——这不是开玩笑。当复制成本接近零，依赖关系图就从"图"退化成"快照"。这是软件工程 30 年来的范式倒转。

跨领域连接：生物学的"水平基因转移" vs 软件的 slop fork

细菌不靠继承传递基因——它们直接吞掉邻居的 DNA 片段、重组、变异。这就是为什么细菌进化比真核生物快几个数量级。而我们人类构建的软件依赖系统（npm、pypi、cargo）一直在模仿真核生物的垂直继承：版本号、依赖树、SemVer。

slop fork 把软件演化从真核生物拉回到了细菌模式。 而 Cloudflare 的 dynamic workers + durable objects 恰好是适合这种"水平基因转移"生态的底层——因为每个 isolate 都是一个可隔离、可复制、可变异的"细胞"。Sunil 没说出口但暗示的事情是：Cloudflare 不是在和 Anthropic 竞争平台，他们在赌一个完全不同的软件进化机制。

隐藏的系统性真相："原创性的稀缺"是 LLM 时代真正的资本

视频末尾 Sunil 几乎在恳求观众"be original，build sci-fi stuff"。这句话表面是鸡汤，但放在他整个对话的语境里就很惊人——

他刚刚承认： - 5000 行代码他都是让 Opus 写的 - 开源仓库已经被 LLM 生成的 PR / 假安全报告淹没（"open-source repos have become adversarial grounds"） - 他们的 agents SDK 关闭了贡献，只接受 issue - 因为反正最后都是 "paste 进 Opus"，那不如他用自己的 token

这暴露了一个所有人都还没消化的真相：当代码生产成本归零，"该写什么"反而比"怎么写"贵 1000 倍。React 当年改变世界不是因为它代码写得好，而是因为 Jordan Walke 想到了"组件树用 XML 描述"这种当时被骂出会场的想法。Sunil 反复说"没人写出 React 等价物的 harness"——他在说的其实是：LLM 时代的瓶颈不在执行层，而在概念层。 现在所有 harness 都在抄彼此，因为没人敢想真正奇怪的东西。

这跟他对开源生态变成"adversarial"的观察是同一个硬币的两面——当 fork 的边际成本归零、PR 的信号价值归零，唯一还有价值的就是"原始的、奇怪的想法"本身。这就是为什么他在结尾几乎情绪化地说 "I'm begging you to be original"——他看到了一个 LLM 把所有事情都拉平之后，人类剩下的最后一块护城河是什么。

📝 转录文本

开场：Code Mode 与 Cloud Managed Agents

Okay, we're here with Sunil, uh creator of code mode.

No. Uh I have to take credit for creation of code mode. Dynamic isolates for a while. I did it. It is mine. I — it is, I did create — uh since I was a child, I've been thinking about it. It's my life. It's my life's mission.

I wanted to just catch up on everything going on in Cloudflare land. And also like, obviously you and I have caught up over the years. The more recent thing which just launched yesterday was this Cloud — uh Cloud Managed Agents thing, which I know that you're also like interested in. Maybe we can talk about that, because it's kind of like breaking news and you seem excited about it.

Cloud Managed Agents is dope, because Anthropic is saying: here is a platform for you to build and deploy your agents that are like long-running. And I like it because I like the people at Anthropic and they're super ambitious about these things. Except I looked at the product and I was like — I think I want to compete. I think we can do something better with Workers and Durable Objects.

Yeah, Cloudflare is an ideal platform for this. You had Workflows for a while. What functionalities are available in Cloudflare? Like why can't you just come out and say "yeah, we already had this" — like what's the difference?

Cloudflare 的两个核心原语：Durable Objects 与 Dynamic Workers

I think that you see — like anything else — I think we are discovering the architecture of the new architecture of building software, of which I'm talking about on stage in a couple of hours. Uh 5:40 p.m., please show up to the main stage.

Uh but interestingly, our primitives — the two primitives that I think are critical, which I suspect every platform will eventually get — one is Durable Objects, which are stateful serverless programming. I think that's a true innovation. It's the world's first implementation of the actor model on an infrastructure layer, not in userland. It means you can spin up millions of these stateful things that run in the background etc., and it runs with serverless characteristics instead of spinning up a whole VM. That's one.

The second, which we announced just a few weeks ago, is what we call Dynamic Workers. Famously, eval has been considered a bad idea — evaluating code, user-generated code, or now LLM-generated code. But we built this thing that lets you run it in a safe environment with zero startup time. And when I say safe, I mean you can expose only the APIs that you want into it, and you can control all outgoing traffic. In fact, the default way we recommend people using it is to cancel all outgoing traffic and only use the exposed endpoints.

So given these things, we feel we can build a much more efficient and much — a new kind of architecture for building AI agents.

案例：Cloudflare MCP Server 与 "Search + Execute"

Uh one of the applications of Dynamic Workers — we made a little noise about it a while ago — is my colleague Matt Carry built our MCP server. The Cloudflare API is 2,600 API endpoints. So if you exposed a tool for each one of them, you're screwed. And you could say, "Oh, I'm going to do progressive disclosure, I'm going to do this." No — we said you can have two tool calls. One is search and execute. And to each one of these, you actually submit code that we run in an isolate.

So you can say, "Here's some JavaScript code that searches the entire OpenAPI JSON." And the second time you can say, "Here's some code to actually run that. Hey, find all my workers, find the DNS things they're used in, and if it starts with the letter Z, then apply DDoS protection for it" — in one tool call. No back and forth with the LLM, and it's type-checked. And uh, well, turns out LLMs are great at running code.

So this is fundamental capabilities. It's not something you can patch on and use. So that makes me excited about Cloudflare for the future of agentic software.

Anthropic Cloud Managed Agents 的看法

I don't know if you've looked at the sort of Cloud Managed Agents thing.

I looked briefly. There's like interesting takes, right? So: separate the context from what's happening, and then there's like a little quote about — okay, recursive models do this. Any takes on that?

I do like their thing. Uh, they made a point of pointing out that the harness runs separately from the execution environment, because they're preparing for a world of software that hasn't existed in the past. What a wonderful way of looking towards the future. By the way, it's not that you're just going to build the next CRUD API — we're like, we don't even know the shape of what's going to happen. And while that needs to run in a sandboxed environment, we do have ideas on how to expose capabilities and an intelligence layer via the harness. I think that's excellent.

Yeah. Do you think that becomes sort of standardized? Like Cloudflare is someone that would probably plug in support. Is there a standard unified layer around that? Are there bets you take, different ways that you see it? Like everyone is building for a version of the future of software engines. Do you have a vision that you see?

"没人写出 LLM 时代的 React"

It's so hard, but the way I'm framing it in my head is: no one has built the React yet. You know, like the moment React came in — it wasn't… I mean, it annoyed a bunch of people because it seemed so different, the fact that you can describe a component tree with XML. I don't know if you remember, in 2013 people were walking out of the talk while they were introducing the thing, saying "Facebook hates JavaScript." But that established every framework in the future, and React is still massive.

I suspect — everyone now, everyone is building a harness, and everyone's like "well, it should be in this shape, it should be in that shape." Someone needs to come out with some original thinking around something that is reproducible across languages, across companies, across infrastructure. I suspect we haven't done that yet. It might just be skills. I feel like skills are the ultimate translation thing.

And they're a version where it's just English.

They scale well. It's English. Uh, you know, you can abstract it down. You can create skills on their own.

Is that it? Like, by the way, I'd be so happy if you say the future of software is Markdown files. Beautiful. Instantly accessible. A multi-language bridges the world.

Uh, could just be skills. But like — do you end up with that problem with skills where you have to start getting more and more specific, at which point you're like, I might as well just express it as code?

Yeah. Yeah. I'm being very precise about my stuff.

Um yeah, I mean, and skills bundle code as well, right? So there's a whole bunch of things.

进入 Slop Forks 话题：Vercel 事件始末

Okay. There's — I mean, there's other topics that we can also touch on. You're happy to indulge on a lot of different ranging topics. Let's talk about slop forks.

I love slop forks.

What happened with you and Vercel? Let's get into it. Let's get right into it. I don't know if you want to maybe —

You know what? I'm happy to talk about it. Okay. Like I'm happy to talk about it because…

Tell some people — you know, they're not always on Twitter, so maybe they missed it, but I think it's an important event.

Sure. [laughter] No, it does. It does. Well, in my life, sure. But like — about the importance of software. Okay. So for context — yes.

行业背景：旋转门的森林

Okay, for context: I've been in this industry long enough that I have friends everywhere — competitors, not competitors, previous jobs, future jobs. Uh, we describe the industry as a forest of revolving doors.

It's why you maintain candor and being friendly with everyone.

In fact, uh, we love Vercel's AI SDK. A whole portion of my Agents SDK is built on top of them. We feel like we built the best backend for them. And we have personal relationships with them — like I'll be grabbing a beer with Niko at some point today.

But there is this tension. Twitter amplifies drama, and particularly like management — and you know what, I kind of stay away from that. There's a little bit of tension amongst competitors, especially because the medium encourages that kind of thing.

事件经过：JSCON、Just Bash 与 Opus 写的 5000 行代码

Okay, the event is: I was at JSCON, I was hanging out with Javi from Vercel, and I love him — 10 on 10 human being, what a wonderful time I had there. I loved Spain. Uh, and I was playing with this project that Vercel Labs had just come out with called Just Bash. I loved it. What a great idea — a pure implementation of bash in JavaScript.

And I was like, "Oh, of course I want to make some of this stuff work with Cloudflare." And the way I did it in 2026 is: I just point Opus at it and I go out for lunch, etc., right? Like, and I come back and it spat out 5,000 lines of code. I'm like, "Yeah, okay, fine, like I'll just publish it."

And my entire plan there was — I would, because I look up to (I won't name names here), because I look up to these people, I was like, "I'm going to have a great time in Spain. I'm going to go back home, polish this, and on Monday I'll send a PR, we'll have an issue description" — which is how I interact with every other open source repo in my entire life.

I went back, I crashed. I woke up, and I have DMs from Cloudflare management saying, "Have you checked Twitter?" [laughter]

I go on Twitter, and the Vercel CTO is trashing my work, saying — well…

It's like, you know, one guy, you know, side projects, and suddenly it's "Cloudflare did this."

Exactly. "Cloudflare did this." [laughter]

I was — dude, I saw my career like fall apart with that message. No, but that's just — like, that was my immediate reaction to seeing the CTO of a $10 billion company. And I was like, I didn't think I would get fired, but I was just like, "Oh, this is not it at all."

So I responded in the most earnest way. I was like, "Bro, I wanted to talk to you. I came back and napped, like —"

You know what's funny? The talk I gave at JSCON Fespa is now online, where I talk so warmly about Vercel and Just Bash and their sandbox project. This was the context it was happening in.

Uh, anyway, so I wake up to this and I responded. I'm like, "I'm sorry, that's how I did it." And then something strange happened, which is like half the internet decided to stand up for me. And they were like, "Sunil's the nicest guy. Why'd you have to do this?" And I spent the next 24 hours just awash in this love.

Slop Forks 的本质：Fork 是文化、是 prestige

So let's talk about slop forks for a while, right? I think we also have to mention the most recent — right? There was a leak of some repo, some Claude Code leak, and what did we do? Someone forked it and then rewrote it to avoid Python. You were — people tagged me in those threads. They're like, "Is this you?" I'm like, "It's not me." And people loved that version, right?

I think it's weird because of the environment. Forking is a great sign of prestige, respect in my culture. You're in my culture. It's how the React community grew. It's like — it's one button. And no one makes money…

Core to what open source is, right? You want to build on it, you should take our work, fork it and do whatever. And why not port it to a different language?

Like, I encourage — every time someone talks to me about the Agents SDK and they tell me, "Oh, we think we can build something better," I'm like, "Bro, go for it. Like, everyone wins if you do this. I don't make less money if I'm successful or not successful." Like, I mean — if you're in it for the love of the game, then do it for the love of the game.

Yeah. Yeah. Actually, that was quite hilarious because they thought that they could get away by rewriting Claude Code in Python, but I think some lawyers saw it and they're like, "Nope, this is a derivative work." Like, it actually breaks copyright.

Slop Forks 作为依赖管理策略

You know, one thing I've been thinking about slop forks is that actually you maybe want to encourage more slop forks, because then you own the dependency, right? So then you don't have the LiteLLM issue, you don't have the Axio issue — you can just vendor Axio, but in your own slop fork of Axio, and then now you own it.

Yeah. [laughter] No, 100%. Like — bro, it's how the internet — like before npm, before whatever, software used to be distributed on Usenet, on email. And then you take it, you change it, you'd share something. And now the cycles have become shorter. But forking is so fundamental to how we build software.

It's interesting. It's very non-agentic, right? Like there's a lot of "will I use a microservice or will I just vibe-code a little replica for that."

Build versus buy has really changed.

Build versus buy — but then there's no fork in that, right? There's no "go see prior work, fork it, and adapt it, simplify it, whatever." We don't see that side of the pattern, right? We just see "rebuild it, use it," but people don't fork.

开源生态变成对抗性战场

I think there's also this tendency now, which is: open-source repos have become adversarial grounds. So on the Agents SDK, for example, we had to shut down contributions. We only allow issues right now.

Yeah.

And like — we can have the longest description there, because the end result is: I'm going to take a resolution and paste it into Opus. Now, whether you're burning your tokens or my tokens — well, I prefer my tokens, because I'm great at asking questions. Uh, and I do like taking credit for the clanker work.

I think open-source repos have become adversarial to the point that people are almost afraid of gaining popularity in that space. I've spoken to at least four or five open-source maintainers here, and they are like, "Yeah, it's really bad out there right now."

Yeah. The worst ones are the fake security reports.

Yeah, they look extremely valid, and you look at it and it just doesn't make sense.

Yeah, we had that talk in the morning with Peter from OpenClaw. It is the number one source of attacks right now. You compromise OpenClaw, you get access to everyone for remote code execution, let's call it.

结尾：Alpha Thought Leading 与"做科幻级的东西"

Yeah. Any other CTAs? Uh, just in general — you know, we do have to wrap up, but I always appreciate you coming by and dropping your wisdom. Alpha thought leading is the term that always comes to mind.

Alpha thought.

Yeah. In 2018, it was the React Rally where you were presenting your VR testing —

That's right.

And you were like, "I'm first going to do some alpha thought leading." And ever since then, I've been trying to do alpha thought leading as well. You are one of the best at the game.

[laughter] I think like — look — this is alpha thought leading.

The real thing I encourage people to do is to be original. Okay? In such a crazy world where any idea seems to work now — like the clanker will make it work for you — don't even try to do something incrementally better. Build sci-fi stuff. Build stuff for your family. Use Agents SDK for doing it.

Don't build it because you think you're going to get a big enterprise deal. You own so much agency in changing the world, and I want people to just be original. I want them to try the wild stuff for which infrastructure and LLM almost fail you at this moment. Like, how do we get to the next step change?

Uh, this is the only thing I desire of people — like, I'm begging you to be original and to have courage to try a new idea. I don't need another agent framework. Nobody needs it.

You should use Agents SDK.

Yeah. Except yours. Except yours.

Okay. Thank you. I'm really looking forward to your talk. Thank you for dropping by and thank you for sharing.

Wonderful. Thank you so much for having — a great, yeah. I'll be talking about it in a couple of hours. It's pretty great. You can find the recording somewhere.

Okay. Great. Cool.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一个让我意外的观察：**"slop fork" 其实是 LLM 时代的版本控制革命**
Sunil 表面上在讲一个尴尬的社交事件（用 Opus 生成 5000 行代码 fork 了 Vercel 的 Just Bash），但藏在底下的是一个**软件分发史的回归**。他自己说漏嘴了：在 npm 之前，软件是通过 Usenet、邮件传播的——**你拿到代码、改它、再分享出去**。中间这几十年我们把 fork 妖魔化了——它变成"不尊重原作者"、"碎片化生态"。但 LLM 让 fork 的成本从"读懂 5000 行别人的代码"降到了"出去吃顿饭再回来"。
**真正的洞察是**：我们正在见证一种新的依赖管理哲学诞生——**"vendor everything via slop fork"**。Sunil 提到的"你不再有 left-pad 问题、不再有 left-pad 问题"——这不是开玩笑。当复制成本接近零，依赖关系图就从"图"退化成"快照"。这是软件工程 30 年来的范式倒转。
## 跨领域连接：**生物学的"水平基因转移" vs 软件的 slop fork**
细菌不靠继

item.title: [Business of Sport] Alonso Hired BUT Chelsea Need to Change More than Just Their Manager
item.dc:creator: Business of Sport
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、最反直觉的观察：Chelsea 不是"管理失败"，而是"治理过剩"

绝大多数球迷把 Chelsea 的混乱归因于"没有好教练"或"老板太业余"，但转录里藏着一个更尖锐的真相——Chelsea 不是缺少决策者，而是淹没在决策者之中。五个 Sporting Director 同时存在，本质上等于零个。这在组织行为学里有个名字叫 "diffusion of responsibility"（责任扩散），最经典的案例不是足球，而是 1964 年纽约 Kitty Genovese 谋杀案：38 个目击者，没有一个人报警，因为每个人都默认"别人会处理"。

Chelsea 的五个体育总监就是 38 个目击者。Alonso 进门看到的不是空椅子，而是五张椅子上都坐着人——这比空椅子更危险，因为他无法知道该看向谁的眼睛。

二、"Manager vs Head Coach" 之争：一场关于符号学的足球幻觉

嘉宾点破了一个语义骗局——title 本身不带来任何权力，真正的权力来自 reporting line。这让我想到 Roland Barthes 在《神话学》里讲的"能指与所指的滑动"：当俱乐部把"Head Coach"换成"Manager"，他们改变的不是结构，而是球迷脑中的图腾。

更有趣的跨领域连接是硅谷的"Founder Mode" vs "Manager Mode" 之争（Paul Graham 去年那篇引爆 X 的文章）。Chelsea 老板表面在赋权 Alonso 当"Manager"，但他们自己仍然是 Founder Mode——把手脚伸进更衣室。Alonso 拿到的是"Manager"的头衔，干的却是"Head Coach"的活，承担的是"替罪羊"的命运。这是足球版的头衔通胀（title inflation）：当一个组织无法给你真正的权力，它就用更响亮的名字来补偿你。

三、被忽略的核心论点：那场"比赛前三天的 15 分钟对话"

整段访谈最有价值、也最少被人注意的，是嘉宾描述的那个赛前 15-20 分钟技术对话——80-90% 时间技术总监只是确认，10-15% 时间他会挑战。这个比例本身就是一个洞察：优秀的治理不是 100% 干预，而是 10-15% 的高质量摩擦。

这和我在别处见过的两个模式惊人吻合： - 航空业的 CRM（Crew Resource Management）：副驾驶必须在 10-15% 的关键时刻敢于挑战机长，否则就会出现"机长综合症"导致的空难。 - Bridgewater 的"可信度加权决策"：Ray Dalio 的整个体系建立在"少数高质量异议"上，而不是"持续微观管理"。

Chelsea 的悲剧在于：他们要么是0% 摩擦（教练自己拍脑袋），要么是100% 干预（老板直接插手转会和首发）。他们从未学会那个 15% 的甜蜜点。Alonso 再天才，如果没有一个能在桌子对面让他点头说"嗯，你说得对"的对手——他就只是一个穿着 Real Madrid 光环的孤独国王。

一个延伸的预测

如果 Chelsea 不改结构，Alonso 的失败模式会和 Pochettino、Potter 完全不同——前两者死于"权力不足"，Alonso 会死于"权力过载"。他的"Manager"头衔会让他在第一次危机时被推到最前面承担所有责任，而五个 Sporting Director 会集体后退一步。这就是组织心理学里的 "scapegoat amplification"——头衔越响，垫背越惨。

📝 转录文本

关于 Alonso 入主：一次成功的公关，却未必是结构性的胜利

A bit of excitement out of this — and we'll touch on a couple of managers, because the good thing they did manage to spin... on a nice little bit of PR for once for the club, was the Xabi Alonso hire, which I thought is well above our station currently, and I'm very interested to see how that pans out.

Yeah. Well, well, I mean there's there's no doubt that in terms of the out-of-work managers / head coaches available to Chelsea, this was probably the most impressive appointment that they could make.

"教练就是救世主"？我是个怀疑论者

Now, you'll be aware — and listeners and viewers will also be aware — that I am generally speaking a skeptic about the idea of manager as miracle worker. I'm a believer that, as part of the overall jigsaw, a really good head coach or manager (I'm going to come on to the moments about what the difference is between those two things) can make 5% difference. Maybe in absolute exceptional situation, 10% difference. But what they cannot do on a sustained basis is turn around a show. Right? A show is a show. And you can blame your manager all you want, but fundamentally, you know, it's going to be about sorting out the show.

Man United have shown that really relatively well in the last — well, decade really.

Right. I'm afraid my concern is for Chelsea is that the fundamental underlying structural problems with the club — I do not believe that having a different head coach, however perceptually better he might be (and there's an interesting argument to be had: is he miles better than Maresca, given where Maresca is about to go? Probably not), right? So, for me it's the kind of thing which is really great for getting the fans off your back.

Right? The fans go: "Hey, we've heard of this guy. He was a great player. We've heard of this guy. He's had a bit of success as a manager one season, but nonetheless he's had a bit of success as a manager. He's been at Real Madrid. That's good brand recognition — sort of brand name recognition type of stuff. Tick, tick. So we feel big about ourselves cuz we're hiring the guy who was at Real Madrid until the end of this year. So we're feeling great."

"Manager" 还是 "Head Coach"？一个被夸大的头衔之争

Oh, and they've made him manager. Cuz you — you just slipped in there and you said head coach — you know, cuz the head coach doesn't change anything. But Charlie, he's the manager. So I'm sitting here going: this is the first manager Chelsea have hired since 2013, with Jose Mourinho. He was the last Chelsea coach to hold the manager title, not head coach. So I think this is a piece of genius from the ownership, don't you?

Um, well, I'm — look, the difference between head coach and manager is just absolute nonsense. It's not often that I agree with Robbie Savage, but I was in the car the other day — on Saturday or Sunday — and he was on 606. And of course he is currently a head coach or manager, right? And somebody phoned in saying it's amazing, it's fantastic, it's transformational that Alonso's going to be a manager not a head coach. And you could hear Savage in the background just laughing, saying: "There is no difference."

There is zero difference, because the only difference you have between different clubs is process — internal processes and responsibilities, right? You can have a head coach who has a very prominent position within the recruitment setup according to their contract, according to internal processes. And you can have a manager who has no say over the recruitment whatsoever. It is entirely perception-driven by fans on the one hand, and then from a status perspective by head coaches or managers on the other hand. They like the sound of it better. It sounds a bit more important. But ultimately, there are managers who report into technical directors, and there are head coaches who report directly into owners. You have to look at what the internal structure is to understand whether actually any significant difference here has been made.

老板的"轻松胜利"：拍拍自己的背

You remember — I think we talked about it on a previous show — but we were talking about the Chelsea ownership post-Mourinho saying they were going to review and reflect and do things slightly differently, or look to change some of the mistakes that they'd maybe made previously. You can see this is an easy win where they're probably turning around internally, patting themselves on the back going "that'll get them off our backs for a bit."

Yeah. So I think there are two things. I think the time I said, you know, is this just — well, we're not going to keep on hiring inexperienced managers? Well, I mean leaving aside the fact that Xabi Alonso is actually quite an experienced manager — but leaving that to one side — you know, a very well-known former super-elite player versus a more experimental type of young head coach like like Recesinha. That's clearly a decision which they've made, and I think that was an appropriate decision, understanding the challenges that face the bosses of super-elite clubs these days.

谁有资格执教超级俱乐部？球员背景 vs 教练经验

To have been somebody who has neither experienced the dugout at the very top level, nor even experienced the pitch at the very top level — I think that's a jump too far. I think if you come from a non-super-elite playing background, then you need to really go and make your bones on the management side.

You know, by the time that Jurgen Klopp becomes manager of Liverpool, he's a very experienced manager by that point, right? Even though he didn't have much of a playing career, he's very, very experienced manager by that point.

Yeah, Jose Mourinho won the Champions League with Porto and yeah.

Absolutely — and so on and so forth. Arsene Wenger, the same.

Um, so I think that bit of it is sound. And I'm not saying, by the way, that I have any problem with Alonso being called manager. I don't. Manager, head coach, whatever. You're saying it's not going to change the —

That itself does not change anything.

真正的问题：五个 Sporting Director

What I need to understand is: do you still have five sporting directors?

Yes.

Right. You do? Yeah. You sure? Yeah. So, can you explain to me what each of them does?

So, exactly. Which is where you'd say — so from your point, from what I'm understanding, if there was any real change, what you would have seen is a structural change above, so that the processes in place previously were clearly not going to be in place.

100%.

我希望看到的改变：一份治理蓝图

What I would have wanted to have seen is the following.

First of all, the ownership making a statement: "We have been far too involved, and far too visibly involved with the playing side of this football club. We are now going to step back into a supervisory role, and we're going to appoint senior people to take up these command-and-control positions, and they are going to be accountable to us and the shareholders, and they're going to be accountable to the fans." That's the first thing I'd want to have seen.

I then want to have seen those five sporting directors rationalized to one. One sporting director. Now, if you need to have three or four senior people who work into that sporting director, that's fine. But you need to have one person who's accountable to the ownership — but also clarity that the head coach or the manager, whatever you call him, is in partnership to, and reports into, that person. Very clearly that person.

为什么这件事至关重要：赛前 20 分钟的那场对话

Why is this important? I'll tell you why it's important.

It's the conversation that happens before a match and after a match. It's the conversation that happens in preseason and in post-season. These are analytical discussions, highly technical discussions, where effectively the head coach / manager needs to be held to account in such a way that the head coach / manager feels, um, on a level with the person who is making the assessments that are being made about his work, right? So he needs to look across the table at somebody and go: "Fundamentally, I might like it or I don't like it, but this guy knows what he's doing."

Because sometimes — this is what I just observed in a functional way; I have observed this at clubs I've worked at — where two days before the game, three days before the game, there'll be a little chat between the technical director and the head coach.

"What are you looking to do this Saturday?" "Well, I'm thinking I'm going to make a couple of tweaks in the system. I'm thinking I'm going to probably bring this player in from the cold," etc.

There's then a conversation — 15–20 minute conversation — with the technical director and the head coach: "Are you sure? Like, can you walk us through your thought process on that?"

And 80 to 90% of the time the head coach / manager comes away, "Yeah, that's [it]" — and that's then what happens. But 10–15% of the time the technical director is able to challenge in such a way that actually maybe that tweak doesn't happen, or maybe there's some other compensation that's made.

结论：换帅之前，先换结构

So that professional relationship — between the most senior football person in the club who is not the manager, together with the manager — that is the most important relationship in the entire organization. And you cannot have five of them.

So until Chelsea implement a functional system, and until the owners get their hands and feet out of the dressing room here, then my suspicion is that whoever the head coach or the manager is going to be is not going to make a very substantial difference.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一、最反直觉的观察：Chelsea 不是"管理失败"，而是"治理过剩"
绝大多数球迷把 Chelsea 的混乱归因于"没有好教练"或"老板太业余"，但转录里藏着一个更尖锐的真相——**Chelsea 不是缺少决策者，而是淹没在决策者之中**。五个 Sporting Director 同时存在，本质上等于**零个**。这在组织行为学里有个名字叫 "diffusion of responsibility"（责任扩散），最经典的案例不是足球，而是 1964 年纽约 Kitty Genovese 谋杀案：38 个目击者，没有一个人报警，因为每个人都默认"别人会处理"。
Chelsea 的五个体育总监就是 38 个目击者。Alonso 进门看到的不是空椅子，而是五张椅子上都坐着人——这比空椅子更危险，因为他无法知道**该看向谁的眼睛**。
## 二、"Manager vs Head Coach" 之争：一场关于符号学的足球幻觉
嘉宾点破了一个语义骗局——title 本身不带来任何权力，**真正的权力来自 reporting line**。这让我想到 Roland Barthes 在

item.title: [Bits + Bips] Kevin Warsh Takes Over the Fed. Here's What He Actually Can Do.: Bits + Bips
item.dc:creator: Bits + Bips
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、最反直觉的观察：Worsh 上任即"被去权"——他越想要独立，越证明他没有独立

表面看，Worsh 想做三件事：缩表、减少前瞻指引、重塑通胀测量。Marc Chandler 的判断却是——这三件事他都做不成。但真正的洞见不在"做不成"，而在于一个更深的悖论：

当一个央行行长必须公开"证明自己独立于总统"时，他已经失去了独立性。

真正独立的权力是沉默的、被默认的。需要"被证明"的独立，本质上是一种表演性独立——它的观众不是市场，而是 Trump。这意味着 Worsh 上任后的第一个货币政策动作，不会是数据驱动的，而是政治信号驱动的：他必须先建立"我不是 Trump 的人"的人设，才有资格谈货币政策。这恰恰锁死了他至少半年内的政策选项——"不加不减"不是他的判断，而是他的人设需求。

二、跨领域连接：Worsh 减少 FOMC 发布会 ≡ SEC 减少季度披露 ≡ 注意力经济的"信息戒断"

这是视频里被一笔带过、但极具系统性意义的一段：Worsh 想砍 FOMC 发布会和点阵图，与此同时 SEC 也在讨论减少上市公司报告频次。两件事被主持人放在一起，但没人挑明它们的共同底层：

这是一场针对"信息过载经济"的逆向革命。

过去二十年，金融市场的微观结构建立在"高频喂养"上：央行喂市场，公司喂分析师，分析师喂基金，基金喂散户。结果是——所有人都在反应，没人在思考。Worsh 和 SEC 同时动手砍信息供给，逻辑上等同于让市场"信息戒断"。

更跨界的联想：这与短视频平台开始隐藏点赞数、Twitter 减少实时指标、苹果推出"专注模式"是同一种文明级现象——当信息消费成瘾后，权力机构开始主动减少剂量。Worsh 不是在改革货币政策，他是在做一场注意力主权的回收实验：让分析师重新"做自己的工作"。

如果成功，二级市场波动率会先升后降；如果失败，他会被迫把发布会加回来，而那一刻就是他权威崩塌的时刻。

三、被忽视的暗线：市场共识从"不降"翻转到"加息"——这是一种集体心理防御

Chandler 提到一个微妙的转折：几个月前她坚持"不降息"是少数派，现在共识来了；但钟摆已经荡到另一边——市场开始押注"加息"。

这里有一个不太被讨论的心理学结构：当市场长期被"鸽派叙事"喂养后，一旦叙事反转，反转速度会超过基本面变化速度。这不是数据问题，这是叙事过冲（narrative overshoot）。

更深一层的反直觉：FOMC 的"分歧重现"被主持人视为健康信号——但它可能也是危险信号。十多年没有 dissent 的 Fed，意味着货币政策是"一个声音"的；而现在的 dissent 增多，叠加 Worsh 想减少前瞻指引——等于市场即将进入一个"多声部、低频率、高不确定"的新央行沟通范式。

四、彩蛋式预测：如果哪天 Worsh 自己投了反对票……

主持人半开玩笑提到"如果未来的反对票来自主席本人呢？"——这看似是脱口秀梗，但其实是Worsh 唯一能向 Trump 证明独立、同时不破坏与 FOMC 关系的核武器。

历史上几乎没有美联储主席投反对票的先例（Powell、Yellen、Bernanke 都没有）。但 Worsh 的处境特殊：他既要让 FOMC 信任他、又要让 Trump 觉得他"自己人"、还要让市场相信制度仍在运转。"主席投反对票"是同时满足这三个观众的唯一动作——一个看似制度灾难的事件，可能恰恰是他最聪明的政治工具。

如果这事真的在 2026 年底前发生，记得回来看这一段。

📝 转录文本

一、Kevin Worsh 接任：他到底能做什么？

Let's let's turn to his successor. I mean, I mean Kevin Worsh, as as I mentioned, uh he's gone through a bit of an evolution in his own thought on whether or not to be doubbish or hawkish.

One thing he made clear during his confirmation hearings is that he wants the Fed to move away from fiscal policy — which I I I I believe he sort of characterized as not as being more of picking winners and losers — whereas monetary policy is a bit more democratic in terms of affecting the whole economy, and uh and and that's where he thinks the Fed should sit.

He also has plans to try to uh create new ways of of measuring inflation so the Fed can, I think, be a bit more accurate and and and more predictive in how it exercises monetary policy. And as you said, Noel, Jerome Powell was front and center and gave a lot of forward guidance. Cameron Worsh doesn't want to do that.

What are your expectations?

二、缩表、独立性与"债市才是老板"

I think that, uh, you know, he can say he wants a lower balance sheet. The markets aren't going to let him have one. It's as simple as that. They're not going to let him have one.

And independence, sure, but the bond market is the boss here, and his, you know, it's relevant for price stability. The Federal Reserve cannot let a disorder take hold in the Treasury market. It just can't do that. So, because that would affect the dollar, which would affect price stability, etc., etc., etc.

So, that is something that we can all, you know, wish for. I wish I were a professional piano player. Not going to happen.

三、前瞻指引的削减：市场会接受信息减少吗？

In terms of forward guidance, I would not be at all surprised to see fewer FOMC press conferences, fewer dot plots, and there's a big debate to be had about whether this is going to be good or bad.

It sort of ties in with what the SEC is doing, talking about also in reducing the number of reporting periods of the year.

  • Does — will the market be okay with less information, or will this add volatility?
  • Does that mean analysts actually have to start doing their job, rather than being fed the information quite so frequently?

I don't know. This is a very, very big change. We get so used to a certain cadence, or used to being spoonfed certain pieces of data, that when that's taken away, is that going to disrupt markets enough that he's going to have to put them back on the table? Or will it be a refreshing change, reduce costs, as well as reduce — some reintroduce some original thinking that will in the end be healthy?

I wouldn't be surprised to see him try that. Whether the markets will let him succeed or not, I don't know. But I think that's pretty much all he's going to be able to do.

He's certainly not going to be able to change the inflation measure, because it's not really up to him. He can influence what people might look at, but we're going to make our own decisions over what we think is important. And he's certainly not going to be able to lower rates.

四、四月 FOMC 会议纪要：鹰派声音浮现

Yeah. And uh, speaking on that last part — not being able to lower rates — uh, I'm sure you saw yesterday, I — I guess it was yesterday — u, minutes were released, I believe, from the Fed's uh April meeting last month.

And uh, although one thing that I guess was revealed was that uh there was a lot more uh, um, acceptance of of of hawkish views than um what came out in the the final vote, which was really just to kind of hold hold the rates steady.

And that speaks directly to what he's going to be walking into, which is:

  • a higher inflationary environment,
  • a president that wants lower rates,
  • a belief that productivity coming from AI might put a bit of a lid on inflation.

But — and then to add to all this — um, Kevin Worsh is going to have to prove his quote unquote independence um from the president.

So, so what are your expectations for the next couple of Fed meetings?

五、Trump、Worsh 与"沉默的喘息期"

I think the number one thing to look at here is what Trump says right now. He has publicly said that, you know, Kevin Walsh is going to do what he's going to do. He has full faith in him, good man, etc., etc. — which again is quite astonishing given the environment that you've said Kevin Walsh is walking into.

We know Kevin Walsh isn't going to be able to lower rates:

  1. Because he's just one vote, and pretty much nobody else is going to be voting with him on this.
  2. Trump can't bash the person he chose so soon after choosing him.

So there's a bit of a respite there.

六、共识的钟摆：从"不降息"到"加息"——但都过头了

I think one thing the market is getting wrong is expectations of a rate hike. I've been saying no cuts for a long time now, and it's reassuring to see that consensus take hold. Now there's a consensus for a hike this year. I think that's going too far in the other direction.

He's not going to be able to lower rates, but nobody's going to, I think, have the courage — or not enough are going to have the courage — to vote for a rate hike when there is still so much uncertainty. We don't know if this is transitory or not. We don't know what the other inflationary factors at work are going to do.

So, there's going to be no hikes. So, that'll keep Trump happy. There aren't going to be any cuts either. That may not keep Trump happy, but I think it'll be quiet. And that'll just simply give the Fed some breathing room.

It'll give W some breathing room to start to work on his relationships — because in the end it does come down to how much the FOMC committee trusts the chair, whether they'll go along with what he suggests or not. That will influence monetary policy going forward.

七、Dissents 的回归：是健康还是危险？

Yeah, I'm — I'm actually a little reassured in a way. I know, like, until descent started happening last year, it had been what — decades — before we had, we had descents.

I'm actually more reassured that there are dissents at this point, because it's sort of like — you have so many different people in that room with different vantage points, and they monitor different parts of the country economically. Like, there should be differences of opinion, and I'm actually —

Yeah, I mean, move away from the group[think]. I'm actually looking forward — perhaps season 52 of SNL we'll have an FOMC meeting. There's enough characters there that people recognize.

Serious, brilliant — I think, I think there really could be one.

八、彩蛋猜想：如果反对票来自主席本人？

Yeah, that — that's actually brilliant. And here's another twist:

What if the next descent comes from the chair himself?

Yeah, that would be — that would certainly be something.

I don't think that's ever happened before. I'm not sure — my history is weak — but that would be, again, kind of mind-blowing. I — I doubt, again, I doubt he'd do that, because his job — his number one job now — is to get the people to trust, get the FMC members to trust him.

九、节目订阅提示

If you've been loving Bits and Bips, don't forget that the show is transitioning to its own feeds on X, YouTube and your favorite podcast player.

If you're not already subscribed to Bits and Bips on its own channels, go there now and hit that subscribe button so you can keep up with our twice weekly live streams and macro-meets-crypto breakdowns.

Bits and Bips will only be on the Unchained feed for a few more weeks, so subscribe today to be ready for launch. You can get all the links at unchainedcrypto.com/bitsandbips.

观看原视频 →

表面看，Worsh 想做三件事：缩表、减少前瞻指引、重塑通胀测量。Marc Chandler 的判断却是——**这三件事他都做不成**。但真正的洞见不在"做不成"，而在于一个更深的悖论：
> **当一个央行行长必须公开"证明自己独立于总统"时，他已经失去了独立性。**
