这是视频里被一笔带过、但极具系统性意义的一段：Worsh 想砍 FOMC 发布会和点阵图，**与此同时 SEC 也在讨论减少上市公司报

item.title: [Bits + Bips] Structural Stimulus Has Replaced the TACO Trade
item.dc:creator: Bits + Bips
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、BLISS 不是替代 TACO，而是 TACO 的"母体"

表面上看，Gita Gopinath 提出的 BLISS（Big, Large and Lasting Stimulus/Support）是用来"取代" TACO（Trump Always Chickens Out）的叙事。但更隐蔽的真相是：TACO 只是 BLISS 在政治人格层面的一次显影。Trump 之所以总是"认怂"，不是因为他怯懦，而是因为他所处的系统已经把"不能让选民承受痛苦"写进了底层协议。换句话说，TACO 是表型，BLISS 是基因型。当我们嘲笑 Trump 退缩时，我们其实在见证一个更深层的事实：任何当代政治领导人，在面对市场剧痛时，膝跳反射都是一样的——这与意识形态、与是否民主，甚至与是否是 Trump 本人都无关。

二、一个让人意外的观察：AI 资本支出与 BLISS 在结构上是同一种"道德风险"

人们习惯把"宏观救助"和"AI 资本狂热"当成两件事来谈，但它们其实共享同一个隐藏机制——对'下行被托底'的集体定价。 - 政府托底让市场敢于在不确定性中 risk-on； - 而 hyperscalers 的资本支出，本质上也建立在一个隐性假设上：如果 AI 需求不及预期，美联储/财政部会通过流动性来"救"估值，而不是让一次彻底的纠错发生。

换句话说，Nvidia 的市盈率里其实嵌入了一个"宏观看跌期权"。这就解释了为什么 Nvidia 连续 8 个季度"卖新闻"却始终没有真正崩——不是 AI 故事在支撑股价，而是 BLISS 在支撑 AI 故事。这是一个被严重低估的跨领域连接。

三、跨领域连接：从生态学看"客户集中度"——AI 产业链正在变成单一栽培（Monoculture）

嘉宾提到 Jensen Huang 试图淡化"客户集中度"的担忧——一半客户不是大型 hyperscaler。但更深的隐喻来自农业生态学的"单一栽培脆弱性"： - 爱尔兰土豆饥荒、香蕉 Gros Michel 灭绝、加州杏仁单一品种危机——这些都不是因为植物本身脆弱，而是因为基因/客户多样性塌缩让一个本地病变会瞬间扩散为系统性崩溃。 - 当 Nvidia 的收入越来越依赖少数几家"自己也在烧钱、也在加杠杆"的客户，整个 AI 资本结构其实是一片金融意义上的单一栽培田。

更出人意料的是：BLISS 让这种单一栽培能持续更久——因为政府救助降低了"基因突变"（即新的商业模式、新的对手）出现的紧迫性。换句话说，结构性刺激不是稳定剂，而是单一栽培的化肥：短期更繁茂，长期更脆弱。

四、被忽视的"市场顶部信号"：大型 IPO 作为流动性的"潮汐计"

嘉宾轻描淡写地提到"历史上市场顶部往往由超大型 IPO 引爆"，但这条线索其实是整段对话最锋利的一刀。OpenAI 即将秘密递交 IPO、Anthropic 也在路上——这不是巧合，而是资本市场流动性即将达到边际饱和的预警。 - 1999 年 VA Linux、2000 年 Palm； - 2007 年黑石上市； - 2021 年 Coinbase、Rivian……

超大型私募公司选择上市的时点，本质上是创始人和早期投资者对"流动性溢价"的反向定价。当最聪明的内部人决定卖给公众时，他们不是在分享盛宴，而是在转移风险。BLISS 给市场注入的流动性，正在被 AI 一级市场的内部人优雅地"提取"——这才是这一轮繁荣最隐秘的、值得放在床头的观察。

📝 转录文本

一、BLISS Trade 的提出：从 TACO 到结构性托底

Steve（主持人）： Who, what do you think is going to give first? And I'd love for you to just also explain sort of this BLISS trade and whether or not that's going to be a determining factor.

嘉宾： Well, the BLISS trade, for those who aren't familiar with it, stems from a really interesting opinion piece published in the Financial Times a couple of weeks ago by, as you said Steve, by a former IMF chief economist and deputy managing director Gita Gopinath, who's now a Harvard professor. And it was — again, this has to be read through the lens of someone that comes from the IMF — but she makes the excellent point that the market assumption that there will be a put, if you will, or a safety net, is not to do with the TACO trade anymore.

That's part of it for sure, and Trump certainly has given the market many things to bank — the to hang the TACO expectation on — many things we want him to walk back. But her point is that this goes beyond that. The TACO trade is temporary; it's limited to Trump's term. But the BLISS trade, which is the acronym for Big, Large, and Lasting Stimulus or Support, is structural.

二、为什么 BLISS 是结构性的？

Her point is that there is no administration now that will not spend to get people out of difficulties, whatever those difficulties may be — be it a market crash, be it a banking crisis, be it high energy prices. We saw this in 2020. We saw this in 2022 with the energy prices. We're seeing this again, especially here in Europe anyway, with the Houthis crisis.

Governments are not going to be voted out of office because they are not spending to save people from whatever difficulty they may be in. And this is irrelevant — it's irrelevant which political party you're talking about. It's actually also relevant whether you're talking about a democracy or not, because we've seen coups across the — especially across the equator economies.

三、对货币贬值与道德风险的影响

Now, this is relevant for the outlook for currency debasement. Obviously, where is the funding for the stimulus going to come from? Well, it's going to come from somewhere. They're going to simply make it happen through one of the many tools in their box.

And longer term, this does add moral hazard, if that's the right term. It does add froth to the speculative side of the market. It's one of the reasons I think why we're just seeing so much risk-on appetite in this very uncertain environment, as you pointed out earlier. But it's structural. The put is now part of the system — that of course will add a whole different layer of fragility.

四、转向 AI 板块：估值、融资与市场领导力

Steve： Yeah, and I'm really curious to see like when or if something might come to a head, because every time anyone who's been trying to predict the doom loop ends up missing out — because the market always recovers, often with like a K or a V or some type of letter that shows divergent returns.

And one area I'm really focused on, I'd like to ask you about before we kind of move on to the next segment, is: what do you think all this means in particular for the AI stocks? I mean, OpenAI I think is expected to file for a confidential IPO perhaps as soon as tomorrow. Anthropic is on the hook likely later this year. These companies need to raise hundreds of billions of dollars in funding to build out all this infrastructure, buy chips from the likes of Nvidia.

Actually, I think Anthropic — it was reported they made an operating profit this quarter, which is pretty impressive. But OpenAI loses a ton of money and they're relying on all this debt to build out for the future. But they're also the ones — this whole segment — this is the one, these are, this is the segment that's driving stock markets higher. How do you think about that particular segment and its broader implications for the market?

五、对 AI 盈利预期的质疑

嘉宾： I follow the reports that insist that the earnings multiples, the P/E forward, are actually very reasonable right now. And what drives me mad is that everyone's assuming that the earnings are going to be met or exceeded. And while that has historically been the case, we can't assume that will always continue, because — again — what is built? What is the basis of these earnings expectations?

In many cases, it's company guidance. In many cases, it's just simply extrapolating demand. We're assuming a ton of demand for chips and for AI infrastructure that may or may not appear. It may. I'm not going to claim to be an AI expert here, but we've seen before, Steve, many times that technological innovations have their hype period in which the expectations are out of proportion to the reality, and they do eventually suffer a correction.

Maybe this is going to be the very first exception in history, possibly, but it's rash to bet everything you have on that. And right now, everything the market has is betting on that, and that's an underlying fragility which I think is being very overlooked.

六、Nvidia 的"Sell-the-News"模式与 IPO 信号

You mentioned earlier Nvidia's earnings were good and the stock sold off. This has actually happened every single time Nvidia reports for the past, I think, eight quarters. And every single time everyone's like, "Okay, this is over. Yeah, the whole AI play is over." But no, what it is is a sell-the-news event. This has become fairly normal. It's related to expectations: expectations before the report run up; after the report, "Okay, they were good, we're done, we're out" — and people sell.

So I wouldn't read anything into it yet, but you're so right — eventually, you think something has to tip. And I will just throw out there that historically market tops have been tipped by very large IPOs.

七、分析师预期 vs. 市场炒作

Steve： Yeah, that is a good point to pay attention to. And Nvidia, as you mentioned, is a really kind of funny case because I read that it had beaten analyst expectations I think either 14 or 15 quarters in a row. But analyst expectations are, I guess, theoretically should be rooted in reality, whereas the Twitter hype machine can just speculate wildly — and those are the ones that are the momentum traders. So it is kind of funny.

I found it interesting that Jensen Huang, Nvidia CEO, and the company — they pointed out how there's so much concern about a degree of incestuousness between all these different AI companies: the chip manufacturers, the consumers, etc. I think they tried to point out that like half of their clients are not the big hyperscalers, to maybe assuage concerns about too much concentration in a few big companies. But it is really interesting to see how that's going to play out.

八、客户集中度与下游健康度

嘉宾： Yeah, the concentration of clients in these companies — and these clients are no doubt also themselves dealing with higher input costs and increasing cost of debt. Are we certain the health of these clients is going to continue to be strong enough to maintain the earnings forecast that these hyperscalers and the chip manufacturers are selling to?

Steve： Okay, great. All right.

嘉宾： But I've been wrong. I've been wrong before — I've been expecting a market correction for a while now and I've been wrong on the timing. So, to be taken with a big grain of salt.

九、节目预告

If you've been loving Bits and Bips, don't forget that the show is transitioning to its own feeds on X, YouTube, and your favorite podcast player. If you're not already subscribed to Bits and Bips on its own channels, go there now and hit that subscribe button so you can keep up with our twice weekly live streams and macro-meets-crypto breakdowns.

Bits and Bips will only be on the Unchained feed for a few more weeks, so subscribe today to be ready for launch. You can get all the links at unchainedcrypto.com/bitsandbips.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一、BLISS 不是替代 TACO，而是 TACO 的"母体"
表面上看，Gita Gopinath 提出的 BLISS（Big, Large and Lasting Stimulus/Support）是用来"取代" TACO（Trump Always Chickens Out）的叙事。但更隐蔽的真相是：**TACO 只是 BLISS 在政治人格层面的一次显影**。Trump 之所以总是"认怂"，不是因为他怯懦，而是因为他所处的系统已经把"不能让选民承受痛苦"写进了底层协议。换句话说，TACO 是表型，BLISS 是基因型。当我们嘲笑 Trump 退缩时，我们其实在见证一个更深层的事实：**任何当代政治领导人，在面对市场剧痛时，膝跳反射都是一样的**——这与意识形态、与是否民主，甚至与是否是 Trump 本人都无关。
## 二、一个让人意外的观察：AI 资本支出与 BLISS 在结构上是同一种"道德风险"
人们习惯把"宏观救助"和"AI 资本狂热"当成两件事来谈，但它们其实共享同一个隐藏机制——**对'下行被托底'的集体定价**。 - 政府托底让市场敢于在不确定性中 ris

item.title: [Bits + Bips] The Bond Market Has Become the New Fed Chair
item.dc:creator: Bits + Bips
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、央行权杖的隐秘易主：当债券市场成为"影子主席"

最让人震撼的洞察并非来自鲍威尔的离任，而是 Noel 那句几乎被一带而过的话——"The bond market is the boss here"。这其实揭示了一个被所有人误读的事实：所谓"Fed 独立性"早已不是问题的关键，真正的权力中心已经从 Eccles Building 转移到了债券交易台。Kevin Warsh 可以宣布想缩减资产负债表，但市场不会"允许"他这么做——这句话的潜台词是惊人的：货币政策的"决策者"并不在 FOMC 投票室里，而是分散在全球数万亿美元的国债持有者手中。

这与一种古老的政治哲学产生了奇妙的呼应——主权的实际所在地，往往不是法律上的所在地。鲍威尔被指责"不够独立"时，他其实早已不在权力的真正位置上。这是一种"幽灵主席"现象：所有人都在讨论谁该当 Fed 主席，但 Fed 主席这个职位本身的实质权力，正在被结构性地掏空。

二、跨领域连接：BLISS 交易 = 央行版的"道德豁免券" = 中世纪赎罪券

Gita Gopinath 提出的 BLISS（Big, Lasting, Structural Support）交易，被 Noel 敏锐地区别于 TACO 交易——前者是结构性的，后者只是特朗普任内的临时现象。但这里有一个被忽略的跨领域连接：BLISS 交易在功能上等价于中世纪天主教会发行的赎罪券（indulgences）。

赎罪券的逻辑是：无论你犯了什么罪，只要你足够重要、付得起价，教会都会兜底你的灵魂。BLISS 交易的逻辑是：无论市场犯了什么过度风险的"罪"，只要它足够大、足够系统重要，政府都会兜底你的资产负债表。两者都创造了一个道德风险的永续债，都通过"印发"某种凭证（信用 / 货币）来实现。而历史告诉我们：赎罪券制度最终引发了宗教改革。那么 BLISS 交易的"宗教改革"会从哪里来？ 比特币恰恰扮演了路德的角色——一种拒绝接受"央行兜底"作为信仰前提的逃逸通道。

但这里有个反讽的意外观察：Noel 指出，2023 年银行危机时比特币上涨不是因为人们对银行系统失去信心，而是因为人们预期央行会再次大放水。换句话说，比特币当下交易的并不是"反央行"叙事，而是"央行必然救市"的叙事。比特币在结构上其实是 BLISS 交易的延伸，而不是它的对立面。这是加密原教旨主义者最不愿承认的真相。

三、被所有人忽视的"沉默指标"：等权指数的1999时刻

Noel 抛出的最后一个对位指标几乎是一颗深水炸弹——S&P 500 市值加权指数与等权指数之间的鸿沟，正在以 1999 年互联网泡沫破裂前夕的速度扩大。这是一个被几乎所有财经媒体头条忽视的信号，因为它太"无聊"——它不是单点的暴涨暴跌，而是一种结构性的地心引力失衡。

这里有一个更深的系统性洞察：当一个系统的注意力越来越集中于少数节点时，这个系统就越接近相变临界点。这不只是金融市场的规律——它在生态系统（物种集中度）、神经科学（注意力过度集中导致认知崩溃）、社交网络（影响力极化）中都有同构现象。1999 年的纳斯达克、2007 年的金融衍生品、2021 年的 meme 股，每一次顶部都伴随着同样的"注意力坍塌"模式。

而当下这个鸿沟的特殊之处在于：它叠加在 BLISS 交易的"永续兜底"预期之上。这意味着如果反转发生，它将不仅是市场的修正，而是对"政府永远兜底"这个底层信仰的修正——后果会比 2000 年的科技股崩盘剧烈得多。Noel 那句"任何顶部太重的东西，最终都会倒下"听起来像句陈词滥调，但放在 BLISS 框架下读，它其实是在预言一场信仰危机，而非市场危机。

一个收尾的意外提醒：Noel 提到"市场顶部历来由大型 IPO 标记"——而 OpenAI 正在准备秘密递交 IPO 申请。如果你把这条线索、AI 板块的 PE 假设、客户集中度风险、等权指数鸿沟、以及 BLISS 交易的道德风险叠加在一起，你会发现一个被完全忽视的可能性：OpenAI 的 IPO 时点本身，可能就是这一轮周期的钟声。

📝 转录文本

开场与节目介绍

Hi everyone, welcome to another episode of Bits and Bips: The Interview, the show where we explore crypto and macro colliding one basis point at a time. Once again, my name is Steve Ehrlich. I'm the head of research at Sharplink, and I'm also your host.

And we've got a terrific show for you today. Lots going on in the macro world. Stocks and bonds are going in entirely different directions. Crypto is getting caught in the wake. We're going to have a new Fed chair tomorrow and much, much more.

But as always, before we begin, just a little bit of housekeeping:

  1. Nothing that you'll hear on this show should be considered investment or financial advice. For full disclosures, please see unchainedcrypto.com/bitsandbips.
  2. Let's take a brief pause so we can hear from some of the sponsors who make this show possible.

If you've been loving Bits and Bips, don't forget that the show is transitioning to its own feeds on X, YouTube, and your favorite podcast player. If you're not already subscribed to Bits and Bips on its own channels, go there now and hit that subscribe button so you can keep up with our twice-weekly livestreams and macro-meets-crypto breakdowns. Bits and Bips will only be on the Unchained feed for a few more weeks, so subscribe today to be ready for launch. You can get all the links at unchainedcrypto.com/bitsandbips.

嘉宾介绍

All right, welcome back. So let me bring in my guest. She's someone that will be very familiar to a lot of long-time listeners — Noelle Acheson, formerly Genesis. I think we've had her as research at CoinDesk, and currently is the author of a very widely read and influential newsletter, Crypto Is Macro Now. So welcome, Noelle.

Noelle: Hi Steve, so good to be with you today. How are you doing?

Steve: I am good. I'm recovering from being about 95 degrees Fahrenheit in Philadelphia — a little summer weather in May.

Noelle: Yeah, I hear you.

Steve: I gather you're going to have to get used to that. Yeah.

当前市场全景：股债背离与加密的处境

But I think, like a lot of people watching and listening, I'm also curious to try to figure out what's going on in the markets today. And that's why you're actually the perfect guest to have on.

Because as I said in my opening, stocks continue to do well. I know. But there are some warning signs. I mean, Nvidia announced another very big quarter, but the response was muted. There's a lot of fear permeating, especially through the bond market — I know that's something you've followed very closely.

And then on top of all that, we got our first inflation print post the Iran war really beginning. No one quite knows exactly what's going to happen there. And Jerome Powell is enjoying, I guess, tipping us on Thursday, his last day as Fed chair, although he's going to be staying on on the board to continue voting, I guess, for the foreseeable future.

And again, crypto is getting caught up in all of this. Bitcoin had been up to about $82,000–$83,000 in recent weeks. ETH was, I believe, in the $2,400s. That's all since retreated.

So I want to kind of get into all of it. Let's start right now, Noelle, with my first question: how are you reading, especially some of the fear that is pushing through bond markets, pushing up yields to 10, the 30-year? To me, these would seem to be worrying signs, but for the most part, the market is kind of taking it in stride.

股市与债市的"两种鼓点"

Noelle: You're right. They are definitely worrying signs. What's more, they're worrying signs at a global level. You look at global bond yields — they are climbing. That's global tightening. That is not good for markets.

But stock markets moved to a different drummer. This isn't new. They always have moved to a different drummer. What is new is the divergence — the scale of the divergence.

You probably remember back in the day, everyone was excited about the 60/40 portfolio, because in theory you'd have bonds and stocks moving in separate directions. Well, we're seeing that, but not quite at this point. At this scale, it's astonishing.

The stock market is being driven, we can talk more about this later, by intrinsic and temporary factors — excitement about AI mainly, look at the chip sector performance — whereas the bond market looks at the macro outlook. What's ahead?

The bond market traditionally has been known as the — and I'm going to put big air quotes here — the "smart money," because they do focus on nothing but macro data and narratives and trends, whereas the stock market can get swept up, as we know, in some of the hype cycles that we seem to be diving into with greater frequency.

So we've got stock market following the hype cycle, which may have some foundations, may not. We can get into that as well. We've got the bond market following the macro indicators, which are not great right now. So that's why two very different drummers — not painting the same story, but they don't need to.

通胀的真实轨迹与"非临时性"

Steve: Yeah. Well, let's talk about some of those macro indicators. I mean, the inflation print is something that I know people have been paying attention to. I think the Producer Price Index is another one that is starting to creep up. But what else are you seeing? And how are you interpreting a couple of those inflationary signs, given the fact that — I don't want to use the word "transitory" — but theoretically, if the Strait reopens, if there is some sort of deal for Iran, energy markets are going to go back at least immediately to where they were before February 28th when the attack happened. But things should calm down a little bit now.

Noelle: Things should come down, certainly in terms of the oil price, but that's not necessarily going to bring inflation down right away for two reasons.

One, inflation moves slowly. I mean, we've seen an uptick in the core index, which is what the Fed focuses on. But it's been small because oil prices do affect everything — it takes time to filter through.

Two, we are also going to see increased volatility in inflation expectations. And this is interesting, especially in the American — the US — economy: the impact of gas prices on inflation expectations. You fill up your car at the pump, you see the tick, tick, tick of the number going up, and that event feels like it's draining out of your bank account almost.

So with gas prices climbing, even though they don't impact the core inflation that investors or savers focus on, consumers feel that inflation is going higher. That's going to influence their expectations, which influences their behavior, which influences actual inflation.

So we could have an end to the Hormuz crisis tomorrow, and one, it will take quite a while for energy prices to resettle. And two, it'll take even longer for that resettling to filter through to the inflation indices and the expectations.

So in other words, this trend of inflation is not going to be over anytime soon, no matter what happens in Hormuz — because it actually also isn't new. Inflation was building well before the Hormuz crisis started.

通胀其实从未真正下降

Steve: Can you double-click on that a little bit? Because I know, again, you're based in Spain, you're European, obviously I'm American. Inflation, the Fed — inflation had been going down, especially since the COVID inflationary boom occurred and the Fed was raising rates to ratchet it down. It didn't get close to the 2% target, but it had been coming down. So what do you mean when you say it had been creeping up even before this happened?

Noelle: I'm going to push back on the assumption that it had been coming down. It actually hadn't. If you look at the graph since '24, it's been flat. The core CPI has been hovering between 2.6 and 3. It really hasn't been coming down.

And even just a year ago, year and a half ago, many of us were saying, "Okay, this is it. Inflation is done. The disinflation is done and we're going to be steady for a while and then start to take up again." Why? Our expectations of continued uptake in inflation — which we have seen, but since even before the Hormuz strike — through the PPI, through import prices, through export prices as well. Why?

Because of the deglobalization trend that even predates Trump's administration. This started during the Biden administration. So that's a longer-term trend. Inflation will be heading up because of that. And Trump is just accelerating and turbocharging that.

We had the tariffs flip-flopping. We don't really know what's going on with the refunds there, but prices were going up as a result of tariffs. And then the Hormuz crisis just lit a match underneath all of that. But seriously, if you look back, if you look at the charts, you see that no, inflation hadn't been coming down for a while.

2% 目标与 Fed 的信任问题

Steve: Yeah, no, I actually think you're right. I remember some discussions about whether or not the 2% number actually had to be adjusted for a kind of neutral rate — neutral Fed target rate.

Noelle: Three is the new two, right? Three is the new two. And this is something that many have been arguing — even many Fed officials have suggested — but they can't change the expectations because of the underlying and perennial problem of credibility, of trust in the institution.

A large part of what the Fed does is manage trust. And if suddenly, "Hey, we can't reach two, so we're going to change our target" — well, that's going to impact the trust in the Fed's ability to reach the target that they set.

Steve: Gotcha. Okay, thank you for that. I'm going to get a lot more into the idea of Fed and trust in probably about 10 minutes or so.

"BLISS 交易"：超越 TACO 与 Fed Put 的结构性兜底

But I do want to press you a little bit on sort of the whole immovable-force-versus-unstoppable-object thing — you know what I mean — the whole idea of stocks versus bonds.

Because in your newsletter this week, you pointed out a really interesting op-ed that was written by, I think it was a former deputy managing director of the IMF. I keep up enough about the BLISS trade and how it's perhaps a corollary or even like a more sustainable version of the TACO trade. And it sort of fits into the same family as the Fed Put.

And a really interesting book that I read a couple months ago about the rise of carry — the idea that there's always going to be a backstop, and how that got turbocharged especially during COVID, when understandably central banks all around the world went into excessive accommodation in order to support economies that were grinding to a halt.

Can you please explain — what do you think is going to give first? And I'd love for you to also explain this BLISS trade and whether or not that's going to be a determining factor.

Noelle: Well, the BLISS trade, for those who aren't familiar with it, stems from a really interesting opinion piece published in the Financial Times a couple of weeks ago by, as you said, Steve, a former IMF chief economist and deputy managing director, Gita Gopinath, who's now a Harvard professor.

Part of it has to be read through the lens of someone that comes from the IMF, but she makes the excellent point that the market assumption that there will be a put, if you will, or a safety net, is not to do with the TACO trade anymore. That's part of it for sure. And Trump certainly has given the market many things to hang the TACO expectation on — many things we want him to walk back.

But her point is that this goes beyond that. The TACO trade is temporary, it's limited to Trump's term. But the BLISS trade — which is the acronym for Big, Large and Lasting Stimulus or Support — is structural.

Her point is that there is no administration now that will not spend to get people out of difficulties, whatever those difficulties may be — be it a market crash, be it a banking crisis, be it high energy prices. We saw this in 2020. We saw this in 2022 with the energy prices. We're seeing this again, especially here in Europe anyway, with the Hormuz crisis.

Governments are not going to be voted out of office because they are not spending to save people from whatever difficulty they may be in. And this is irrelevant which political party you're talking about. It's actually also irrelevant whether you're talking about a democracy or not, because we've seen coups across especially the middle-of-the-equator economies.

Now, this is relevant for the outlook for currency debasement, obviously. Where is the funding for the stimulus going to come from? Well, it's going to come from somewhere. They're going to simply make it happen through one of the many tools in their box.

And longer term, this does add more moral hazard, if that's the right term. It does add froth to the speculative side of the market. It's one of the reasons I think why we're seeing so much risk-on appetite in this very uncertain environment, as you pointed out earlier. But it's structural. The put is now part of the system. That of course will add a whole different layer of fragility.

AI 股、IPO 与市场顶部的信号

Steve: Yeah, and I'm really curious to see when or if something might come to a head, because every time anyone has been trying to predict the doom loop ends up missing out, because the market always recovers, often with a K or V or some type of letter that shows divergent returns.

And one area I'm really focused on — I'd like to ask you about before we move on to the next segment — is what do you think all this means in particular for the AI stocks?

I mean, OpenAI, I think, is expected to file for a confidential IPO perhaps as soon as tomorrow. Anthropic is on the hook likely later this year. These companies need to raise hundreds of billions of dollars in funding to build out all this infrastructure, buy chips from the likes of Nvidia. Actually, I think Anthropic — it was reported they made an operating profit this quarter, which is pretty impressive. But OpenAI loses a ton of money and they're relying on all this debt to build out for the future. But they're also the ones, this whole segment — this is the segment that's driving stock markets higher. How do you think about that particular segment and its broader implications for the market?

Noelle: I follow the reports that insist that the earnings multiples, the PE, forward PE, are actually very reasonable right now. And what drives me mad is that everyone's assuming that the earnings are going to be met or exceeded. And while that has historically been the case, we can't assume that will always continue because, again, what is the basis of these earnings expectations?

In many cases, it's the company guidance. In many cases, it's just simply extrapolating demand. We're assuming a ton of demand for chips and for AI infrastructure that may or may not appear. Again, it may. I'm not going to claim to be an AI expert here, but we've seen before, Steve, many times that technological innovations have their hype period in which the expectations are out of proportion to the reality, and they do eventually suffer a correction.

Maybe this is going to be the very first exception in history, possibly, but it's rash to bet everything you have on that. And right now, everything the market has is betting on that. And that's an underlying fragility which I think is being very overlooked.

You mentioned earlier Nvidia's earnings were good and the stock sold off. This has actually happened every single time Nvidia reports for the past, I think, eight quarters. And every single time everyone's like, "Okay, this is over. The whole AI play is over." But no — what it is, is the sell-the-news event. This has become fairly normal. It's related to expectations: expectations before the report run up, after the report, "Okay, they were good, we're done, we're out," and people sell.

So I wouldn't read anything into it yet, but you're so right. Eventually, something has to tip. And I will just throw out there that historically, market tops have been tipped by very large IPOs.

Nvidia 与客户集中度风险

Steve: Yeah, that is a good point to pay attention to. And Nvidia, as you mentioned, is a really funny case because I think I read that there had been analyst expectations, I think, for either 14 or 15 quarters in a row. But analyst expectations should theoretically be rooted in reality, whereas the Twitter hype machine can just speculate wildly. And those are the ones that are the momentum traders. So it is kind of funny.

I found it interesting that Jensen Huang, Nvidia CEO, and the company — they pointed out how there's so much concern about a degree of incestuousness between all these different AI companies, the chip manufacturers, the consumers, et cetera, that I think they tried to point out that half of their clients are not the big hyperscalers, to maybe assuage concerns about too much concentration in a few big companies. But it is really interesting to see how that's going to play out.

Noelle: The concentration of clients in these companies — and these clients are no doubt also themselves dealing with higher input costs and increasing cost of debt. Are we certain the health of these clients is going to continue to be strong enough to maintain the earnings forecast that these hyperscalers and the chip manufacturers are selling to?

Okay, great. All right. But I've been wrong. I've been wrong before. I've been expecting a market correction for a while now, and I've been wrong on the timing. So to be taken with a big grain of salt.

Steve: Yeah, well, I've never been wrong ever in anything I've written. So I don't know that, Felix. Just kidding.

广告插播

Okay, with that, let's take a very quick break so we can hear from some sponsors who make the show possible.

A 3% top off on my entire portfolio? Uhhhh yes. Who is saying no to that? That's free money. I don't care what's in your portfolio. That's a yes. If you've been looking for an excuse to consolidate your assets, this is it. Until May 31st, Coinbase is giving a straight 3% Bitcoin boost on any crypto or cash deposits all month.

Coinbase One is the ultimate membership to make the most of your money, which, as you know, is how I optimize my finances: - 0 trading fees on thousands of crypto assets - 3.5% APY on USDC - Boosted staking and lending rewards - And up to 4% Bitcoin back with the Coinbase One card

Plus, you can still claim 20% off the first year of Coinbase One annual plans, a $50 Bitcoin bonus when you spend $100 on a new Coinbase One card in the first 30 days through May 31st, plus your share of 5 Bitcoin if you outpredict pro basketball coach Lethal Shooter, and a chance at a private shooting session with him.

It's the perfect time to centralize your assets and maximize your earnings. Get your 3% boost at coinbase.com/unchained. Visit coinbase.com/unchained to claim your Bitcoin bonus. No purchase necessary. See rules and other ways to enter. Terms apply to other offers. Futures swaps via Coinbase Financial Markets. Risk of 100% loss. Payouts event-based. Not investment advice. Not available in Nevada. Coinbase One Card is offered through Coinbase Inc. and Cardless Inc. Cards issued by First Electronic Bank. Bitcoin back rates are based on cardholders' assets on Coinbase.

鲍威尔时代的盖棺论定

All right, welcome back. So again, I said Noelle is the perfect guest to have on this week, and the big reason why is because tomorrow we're going to have a new Fed chair.

So Noelle, I want to give you the chance to either eulogize or provide a bit of a valedictory speech to Jerome Powell — or maybe a little bit of a vote. He's been Fed chair for pretty much most of crypto's era of maturation, for lack of a better term. And given how Bitcoin and crypto were really designed to sort of be a counterweight to everything that happens at the Fed, I would really love to hear your thoughts on what his tenure meant for this industry.

Noelle: It's easy to get caught up in the personality cult around Jerome Powell. He certainly seems like the kind of grandfather everyone wished they had — the kind of guy you'd happily go out for, you know, a marshmallow latte with. But we really can't forget that he was also behind the debanking of many crypto firms. He was behind the closure of Silvergate and what happened in March 2023. He was behind a lot of really bad things that happened to the reputation of the United States banking regulators over the years. And he also really got the inflation story wrong.

So while I do confess to feeling a certain fondness for the man — because I have watched the FOMC press briefings, and I think he's done very well in the communication of the Fed's objectives and its inner workings — we have to remember that there were many things he either knew about and supported that ended up hurting not just crypto, but also the reputation of banking in the United States generally, or he was unaware. And that's also something to hold against him.

And this is even before we give the credibility of the case against him by the Department of Justice a second thought. The main objection was he's just not responsive — not even answering any of the subpoenas, which does also hint at a certain arrogance and an unwillingness to cooperate. Even if you disagree with the premise behind these White House moves, you sort of have to pretend to go along with them for the sake of precedent and protocol.

So again, mixed. He had a lot to deal with. I mean, there's also — there's the repo crisis, there was the pandemic, there was the inflation thing.

Steve: I forgot he was there during the 2019 repo crisis.

Noelle: Exactly. So he certainly had a lot to deal with. But in my book, again — affection, but does not get a free pass.

Fed 独立性的真实含义

Steve: Yeah, I think that's probably how I would put it too. I'm an institutionalist. People who have listened to me on the show before know that I used to work for the U.S. government, the U.S. military. So I have a strong belief in the objectivity, the non-partisanship of key institutions in the government.

And his insistence on maintaining Fed independence to me is admirable, because he's clearly facing a lot of pressure. And it's not even just, in my opinion, directly coming from the White House. But I think it also comes from the fact that a lot of Congress, especially in recent years, has sort of abdicated their responsibility to enact — not monetary, to enact fiscal policy. And that's led to the Fed having to pick up the baton there.

And I know that's something that Kevin Warsh is actually going to try and push back on, because he wants to reduce the Fed balance sheet. He wants it to refocus strictly on monetary policy. So I understand that.

But again, to me, like, if the first line in his obituary as Fed chair, I guess, is that he stood for independence, his second has to be: he missed inflation. As we mentioned. I mean, we all got sick of hearing the word "transitory" in 2021, 2022. And I have to admit, at that time, there was a certain logic to it. COVID seemed to be a one-off event. And it was definitely a long-tail event that people hadn't expected. And if markets reopened, theoretically it should come back down — but that's clearly not what happened. There were the deglobalization factors you mentioned and just ways that supply chains had changed after that. And he missed it.

And that led to record inflation highs in decades. It had to come back down. And that led to the banking crises that you mentioned, because a few of those banks got caught on the wrong end of it with their treasuries. And that led to another unprecedented bailout. So it's hard to reconcile those two things.

Noelle: And it wasn't even necessarily — I mean, he has a reputation for having defended independence, but perhaps that's worth picking at a bit. He certainly came out swinging after the subpoena was served, and that was startling and good for him and everything necessary, in my opinion, especially when his job, as he himself has said, often is messaging and cultivating trust in the institution. I think he did a good job there.

But we do have to remember that there wasn't a lot of independent thinking when it came to shutting down crypto-related banking. That was influenced by politics. That was not showing much independence there.

And we also have to ask ourselves: to what extent does independence mean you are not accountable for any decision? To what extent does independence mean you can simply ignore subpoenas? So again, there's some pushback to be had on what independence even means, and whether this Fed actually did exhibit that level of independence. And then that opens up the door to a fascinating conversation of: what do we mean by central bank independence? When is it a disadvantage rather than advantage?

Steve: Yeah, I guess for me it means he's much more like Paul Volcker and a lot less like Arthur Burns. But as you mentioned, there's different definitions of independence, so that makes a lot of sense.

接班人 Kevin Warsh 与债券市场的"老板地位"

Let's turn to his successor. Kevin Warsh, as I mentioned — he's gone through a bit of an evolution in his own thought on whether or not to be dovish or hawkish. One thing he made clear during his confirmation hearings is that he wants the Fed to move away from fiscal policy, which I believe he characterized as being more of picking winners and losers, whereas monetary policy is a bit more democratic in terms of affecting the whole economy. And that's where he thinks the Fed should sit.

He also has plans to try to create new ways of measuring inflation so the Fed can be a bit more accurate and more predictive in how it exercises monetary policy. And as you said, Noelle, Jerome Powell was front and center and gave a lot of forward guidance. Kevin Warsh doesn't want to do that. What are your expectations?

Noelle: I think that, you know, he can say he wants a lower balance sheet — the markets aren't going to let him have one. It's as simple as that. They're not going to let him have one. And independence, sure, but the bond market is the boss here. And it's relevant for price stability. The Federal Reserve cannot let a disorder take hold in the treasury market. It just can't do that. Because that would affect the dollar, which would affect price stability, et cetera, et cetera, et cetera.

So that is something that we can all wish for. I wish I were a professional piano player — not going to happen.

In terms of forward guidance, I would not be at all surprised to see fewer FOMC press conferences, fewer dot plots. And there's a big debate to be had about whether this is going to be good or bad. It sort of ties in with what the SEC is doing, talking about also reducing the number of reporting periods of the year. Will the market be okay with less information, or will this add volatility? Does that mean analysts actually have to start doing their job, rather than being fed the information quite so frequently?

I don't know. This is a very, very big change. We get so used to a certain cadence, so used to being spoon-fed certain pieces of data, that when that's taken away, is that going to disrupt markets enough that he's going to have to put them back on the table? Or will it be a refreshing change, reduce costs, as well as reintroduce some original thinking that will in the end be healthy? I wouldn't be surprised to see him try that. Whether the markets will let him succeed or not, I don't know. But I think that's pretty much all he's going to be able to do.

He's certainly not going to be able to change the inflation measure, because it's not really up to him. He can influence what people might look at, but we're going to make our own decisions over what we think is important. And he's certainly not going to be able to lower rates.

下一次 FOMC：既不加也不减

Steve: Yeah. And speaking on that last part — not being able to lower rates — I'm sure you saw yesterday, I guess it was yesterday, minutes were released from the Fed's April meeting last month. And although — one thing that was revealed was that there was a lot more acceptance of hawkish views than what came out in the final vote, which was really just to hold rates steady.

And that speaks directly to what he's going to be walking into, which is a higher inflationary environment, but a president that wants lower rates, believes that productivity coming from AI might put a bit of a lid on inflation. And then to add to all this, Kevin Warsh is going to have to prove his quote-unquote "independence" from the president. So what are your expectations for the next couple of Fed meetings?

Noelle: I think the number one thing to look at here is what Trump says. Right now, he has publicly said that Kevin Warsh is going to do what he's going to do. He has full faith in him, good man, et cetera, et cetera — which again is quite astonishing given the environment that you've said Kevin Warsh is walking into.

We know Kevin Warsh isn't going to be able to lower rates, one, because he's just one vote and pretty much nobody else is going to be voting with him on this, and two, Trump can't bash the person he chose so soon after choosing him. So there's a bit of a respite there.

I think one thing the market is getting wrong is expectations of a rate hike. I've been saying no cuts for a long time now, and it's reassuring to see that consensus take hold. Now there's a consensus for a hike this year. I think that's going too far in the other direction. He's not going to be able to lower rates, but nobody's going to have the courage — or not enough are going to have the courage — to vote for a rate hike when there is still so much uncertainty. We don't know if this is transitory or not. We don't know what the other inflationary factors at work are going to do. So there's going to be no hike.

So that'll keep Trump happy. There aren't going to be any cuts either, that may not keep Trump happy, but I think he'll be quiet, and that'll just simply give the Fed some breathing room. It'll give Warsh some breathing room to start to work on his relationships, because in the end, it does come down to how much the FOMC committee trusts the chair, whether they'll go along with what he suggests or not, that'll influence monetary policy going forward.

Steve: Yeah, I'm actually a little reassured in a way. I know like until dissents started happening last year, it had been what — decades before we had dissents. I'm actually more reassured that there are dissents at this point, because you have so many different people in that room with different vantage points and they monitor different parts of the country economically — there should be differences of opinion.

And I'm actually — yeah, I mean, move away from the groupthink. I'm actually looking forward — perhaps season 52 of SNL will have an FOMC meeting. There's enough characters there that people recognize. I think there really could be one.

Noelle: Yeah, that's absolutely brilliant. And here's another twist: what if the next dissent comes from the chair himself?

Steve: Yeah, that would certainly be something. I don't think that's ever happened before. I'm not sure — my history is weak — but that would be again kind of mind-blowing.

Noelle: I doubt — again, I doubt he'd do that, because his job, his number one job now is to get the FOMC members to trust him.

Steve: Yeah, I would say at least for his very first meeting, it would be very unlikely. But who knows after that?

加密资产的现状与 Bitcoin 的双重角色

All right. So we talked a lot about macro. Let's talk about crypto.

Bitcoin and Ether in the market, they kind of got caught up, especially over last weekend when news came out on Sunday about potentially renewed strikes in Iran. There was put on hold, I guess, on Tuesday when President Trump mentioned that a few Gulf countries asked for a little more time to negotiate. But crypto hasn't really recovered yet.

I know there was, I guess, some encouraging news that a few Chinese tankers made it out of the Strait. Reporting seems to suggest that they paid some sort of fee or toll to Iran to do that. I don't know if that's going to be a model moving forward. But crypto seems stuck.

Once again, it seems to be caught between: is it going to be like a high-beta risk asset, or in a world of higher inflation, etc., is the debasement trade coming around, and will Bitcoin and other crypto be able to take advantage? Or is it going to lose to gold again? How do you see, especially the blue-chip crypto assets, behaving right now?

Noelle: Possibly all of the above, to be honest. I mean, right now I don't see any catalyst to push it out of its current range — at least not any positive catalyst. There's always the danger of a stock market rout, which will take the major crypto assets down with it, even though correlations have become somewhat unrelated right now, uncorrelated. Law of nature, gravity will do its thing.

However, will there be a stock market rally? Again, this is up in the air. We just don't know. And the debasement trade continues. I mean, Bitcoin is a debasement hedge. And when people are worried about currency debasement, that tends to be often when Bitcoin does better.

Back in the banking crisis in '23, Bitcoin jumped, and everyone was like, "Oh, it's because people realized the banking system is corrupt and fragile." And I was like, "No, actually, this is because people expect the central bank to step in with stimulus." And that's what Bitcoin tends to react to.

If things go really, really badly in markets and there are signs of the stimulus — the BLISS trade that we were talking about before — then yes, that could well boost crypto out of its slumber. But when it comes to just risk-on appetite, which we are swimming in at the moment, Bitcoin's not doing very much.

In respect, it's because there's so much else at the moment for the risk-forward investors. You've got an endless number of AI-related plays. You've got prediction markets if you really want some fun stuff. There's just so much else to choose from. And this is one of the disadvantages of Bitcoin becoming a macro asset — and this is something I've been focusing on for quite a while. It's a good thing because Bitcoin does have increasingly a position, a role, a place in macro portfolios. But the downside is it's now one among many assets, and other investors are going to choose the higher volatility ones, which right now is not Bitcoin.

So in sum: no catalyst to push it out of its range at the moment, until it does emerge from its range and the momentum will kick in.

Clarity Act 与 DeFi 的解锁可能

Steve: Yeah. I mean, I guess the one catalyst that could be somewhere in the future is the Clarity Act. We haven't had a chance to talk about it, but I'm curious if it's been discussed ad nauseum everywhere. But I'm curious if you have any just quick thoughts on it — either the nature of the bill itself or likelihood of anything being signed into law.

Noelle: I think we will get Clarity Act this year — she says, hopefully. I mean, I wouldn't put it at a high level of confidence, but I just really think we will. That might be wishful thinking.

I don't think it's going to affect Bitcoin that much. I mean, let's face it, Bitcoin does not lack regulatory clarity. What does is ETH. We could see ETH benefit. And if ETH goes up, Bitcoin will go up also, because they often tend to trade in tandem. But overall, investors perhaps be more comfortable with Bitcoin because of clarity. Maybe. But Bitcoin doesn't lack regulatory clarity today.

Steve: Yeah. I mean, one thing that we're paying attention to — I put out a couple of threads on this — is, I mean, you're right. Bitcoin already has that clarity. ETH, for all intents and purposes, also has that clarity. There's even been guidance from the SEC pointing out how a lot of staking is not a security — that staking activities are not the offering of securities, which is at odds with what Gary Gensler's SEC had thought.

It could be an unlock for DeFi just because it gives a little more certainty, especially for some of the TradFi firms to participate in. Whereas — like, DAOs aren't treated as a general partnership, which could be a big liability in certain cases. And hopefully there's going to be better tests to determine when someone falls under FinCEN versus not and has to comply with AML regulations and things like that. That could be an unlock from Clarity.

But you're right. Bitcoin, ETH, even like XRP and Solana, etc. — I mean, even if there has been formal guidance speaking to those being commodities, there's enough judicial rulings, and if nothing else, there's the SEC blessing — what is it, '33 Act ETFs, which are by the very definition commodities — being like wrapping commodities into an ETF wrapper. And I think that gives it that clarity. I hope I got that right. Sometimes I mix up the '33, '40 Act funds. So…

Noelle: Who can blame me on that one? It's seriously very complex.

I have a question for you, actually, if I may. Do you think that Clarity passage is priced in at all? Another way to ask this is: do you think that if it falls through, there would be a sharp drop in crypto prices? Or do we just not care anymore?

Steve: It's hard to tell. And maybe it depends on the asset itself. I tend to think that it's not, just because crypto has been in such a big malaise for the last several months. It's really been coming down ever since President Trump was inaugurated again in January 2021 — er, 2025. If there is some real momentum suggesting that we're going to see passage in the next few weeks — it really has to happen by early summer or I don't think it's going to happen at all — we could see another kickstart.

But I don't think that necessarily means Bitcoin is going to go to 140,000, ETH is going to go automatically to 5,000, and so on and so forth. There's going to be a longer tail.

But I don't think it's completely priced in, and probably just because people still understand how many hurdles still have to be overcome in a short period of time for it to happen. I mean, the Senate bills have to be reconciled, and it has to be reconciled with the House bill. At some point, there's going to need to be ethics language that is palatable to the White House. And all that has to get done and signed into law. I think they're targeting July 4th, which is really six weeks away, maybe.

So I guess that's — I guess I was thinking a little bit real time as he asked me that question, but I think that's kind of where my head's at right now.

Noelle: Yeah, and the devil's in the details. I mean, one thing is the law. The next is the rulemaking that then gets layered on top of that. But one thing I've been wondering about is: if it falls through, it's not the end of the world for crypto, because the SEC is on board, most of the financial regulators are on board, and they can just rule-make away until the end of President Trump's term. And if indeed a party opposed to crypto gets into the White House in '28, well, perhaps by then crypto is just too big to unwind.

Steve: Yeah, you would hope. Or at least — I mean, crypto has proven itself to be such a powerful lobby, a powerful interest group, that I would imagine that, like you said, even if it is a Democrat or anyone, would not be as antagonistic as perhaps Gary Gensler's SEC was, just because there really was not much benefit that came from taking that particular posture.

代币化"创新豁免"的隐忧

I wanted to ask you about one more quick thing, and then we have to start to wrap up here. But I wanted to get your sense of this innovation exemption when it comes to tokenization and how that's going to interplay between these two markets.

Noelle: The innovation exemption — devil in the details. One thing that I'm concerned about is the rumor that it's going to allow third-party issuance. In other words, anyone who wants to can issue a token wrapped around an equity that they actually have nothing to do with. Even possibly — I've heard some rumors — that this would be without the company behind the stock's permission, which I think is absolutely nuts.

I mean, markets, for me, should be about capital formation. Derivatives enable capital formation by creating more liquid markets and giving some assurance to investors putting their money into certain stocks. But when you're creating a market that's just for derivative speculation — tokenized stocks, for instance — and that sort of subverts the ideals of markets, the inherent ideals of markets. And it's not necessarily favorable to crypto, given the reputation that crypto, to be fair, has of just pure speculation. "The only thing it's good for is speculation." We've heard that many, many times. It's obviously not you and I think, but we've heard it many, many times.

That was a concern. However, devil in the details — that may hopefully not happen, in which case the tokenization innovation exemption is great news. Great news in that it encourages some experimentation. It's going to be careful — they're not going to be crazy, they're not going to let anyone come in, they're not going to let unlimited amounts be spent. But to encourage entrepreneurs and market participants and innovators to experiment with this new type of marketplace that we know is going to be a big feature of markets five, ten years from now — I think that's a big step forward. The confidence that they're not going to get in trouble for trying new asset formats.

收尾：通胀与被忽视的等权指数鸿沟

Steve: Yeah, absolutely. Just last question. I'd love to have you answer one of two things: one, what's one chart, one indicator that you're really paying attention to over the next few weeks or months? Or what is one contrarian opinion that you'd like to share?

Noelle: Hmm. I think what I love about the questions you ask is that you don't pull punches. You do go for the overlooked stories. So congratulations on that.

Indicator — I'm going to go with inflation. Inflation kills societies. And if we don't keep an eye on it, then there could be some very bad scenarios going ahead. Inflation will move the bond market. Inflation will dictate monetary policy. Inflation could also have a very big impact on fiscal policy at a global level. So inflation — and inflation is something you can't hide from. You can massage the indices, as the incoming chair has said he wants to do. It's not going to help. You can't hide from inflation. So that.

And the contrarian indicator — I'm going to go with something that I think people are ignoring. And that is: we're so excited about the S&P 500 reaching all-time highs. We're overlooking the widening gap between the S&P 500 and the equal-weight index. There's the market-cap-weighted index that we all follow that's reaching all-time highs after all-time highs. And there's the equal-weight, which isn't. And the gap is widening. The last time the gap was widening at this rate was in 1999. For the dot-com bust.

So yeah — anything that gets top-heavy, we know there's laws of physics: anything that gets top-heavy ends up tipping over.

Steve: Yeah, absolutely. All right. Well, Noelle, we'll have to have you back on again. Thank you so much for joining us. Thank you, everybody, for watching and listening.

节目预告

That concludes this week's episode of Bits and Bips: The Interview. But don't go anywhere. Laura is going to be right back on Unchained. She's going to speak with Dio Casares on how pre-IPO pricing is moving — Unchained. SpaceX perps just launched from Hyperliquid. Anthropic and Open — I'm sorry — and then Anthropic and OpenAI shares are there too. And NASDAQ Private Market is teaming up with Pallet Market. So stick around.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一、央行权杖的隐秘易主：当债券市场成为"影子主席"
最让人震撼的洞察并非来自鲍威尔的离任，而是 Noel 那句几乎被一带而过的话——"**The bond market is the boss here**"。这其实揭示了一个被所有人误读的事实：所谓"Fed 独立性"早已不是问题的关键，**真正的权力中心已经从 Eccles Building 转移到了债券交易台**。Kevin Warsh 可以宣布想缩减资产负债表，但市场不会"允许"他这么做——这句话的潜台词是惊人的：货币政策的"决策者"并不在 FOMC 投票室里，而是分散在全球数万亿美元的国债持有者手中。
这与一种古老的政治哲学产生了奇妙的呼应——**主权的实际所在地，往往不是法律上的所在地**。鲍威尔被指责"不够独立"时，他其实早已不在权力的真正位置上。这是一种"幽灵主席"现象：所有人都在讨论谁该当 Fed 主席，但 Fed 主席这个职位本身的实质权力，正在被结构性地掏空。
## 二、跨领域连接：BLISS 交易 = 央行版的"道德豁免券" = 中世纪赎罪券
Gita Gopinath 提出的 BLISS（Big,

item.title: [Dwarkesh Clips] The Difference Between a GPU and TPU
item.dc:creator: Dwarkesh Clips
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一个被忽视的悖论：GPU 其实是"分形的 TPU"

最反直觉的观察是：GPU 与 TPU 的差别并非"通用 vs 专用"，而是同一种结构的不同分形层级。视频里那句"GPU 其实是把一大堆小型 TPU 平铺在芯片上"几乎是一句颠覆性的判词——它意味着我们长期以为的"架构之争"，本质上是一道粒度选择题（granularity problem），而不是哲学分歧。一旦你接受这个框架，"NVIDIA vs Google"就不再是路线之争，而是在同一条频谱上选择不同采样点的工程权衡。

隐藏的物理学：架构选择其实在和"导线"作斗争

更深一层的洞见在于：决定 GPU/TPU 形态的，不是算法，而是"线"。TPU 把所有数据压缩到两条主干线进出大型 systolic array，所以带宽被几何形状卡死；GPU 因为有 16 条以上的小路径，反而能在小尺度内移动更多数据。这与城市规划里的"多中心 vs 单中心"困境惊人地相似：单中心（TPU）摊薄固定成本但形成交通瓶颈；多中心（GPU）冗余高、但局部通勤极快。芯片设计师其实在做和城市规划师一样的事——只不过他们的"通勤者"是电子，他们的"红绿灯"是 register file。

跨领域连接：systolic array 与"流水线生产 vs 工坊制造"

systolic array 越大、register file 的成本就被摊得越薄——这与工业革命时期工厂规模化的逻辑完全同构。亚当·斯密的扣针工厂之所以高效，是因为固定的工位、固定的工序，让"切换成本"无限趋近于零。而 GPU 的 SM 更像手工作坊集群：每个作坊都有自己的小工具、调度员（warp scheduler）、库存（register），灵活但冗余。

由此可以做一个大胆预测：未来 AI 芯片的真正突破不会是"更大 systolic array"或"更多 SM"，而是可重构粒度——视频末尾提到的"splittable systolic array"正是苗头。这意味着芯片正在向生物组织学习：细胞既能独立工作（小 SM 模式），也能在需要时融合成肌纤维（大 systolic 模式）。真正的下一代架构，可能不再是 GPU 或 TPU，而是"可以在运行时改变自己分形维度的硅"。届时，"硬件"和"软件"的边界会再一次被改写——因为芯片本身会成为一种可被编译的形状。

📝 转录文本

GPU 与 TPU 的高层组织差异

问： what is the difference uh at high level between how a GPU and a TPU work?

答： Yeah. So I mean I think there's sort of a high level organization principle that is different. Um and then there's sort of inside the cores what are different, but we'll look sort of uh outside the uh — like at the high level.

GPU 的顶层结构：均匀网格

Um, so we'll take a GPU um and a TPU, and what does like sort of the top level block structure look like? Um if you think of this as the whole chip, um in each case, um the organization of of the GPU um uh is mostly a bunch of uh almost identical units which are these um — these are the SM. Um and then they've got a an L2 memory in the middle, and then a bunch more of these SMs on the bottom. Um and so uh there's sort of this fairly regular grid of of cores.

TPU 的顶层结构：粗粒度大单元

Um uh and then like if we look at a at a TPU in in comparison, um you you uh you end up with much coarser grained units of uh logic. And so you you end up with something like um uh some large number of um — maybe like maybe just a few — m uh matrix units. These are the um — these are the big like um systolic arrays. And then in the middle you've got some vector unit. And then you've got your um matrix units at the bottom.

So um now sort of like matrix units with a vector unit in the middle — sort of this is the whole TPU chip. You can sort of think of take scaling this thing down into a really tiny unit with a smaller matrix unit, smaller vector unit. And that is sort of what a an SM is.

核心类比：GPU 就是"一堆小 TPU"

So sort of at a very high level point of view, the the GPU has a lot of tiny tiny TPUs um sort of tiled across the whole uh chain.

问： So like you're suggesting the tensor core within a streaming SM is anal— analogous to an MXU?

答： Yeah, it's very very similar. Yeah.

问： I see. And so if you had more like more lack of structure, having a bunch of tiny TPUs makes a lot of sense. Whereas if you kind of just have like huge matrix multiplications, you're like, why don't we just — why don't we avoid the co— the cost of having the individual SMs with their own registers and warp schedulers and things like that? Why don't we just like make a huge thing and like amortize those costs across the whole thing?

规模化的权衡：systolic array 越大越省，但代价是数据搬运

答： And I mean I think this shows up in how large you can grow things. We've we've sort of seen this theme — like especially with a systolic array where larger systolic array amortizes the register file costs better. Um this sort of design allows you to have larger systolic arrays. This whereas the sort of GPU design constrains you to having small units uh of everything.

Um there is a trade-off however. Um the there ends up being um — because of this sort of coarse grained separation of things, there you need to move a lot of data from the vector unit to the matrix units. Um and so like you need to move a lot of data through through a sort of like two two lines of parameter here.

Um whereas if you sort of look at the equivalent thing here, you've got vector units everywhere and you need to move data through this line, through this line, through this line, through this line, through this line, through this line. So the amount of data you can move between a vector unit and a matrix unit is actually much higher in in a GPU than in a in a TPU. Because — because like — it's like instead of having to like move all the data through these just two lines, you're moving all these data through like 16 lines or something of of of wiring instead in a GPU.

数据搬运的距离与能耗

问： Right? But also you might have to move across less area, which I mean is also saving like — it's energy.

答： So, so data ends up moving — like if you can operate entirely within an SM, the data movement is much smaller. But then the moment you want to operate across SMs, like it becomes sort of more more complicated and expensive.

一种可能的融合方向：splittable systolic array

问： So you don't have comment, but one might expect that a thing Maddox might try to do is to get the GPU-like smaller structure of um systolic array surrounded by um SRAM, but also at the same time make it so that like the things you need in an SM to support the CUDA architecture uh but take a bunch of — bunch of space, you might discard.

答： Yeah. Um, we've talked publicly about something which we call a su— splittable systolic array, which is sort of in in some sense you can think of as like big systolic arrays that can be small systolic [ar]rays as well.

If you enjoyed this clip you can watch the full episode here and subscribe for more clips. Thanks.

观看原视频 →

最反直觉的观察是：**GPU 与 TPU 的差别并非"通用 vs 专用"，而是同一种结构的不同分形层级**。视频里那句"GPU 其实是把一大堆小型 TPU 平铺在芯片上"几乎是一句颠覆性的判词——它意味着我们长期以为的"架构之争"，本质上是一道**粒度选择题（granularity problem）**，而不是哲学分歧。一旦你接受这个框架，"NVIDIA vs Google"就不再是路线之争，而是在同一条频谱上选择不同采样点的工程权衡。

item.title: [邦妮區塊鏈 Bonnie Blockchain] 川習會世紀談判五大關鍵！習近平「修昔底德陷阱」沒說的事？【邦妮區塊鏈】
item.dc:creator: 邦妮區塊鏈 Bonnie Blockchain
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、表面叙事的反转：谁在朝贡谁？

这场会面被包装成"川習會世紀談判"，但若把鏡頭拉远，會发现一个被主流叙事掩盖的悖论——真正"出访"的不是国家元首，而是美国的产业版图本身。AI 芯片、电动车、iPhone、华尔街资本、支付系统、半导体、农业供应链——这不是一场谈判随行团，这是美国经济的"全息投影"被打包送到了北京。

历史上类似的场景，是 19 世纪英国马戛尔尼使团带着工业革命的精品去见乾隆。当时英国以为自己在"展示实力"，乾隆却把整船的钟表机械当作"贡品"收下。今天的剧本是否在某种意义上反向重演？当一国把全部产业代表团作为筹码摆上桌时，对方读到的不是威慑，而是依赖的证据。

二、被刻意"没说出口"的修昔底德陷阱

标题中提到习近平的"修昔底德陷阱沒說的事"——这本身就是一个值得停下来思考的语义结构。修昔底德陷阱的原意是崛起国与守成国的战争宿命，但习近平真正没说的，可能不是"我们会不会打"，而是一个更隐蔽的命题：当两个超级大国的产业链已经像神经网络一样彼此嵌套，"战争"的定义本身就需要重写。

这里有个跨领域的连接很少有人提：生物学上的"共生衰退"现象——两个深度共生的物种一旦试图分离，往往不是一方胜出，而是双方同步退化（想想珊瑚与虫黄藻的白化）。今天的中美关系，更像珊瑚白化，而不是冷战式的零和搏斗。波音 200 架飞机的订单、芯片的灰色通道、支付系统的清算节点——每一项都是"虫黄藻"在拒绝离开宿主。

三、最让人意外的观察：CEO 们是真正的谈判主角

一个反直觉的洞见：这次会面的"地缘政治内容"——台湾、伊朗、朝鲜——很可能只是 CEO 议程的烟雾弹。

为什么？因为元首之间的政治议题在过去十年里几乎没有实质性突破，而每一次"会面"之后，真正发生改变的总是产业接口：关税豁免清单、出口许可证、支付通道、农产品配额。政治家在台上谈台海，CEO 在台下重画供应链的版图。

更深一层的连接来自博弈论中的"信号成本"模型：一个谈判方派出的随行代表，比他口头承诺更能暴露其真实优先级。Trump 带去的不是国务院的中国问题专家，而是英伟达、苹果、波音、摩根的高管——这等于用最高昂的"信号成本"告诉北京：我们真正在意的不是意识形态，是市场。习近平没说出口的那句话或许是："我早就知道你们的优先级，但我会让你们以为是你们说服了我。"

这才是这场谈判最隐秘的层次：它不是大国博弈，而是产业资本通过国家元首的身体完成的一次跨境握手。

📝 转录文本

开场：川習會的全景定位

This video is the focus of the Xi-Chuan meeting.

President Trump and President Xi met in Beijing on May 14th. You may have seen many news headlines.

谈判桌上的明面议题

  • China promised to buy more agricultural products.
  • Boeing got 200 early purchase of aircraft.
  • All sensitive issues such as Taiwan, Iran, North Korea were discussed on the table.

真正的"代表团"：美国产业全员到场

The entire US CEO also went to China with Trump. These people represent the most important economic networks in the United States:

  • AI chips
  • Electric vehicles
  • iPhones
  • Airlines
  • Wall Street capital
  • Payment systems
  • Semiconductors
  • Agricultural trade

结论性观察

So this meeting is more like the United States bringing all the benefits of finance, technology, manufacturing, payment, agricultural supply chain to Beijing.

观看原视频 →
