item.title: [Business of Sport] The Battle for the Future of County Cricket
item.dc:creator: Business of Sport
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、最深的悖论：拯救者必须被驱逐

Daniel Gidney 的故事是一个让人不舒服的寓言——他做对的每一件事，恰恰构成了他必须离开的理由。900万英镑的年收入翻到3500万、收回了 Test status、建了两间希尔顿——按照任何 MBA 教科书的标准，这是案例研究级别的成功。但 Lancashire 的会员投出不信任票，不是因为他失败了，而是因为他把一个 cricket club 治理成了一个 hospitality 集团。

这里藏着一个被广泛忽视的"代理人陷阱"：当你雇一个人来救火，他往往会把整个房子改造成防火建材厂。Gidney 来自 Ricoh Arena 和 Sodexo——他的潜意识里，球场是"有座位的会场"。13 年后，他交付了一个商业奇迹，但交付的不是会员们想要的那个奇迹。这不是 KPI 没达到，而是 KPI 本身被偷换了——这是所有职业经理人体系最隐秘也最致命的失败模式。

二、跨领域的意外连接：Lancashire = 维基百科 + Tottenham + Lululemon 的混合体

把这场"内战"放到更广的坐标系里看，会出现一个奇怪的三角：

  • 维基百科式的治理困境：mutualized 结构意味着"所有权 = 使用权 = 投票权"三位一体，这在和平时期是优雅的，在需要资本支出时是瘫痪的。维基百科基金会近十年也在反复经历同样的张力——社区觉得专业管理层"离我们越来越远"，管理层觉得社区"不懂运营现实"。
  • Tottenham 隐喻的精妙之处：你注意到 Charles 自己讲完这个类比就立刻反驳——"Tottenham 球迷不拥有 Tottenham"。这句反驳其实揭穿了现代体育最大的幻觉：球迷文化全世界都在模仿"所有权感"，但只有 mutualized 结构真正把那种愤怒武装成了法律权力。Lancashire 的会员不是在抱怨，他们在行使股东权。
  • Lululemon-Sarah Story 信号：注意 Dame Sarah Storey 的辞职声明几乎一字不差地复制了 ESG 报告的语言——"gender equity"、"diverse leadership"、"beyond the financials"。这是上市公司治理话术，不是体育俱乐部话术。当一个 cricket club 的董事会开始用 Lululemon 的年报口吻说话，会员的不信任票几乎是必然的——这是一种语言层面的"文化漂移"，远比财务数字更早预示治理危机。

三、最反直觉的洞察：Yorkshire 用"放弃所有权"换"竞技尊严"，Lancashire 用"保留所有权"换"商业繁荣"——两者都在交易，但只有一个清楚自己在交易什么

这是这期节目最值得停下来想的地方。

Sanjay Patel 在 Yorkshire 做的事情，本质上是一次坦白式的谈判：他不掩饰 demutualization 意味着控制权外流，他反复要求会员"理解后果"。他在做的是一笔明牌交易——用部分所有权换 Headingley 的世界级地位。

而 Lancashire 的董事会做的恰恰相反：他们保留了 mutualized 的法律形式，却在治理实质上单方面修改了俱乐部的 telos（目的因）——从"打好 cricket"悄悄换成了"经营好场馆和 ESG 形象"。他们没有要求会员同意目的的转换，因为他们甚至没意识到自己在转换。

这里出现一个让人脊背发凉的系统性规律：最危险的治理失败，不是那些公开的权力争夺，而是那些目的被静悄悄替换、但形式仍然原样保留的组织。教会、大学、家族企业、工会——所有 mutualized 或 mission-driven 的组织，都死于同一种病：形式还在，目的已经飘走，而内部人浑然不觉。

电子投票被取消、AGM 安排在 bank holiday——这些"北朝鲜级"的小动作不是策略，是目的漂移到了某个临界点后的自动免疫反应：当组织的实际目的与章程目的分歧足够大，治理层就必然开始本能地规避真正的民主程序。这是诊断标准，不是道德败坏。

Mike Smith 的尾声其实是隐藏的论文摘要：Boycott 说他"从不是最强的击球手，但他能让最好的球员发挥出最好"。这恰恰是 Lancashire 董事会丢掉的东西——elite sport 的领导力不是 KPI 管理，是让顶级人才信你、跟你、为你打。Gidney 优化的是表格里能填的东西；Mike Smith 留下的是表格之外的东西。这个对照，节目里没明说，但放在结尾的时机几乎是导演级的精准。

📝 转录文本

引子：一场被低估的"内战"

One that I've been keen to do. We've kind of, you know, moved around it a little bit in the past as we've talked about cricket and cricket in this country in particular in England, but it's a particular relevance at the moment because there is both a broader issue across English county cricket that's been far more historical. There's also a very kind of real-time example where we can bring some of the information out and give you some proper context to it.

What I'm talking about is a disagreement. I don't really know — you'll probably say I should put a stronger word on there — but a disagreement between…

Furious.

A furious row between Lancashire County Cricket…

Civil war.

…between Lancashire County Cricket and its members, or a rebel group.

Well, you see, you've just made a false distinction there. You've said there's a row between Lancashire County Cricket Club and its members. But of course, Lancashire County Cricket Club is its members, because it is a mutualized entity, which means that it is member-owned. And effectively it's a row between the members of Lancashire County Cricket Club and the executives and non-executives who are employed and engaged to act on their behalf.

And that makes it a proper civil war, right? That's not like an owner of some club and his paid executives in some fight against the fan base. This is the owners of the club fighting against the people who, in theory, they appoint. Quite an interesting situation.

英国郡级板球的四大豪门

So Lancashire County Cricket Club — let's just put a bit of context around this. There are three, maybe four giants of English county cricket.

Surrey — the biggest county cricket club by revenue and by success in their history in the country. They own the Oval and have a great catchment area. They're right in the center of London, and so on and so forth. They have been very successful in the last 10 years or so, as well as having big revenues and updating the Oval. Big success story.

Surrey County Cricket Club — very successful, just because it's going to feed in on cricket, very successful on non-cricket.

Absolutely. Warwickshire County Cricket Club plays at Edgbaston, a real bear-pit for England cricket where, you know, it's probably one of the grounds with the biggest atmosphere. It's a really rowdy, loud place. Again, they make good money. Again, generally speaking, Warwickshire have been more or less successful in the last 10 to 15 years.

Then you have Yorkshire, who play at Headingley. Headingley is a historic Test cricket ground of England, which is currently not being used very much by England because it's in bad nick. We'll come back on to that later on after discussing Lancashire.

And then you've got Lancashire. Lancashire play at Old Trafford. Old Trafford is another of the great historic Test venues of English Test cricket. But Warwickshire are at Birmingham, you've got Surrey in London — albeit they share London with Middlesex, but they're much the bigger club within London. And then you've got Lancashire in Manchester, which would be the third biggest city in the country. And then you've got Yorkshire at Leeds, which would be one of the next contenders for the biggest cities in the country.

Lancashire 的辉煌与坠落

So where do we start this? Lancashire County Cricket Club — huge county cricket history, very successful in the past, particularly in one-day cricket. Responsible for a lot of the most famous England cricketers: Andrew Flintoff, Mike Atherton, Jimmy Anderson. These are quite recent superstars of English cricket. And also the home of a lot of very famous overseas players who have come to play here — people like Clive Lloyd, the West Indian great, so on and so forth.

This is a big, big club. And if you go to Old Trafford, you're very aware: wow, this is a big place. This is a serious, serious place.

But around about 2010, it effectively was facing bankruptcy. Old Trafford had lost Test match status because it was deemed as a facility not to be up to holding Test matches. It was crumbling, basically. And as a result of that — because when you're not hosting international cricket, when you are one of these big county cricket clubs, that places a lot of pressure on your finances, because you're still financing a big arena, but you're not having the international matches that help you really do that. Whereas if you're a smaller county cricket club, your operating costs are much lower. You don't get the England matches to bring that extra revenue in, but you might at least be able to just operate in a rather smaller town.

数字说话：一场国际比赛值多少钱

It's very important. And I spoke to the chief executive of Surrey, Steve Elworthy, and he outlined that an international cricket match for Surrey is worth £5 to £6 million. And Surrey's total revenues are £55 to £60 million.

Right. So one single Oval Test match is 10% of Surrey's total revenue, and they will quite often have two.

Yeah. So if you think about Surrey's revenue of £55 million quid, £10 million quid of that coming from a couple of Test matches — but they also have one-day internationals and so on — you might end up getting towards almost half of their annual total revenue coming from hosting England matches.

It's a big business. And that's where Lancashire — or Trafford — losing the status to host it, where it is still good but not at £55 to £60 million, is a big problem for them, and therefore required something to happen to change that.

Daniel Gidney 登场

So in 2012, in a terrible situation, they appoint a man called Daniel Gidney. Daniel Gidney had previously been running the Ricoh Arena, which is Coventry City's stadium, but at the time Coventry had lost control around that time of their own stadium and it was being run as a general leisure facility.

They've got a casino, they've got a hotel. Yes, they also have a pitch which I think was played there. Wasps were playing there and Coventry sometimes were playing there, sometimes not, depending on what the deal currently was between Coventry City and the Ricoh Arena. But the Ricoh Arena was a significant size standalone venue without operating its own sports club. So Daniel Gidney was operating the Ricoh Arena as a sports business executive with no responsibility for playing matters.

And prior to that, he ran hospital catering services for Sodexo. Again, large-scale venues with lots of varying catering needs, etc. So these are quite complex jobs, where you operate under quite a lot of pressure and where the margins are thin.

So Daniel Gidney arrives at Lancashire County Club having made a good job of what are difficult jobs — operating significant-size leisure venues — having lost all of their England contracts and having a crumbling venue, not very well-run club, going bankrupt, etc.

So Daniel Gidney comes in. This is the important part of the story. Daniel Gidney comes into Lancashire Cricket Club with the club facing bankruptcy, in the abyss, in absolute total catastrophe — with the club very much acting as a county cricket club, mutualized, owned and run by its members, but effectively in appointing a chief executive like him, they delegated authority, pass the parcel, for turning the club's finances around.

13 年后的成绩单

And it's hard to say that Daniel Gidney has not done a very, very, very good job of the job that he was appointed to do. So 13 years later, that £9 million has turned into £35 million a year of revenue. They've regained their England status. Old Trafford has been effectively redeveloped. There are two hotels there, conference center, and the whole thing is now not quite on the same level as the Oval, but getting there. It's a very serious, great venue.

I was there last summer for the Test match — for the England-India Test match. It was great. What a great venue.

But the problem is, when you have a brilliant thing happening at a cricket club — at a sports entity, sorry, a sports team — and really the standout success that you have is the opening of a Hilton hotel in your tenure, you can see where there's going to be probably some tension.

为什么 Gidney 还是必须走

So as a sports executive and a sports investor, I just want to give the beleaguered Gidney a round of applause. Right? What this guy has done in 13 years is going to have positive repercussions for Lancashire County Cricket Club for 5, 10, 15, 20, 25, 30, 35, 40 years. So why has he just had to announce his retirement?

Because the membership are very upset with the state of the cricket team and, most importantly, the traditional form of cricket, which is the four-day county cricket system. And fans have —

They've been crap at one-day as well.

Yeah. Right. So it's not that they're very upset — it goes well beyond that. They've submitted a vote of no confidence. In the board. This is not just in Gidney. This is in the board as a whole.

So these rebels aren't just some dusty members. This includes the aforementioned legends — people like David Lloyd and Mike Atherton, the elite of the England cricket establishment. These are the people who played for England, coached England, who hold the biggest media jobs in the country. Some of the most high-profile cricket people that there are in the whole world, who also happen to be legends and members of Lancashire County Cricket Club, have submitted a vote of no confidence backed by thousands of the members in the board, that has effectively in some way, shape or form made Gidney's position untenable, and he has announced his retirement.

But the board is still in place, and the board is trying to resist this. You can't even call it a takeover, because they'd be taking over their own club.

"夺回自己的俱乐部"

How would you describe it?

It's like taking back control of their own club. Now, what's the complaint? Charlie mentioned it really, right — which is that during those 13 years of business regeneration, Lancashire County Cricket Club have been, frankly speaking, crap at cricket. During that time, they have won one trophy — was a one-day trophy of some kind — and they have been relegated from the first division of the championship.

So if you think about Lancashire's status within the game as being one of the big three or four clubs, that's kind of the equivalent of Tottenham going down. So Tottenham have gone down, and on top of that, all the former Tottenham legends have basically said: "We don't think that there is anybody at this club who understands anything about football."

Hang on, saying that's just like Tottenham. Anyway, that's just what Tottenham fans say. But the difference is that, whereas Tottenham fans do not own Tottenham, Lancashire County Cricket Club members do own Lancashire. But the board they have is resisting this and have basically started tweaking the rules around how such a no-confidence motion might be treated.

治理戏法："North Korean levels of governance"

Didn't they put it — there was no wet signature, wasn't that right?

So first of all, historically they have allowed electronic voting. Now they've said you actually pretty much have to turn up with an ink and quill and literally sort of do this type. And secondly, they decided to hold the AGM on a bank holiday Monday.

So this is like North Korean levels of governance, right? This is sort of — yeah, it is, it is a former genius. You're right.

So this is civil war.

多元化叙事 vs. 板球叙事

Now, there's something which I found interesting about this. Having just applauded Daniel Gidney for his business acumen and what he's done from very difficult circumstances to turn around the financial fortunes of Lancashire County Cricket Club, there's another element to his legacy which he is very proud of, and which the chairman of Lancashire County Club, Dame Sarah Storey — the Paralympian, multi-gold-medal-winning Paralympian — she is also very proud of.

So this is a quote from Dame Sarah Storey on Daniel Gidney's resignation:

"In addition [brackets] to turning the business around, Daniel has driven gender equity at both board and executive level, creating one of the most diverse leadership teams in UK elite sport. Beyond the financials, he has also been a passionate and influential advocate for gender equity."

So this is regarded by the board as being a key part of Daniel Gidney's strength. What he's done is basically really focused on women's cricket, on pushing girls' cricket, and on getting lots of people onto the board who either come from different communities, or are female, or whatever it might be.

What the rebel leaders, including the former England legends, are saying is: you don't have anyone on your board who understands anything about cricket. And the result of that is that we're crap at cricket. And the point of our club — because we're a non-profit-making organization — is not to open hotels. The point of our club is to be good at cricket.

精英体育的残酷逻辑

So this is where the whole thing sort of really runs up against each other. And my experience of elite sport, having seen it through Formula 1 and horse racing and football, is that — how can I put this without running foul of the cancellation police? Have I already…? I think I've probably already been cancelled.

Charlie, I think you've been long — that's why you have to come on the podcast.

I should be on the BBC. I have to come on a podcast and say — because basically I've been cancelled. Someone did actually say in the comments last week that you were obviously very misunderstood on Sunderland 'Til I Die, and he's delighted that you've come across in this manner.

I don't know what he thought I came across like on Sunderland 'Til I Die. Anyhow, there we go.

Now, elite sport — and I'm just going to put this blandly — has no space for these bromides. Unfortunately, elite sport is brutal. You cannot afford to be box-ticking when it comes to elite sport.

Can you run a successful county cricket club that encourages girls' sport? Absolutely. Yes.

Can you stuff your board full of people who are there to tick various diverse boxes? No. No, you can't. Because if your competitors are stuffing their boards with people who are experts in elite cricket and you do not, then you are going to fail at cricket, and then you're going to have problems.

What's happened here is that the board of Lancashire County Cricket Club have been marking their own homework. They've been saying, "We built two hotels and we've welcomed onto our board lots of people from diverse backgrounds." And the members of Lancashire County Club are going, "We don't care about the extra hotel, the second hotel. We don't care about how diverse your board is. We want to beat Yorkshire."

And we are members of this cricket club because we want to be part of something which is successful in sporting terms.

Gidney 的反击与"霸凌"指控

But they also want to exist. And that's the balance, right? That's the friction.

Come on that with Yorkshire.

But can I just jump in there, because on your point, Daniel Gidney's response to a lot of the attempts to remove him and the board have been interesting, and I have a Belgian football quote that he put out.

Yeah, well, yeah, I'm glad. Very good job.

He said: ECB chairman Richard Thompson said we want cricket to be the most inclusive sport, which is a big thing to say. Let's look at a vote of no confidence in our board. We've got some amazing people. We've got a number of women and we've got John Abrahams and Naveen Singh. And this feels a bit like "I'm an old white entitled man that wants to get on the board of the club." And they don't like the fact that in our county partnership agreements we have to have 40% women on our board and 22% representative of ethnic minority. They acknowledge that it is not legally binding, but they want to put pressure on the board to resign. Effectively, that is bullying and intimidatory behavior.

Of course, he has to resign after that, because he's basically abusing his own employers, which is not viable.

That's when they're going for the board.

Yeah. My point — we have fans that want it all, and we're all guilty of it. There is an element here of: as a chief executive of a sports club, as you know, any decision that you make, you have to appreciate the fans need to come on that with you in some way. Because the majority of the time, if you're coming in to really shake something up, it's probably not going to be in a way that the fans are used to experiencing their club or their sport.

So if you are going to try and save a club from bankruptcy and it's not going to be done by improving how they're playing on the pitch, you need to make sure that that membership is absolutely integral to how you bring that club forward over the next 10 years, whatever it may be. Even if some of it is completely pointless posturing, even if some of it doesn't have any influence on the outcome — this is a hearts-and-minds game at the top level as much as it is a delivery game.

对照案例：Yorkshire 与 Sanjay Patel

Totally agree. So yesterday I was listening to the radio in the car, and they were interviewing the chief executive of Yorkshire County Cricket Club, a guy called Sanjay Patel. I'd never heard Sanjay Patel speak before, but I was very, very impressed.

Why? Because Yorkshire County Cricket Club is in the middle of a controversy. It's not a civil war. It's not like that. But the board of Yorkshire County Cricket Club wants to demutualize Yorkshire County Cricket Club. That is, to take it away from being a member-owned organization to taking it into being a private limited company, because that then gives it a structure which means it can attract external capital to come into the club to do what needs to be done with that club.

Chief of which is developing Headingley. Because if you remember what we said, Old Trafford had lost its Test match status 10 years ago, 15 years ago. Headingley has not officially lost its Test match status, but it's not due to hold a Test match for the next two or three years, which is pretty dramatic for Headingley, right? And disastrous financially, as we discussed earlier on.

I'd say as a fan, I'm devastated by that, because I think Headingley is an amazing place to have a Test match.

And Leeds is a great place to have a Test match. It's just the history of the place is unbelievable, and the atmosphere — we're saying at Edgbaston earlier on, you know, the West Terrace, Headingley is, you know, serious, serious atmosphere.

So there are many amongst the membership base who are in favor of this, and there are also many, as you might imagine, amongst the Yorkshire County Cricket Club membership base who are against this, because they don't like the idea of private money coming into the club where it might "quote" expect a return — cuz that's what investment, that's the key, right?

"如果我们想成为精英"——Sanjay Patel 的明牌谈判

So Sanjay Patel was being quizzed in some detail, and quite, you know, forcefully, by the radio interviewer who was putting the points very eloquently actually of the people who do not like this move. His repost, which was delivered very calmly and in a very balanced way, was:

"If we want Yorkshire County Cricket Club to be amongst the elite performers in cricket, and if we want to make sure that we offer young people from Yorkshire the opportunity to become what they need to be able to become, Headingley has to be able to become a world-class venue. The amount of capital that is required to do that is not achievable from within our membership base. We can't simply put out a capital call to our members saying, 'By the way, we need £10,000 from each of you.' Therefore, the money has to come from external sources. We can't achieve it simply through bank lending, and therefore it needs to be private capital. Private capital can't come into a charitable situation except as a donation. If we could achieve a donation, that would be lovely, but no one's come forward and offered a donation. What we do know is that there are people with private capital who would be interested in taking an equity stake in Yorkshire County Cricket Club in return for putting money in — not to then control the cricket club, because the structures we're looking at putting in place would not enable that, but to make sure that if Yorkshire County Cricket Club did become very much more successful, and the commercial limited company bit of that therefore was perceived to become more valuable, that ultimately, of course, they could potentially sell their stake and get their investment back, maybe even including a bit of a profit, maybe including a bit of a loss. But without that, how can we actually bring in the money to redevelop Headingley and get England cricket matches coming back to Leeds?"

Right. So the answer is, you can't.

He could so easily have gone for the Yorkshire County Cricket Club members as Daniel Gidney did. He kept on resisting it. He kept on saying: "If it is decided by the members that they want to stay fully mutualized, that is their right. It's their club. It's my job as chief executive to lay the facts out in front of them as to what the implications of that would be. They would have to accept that there will not be England matches here. They would have to accept that as a county cricket club we would not be able to compete financially at the top of the county championship against the other big boys of English cricket. If everyone says that they are okay with that, then I too am okay with that. But I want to make sure that they fully understand the implications of it."

I thought he handled it absolutely brilliantly. He kept coming back to — sorry, the reason why I brought it up is that Sanjay Patel kept coming back to being successful at cricket. This was a continuing thread in the conversation: we want Yorkshire Cricket Club to be successful at cricket.

目的的漂移

And this, I think, is the thing that has provoked the massive divide at Lancashire. Is that, if you read the quotes from Daniel Gidney and Sarah Storey — the chief executive and chairman of Lancashire County Cricket — not just now but over a period of time, their own priorities don't appear to be the same as the people who ultimately employ them. And that doesn't matter which business you're in, Charlie — that's a problem.

That's what I meant by communication. You know, if Gidney is great in one thing, right, and he's smashing it out the park, it doesn't really matter if he's not bringing the fans along with him.

But would you not need then a chairman who did represent…? You would, right? And the problem here is they've doubled down on two people who come from exactly the same point of view, and Sarah Storey's and Daniel Gidney's point of view is not the same as the members'.

In context, Yorkshire have a chairman called Colin Graves — who used to be the ECB chair, and he is Mr. — he's brilliant. Mr. — and actually I think he'll be leading the meeting with the shareholders very soon to decide on this.

"The Hundred"、私有化与可投资性

And the demutualization piece is an interesting one right now, because English cricket has just had significant investment. People will know about The Hundred.

Yeah.

Interestingly, Yorkshire were the only team to sell 100% of their franchise. So they had gone from the Northern Superchargers to Sunrisers Leeds, one of the IPL groups, and they sold for — it was about £100 million. And this money was very, very carefully segmented in certain ways. So the clubs couldn't just use it to offset their running losses, basically. It had to be used for projects.

So when you're looking at this kind of double thing of demutualization plus selling the stake having a very specific purpose to go and create long-term value, bring Test matches back — that's an investable model. So if I was looking at demutualization right now, I go: as an outside investor, I could actually attract something. I'm making my county cricket team very investable, which they haven't been for a very long time.

Well, I mean, that's right. They have been literally uninvestable as a mutualized business. You are literally uninvestable.

整体生态数据：18 个郡赚的钱不如一个 Aston Villa

You know, to be really clear, and as we were going through this, I just took a slightly broader look at some of the numbers.

Leonard Curtis last year did a very comprehensive financial study conducted on English domestic cricket. The total revenue of all 18 counties was £306 million. To put that into context, Aston Villa last year was £378 million.

So all 18 counties made £306 million. Eight of the 18 counties ran at a loss in their financial report. I actually thought it'd be more than that. But without ECB support and 100 income, you know, it's very questionable whether some of the counties would survive — and that came through when looking at the percentage of revenue that was reliant on ECB funding.

So Surrey, for example — we've mentioned £55-ish million in revenue, 6% of that revenue is from ECB funding. You look at Northamptonshire at the bottom end, 71% of their revenue is ECB funding.

Yeah. So you're looking at ECB funding basically being about £3 million per club, but that £3 million at Surrey is 5%, whereas the £3 million at Northampton, whose total revenues are five and a half — yeah, 5 — or 70% revenue ratio, 12-to-1 really between Surrey there.

You've got the big three — Surrey, Lancashire and Warwickshire — right at the top, generated 44% of all county cricket revenue between them.

Yorkshire 的历史尊严之痛

And going back to the Yorkshire point, it will be a dagger through the heart of Yorkshire to hear the big three being described as Warwickshire, Lancashire and Surrey. Which is Sanjay Patel's point — which is that the two big historic power bases of English cricket have been Yorkshire and Surrey. Now Warwickshire and Lancashire absolutely in the next tiny little next tier, but Yorkshire and Surrey are the ones who've won the most county championships. They're the ones who provide most English legends, and have had two of the stadiums that have always been Test venues, always the most famous Test venues.

没有"生存的权利"——观众数据

And this is a challenge though to the members and the fans as well, right? We've talked about this a few times. No club, no sports team in this age has a right to survive. You have to support it. And the numbers —

So interestingly, the ECB actually stopped reporting county championship figures after Covid, but what they have been able to see is that in 2018 it was around 600,000, and that dropped to about 545 in '21, and in '22 the guess is around 410, and it's gone to 390 since then. So you've had a 35% decline in county championship.

They've also switched the calendar, which is not helpful to county championship cricket. You know, it used to be played at various moments throughout the summer, but the advent of The Hundred and the prevalence of T20 cricket has meant that county championship matches have been shunted to the first part of the season and the back end of the season — when really county championship cricket is the type of thing that needs to be watched on a nice sunny day. You want to be there all day. But also T20 Blast matches regularly exceed 15,000, 20,000.

So prioritizing in a calendar based on the interest of the fan, you know — if there is this absolute determination to establish these county teams as four-day teams, they need to start making that county championship match viable.

暂留的大议题：Test vs T20 与 McCullum 的"假期"

Well, I think that's a story for another day, because that goes back into a discussion we had right when we started doing this podcast about the relative importance of Test and one-day cricket and the importance of English cricket prioritizing the development of Test players over necessarily T20 players. Because ultimately that's where the general public in this country views the success of the England cricket team — primarily through the lens of: are we beating India and Australia at Test matches?

So there are complications in that. Brendan McCullum — the shift in narrative as part of retaining the services of McCullum has been that they were going to pay far more attention to county cricket in some of that selection, even though he may not be attending a lot of it, as we know.

You say a lot of it.

Um, any of it.

Why?

Well, because he's on holiday.

Still?

Still. But just to be quite clear, we said — Taylor is far from — we said about two to three months ago, by the way: do you know the England cricket coach is going to be on holiday at the start of the English cricket season? Really? Yeah, apparently he's still on holiday now. Cricket — right, nice work if you can get it.

尾声：致敬 MJK Smith

Yeah. To wrap up, I just want to say one thing, which — very sadly this weekend, an absolute legend of cricket died. Mr. Mike Smith, MJK Smith as he was known. He was 92. I'm playing — he's the grandfather of one of my best friends. He played over 50 Tests for England. He played for Warwickshire for 19 years. Scored 39,800 runs in 637 matches. Also, Charles, the last person to play cricket and rugby for England.

What a legend.

And that's what I was going to say.

What a legend. I remember — yeah, I remember seeing pictures of him playing in his specs. And it was interesting reading a very affectionate article about him in the papers this morning by Geoff Boycott. Really affectionate. I mean, you don't hear Geoff speaking generously about that many people that often. It was like an absolute hagiography. It was almost a tear-in-the-eye job, where Boycott was saying:

"In the early stages of my career this guy basically was part of the reason why I ended up making it. He was my captain. I'll always call him my captain."

And it was a sort of remarkable tribute to the power that that style of leadership can have — where, because he was never the greatest batsman, but his ability to get the best out of the very best players, like Boycott, is why actually his captaincy was a success.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一、最深的悖论：拯救者必须被驱逐
Daniel Gidney 的故事是一个让人不舒服的寓言——**他做对的每一件事，恰恰构成了他必须离开的理由**。900万英镑的年收入翻到3500万、收回了 Test status、建了两间希尔顿——按照任何 MBA 教科书的标准，这是案例研究级别的成功。但 Lancashire 的会员投出不信任票，不是因为他失败了，而是因为他**把一个 cricket club 治理成了一个 hospitality 集团**。
这里藏着一个被广泛忽视的"代理人陷阱"：当你雇一个人来救火，他往往会把整个房子改造成防火建材厂。Gidney 来自 Ricoh Arena 和 Sodexo——他的潜意识里，球场是"有座位的会场"。13 年后，他交付了一个商业奇迹，但**交付的不是会员们想要的那个奇迹**。这不是 KPI 没达到，而是 KPI 本身被偷换了——这是所有职业经理人体系最隐秘也最致命的失败模式。
## 二、跨领域的意外连接：Lancashire = 维基百科 + Tottenham + Lululemon 的混合体
把这场"内战"放到更广的坐标系里看

item.title: [All-In Podcast] SpaceX’s $2T Case, Nvidia’s Shock Selloff, America Turns on AI, Trump Pulls AI Order, Bond Crisis?
item.dc:creator: All-In Podcast
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、最反常识的观察：Anthropic 在向 SpaceX 付租金，而非 AWS

每月 12.5 亿美元、三年 450 亿美元——这个数字背后藏着一个被几乎所有评论员忽视的范式转移：云计算的霸主不再由"数据中心运营经验"决定，而由"建造速度"决定。SpaceX 三个数据中心的建设周期是 122→91→66 天，这条曲线本质上和 Falcon 9 的复用次数曲线是同一条曲线——一个造火箭的学习率被直接迁移到了造算力的学习率。

这意味着什么？SpaceX 不是在跨界进入云计算，而是云计算本身被重新定义为"重工业制造问题"。 当 AWS/Azure/GCP 还在用 IT 思维迭代时，Elon 用航天工业的"制造良率"思维降维打击。这条 90 天合同终止条款看起来是 Anthropic 的退路，但反过来看——这是 SpaceX 在告诉市场：我有信心你 90 天后还是离不开我。

二、跨领域连接：Copernicus、核扩散与 AI 的反人类中心论

Friedberg 那段被埋没的洞见值得再放大一倍：他把 AI 的社会抗拒类比为哥白尼革命的心理冲击。但更深的连接他没说透——

哥白尼革命之后人类用了 400 年才在心理上完成"我们不在宇宙中心"的接受过程（从伽利略到达尔文到弗洛伊德是连续的"人类自尊三连降"）。AI 是第四次降维——智能不再是人类专属。而这一次，社会被要求在 4 年内完成同样深度的心理重构。

这就解释了为什么 Cloudflare 的 Matthew Prince 用 "the measurers"（测量者）这个词会引爆愤怒——它不是冷酷，而是把人类从"主体"降格为"功能"，是哥白尼时刻在职场版本的瞬间上演。Chamath 骂他"PR 学校的弱智"骂错了角度：Prince 没有犯传播错误，他犯的是本体论错误。

而 Karpathy 的"recursive self-improvement"和核扩散的类比更精准：Freeburg 提到 1949 年苏联拿到核武图纸是"被故意泄露的"——因为科学家们认为单极世界更危险。今天 Anthropic 把 Karpathy 招进去做模型自我改进，本质上是在加速一个不可逆的"智能扩散"过程，逻辑和当年罗森伯格夫妇是一致的：与其让某一方垄断，不如让平衡尽早到来。

三、隐藏模式：这一集所有"反派"都在做同一件事——把不确定性转移给他人

把这一集所有看似无关的话题串起来看：

  • Dario 制造监管恐慌 → 把 AI 的不确定性转移给小竞争者
  • Zuckerberg 在员工电脑装录屏软件 → 把"被替代的不确定性"压在每个程序员身上
  • Trump 撤回 AI 行政令 → 把"什么算 frontier model"的不确定性留给未来博弈
  • 特朗普 vs 习近平的会谈 → Gavin 一针见血："every day the Strait of Hormuz is closed is relatively good for America"——把能源不确定性导向对手
  • Cloudflare 的 Matthew Prince → 把"被裁理由"的不确定性贴在员工身上（用"measurers"这个标签）

这是一个系统级的"不确定性外包"现象。所谓的"AI PR 危机"本质不是技术问题，而是社会发现：原本由企业承担的不确定性，正在被系统性地下放到个体身上。年轻人在毕业典礼上 boo Eric Schmidt，骂的不是 AI，是这种风险下放的机制。

最锋利的洞察留在最后：Chamath 说他只追踪 5 只以下的股票，"the vicissitudes of the market don't give me anywhere near the sugar high it used to"。这句话和整集的 AI 焦虑形成了诡异的镜像——当 AI 让一切加速时，最聪明的资本反而在做减法。这可能才是这个时代最被忽略的 alpha：注意力的稀缺性已经超过了算力的稀缺性。Karpathy 离开特斯拉去做 5 分钟实验、Chamath 只看 5 只股票、Gavin 守着 Pareto frontier 上的几个点——顶级玩家都在收缩 attention surface。这与市场鼓吹的"AI 让你能做更多"完全相反。

Freeberg：AI 公关危机的三重根源

Yeah. Freeberg, what's your take here on the AI PR crisis, if we'll call it that? We had three different commencement speeches that were booed. Eric Schmidt being one of them, two other ones by maybe less notable folks. When you hear young people booing AI vociferously, why are they doing that, Freeberg? And what's your take on the overall PR problem and how to turn it around?

Uh, there's a long answer to that question. It relates in some ways to your concerns about socialism and polarization of wealth.

What is the long answer? What's the long answer?

I mean, that's like, why do people hate technology? Why do they hate this technology? They love their phones, they love the internet, this technology they hate.

第一重：杠杆与不对称收益

I think that there's an underlying view that technology creates leverage for a small group of people, which creates power imbalances. Nothing represents that more than AI — that a small number of people that control and profit from and benefit from AI are gonna end up getting outsized returns relative to the broader population. That the time to diffusion of the technology, because ultimately all technologies like commoditize and diffuse, but the time to diffusion here is such that it's gonna be like extremely asymmetric for society.

And I think that there is something fundamental about that. It's like, you know, nuclear bombs really created this moment in people's minds in the mid 20th century that by the back half of the 20th century gave everyone a high degree of skepticism about technology and science generally. That those who have the knowledge and those who engineer solutions with the knowledge can create outsized advantages for themselves and it puts the rest of us at risk, the rest of the world, the rest of the population at risk. And because those questions about when does this benefit me, how does it benefit me can't be answered today, the economic benefit that's accruing to the few today becomes the narrative, it becomes the story and it becomes this like power system that a few people take from the many. And so there's something deeply disturbing for the average person about that. They don't understand how it works, why it works, what it'll do for them, when it will do it. And all that they're being told is that some people are making trillions of dollars. So I think that it's pretty obvious why this has got such a backlash.

第二重：境外势力的长期渗透

Secondly, I think that there's a deep amount of external energy that's fueling this anti-technology sentiment in the United States and has been for decades. I think to Gavin's point, I don't think it's just China with NGOs today. I think that there is a long history of state actors intervening in media activities in foreign nations to try and create the sentiment and fuel a sentiment that reduces progress in that competitive state. I think this goes all the way back to KGB design during the Cold War, and it's been refined and honed and improved over time. This is not just some conspiracy theory. There are plenty of great books about this. The techniques of what's going on specifically today I don't know enough. I don't have any great details on that. But I don't think that there's no foreign interest in seeing technology advancement slow in competitive nations. The United States probably does similar things to other nations. And I think that that's probably a key part of this.

第三重：哥白尼式的心理冲击

And then I think this third piece is like when the Copernican revolution happened, it was a mindfuck, you know, like Heliocentricity was a totally new way of thinking for humans, and it was deeply disruptive to the church. And it was deeply disruptive to the power centers, which were the centers that could tell people, Earth is at the center of the universe, we're in control, we're the direct channel to God. And the idea that the sun is at the center of the solar system, and we spin around it, and we're a tiny speck in the universe was very hard for people to grasp. There's something about AI that's very like, not human centric, and it kind of shifts and with the ego of the human, it's almost anti humanist. And I think that that's like a deep psychological current — a lot of people and their disdain for this technology, it fuels it, it's not the cause, but I think it fuels it.

So I think there's a lot of complicated aspects to this, J. Cal. You know, I don't think there's like a simple "put Chamath on a podcast, and he'll solve the problems with AI right now." I think that there's a real set of shifts happening. And there's a real set of global competition underway where various state actors and interests are competing with each other.

我们应该减速吗？核扩散的类比

Yeah. Freeberg, do you think that we should slow down?

I don't think you can.

No, no, no. Do you think we should slow down?

No, I think — I was just talking to some people on Zoom right before this — but I think after the Manhattan Project, the research labs were set up to maintain our scientists that worked on the Manhattan Project from effectively leaching back or leaking back to Russia and Germany and other places that were adversary to the United States. And they all were against the nuclear bomb. They worked on it because it was necessary for the United States security. But then when Russia got ahold of the secrets, they were leaked because people were worried that if the US had all the power, there would be no counterbalance to the power. And so the nuclear secrets were leaked to Russia for that purpose. Then when Russia had the nuclear secrets and they began developing hydrogen fusion bombs, and it was clear that they were gonna race ahead, the United States raced ahead with developing nuclear bombs as a counterbalance to Russia. When the proliferation began, there was no stopping it. It began, and you had to have this balance in the world. Otherwise you have effectively an asymmetric power that can do whatever it wants globally.

I think there's that moment in the world right now where if the United States does not advance its AI technology, the availability of it, TBD, industry, taxation, all these things that we're talking about doing, there will be someone else that will. And if someone else does, we can go through what would happen. There's a complicated game theory on this, but what would happen if China had sufficiently advanced models and sufficiently advanced scaled deployment of those models relative to the United States? As you do that analysis, you realize, wait a second, that's probably not a healthy place for the world to be. It's also probably not a healthy place for the United States to be the only one with AI. And so I think what we end up seeing is if we do try and slow down AI, we kind of lose this moment of balance that's necessary when you have a technology proliferation, like we saw with the arms race after World War II, that we're going to see again here.

自动驾驶 vs 仓库分拣：分阶段过渡？

Chamath, you, I think bring up a good point. You know, should we slow it down or could we slow it down? There actually have been some discussions about ways to do this.

One of them would be, hey, with self-driving, people are scared that all these cab drivers are going to lose their jobs — Uber drivers, cab drivers, bus drivers, truck drivers. This is, you know, over 10 million people in the United States driving things for a living. Would you be in favor of some of the announcements that that will be a phased rollout, it won't happen all at once. In other words, those people will be given some amount of job security to stay behind the wheel with it.

Another example that's been given is if you put Optimus into Amazon factories — or the figure robot just did like a week of just sorting packages. I'm sure everybody saw that video. We'll insert it here. That figure robot sorting these. Hey, if Amazon deploys those, there'll be a tax on those per hour and we'll tax humanoid robots in some ways and then use that for, say, retraining people. Those are two very specific conditions and approaches that people have been promoting. Do either of those resonate with you in any way?

I think it's interesting that in all of those discussions, I've yet to see an actual survey of only the truck drivers and only the package sorters. The question that I would have is, do the people that do these jobs want these jobs? And if they do, then there's a reasonable claim to make to keep those jobs the way that they are. If you're saying this is the job that I do, I love it, I'm able to provide for my family, great. That's a very different argument than, well, you know what? Amazon has 35 or 40% churn inside of their warehouses. And we should probably ask the question, why is that? Because if it was such a great job, I suspect the churn would be three or 4%. So what exactly is it that we want to protect and have you asked them?

And I think that this is just, again, a bunch of people in the peanut gallery who want to take a moral high ground and try to make some other group of people feel guilty or feel bad. At no point are we actually asking the conversation that we should be having.

被撤回的 AI 行政令

Which is, it's interesting to me that there was supposed to be an EO, a presidential executive order that was announced today, and then it was pulled, it was scrubbed at the last minute. Did you guys notice that? Yeah. And yesterday, what was leaked was everybody that was attending, it was all the big neo labs CEOs, and it was all the big hyperscaler CEOs, including a friend of the pod, Nikesh Arora, shout out to Nikesh. And then it was scrubbed an hour ago.

Why was it scrubbed? And the president said that there were aspects of the bill that he didn't agree with. And as far as we can tell, the aspects would have required some amount of supervision, insight, review from the federal government. Of language models specifically, of these frontier models is what I read. Not language models, just of AI, because there's going to be many different kinds. They're not always going to be language models, but of AI.

So look, I think Freeburg is right. We are in a proliferation with China. I think it's actually good that China is less than nine months behind us. I think it allows us to find a détente where we have a certain magnitude of capability that they also have, and that allows all of us to then seek peace and abundance. And the fact that we are orthogonal societies, we are organized differently, increases the probability of finding peace using the René Girard kind of framework of mimetic theory, than if it was like us and another country that was exactly similar to us.

So I think what we need to do, we probably need KYC. I think that that should be something that us and China get together and say, you don't want it to get into the hands of people you can't control. You probably already KYC those models anyway, inside of China. You already review those training runs before you allow these models to get released. We already know that that's happening. Yes. So we should probably do some sort of KYC so some crazy person doesn't create some biological weapon. I think that those are some reasonable ground rules. But otherwise, Freeburg is right. You have to take a little bit of a deterministic view here, which is that we are in this existential race. And we need to get to the place where each of us, meaning us in China can look each other in the eye and say, all right, weapons down, so to speak.

Gavin：自我监管 vs 政府监管

Gavin, I'm going to hold you to answer two questions. One, should we run frontier models? Because that's specifically what was mentioned in the leak about the EO — frontier models, the powerful ones. Should they be run through some sort of testing before they're released? And should there be some regulatory framework for that? That's my first question to you. Yes or no question, and then you can explain your answer.

I just think it's such a complicated topic. It feels we're a little early for that. I don't love the idea of the United States doing it and no one else doing it. I think in a world where we hold hands with China, I think that's much more palatable and we're aligned and we trust each other and have verification capabilities, I do think.

Great. Yeah. Let me then rephrase it. Should China and the US come up with a simple battery of things that have to be tested before these go out, including bioweapons, terrorism, and that genre of, and that vertical of just really known dangerous things, just like the FDA might test for poisons or contaminants in a food or a drug. Would you be in favor of that? I'm curious.

So a few things, like the one thing that's great about America is there is — we have other forms of regulation. Self-regulation, sure. One, we have self-regulation. Also, we have the courts. If an AI model company behaves irresponsibly, they know that there are ways that people who have been harmed can seek recourse. 二、SpaceX 的 S1 与 1.75 万亿估值

All right. Topic two is SpaceX just filed their S1 on Wednesday. They are aiming to raise 75 billy at a 1.75 trillion with a T valuation. This would be the largest IPO ever by more than double Saudi's Aramco $29 billion IPO a couple of years ago. Listing is expected mid-June, likely June 12th, ticker will be SPCX.

We got a lot of interesting information in the S1 teardown. Obviously, SpaceX has three main business units. Starlink is the money printer right now, but there is a second one that's emerging.

  • Starlink did 11.4 billion in revenue last year on 50% growth with 4.4 billion in operating income. Over 10 million people are now subscribing to Starlink. That business could easily be hundreds of millions of paying subscribers.
  • The Space business is but 4 billion in revenue, it's growing 17% growth which would still be strong growth, but had 650 million in operating losses.
  • AI did 3.2 billion in revenue, that's more than double year over year growth, but it had 6.4 billion in operating losses.

SpaceX had 20 billion in capex spend last year, over 60% was for the AI compute build out. And obviously they were trailing Anthropic and OpenAI and Gemini in terms of XAI playing catch up. And he did a big reboot of that, as we saw on the Twitter.

Gavin：数据中心建造速度的指数曲线

Well, I think what's important about Elon Web Services — does make me laugh — but $15 billion, that means the AI business right there is going to quadruple. It has already effectively quadrupled. I think what's important about that is there's a stat in it that for, I think their first data center was 122 days. The second one, it took them 91 days. The third one was, I think, 66 days. They build data centers dramatically faster than anyone else at a lower cost. And now that you have a clear off-take partner, and I would expect partner to become partners, there is no reason they can't start stamping these data centers out really fast.

And having watched Jensen for a long time, it is important to Jensen that his GPUs be used. And so GPUs will be allocated to who can plug them in, turn them on, and start converting electrons into tokens. And so I think this business can grow dramatically faster than I think maybe what anyone could have contemplated three months ago. But $15 billion from Anthropic is extraordinary.

Important note, it can be canceled by either party with 90 days notice. Just want to make sure we also have that in there. So that means Elon might want his compute back or Anthropic may find another solution. So they do both have an out. And I think that's probably an important provision for everyone.

Cursor Composer 2.5：Pareto 主导的实证

But I think the other thing that came out this week, which was not in the S1, Nick, can you throw up the Pareto Frontier and maybe don't include the email and the names and everything, but the Composer 2.5 stat? I think this is really extraordinary.

Cursor's Composer 2.5 model came out this week. This is Pareto dominant. This is just three, four weeks of doing reinforcement learning on Colossus 2 with Cursor's data. We will never know, but Cursor allegedly has more tokens of coding data that exist on the public Internet. That is a stat from, I think, more than a year ago, so I'd imagine it's grown significantly. I think Cursor and Anthropic probably have the most proprietary tokens of coding data.

What this jump from Composer 2 to Composer 2.5 showed us is that when you do an appropriate amount of reinforcement learning using that data, let alone injecting it into the pre-training of a new base model. Because Composer 2.5 is the same base model as Composer 2, which is KMI K.25. This is amazing. This is three or four weeks and it is Pareto dominant, the Pareto frontier. If you draw a curve of the blue dots, you can see Composer 2.5 is literally well outside the Pareto frontier. That's after three weeks.

And what's going to happen next is you're going to have a new base model with a cursor model in it. Then the cursor model are held using the biggest coherent compute cluster in the world. And I think this is — It's significant. It's extremely significant for XAI and Cursor. And Cursor was dead in the water in terms of access to compute, and they were falling very far behind Codex, Google, Anthropic, and then Elon let them on Colossus and boom, instantly their models are growing faster. And this could be — we could be sitting here a year from now and they're the dominant player.

Elon 卖算力给 Google 和 OpenAI？

And could we be sitting here, Gavin, in a year and Elon is selling compute to Google and OpenAI? Is that a possibility or not?

Well, I think it's much easier to see him selling compute to Google. And I think there have already been posts about that. And for sure, Google is going to want to be part of orbital compute. You know, it's very funny. The only people who are skeptical of orbital compute are those people who are not involved in data centers or space. Google, Anthropic, Amazon, NVIDIA — they are all very convinced that orbital compute is going to be reality and obviously SpaceX is extraordinarily well positioned for that. But I do think that Composer 2.5 data point is really powerful. Keep an eye on it.

Grok 4.3 与 GrokBuild：harness 的重要性

Yeah. And then the other thing that's come out is Grok built — so what Grok lacked that a lot of other models had — and I do think it's important to remember that the newest version of Grok 4.3 is on the Pareto frontier for all frontier models, and you're either on the frontier or you're not. And the companies on the frontier are XAI with one build of Grok 4.3, which is a 500 billion parameter model, Google 3.1 Pro, and then OpenAI and Anthropic. And that's it. Those are the companies on the frontier. And Google today each have one dot on the Pareto frontier. And obviously you want as many dots as possible.

But Grok lacked a harness. So Claude had Claude Code, OpenAI had Codex, and now with GrokBuild, there is a harness that is available to Grok and, you know, as I'm sure—

A downloadable app to translate into English, that has integrations to all your favorite stuff, whether it's Notion, Gmail, Slack, et cetera. And if you don't have that, it's just like using a basic chatbot from a year ago. So now they have their downloadable, it's in market and they are cooking with oil on it and they're playing catch up, but they're moving fast.

And it's more than just an app. It's a runtime, it's an environment, it manages state, it manages memory. It makes these models dramatically more useful to the extent that I think the people at the Frontier all agree that the harness is essentially as important as the model, especially in an agentic world, and the harness and the model need to be developed together. So the release of GrokBuild and the pace at which they're iterating is I think also really encouraging.

So now you have Cursor, you have the Cursor data, you have a clear existence proof that the Cursor data is really important because Composer 2.5 is now Pareto dominant and the most selected model on Cursor. And that's also important because these evals don't capture everything. This is why people on X talk about the vibes and the vibes on Cursor 2.5 are also really good. They're immaculate. With GrokBuild, I think these are really important developments.

Yeah. There was — Elon was incredibly frustrated by the state of affairs at XAI. He was very public about that. And he's less frustrated now, and he's shipping a lot faster. And so I think that says something, and he has been very focused on it.

Freeberg：太空数据中心作为"文明备份"

Freeberg, your thoughts on the SpaceX IPO and what this collection of companies might look like a year or two from now, especially if, like many people believe, Tesla and SpaceX merge. We can revisit our earlier conversation about an anti-tech, anti-AI, anti-progress world and society ahead. And if there is an effort, a concerted effort, an organized effort by governments to stop or block access to information, restrict freedom of speech, restrict freedom of purchasing or buying things to control more things. And I think there's a trend line in this direction right now globally.

The internet has always been lauded as this kind of system that provides an open alternative to physical commerce — that you could create digital commerce, digital information, digital media that you could share. And it's almost this digital representation of society. But the internet has to sit physically somewhere. And the assault on data center buildout in the United States right now, I think may indicate the importance of having an alternative internet from the ground layer up.

If you have a communication network that isn't restricted and controlled by a government on Earth — it's almost like a backup for civilization, but it's a backup for progress. And I don't own any SpaceX shares, and I'm not trying to sell the book of SpaceX, but I think that there's like an important aspect of can you create a system that's not under the control of governments as a way to ensure humanity's progress, to ensure civilizational continuity if things go south, if things aren't good, if things are restricted, and if, you know, fundamental forms of tyranny start to restrict speech, restrict commerce, restrict information flow and whatnot.

And I think having like a space based communication network, space based data centers, and space based communication back down to Earth wireless, I think is generally a good thing, it's good to have a backup. Yeah. So put all the economics aside and the multiples and valuations and whatnot, and whether it's SpaceX or not, I think the idea that you could have data centers store information, transmit information, route information and access information through space based systems that can't be controlled, manipulated or destroyed by governments is important. And I just I like that.

Yeah, if you — most people don't remember this, but when Elon was starting SpaceX, the original idea when he was running around with the day out and they were looking at some rockets and getting carriage from Russian rockets was to back up the biosphere. And he came back from that trip. And I remember talking to him about it. And he said, I think I just have to make my own rockets because that's actually where the problem is. And it would be easier just to make my own rocket to back up the biosphere. And he wanted to put geodomes, like geodesic domes in space with all the plants and wildlife and creatures. And what incredible vision and then it, you know, there was the necessity of actually getting that up into space, and that's the unknown origin story.

I will say this, Chamath, the idea of putting data centers in space seems completely doable, even though there are a bunch of people who are saying it's not. When you compare it to what happened with SpaceX with Starlink, which people said also wouldn't work, and now he's got 10,000 Starlinks up there, the difference between a Starlink satellite and a data center satellite is really not that different and—

They're pretty different.

Well, conceptually, of course they're physically different, but conceptually, Elon put 10,000 Starlinks up. Is he capable of putting 10,000 — No, look, the size is much bigger, Jason. The foils are much bigger. The wings are much bigger. But it's, yeah, my point is, it's not different if he has the new starship, because that's 10 times bigger. Yeah. You can't just scale like this. That being said, it's technically possible. I think he will be the first one to figure it out.

Chamath：如何为 2 万亿 SpaceX 定价

But I'll just take a much more pedestrian take, which is, okay, you're sitting here, and if I'm asking myself, "Chamath, how do I underwrite SpaceX at $2 trillion?" Here's the basic math that I would do.

Well, last year it did $18, $19 billion. It'll probably do 25 to 30 this year. Okay, so I'm buying this thing at a fairly costly premium, right? So what am I buying?

Well, I'm buying probably the most important internet infrastructure project that's happened since the internet itself. That's going to scale to hundreds of millions of users. And the reason that's going to scale to hundreds of millions of users is it's just very useful and it's just going to become cheaper and cheaper and cheaper. So that's number one.

I'm buying a delivery infrastructure, but I think over time GDP plus 10, GDP plus 15, kind of a grower. So good business, valuable business, but it's the underlying platform that allows everything else to happen.

And then I'm buying an AI business, which will be at the top level the apps, but at the bottom layer, all the compute capability. And I think when you scale that out, like, why is Colossus so valuable to Anthropic? Maybe that's like a good question to ask. It's because if you look at who's actually capable of delivering a gigawatt data center, these guys are the closest. Like an actual gigawatt. And the reason is, is that this stuff is very complicated and very, very hard. I think you've probably heard this famous story where Jensen was like, yeah, he was the one that figured out this one thing that nobody else could figure out so that you could strip a bunch of racks and drive a bunch of east-west traffic and make the whole thing work together.

So I suspect what happens is next year, it's probably 40, 45 billion. And then the year after that, it probably doubles again. So now I'm buying it at 20 times revenue. And you would say, well, why can you buy a company like this on revenue versus earnings and cash flow? And I think the reason is because what the revenue does is it gives him the operating leverage to go and invest in all of these other businesses that ultimately consolidate his differentiation and his competitive moat. Because what he creates is a capital moat that then accelerates a technology moat, that then accelerates an execution and a learning moat. And that flywheel, when it starts to spin very quickly, and you would say, hey, hold on a second, it's probably spinning quickly now. I would say we're at the beginning of the beginning. Because he's, again, he still has all these disparate assets. I still don't like the fact that Tesla's over here. And as I've told you, that will get merged in. And now you have this incredible corpus of physical capability — movement of all kinds, X, Y, and Z, right? You have learning capability, you have infrastructure, you have all the connectivity. That thing will look very cheap, I think, in a few years.

And he has this one thing that nobody else — if you look at the big CEOs, who steps on stage where you're always curious, okay, what has he got up his sleeve? You know, the Steve Jobs, "Oh, and one more thing." This is the only guy. At the scale of civilizational out of left field, he's the guy. Whether you like him or you hate him, he's the guy. And there's a premium that is well-deserved that comes with that.

So if you had to pick an underwriting case, Jason, I would flex the revenue and realize that terrestrial data centers alone are $100 or $200 billion of revenue by 2030, 2032. And that means just building it. So already you're buying it at 20 times revenue just for that business. Everything else is great.

And that's just the Colossus on the ground, earth-based. It'll be Colossus 3. No, no, forget space for a second. It's like Colossus 3, Colossus 4. It pencils out with that, yes. Getting a nameplate one gigawatt — look, it is freaking hard, man. Getting a gigawatt nameplate working is almost — and then, by the way, there's all the stuff that he can do on land that he's the best position to do.

DC-to-DC：与 Jensen 的下一个合作

I'll give you one example. There's a great push that Jensen's making, which he needs a partner, and I think Elon becomes a natural partner — to do DC to DC. Forget all this DC to AC to DC nonsense that goes inside of a data center, all the lossiness.

Explain what that is in English for everybody.

Just like, look, you go through a bunch of power transformations to actually deliver the electrons into the rack so that, as Gavin said, you can generate the token on the other end. Today, it's very inefficient. It's very costly. It requires a lot more power. It requires a lot of cooling. It requires complexity. And what people have said is, wow, if we could just do DC to DC, like it comes in as DC direct current, it goes right to the rack as DC, but it requires a fundamental re-architecture. Jensen needs a design partner and a thought partner to get that done. He's probably the only one.

So I just think there's a lot of reasons where you can underwrite this to a multiple of revenue plus the X factor, which is just the creativity and the one more thing.

Starship 的规模与可重复使用性

Love it. And then here's — I'll have you comment on these, Gavin, after it. Here's the rocket sizes, just in terms of scale. And most people have not actually seen a Starship in person. When you see this thing in person — and I've been inside that rocket. I think we were together, Gavin, when we were in the first build. And inside of that, you can fit 300 people. It's basically like a giant, if you thought of a commercial aircraft, that's what it feels like when you're inside, right? Like a 747 in terms of the amount of space in it, especially when you compare the Falcon Heavy, which is their workhorse. Correct, Gavin?

Yeah. And Starship's going to get bigger based on their roadmap. It's going to get a lot bigger. A lot bigger.

And then this one is the most interesting that this started trending last week. This is cumulative payloads launched 1957 to today. SpaceX is basically about to — and just that, and this is really what exponential growth is about, and this is what disruptive technologies are about. Just from 2012 to today, SpaceX is about to dwarf the rest of the world's cumulative payloads into space.

So, Gavin, maybe take the other side of it. When do these data centers in space happen? What has to happen for those to be a reality? When does that hit SpaceX's bottom line? We've heard from Chamath, hey, here's all the things that hit the bottom line in the short term and midterm. But I think data centers in space would be a midterm to long term play. Three years is what I'm hearing. So tell us about that business in relation to the two charts I just shared.

Well, the one thing I would just say — well, first, all those charts about launch are before Starship was operational. And most of that mass to orbit was done by Falcon. Yes. And Starship — Falcon is reusable. Starship is designed to be rapidly reusable. This is a critical difference.

Let's say Blue Origin successfully solves reusability. They're where SpaceX was 10 years ago. Let's say China solves it 10 years ago. Reusability means that you extensively refurbish the rocket, the engines, everything, the fairing, it takes a lot of time. Maybe you can fly that rocket again in 30 days, 60 days. Rapid reusability means that you can fly and land the same rocket multiple times per day.

If SpaceX — and it's really hard to do rapid reusability, I think it would have been not trivial but much easier to have Starships working if it was just designed to be reusable. That's not enough for what Elon wants to achieve of a Moon base, a colony on the Moon, a colony on Mars, mass drivers on the Moon. You need rapid reusability, and that is why Starship is such an engineering challenge and will be such an impressive achievement when they have rapid reusability. But I do think that mass to orbit, rapid reusability in Starship means they—

When do you predict they'll have that rapid reusability to space, you think?

I mean, we're gonna find out. We're gonna find out, you know, we find out. I'm gonna be at Starbase today for the launch. Yeah. So, you know, we turn over cards and, you know, it's important for everyone to remember, like, let's just say it's a fireball — SpaceX will still learn from this. Yes, they learn from failure. If you don't fail, you're not learning. Same way if you're not wrong, you didn't learn anything in that day. And this is a brand new rocket, a brand new booster, a lot of new technology. There's a lot of instrumentation on it. So whatever happens today, SpaceX is going to learn and rapidly iterate.

I don't know when. I don't want to make a prediction. I would guess a year or two, maybe sooner. I think that's most consensus.

A year or two is, I think, perfect consensus, yeah. We'll see.

Even if everybody else solves reusability, mass to orbit from everyone else will quickly asymptote to a very small number.

太空中的 H100

As far as when will orbital compute be a reality? I would say, well, it is important to realize there is a working H100, an NVIDIA H100 GPU in space today. Andrej Karpathy both trained a model on and used for inference. So this is, you know, it's... There's a working GPU in space today. And NVIDIA is making a space designed version of this, which will be different because the heat sink has to be different. There's a bunch of weight that you put on it when it's in a data center that you don't need in space. And you also have to reinforce it for the journey to space because these things are going to shake and break apart. The data center ones are not made to have that many Gs put on them. So you're going to need an industrial strength one that gets to space that has a different profile.

Yeah, Gavin?

Well, one of the things that's been so magical about SpaceX is they're very good at engineering the rocket and the payload so that you can use semiconductors that are not designed to be in space or satellites in space, and those semiconductors are a lot cheaper. My firm, Atreides, is an investor in a company called Exit Labs, that it's a matter of public record is going to be in essentially every Starlink. And the chips were not designed to go to space. They're not radiation hardened. You know, everyone, you know, SpaceX really liked a lot of the specifications on the chips. And then it's like, well, we'll see how they do with rad testing. And they just so happened to pass. And so that is one of like, one of — something that's very underappreciated, I think, about SpaceX. One of their specialities. One of their specialities.

Yeah. All right. Second half of '28 to first half of 2030 would be my point prediction.

I have a question for Gavin. He did a really interesting talk with Patrick O'Shaughnessy. And there was this one thing that I wanted to ask you about, because I thought it was so interesting. Well, so maybe take the questions in reverse orders. For Leopold, who's clearly a brilliant man, I think he's a Rhodes Scholar at like 19. And I think my understanding is he's putting up pretty extraordinary numbers. I've yet to meet him. He actually shares an office in San Francisco with a friend of mine. Second thing, I think cross-sectionally, if you look at the valuations for all these AI names, they just, they can't all be accurate. You have memory makers that, you know, three to five times PE. You have NVIDIA at a really low PE. You actually have, you know, some other accelerator companies at reasonable multiples. And then you have everything else — everything in power, everything in cooling. And when I say power, I don't mean utilities. So IPPs are actually quite reasonably valued. But power, cooling, even probably some of the optical names, these are discounting very different things. If the multiples on the power, cooling, optical names are correct, NVIDIA, memory, they're going up a lot. If the multiples on NVIDIA and memory are correct, everything else is probably going to underperform. The AI market is cross-sectionally inefficient right now, which is what I was trying to say.

NVIDIA 失市场份额？还是被低估？

As far as the NVIDIA quarter, they went to a new reporting structure, Data Center and Edge, and then within AI, they have hyperscalers, and then I think they call it AI clouds, industrial, and enterprise. What I believe is if we were to make a true apples-to-apples comparison — and Broadcom, there's a narrative that NVIDIA is losing share to the TPU. And Broadcom guided for 143% year over year growth in their AI semiconductor revenue in the quarter that they will report that's comparable to the one NVIDIA just reported.

I think if you were to — and I just, I so wish they had reported slightly differently. I wish they'd done hyperscalers, AI clouds, and then industrial and enterprise. Because I think the segment that is comparable is the sum of hyperscalers plus AI clouds stripping out China, because Broadcom just did not have the China business that NVIDIA did. And I think on that basis, in other words, within the Western AI world, within data centers that need being built, whether they're being built by CoreWeave, XAI, Amazon, Google, NVIDIA's AI business is growing faster than Broadcom's. And faster than a lot of other companies that are, you know, seen as part of this ASIC share gain story.

And, you know, I think Jensen has become, you can hear it, increasingly frustrated, rightfully so, at two things. I would say, what is the performance of the stock to — He's been vocal about that. Like, what is going on here? Yes. We're putting up record numbers and we're getting no, like, credit. Yeah, I get it. And just how could there be a share loss narrative if I am gaining share? And it is indisputably true that he is growing faster than hyperscaler capex, even without these adjustments.

Yeah. I think the other thing that's so frustrating to him is these other ASICs are not being submitted for benchmarks. They're not in the SemiAnalysis Inference Max. They're not in MLPerf. I think the reason they're not being submitted is they will lose. And you can't fight shadows. And until we see a clean benchmark of whether it's GB300s versus TPU V7s or, you know, versus — versus Inferentia, yeah. Yeah, versus Trainium. We're not going to know. And that's why a lot of these other chips, I think Trainium's in a great spot, aren't being submitted. But nonetheless, NVIDIA's doing well.

Once you become the largest company in the world, you kind of — my observation would be at stair step patterns where you kind of the multiple compresses, compresses, compresses because people are skeptical of the size. Then you have a re-rating. And then you re-rate. New floors established at a higher rate. Yeah.

I think there was one other really important thing in the NVIDIA quarter. It's they said if they thought their CPU business was going to be $20 billion this year. Yeah, yeah, yeah. That's extraordinary. It means overnight for one of the world's largest CPU manufacturers.

And I think that is a testament to NVIDIA has a unique position. They're the only company that works with every lab. And so that puts them in the best position to architect their chips. They call it co-design for where the models are going. And I think that $20 billion CPU figure is pretty extraordinary.

This is the thing, like at the end of this Grok transaction last year, my kind of prevailing thought on this is we're going to move to these domain specific architectures. I thought that was like a fait accompli. We're just now waiting for which models. But the reality is that that DSA market evolution is actually happening inside of Nvidia. That's what's so insane to me. That was my takeaway from the quarter as well, which is like, holy shit, these guys actually have domain specific architectures because they're doing these design programs with every — this is why back to sort of the, you know, when he does DC to DC with Elon and Colossus 3 or whatever, it's just it's another game changer for everybody. He doesn't make a Moon trip. He makes nine.

And then I think the cost at which you can finance these chips and these useful lives is really important. I thought that was a great insight.

No, thank you, Chamath. I appreciate it. So when CoreWeave and all these neoclouds came public, and by the way, this goes for the hyperscalers too, there was a big bear case that, hey, these guys are amortizing their GPUs and CPUs over four or five, six years, and that's way too short of a lifespan. The true lifespan of a GPU is more like two years, and therefore, the profits of all these businesses are overstated. The reality is—

This was Michael Burry who put this out there, to be clear. Yeah. Yeah. And thank you, Michael Burry. We need bears. Thank you. Yeah. It's like asking Gerardo about modern music. What a waste of time. What a waste of time. Oh, poor Mark. Come on the program anytime, Michael Burry. Go ahead, Gavin. Keep it sidetracked. I'm happy to chat.

But now that we've just aggregated in front of us, we have these different domain-specific accelerators. You can mix and match them. And I think the GPU stays in a lot of ways at the center of this constellation for a while. And you can put, whether it's a Groq accelerator, whether it's a Cerebras accelerator, in front of old GPUs, use Groq or Cerebras for decode. And then those older GPUs, they have a useful life for 10 or 15 years. And this means that you can finance GPUs.

I think CoreWeave's lowest financing, I can't forget if it's 6% or 7%. 6%. It's going to come down. And if you can get an asset-backed loan and asset-backed financing for GPUs at a lower rate than other chips, that's a profound advantage. That quarter single-handedly saved the Neo clouds this quarter. I mean, they single-handedly saved them all. I think they should all say an incredible thanks to Jensen because—

I interviewed the CEO of CoreWeave, Michael Intrator. Michael Intrator, yeah. And he was saying, hey, listen, people have no problem buying and financing these over a six year period. And people are asking for things that are coming off and that he thinks they're going to have year seven, eight, nine, they'll have some useful life, you know, in addition to that. So, yeah. So he's like, I don't know what anybody's talking about here. Like, this is just not informed analysis was his point, like the game on the field and people are betting with their dollars with him. He has them pay in advance and sign six year contracts. If they didn't think it has a six year lifespan, they wouldn't be signing a six year contract. Pretty straightforward. And they can't get enough of them.

The force of gravity is inevitable. The roller coaster will roll down. We will throw our hands in the air and we will scream, "weeee," as we go for the ride.

Global debt to GDP is 310%. Reserve currency status to the side. The spending problem at the federal, state, local level, the spending problem at every country to basically keep economies growing to support existing leverage ultimately creates a cascading effect. It ultimately breaks. And as it starts to break, you have massive inflation because the value of your underlying currency collapses, and then you have money printing and all this other sort of stuff, which inflates the value of assets, which allows you to keep servicing your debt, and the spiral takes off. And so we will just enjoy the ride.

This is the moment, you know, 30 year Treasury 5.2%. You know, this is — okay, this is water leaking out of the bucket.
