Do you know why it is? I think it's because it's very easy to overlook. We get caught up so much in looking at the successes — the big successes in football, and the big failures. Who won this trophy? Who won that trophy? Who's having an absolute disaster? Who's been fired? Which player's been amazing? And in a lot of sports conversations, that's what drives it. In our sports conversations, we can't just get sucked into this.

And some of these stories across Europe this year, as we'll just touch on now, are absolutely incredible — of small teams outperforming some of the bigger ones. And we're not just talking about, you know — okay, look, Aston Villa doing really well, but there's still 378 million in revenue, former six biggest wage… former European Cup winner. Exactly.

What we're talking about here is FC Thun in Switzerland getting promoted last year. A budget of four million euros. Pretty much bankrupt three years ago, being saved by a businessman, a local CEO injecting three million into the club. They then get promoted, and they've now won the Swiss league and will be back in the Champions League. I say "back in it" because they were actually in it 20 years ago, playing the likes of Arsenal — Dennis Bergkamp, 90th-minute winner, in 2005. But basically, from that moment, they got about 20 million in Champions League revenue, and they just couldn't handle it, and it was a disaster for the club, and they've had a real challenge ever since.

Now they've come back into the league, and it's the first ever team — even Leicester didn't do this — the first team in Switzerland, but only Kaiserslautern and Nottingham Forest, I think, had done it before, where they got promoted and won the league the year after.

These stories are actually cropping up a lot more. We've had Bodø/Glimt doing unbelievably well in the Champions League — the Norwegian team who play very far north of Norway, on a 4G pitch. I hadn't made the link with your Norwegian background, Charles. No — none of my family in there, because they're all footballers. Mine are… Oh, because it probably is a ski-jumping part of Norway, isn't it? It's very snowy up there. Norway's very snowy. I wonder if it's difficult for them at academy level to try and work out which of their kids are going to be better footballers and which of them are going to be ski jumpers. Very easy, as we know.

But look — as I said, I actually know somebody who used to be the technical director at Bodø/Glimt, so I'm going to ask him that question. Ask him on the selection process. Either way — it was a very small town. I haven't got that data in front of me directly. I think it's about 50,000. Ground's 8,000. Make it to the last 16 of the Champions League.

Yeah. These stories have been far more frequent. And in the Premier League, there are still incredible moments this year — where you've looked at Bournemouth and Brighton and Brentford, all competing now. Not moments. What's the current EPL table below the top five? I haven't got it in front of me. I'm going to go Bournemouth. Yeah. Brentford. Keep going. Brighton. Keep going. Sunderland. By the way, that's pretty good, Charles. Bournemouth, Brighton, Brentford, Sunderland. With the exception of getting Brighton and Brentford the wrong way round, you were spot on there. Well done.

And then Chelsea? Chelsea — bottom half? Yeah, I'm 10th. Oh, so still top half. Okay, let's see what happens. Well — after tonight, obviously.

So Bournemouth, Brighton, Brentford and Sunderland this year in the EPL have outperformed Chelsea and Spurs. And that's quite interesting, because in the case of Bournemouth, Brighton and Brentford, that's not a one-off. They have consistently been pushing towards the top half of the Premier League. And these are all clubs that, in the relatively recent past, have been in the bottom two leagues in England. And not there on an occasional basis — they're on a semi-permanent basis.

I mean, when I was young, Bournemouth were always in the bottom division. They almost went out of the whole league. Brentford were a sort of perennial League One, so Tier-3 type team. And Brighton — well, I remember seeing them in the bottom division as well. They were literally a game — a half — from going out of the football league, weren't they? I thought it was Bournemouth, but anyhow. But maybe Brighton as well.

Anyway, look — I actually want to get on to the other countries. So in Denmark, AGF Aarhus are the champions. Not one of the very smallest clubs, but absolutely not FC Copenhagen or Brøndby, right? So not one of those really famous big clubs you rarely see in European football. Midtjylland is kind of very… or Midtjylland is second. And they're also a small club, fundamentally.

Viking in Norway, coming after Bodø/Glimt winning four of the last five seasons — Viking this season. Mjällby in Sweden — they're the Swedish champions for 2025. Again, a small to medium-sized club. Hearts in Scotland — obviously just failed to beat Celtic. We didn't mention that, did we? I mean, how did we not? Devastating. Yeah — unfortunate. We'd require an entire episode.

LASK in Austria — again, you know, we've got some big, big clubs in Austria — a domestic double there. Again, we're talking about some much more famous clubs: Rapid Vienna and Salzburg, and these types of clubs. Thun in Switzerland, you've mentioned — again, Grasshoppers of Zurich and Basel and these types of clubs, Young Boys — again, forget it. Thun, with their 5-million-euro budget.

Como in Italy, fifth in Serie A — ahead of Juventus. Ahead of Juventus, with a budget of 35 million. Do you know what — even Villarreal, I looked at. Villarreal ahead of Atlético Madrid — third place. Villarreal is also on my list. Third in La Liga would very much be regarded, in their own region, as being the minnows compared to Valencia — let alone compared to Atlético Madrid, and so on. And they're third in La Liga. NEC third, I think. Ajax having a terrible year — I think their wage bill is 8.75 million euros.

And in France, we've had a slightly closer fight for the title this year. It does look like PSG have done it — they have actually done it — against Lens, who they've been fighting toe-to-toe with all year. Now, I just had a look at this. Because we discussed Thun being on 4 or 5 million, and that being maybe 20%. Lens' budget is 30 million euros — so, to give you some sort of figure, 26 million pounds; that's roughly what a mid-table championship side in England would spend. They're up against PSG, arguably the best and one of the most expensive club sides in the world — 200 million euros. So PSG is spending seven times what Lens are spending, and yet at domestic level they're almost on a level pegging.

And then we've got — even in Italy — Atalanta, who have been outstanding in the last few seasons. Not having a good year this year. I know — it's not a good year this year. But I think sometimes with these clubs, having to also play European football can end up catching up with them after a little while — just the sheer number of games, and the required squad depth, becomes a bit of a problem. Which again has happened a bit to Bodø/Glimt as well this year.

You talked about squad depth, and how PSG have actually come out on top, and how difficult it is to compete. They have pretty much — if you look at the statistics this year for their players — they have been so comfortable in this league over the last years that Dembélé started nine games in the league this year. Marquinhos is something similar. They've just been after the Champions League. And the strength of this team is that they can rest their players to that extent and still win the league.

But I think it's nonetheless worth pointing out that Lens have been able to come second in a league where, even if you take PSG out of it, you've got clubs like Lyon and Marseille who would be spending much more money than Lens would be spending — Lille as well, which would be a more local competitor to Lens.

So it's part of a pattern of — I'm not going to say "small clubs", except maybe for Thun — I'm going to say kind of "ambitious mid-sized clubs". It appears that, in the last five to ten years, they have gained an ability to be, if they are very well run — and we'll come on to what that means in a moment — to be highly competitive at the top of European football, even with budgets that you would think, and certainly most fans would think, you wouldn't be able to achieve that with.

Why It Works — Data and Analytics

So yeah — and the "why" is the interesting question. I was just trying to put it thematically, and I didn't want to tell you beforehand, but as someone that has operated in these environments and knows how this works, I wanted to see your thoughts on thinking about it thematically.

So just to lay out a few of them that I put in — some of them will be obvious: data and analytics, and the leveraging of that. Identity over individuals. Sell smart, reinvest smarter. Financial discipline as a strategic advantage — most importantly. And finally, coaching clarity. I thought the coaching clarity in there was one I'd like to throw in, because I know of your opinions of coaching. Well — I think it's very important.

I want to ask about the first one there, which is — what did I say? I said data and analytics. You said something to me yesterday which really interested me. When we always hold Brighton and Brentford up as this incredible case study of how to find a model that beats the established — you then turned around to me and you said, "Yeah, but, you know, kind of necessity is the mother of invention. They're not going to be making the money that Manchester City and Arsenal and Liverpool are, in the same ways — i.e. through massive matchday revenue, through big commercial deals. So they have to go and find different ways to do it. And player trading is the way that they decided to elevate their position." And they've done it incredibly well. What's the influence of that in these stories?

Okay — so I think the phrase "data analytics" has become a bit of a sort of thing, right? A lot of people say it, and it's become popular parlance even amongst the fan bases and the football media. But when I ask a lot of people who talk about it, "Have you actually ever seen what this looks like? What it actually means?" — all it basically is, is like the gathering of evidence and then effectively putting it through… rather than just saying "here's some facts", which is relatively easy to do, it's saying "well, how can we then put those through various analytical models which then make sense of those things, and end up with calls to action — decisions which a club can then make which are smarter than the decision they might have made if they hadn't been able to use that evidence in that way."

What does this mean? What this means is that, prior to the availability of electronic data about players in lots of different parts of the world — for Brighton to start recruiting players from Ecuador, they would need to have a massive global scouting operation. Which, again, is very expensive — where they will get disintermediated by the very biggest clubs who have the ability to employ more scouts effectively. And they would then have to work out from that scouting operation, "Okay, we found this really interesting player in Ecuador — but how does he compare to another player we found in Cameroon?" I have no idea.

Now, what's happened — for instance, the use of data — is that the ability to compare different leagues around the world has very significantly improved. So all the players who get transferred out of all the various different leagues, and what their subsequent performances go on to demonstrate, gets fed into the mix, and it gets spat out — there's a sort of ranking list of leagues. So you can say, "Okay, if a player is performing to this level in the Ecuadorian top division at this age, that roughly equates to over here."

What that then means is, once that player has been highlighted in Ecuador, Brighton can then put a scout on a plane to go to Ecuador to watch Caicedo play. And then the whole thing can work from there. So it's cut out an area which previously would have been unbelievably expensive and difficult for a medium-sized club to compete against a larger club — and enabled them a smarter, cheaper, better way to access this talent from around the world.

Now, the next thing is that the various different geographies of the rest of the world have become much more accessible, for all sorts of different reasons — but chiefly because the football development patterns in these other countries have improved. Whereas previously, quite often, even if there was significant talent in a particular country, by the time a player of that talent had reached the age of 18, 19, they were so far behind technically that actually getting them to translate from there into the English top division, or the French top division, or the German top division, was just a really, really big jump. They pretty much had to be a genius to be able to bridge that gap in technical and physical preparation.

FIFA, over the last 20, 30 years, has been investing large amounts of money in all of these geographies. This is how FIFA presidents get re-elected. What mostly happens is they put a lot of money into development systems and facilities in all of these parts of the world. And I've known this through working the Caribbean — FIFA is ploughing a lot of money into football facilities in the Caribbean to enable young Caribbean talent to be more competitive, so they don't get left too far behind, so that at the age of 17, 18 they've still got a chance of making it on the global stage.

So you've got a higher underlying level of football development. Then, digitally, you've got the ability to assess at an early stage where that talent is. And then, through a much more developed understanding of how the visa systems work through the various different geographies, you've now got people at these football clubs working out how they are actually going to move that player, within the existing visa rules, from here to here to here to here.

So why aren't all the top clubs just…? You've already answered it — necessity is the mother of invention. Once you know that you cannot sign a fully matured super-elite talent — once you know that — then you come to the opinion: "Well, we're going to have to find one which the market has not yet worked out is that, but we think he will be in two or three years' time. And therefore we're going to get two advantages from this signing. One: we're going to get the benefit of his playing services for the next year or two. And then at the end of it, one of these very biggest clubs is going to buy him, fully formed, off us."

So interesting. If you look at Chelsea — just take your club as an example of why I think it's easier said than done for the very biggest clubs to do this. What you need is for that player who is being signed as a development prospect to then be given consistent game time. Because the only way in which they are realistically going to end up being what they need to be is if they play.

The problem with the very biggest clubs is that the expectation, on a week-to-week basis, is that they compete at the very top of their leagues. And this produces a sort of unfortunate vortex, because it basically means that you need ready-made finished products to come in. That's very expensive, on the one hand. And it makes it very difficult to make a profit out of them, on the other hand, because they were already fully priced. So unless they do something really spectacular with you, they're probably going to be worth less rather than more at the end of their contract, because they're going to be older.

These mid-sized clubs are sourcing these players aged 19, 20, 21, 22. They're then getting a couple of their best years out of them. They're then selling them at the peak of their powers, for massive amounts of money, to the very biggest clubs — who then get maybe the last couple of years of their peak years out of them before they start to set off, and they get sold at a loss.

So it's quite a big call, I think, for one of the very biggest clubs to say, "Right — even though we can afford to buy these fully formed players, actually it's bad practice. We don't want to do it. So what we're going to do instead is, we're going to bite the bullet of explaining to our fan base that, even though we could afford to buy £60 million players, we're actually going to start buying a whole bunch of £15 million players."

Yeah, that's not good. Well, it's kind of what Chelsea have done. I know they're not all 15 million — they're more like 20, 30 million. Yeah, but they do drip-feed it in with 100 million. That's true. Which — I'm just saying — as a fan, like, placates you.

Right. This is the point. That placation is the point. All the clubs we're looking at here have no need of placation, because their fans are not expecting to win the league. Their fans are happy to see their clubs just be competitive. And even if they have a season where they're not that competitive — "Oh well, we're not that big a club, so it doesn't matter." That takes the pressure off the executives and the board, which means they can pursue a medium-term strategy without constantly having to go out of the stadium with the coats over their heads, trying to make sure they don't get bombarded.

If we look at the Premier League, the six then that you have at the top — they're all playing the same game. It's highly likely that a couple of them may screw that up. That leaves an opportunity for the clubs operating like Brighton, Brentford, Bournemouth to come up and compete at a high level with a really successful execution.

I mean, I was looking at Bodø/Glimt. They've averaged kind of a transfer budget of about 6 million euros. The squad value total is 53.4 million euros, or has been. Now, they'll go — no, the Champions League run has done an amazing job for them, some of those players will go for significantly more than that. But that squad value alone — I just had a look on Transfermarkt — there are 122 individual players in European football valued higher than 53.4 million euros. And that's their total squad value, playing in a Champions League last 16.

Identity Over Individuals

So this speaks to another point you mentioned right at the start, which we need to come back to. We've mentioned data analytics, and the way in which it's provided — obviously, I'm not saying it's easy. The development of these systems to do it at the very highest level, which is what Brighton and Brentford's owners have done, is extremely difficult — otherwise, everyone would do it. So just to make that clear. And there are other clubs within this group in Europe who are also quite far forward in the use of those systems.

But the next bit — which is actually a more common theme within this group — is the extent to which football is a team game. And I think this is the bit which is often underestimated by fans and by new owners.

So if you think about Chelsea's new owners' strategy of garaging 30, 40, 50, 60 top young players, and hoping that somehow, if you throw all these ingredients into the mix, a quality team will emerge out of that mix — what you're looking at, with a lot of these other clubs, is realistic playing squads which are more like 20, 21 players in total who have got any chance of playing, and where every single spot in that squad is very carefully chosen for: "Which bit of the jigsaw is this player solving for me?" So this is what I term as identity over individual. And that's super important.

Now, the consistency of the coaching position is very important to that. Because what you're doing is, every transfer window, you're working out which little bit of the jigsaw isn't quite right. You're removing that piece and replacing it with one piece or two pieces. It's small tweaks, small changes.

And then this is another thing which I think — that mid-sized clubs find it easier to maintain a consistent coaching setup, because, again, the pressure on instant success is lower. So you don't get those sackings which happen almost not by mistake, but happen almost as a sacrificial lamb to placate the fan base because we've been through a bad patch. Mid-sized clubs are more able to say, "Hey — yeah, we've been through a bit of a tough patch, but I think we're going to come through it. Hey, everyone, it's fine, we've got this," type thing. And again, you're not having to grow a fake moustache and try to flee the country if you say that. You can still walk around the town in one piece.

As you know my view on the difference that an individual head coach or manager makes — it's 5% or 10% here or there. But, by the way, 5% or 10% is quite a big difference. Because it's often probably that final 5% or 10%. Absolutely. My argument is not that 5% or 10% is a small number — I think it's quite a big number. It's just not 50%, which is what a lot of fans think. A lot of fans think, "Our players basically are really good. If we chuck out the current clown in the dugout and replace him with a genius, then everything will be fine." I don't think that really works.

But yeah — so just looking through these different clubs and the currents, or levels, of achievement: I don't think it's as simple as saying, "If you are the owner or on the board of a mid-sized club, we're going to do the same as Thun, we want to have a budget of 4.5 million." There'll be all sorts of particularities to their particular situation, and the Swiss league as a whole, and the immigration system operating within Switzerland.

But I think what is interesting is, one conclusion that can be drawn is that the amount of money being spent as a cause of success is significantly overestimated. I think we can say that. The level of success across this number of leagues, with massive differences compared to what you would expect if it was causation rather than correlation — there's clearly correlation, but not causation, I think.

Spurs — Caught in Between

Because if you look at, for instance, Bournemouth, Brighton, Brentford, Sunderland — what would you say their wage budgets are? I think it's like between 40 and 60, isn't it? About 70 million. What is Spurs' playing budget? We've done that — to 240. No — 160. And they're accused of underspending. They're accused of underspending by only — quotation marks — spending 160.

So if you were to say to a Spurs fan, "Do you know what your problem actually is? You're spending too much" — because effectively, even though you're comparing your spending levels against another club who's spending 180, and you're going, "We should be spending an extra 20 million" — actually, the issue here is that your culture is not to be smart. Your culture is simply to say, "We've got a lot of money to spend. We'll go out in the sweet shop. All the other big boys will be in the sweet shop, and we'll try and get the same sweeties as them, but we'll spend a little bit less than them, so we'll get slightly less good sweeties."

Whereas these other clubs are saying, "We can't afford to go in the sweet shop. We have to go and find the sweet factory. We have to go and find these sweets before they get in the sweet shop."

So if you're a club like Spurs, and you're caught in between — neither able to sign the really marquee player who is absolutely proven and who absolutely is going to deliver, nor thinking smartly about, "Well, yeah, but how are we going to find the one that hasn't been discovered yet?" — they end up somewhere in between, ending up signing very expensive players who are actually probably no more proven than some of these others.

Now, that is what Chelsea have been doing. That's not me being critical — that is exactly where they've been. They've been signing these players for 100-plus. I mean, Enzo Fernández played six months at Benfica before joining Chelsea, and he had a great World Cup. That's not proven, right? He's had a good period of a short amount of time.

So really, what you're saying is — if we go back to that, "necessity, mother of invention" — through the way that football has developed, some of these smaller clubs have been forced to think differently. The way that they've been able to identify value is looking at a lot of the playing side of the game, by identifying where the top players can come from. That has helped them massively on the pitch. Shock horror, right? Yeah — we're still about playing.

What it's been able to do is create a really exciting group of players across all of these different squads, capable of competing with the top ones — who may lack some of those core things that we've talked about. Whether it's the cultural fit to the organisation… they're just a great brand, or a great global name — those things are actually not as important as having 21 to 26 players in there specifically put together for a reason.

Very nicely put, Charlie.

Sign-Off

So — Mr Kramer, thank you. Mr Kramer, thank you for that. This is the type of topic which I'd be really interested in readers' and listeners' views on. Because, look — we don't have a monopoly on wisdom. A lot of what we're saying there is observations based on some facts, with some presumptions and some assumptions that are made alongside that. But there will be individual instances within these clubs which we are not necessarily aware of. I'm not aware of what LASK in Austria's particular strategy has been when they were winning the domestic double. I am somewhat aware of what's happening in Como. There'll be others who are more aware than me. So — any thoughts and details that people have out there, I'd be really interested to hear on those topics.

Absolutely. And thank you for putting that in. I also like the fact that it's kind of a positive take. I think it's very easy to get caught up in the clubs that are doing things wrong, or getting all negative. So looking at these clubs that are doing great things is quite refreshing to our conversation.

It also offers hope to everyone, right? It does. It means that even if you're not a glory-hunting Chelsea fan who only follows the club because, when you were young, they won everything — even if you aren't that person, you can still dream of success for your local club, which is what you should be supporting in the first place.

You know — it kind of was my local club. No, your local club's Fulham. I know. I just — I supported them because I love John Terry. Charlie.

And on that note, we're off for a beer. Thank you very much, guys. We'll see you next week. Thanks, John. Thanks a lot.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
# 这期节目其实只在讲一件事：谁有资格"等"
表面上这是四个互不相干的话题——切尔西换帅、利物浦内乱、兰开夏郡板球内战、欧洲小球会逆袭。但如果把它们叠在一起看，会发现一个被主持人自己都没点破的隐藏主线：**整期节目从头到尾都在讨论"资本的耐心半径"**。
切尔西在车库里囤积五六十个年轻球员、却不敢真正让他们踢球，因为球迷不允许"输球的赛季"；兰开夏郡的会员要的是**立刻**赢约克郡；而布莱顿、布伦特福德、博多/格林特之所以能用六分之一的预算赢球，唯一的、也是最被低估的优势，是**他们的球迷不期待夺冠，于是管理层获得了"难看一阵子"的许可证**。Charlie 把它说成"necessity is the mother of invention"，但更准确的说法是：小球会买到的不是球员，而是**时间**。Gidney 那句"placation is the point"才是全场最锋利的一刀——大俱乐部花的钱里有一大块根本不是买竞技力，而是买"球迷别在停车场堵我"的赎金。所以这期真正的命题不是"钱能不能买来成功"，而是"你被允许等多久"。
# 一个会让你意外的观察：Gidney 是被自

item.title: [投资TALK君] ✨【投资TALK君1434期】一个坏消息，一个好消息！英伟达财报剖析✨20260522#CPI #nvda #美股 #投资 #英伟达 #ai #特斯拉
item.dc:creator: 投资TALK君
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、本期最反常识的观察：主持人把"数据源本身"变成了坏消息

绝大多数财经博主的"坏消息"都指向市场——某个收益率破位、某个财报暴雷。但这期视频里，那个被郑重其事提醒"千万不要错过"的坏消息，竟然是他自己引用的彭博终端数据错了。这是一个极罕见的、把镜头对准自己工具的时刻。

这里藏着一个被忽略的系统性真相：当一把尺子可以被悄悄重新校准，它丈量出的"贵"与"便宜"就失去了意义。纳斯达克综合指数的 forward P/E 从"30倍"被修正到"25–26倍"，差距不是市场变了，而是计算方法变了。他随后果断弃用综合指数、改用成分股仅100只的 NDX——这本质上是科学仪器里的"信噪比"决策：3000多个标的的指数，估值口径越容易被噪音和方法论变动污染。这其实是 Goodhart 定律的一个变体——一个指标一旦被广泛当作锚，它就开始变得不可靠。一个愿意公开承认"我上次给你们的数据是错的"的分析者，比一个永远正确的分析者更值得信任，这件事本身也是个反直觉的洞见。

二、跨领域连接：今晚整期视频，其实在反复讲同一个"分叉"故事

表面上这是四个互不相干的话题——估值修正、英伟达、沃尔玛、Intuit。但如果退后一步，你会发现一个令人意外的自相似（分形）结构：同一个"高端繁荣、低端萎缩"的分叉模式，在三个完全不同的尺度上重复出现。

  • 消费者层面（沃尔玛）：高收入人群"花钱很自信"，低收入人群谨慎到"每次加油不到1加仑"。
  • 软件客户层面（Intuit / Adobe）：低端客户流失甚至离场，高端用户 ARPU 反而上升。
  • 英伟达营收层面：曾经"大科技"独大的数据中心收入，如今被拆成 hyperscale 与 AI-native 两半——大厂环比增速12%，初创公司环比增速31%，尾部正在反超头部。

主持人没有点破这一点，但这正是本期最大的"啊哈时刻"：你以为他在讲四家公司，他其实在讲整个经济体的 K 形撕裂如何在每一层级复刻自己。从加油站到 SaaS 续费再到芯片订单结构，分叉无处不在。一个加油习惯的微观行为，和英伟达营收结构的宏观变迁，是同一种力的两个投影。

三、被低估的那条线：英伟达的护城河，其实建在"电网"上

市场都在问"GPU 还是 CPU""大厂会不会砍资本开支",老黄的回答里却埋了一个几乎没人接住的信号：电网利用率不到60%。他真正的野心不是卖更快的芯片，而是用软件让数据中心去"吃掉"电网那30%–40%的闲置电力——把供电从一个固定约束，变成一个动态变量。

这是一个跨领域的精彩错位：当所有人把英伟达当半导体公司估值时，它正在把自己重新定义为一家"电力调度+算力工厂"的公司。这与一个世纪前的故事惊人地相似——爱迪生卖的从来不只是灯泡，而是整个电力系统；真正的护城河不在单品，而在"谁定义了整个系统的吞吐"。老黄那句"别人在想单一节点能不能打败英伟达，我在想整个 AI 工厂的输出能不能领先全球"，就是这个意思。

还有一个埋得很深的洞见值得你警觉：主持人坦言愿意把报税完全交给 AI agent，理由是"一年只用一次"。这反而点破了一条规律——使用频率越低的高风险任务，越容易被代理化，因为用户对它没有日常情感依附、没有肌肉记忆。这意味着 Intuit 的危险不在于产品不好，而在于它的核心业务恰好坐落在"最适合被 agent 接管"的象限里。它的"杀估值"杀的不是当下盈利（EPS 增速并未放缓），而是市场提前为这个结构性脆弱点定的价。

📝 转录文本

✨【投资TALK君1434期】一个坏消息，一个好消息！英伟达财报剖析

开场

各位观众大家好，欢迎来到一个不单只聊新闻的专业美股频道，我是市场分析员小涛。

不知道大家过去几天过得如何，有没有想我？这边的一个更新，先和大家打个招呼。那么周二原本固定是要更新的，但是因为我个人的原因非常的忙，所以没有时间为大家做更新，这边先和大家打个招呼。

那不知道过去几天大家过得如何，周一周二出现了下跌，但是周三、周四还是出现了反弹。起码在周日的那期视频里边，我和大家聊过市场的一个参与度，在一个相对比较低的这个位置，所以在这种时间段就没有必要过度的悲观。随以着周一周二的下跌之后，还好周三周四也出现了反弹。

本期概要

那今天我们聊什么？主要有聊四点。第一点的话，有一个坏消息和一个好消息要带给大家，所以希望大家前面的那一部分千万不要错过。然后我们聊三家公司的财报，分别是英伟达的财报、还有沃尔玛的财报，然后最后是 Intuit 财报。那后面两家公司发布完财报之后都出现下跌——应该是三家公司吧，发布完财报之后都出现了下跌——所以我估计大家应该都会比较感兴趣。

那我就废话不多说了，直接开始。在开始之前，老规矩提醒一下大家，记得留言、点赞、加转发。如果你们的留言非常的多、点赞非常的多，那么也许以后我就不好意思逃课了，你们说是不是？所以看今天大家积极的表现，好嘛，开个玩笑。

周四市场回顾

先来回顾一下周四资产价格的走势。像我前面说的，周一周二出现了下跌，然后周三周四出现了反弹。那在下跌的时候，市场里面那些负面的消息面和情绪面、或者一些噪音就会被扩大，比如说美债收益率30年期去到了5%以上，是不是会使得美股出现下跌——这些都是市场里面的一些噪音。

而稍后和大家分享的一个坏消息和一个好消息里边，如果从一个估值的角度去看，比较合理地解释了为什么从4月份以来美股的这一波上涨，反而估值并没有去到一个非常高的位置。这背后的核心理由就是：美股这边公司 EPS 增速的速度非常的快。

那我们就先看一下周四资产价格的走势。周四其实还好，基本上是出现了小幅的上涨。美债收益的波动也不大，黄金的波动也不大，原油的波动也不大——原油是在周三出现了下跌，然后周三一些受到原油相关负面影响的个股，在周三也是出现了反弹，比如说 GE。

那么 VIX 出现了4%的下跌，16.8 的 VIX 还是在一个相对比较高的位置。通常来说，如果在一个非常长时期牛市的情况下，VIX 可以保持在13至14之间。罗素2000上涨了0.9%。从板块这个角度去看，前七大科技股相对会弱一些，然后等权指数相对会强一些。基本上因为指数上涨的幅度也不大，所以周四算是一个相对比较平静的市场。

市场参与度

从市场参与度这个角度去看，在上周日那期视频里面和大家聊到，差不多三大指数都是在30几。我说虽然指数从最高点回到了2%都不到，但是参与度将近去到了一个超卖的位置了，所以没有必要太过于担心。

那随着这两天周三的反弹幅度比较大、然后周四小幅反弹之后，我们看一下市场参与度，三大指数基本上回到了一个中性的位置。20日均线分别在纳指44、罗素2000 50% 和标普500 50%；50日均线的话，也基本上是在一个中心的位置。所以现在从一个比较短期的角度去看，从市场参与度的角度去看，三大指数都处在一个中性的位置，完全没有非常的超买，也没有非常的超卖。

一个坏消息：纳指估值数据有误

OK，然后就来和大家聊一下一个坏消息和一个好消息。

坏消息是什么？坏消息是我前段时间和大家分享，纳指的估值现在已经回到上年10月份的水平——当时所用的数据并不准确。大家知道我基本上平时在视频里面和大家分享的数据全部是来自于彭博终端。这两天其实我有和彭博后面的一些客服人员去进行一定程度的沟通。为什么要沟通？是因为我用同样的指标、同样的数据，最后出来的一个 forward P/E 的结果不一样了。

在这个月月初的时候，5月8日、5月9日当时和大家分享，那时这个估值是去到了30倍左右。30倍就是在上年10月初的水平——当年的10月底甚至去到了30倍以上了。然后5月初的时候，我说纳指的估值再次回到了30倍，那接下去可能从贝塔的角度去看要相对往下降一些：如果继续往上突破的话，可以主动去降；那么如果往下跌的话，可以做一个技术层面上的移动止盈，就是被动止盈。

但是我这两天回过头去看的话，这个 forward P/E 竟然降到了25倍，差不多25倍到26倍之间，就是和30倍有一个非常明显的差距在那边了。那背后到底发生了什么？中间的那一些细节我就不多做展开。最终的结论就是：彭博那边关于纳斯达克综合指数的 forward P/E 这个数据并不准确，他们对这个数据本身进行了一个修正，修正完之后在25倍至26倍之间，并没有回到上年10月份的水平。

所以这是一个坏消息——我在5月初和大家分享的那组数据并不准确。

改用 NDX 作为更好的估值标的

那经过了我的验证和思考之后，相对于纳斯达克综合指数这个标的，一个更好衡量的指标可能是 NDX。纳斯达克综合指数里边有3000多个标的，而通常我们所聊的 NDX 或者 QQQ 里边只有100个标的。

我和大家说过，纳斯达克综合指数这个估值它进行了修正——因为如果它以后再去做变动的话，你去看这个指标本身，背后计算的方法一旦进行调整之后，突然之间原本估值是贵的、现在变成不贵了，那就不是一个非常好的标的。所以我思考下来、包括验证完之后，可能一个更好的参考标的是 NDX，也就是纳指100这个指数，从估值这个角度去看会是一个更好的衡量标准。

图里蓝色这条线，如果去看 NDX 现在 forward P/E 的话，是在24.3倍。24.3倍是什么概念？从今年3月底，当时是跌到了20.5倍左右——20.5倍其实是比上年的4月份要低、比上年的3月份要低的。那从20.5倍现在回到了24.3倍，其实上涨的幅度也将近是20%了。但是离上年10月份相对的高点27.4倍，其实还有将近13%的空间。

这13%的空间，它是估值的一个上升空间——我并不是一定要上升。只是说因为估值这个角度它是一个"模糊正确"，它没有办法告诉我们一定会从现在这个位置上升或者下跌，但起码可以告诉我们：从历史估值的角度去看，现在的估值到底是在一个贵、还是便宜、还是中性的阶段。

那从 NDX forward P/E 这个角度去看的话，现在纳指 NDX 这个估值，我认为是处在一个中性的位置，并没有到一个贵的位置。所以这是第一点：起码和过去一年以来相对比较高的估值去做对比的话，现在的 NDX、QQQ 估值并不贵。

关于估值的四点说明

第二点，我们这边所聊的估值，它是一个 forward P/E，也就是基于未来一年的盈利。未来一年的这个估值，其实就是建立在现在的股价除以未来一年的 EPS 增速。某种程度，P/E 是一个结果，它并不是一个理由、并不是一个原因——它只是一个结果，这个结果是由于市场对于未来一年 EPS 的上调或者下调。

如果价格不变，未来的 EPS 往上调，那自然未来的 PE 就下跌了；如果指数价格不变，未来的 EPS 往下调，那你的估值自然就往上调了。

那为什么我们要看未来一年的 PE、要向前去看 forward PE？是因为整个市场交易的是未来。这就是为什么用后视镜去看 TTM（trailing twelve months，过去12个月）的 PE 意义并不大——因为这已经是历史了，而市场交易的永远是未来。既然交易的是未来，就是交易未来的不确定性。

你说去看未来一年的 PE、或者未来一年的 EPS，它有没有不确定性？当然有不确定性，这也是整个市场里面交易的一个乐趣——我们都是在对未来去做预测。未来如果非常确定的话，市场里边就不会存在分歧了。所以未来一年这个 EPS 有没有不确定性、有没有风险？它是一定有风险。但是对于一些大科技来说，特别是对于芯片公司来说，未来一年的 EPS 有没有非常大的风险？其实我个人认为对于很多公司、特别是很多芯片公司来说，这个确定性就相当的高，因为他们未来一年的这些订单……包括现在对于很多能源公司来说，起码未来半年、未来9个月、未来三个季度，订单、营收、盈利确定性都是相当的高。

第三点，估值从现在这个位置如果回到上年10月份的水平，有13%的上涨空间，但不代表价格可以上涨13%。因为在这一段时间里面，如果 EPS 继续扩张的话，那价格、指数的上涨幅度会超过13%；反过来也一样，如果你估值去到上年10月份的水平、但 EPS 往下收的话，那价格的上涨空间就不会到13%。因为价格等于什么？价格等于 forward P/E 乘以 forward EPS。

不过后面那种情况的概率并不会非常高。为什么？因为如果市场把 EPS 往下调的话，通常来说它会伴随估值的收缩，而并不是估值的扩张。

最后一点，估值如果回到了上年10月份的水平，也不代表接下去股价就一定要下跌、或者股价就没有再次的上涨空间。其实和我前面聊的第三点是类似的，我给大家举两个例子。

第一个例子：上年10月份到今年3月份这段时间。图里蓝色这条线是估值（PE），白色这条线是价格（NDX 的走势）。白色这条线在上年10月份、9月下旬到今年3月份差不多是在横盘的，而蓝色这条线（估值）非常明显地在下跌。当价格在横盘、估值在下跌，说明什么？说明背后市场对于 EPS 在这段时间一直在上修。EPS 一直在上修，价格在那边横盘不动，你的估值自然就会下降。这就是一个非常典型的"通过时间去换空间"——财报慢慢出来之后，财报只要变得越来越好，市场自然就会上调未来的 EPS，价格仍然不变，估值自然就下降了。

第二个例子：2025年5月份、差不多5月下旬一直到10月份这段时间，也就是图里绿色的框框。在绿色框框里边可以看到估值（蓝色这条线）基本上是没有变的，在一个区间里面横盘；但是价格（白色这条线）却出现了比较明显的上升。为什么？这是因为在这段时间 EPS 不断地往上修，即使在估值不变的情况下，价格仍然可以往上涨。这个价格的上涨并不是来自于估值的扩张，而是来自于 EPS 的上修——我们可以理解为是非常标准的、由盈利所驱动的价格上涨。

4月以来这波上涨的拆解

介于第四点这个基础之上，我们回到4月份以来这波市场的上涨。图里白色这条线是指数、是价格的走势。从4月份以来，价格上涨了多少？从3月底最低点到现在，NDX 总共从最低点到最高点上升了29%。而 forward P/E（蓝色这条线）从最低点上升了22%。

也就是说，forward P/E 上升的速度没有价格上升的速度快。这背后的理由是什么？就是在你 forward P/E 上升的同时，它的 EPS 在不断地往上调。因为价格等于 forward P/E 乘以 forward EPS——你 P/E 的确扩张了22%，同时你的 EPS 扩张了可能7%、8%左右。所以自从4月份以来这一波上涨，它不单是估值的回归，同时也是由盈利、由 EPS 所驱动的一波上涨。

所以指数的上涨可以单纯来自于盈利所驱动，就像绿色框框里那样：P/E 可以不扩张、在高位，单纯来自于 EPS 盈利的驱动。另外一种上涨是一种加速度、给你一种推背感，这种上涨是来自于估值的扩张加上 EPS 的抬升。通常来说，伴随着估值这种扩张，通常都是估值由低位开始出现反弹——就像今年3月底的时候，估值已经是上年3月底、4月初的那种水平，那从3月底到现在这一波上涨就是估值的反弹加上 P/E 的扩张，两者加在一起就是一个推背感、一个加速度。

坏消息与好消息小结

所以总结一下：坏消息就是我前段时间和大家分享"纳指估值现在已经回到上年10月份的水平、在一个高位"，这是一个错误的信息，这个数据本身是错误的，现在已经进行了修正。

好消息就是现在纳指的估值并没有到上年10月份的水平，并不是在一个贵的水平。纳指估值修正完之后，如果我们现在去看 NDX，它和 SPX（标普500）、SOX（芯片指数）所传递给我们的信号是符合的——现在处在一个中心的位置，并没有到上年10月份的水平。从标普这个角度去看，回到上年10月份差不多有10%的空间；SOX 回到上年10月份差不多有10到15%的空间；NDX 回到上年10月份差不多是13%的空间。所以三大指数基本上都在同样的水平，离上年10月份估值的高点还有一定的空间。

英伟达财报

OK，然后我们就来聊财报。第一家公司是英伟达。

首先从数据这个角度去看，它基本上没有超出市场预期非常多的幅度。英伟达的财报其实和特斯拉的财报是一样：如果放到两年前的话，市场对于英伟达或者特斯拉的财报基本上是很难猜对的，每一次都低估了最后财报出来的结果，所以才会出现财报比市场预期要好很多——EPS 增速、EPS 体量可能好于市场预期50%、60%，就非常夸张的这种财报。这也是建立在华尔街分析员对于这家公司业务增速判断不准确，完全低估了这家业务短期之内的增速。

那现在随着相对体量上去之后，你的增速就稳定下来了。稳定下来之后，市场对它的摸底、对它财报数据的预期就相对会非常准确。无论是英伟达也好、特斯拉也好——以前市场对于特斯拉的交付还没有一些散户分析得准，但是现在市场把握的准确度会非常高。

所以英伟达的财报，我并不会期待它的财报数据会超出市场预期多少。但是你没有办法去否定，比如说它这一个季度 EPS 增速同比95%——那么大体量的情况下，EPS 增速同比95%，你很难从其他公司身上找到这种 EPS 增速，世界上唯有英伟达一家。

虽然很多时候市场发布完财报之后，交易的点是"你这一季度财报到底好于市场预期还是差于市场预期、好了多少、差了多少"，但终究我们退后一步去看，看一家公司的基本面，不是看它这一次财报好预期多少、差预期多少，而是这家公司这一个季度的财报到底同比增速是多少、环比增速是多少——这才是代表这家公司长期的基本面。所以从 EPS 同比增速95%这个角度去看，包括接下去的几个季度还是在50%至80%之间，从这个增速去看，还是非常好的财报，只是财报超出市场预期的幅度可能让市场会稍微有一些失望。

财报会议重点一：数据中心业务拆分

财报会议里面有两个重点。第一个重点：在接下去的财报里边，英伟达将会把他们 datacenter（数据中心）这一块的业务拆分开来，拆成两块——第一块是 hyperscale，第二块叫做 ACIE，你可以理解为就是 AI native，就是除了这些大科技以外、其他所有 AI 公司给英伟达在数据中心这一块所创造的营收。

在两年前，市场认为英伟达数据中心这一块的营收会有非常大的风险，因为这一块的营收主要来自于前大科技——如果大科技资本开支放缓的话，那带来的营收也会放缓。但是现在你会发现，从数据中心营收占比这一块：hyperscale、大的云厂这一块占到了380亿美金，而其他剩下的这类公司营收占到了370亿美金。也就是说，现在英伟达营收的组成里边，一半来自于大科技，剩下的一半全是来自于 AI 的初创公司。

当然，AI 的初创公司包括了什么？包括了现在最大的两家大模型 Anthropic 和 OpenAI，同时还有现在对英伟达营收增速、营收贡献非常高的这些 neocloud。

英伟达之所以要把数据中心这一块的业务拆分开来，也是市场最近这段时间一直在问的：未来大科技有没有可能资本开支放缓？那资本开支放缓你们怎么办？他们拆分开来就已经要告诉市场：拆分开来，自然会把增速也拆分开来。

如果你去看，hyperscale 这一块环比增速12%，不低，但也绝对没有以前那么高了——环比增速12%年化的话要50%以上了，那么大体量已经非常夸张。但是拆分开来，我们会看到大科技贡献了12%的环比增速，而剩下的公司环比增速多少？31%。环比增速31%年化的话就是1.5倍、1.6倍了，非常高的增速。

对未来的期待是什么？就是大科技占英伟达数据中心营收占比将会越来越低，这是一定的趋势，因为大科技这一块的营收增速已经赶不上其他公司的营收增速了。

那然后市场可能又会说：你未来的营收增速可能太依赖于 OpenAI、太依赖于现在的当红炸子鸡 Anthropic，可能又会运行这样的故事线，直到下一家 Anthropic 公司出现之后……实际上 AI 是一个演变的过程。站在两年之前，可能没有人会想象到，非大科技对于英伟达数据中心这一块的营收贡献，竟然马上要超过大科技了。两年之后马上要超过大科技，没有人会想到这一点。

大家还记不记得在半年前，我记得在8月份的时候，我在国内做线下，包括我当时去了新加坡、去了台湾，包括我上年11月份在美西做线下的时候，我和大家分享过老黄在8月份发表过的观点。他说 AI 的初创公司、AI native 这些公司，整个24年底的时候所有公司营收全部加在一起是20亿美金（two billion）；他预计25年底能够翻十倍，达到200亿美金（20 billion）；然后他说在26年底的时候，如果再翻十倍达到 two hundred billion、2000亿美金，他也不会感到意外。

当时半年之前，大家可能会觉得老黄是疯了——一年翻十倍。你从20亿到200亿、增加18个 billion 还可以讲得过去，但是从200亿到2000亿、1800亿美金的增长，这不是轻轻松松可以做到的。但是站在当下，我们看一下 Anthropic 的 ARR、看一下 OpenAI 的 ARR，可能 Anthropic 一家公司到今年年底 ARR 就能够做到200亿美金了。

我记得非常清楚，在上年我做线下的时候，我特地拿了所有 AI 公司、所有 AI 这一块的落地、把这些初创公司的营收全部拿出来，当时我特地聊到了 Anthropic。我印象非常深刻，是因为当时 SemiAnalysis 的 founder——Dylan Patel——他说相比 OpenAI 来说，他其实更加看好 Anthropic 这一块的落地。半年之后全部得到验证：半年之前大家都在讨论 OpenAI，半年之后现在已经说 Anthropic 一个营收就已经超过 OpenAI 了。然后大家就对 OpenAI 觉得不行——但未来我相信 OpenAI 也是会回来的。

起码站在当下，我们回过头去看半年之前，你是没有办法想象那么这些 AI 初创公司营收这一块的落地的，因为 Anthropic 这一块的落地就是非常标准的 AI 落地了。所以从这个角度就可以看到：在整个行业里边，老黄所做的这些预期、预测，一年之前、半年之前你会觉得非常荒谬，半年之后你会觉得"是不是低估了、这个饼是不是画小了"。

财报会议重点二：GPU 与 CPU 的配比

第二点就是最近这段时间市场讨论度比较高的：未来 GPU 和 CPU 之间的比例。如果偏向于 CPU 的话，从原来的8比1到2比1到1比1，甚至有可能 CPU 和 GPU 之间的比例可能是"负"的——就是 GPU 会更少，1个 GPU 可能要配2个 CPU、可能要配4个 CPU。

针对这一点，也有分析员问了老黄这个问题：因为如果说 CPU 的配比会更高的话，是不是对你们那个 GPU 业务会受到一定的影响？

关于这个问题的回答，老黄其实从一个比较全面的视角去解答了。其实如果我们把英伟达所有的业务全部拆分开来去看的话，基本上每一个业务现在都已经或多或少会有一些竞争者了。比如说 GPU，有 AMD 的 GPU；如果是 CPU 的话，现在最新马上推出来的 Vera CPU——Vera CPU 本身为什么周四的时候 Arm 涨得非常好？因为 Vera CPU 它是可以卖给外部客户的，它可以单独卖，也可以和英伟达的 GPU、和它整个的 AI factory 一起去卖，两方面他都可以去做，所以周四 Arm 走势非常好，因为 Vera CPU 上面搭的是 Arm。

那么如果去看 CPU，现在除了英特尔是一个竞争者，英伟达又是一个竞争者，然后现在的大科技他们也有自研的 CPU，所以 CPU 这一块也有竞品。然后软件这一块虽然 CUDA 的护城河非常非常深，但是 ROCm（AMD 下面的软件这一块）似乎现在也有一些 traction 在背后、也有人在用了。

那么老黄现在讲的是什么？老黄现在讲的是：你不是拿单品出来去和其他公司的单品做对比——包括自研芯片 TPU 这一块也是非常好。老黄现在讲的概念是：整个 AI factory，包括其中的互联、GPU 到 CPU、到互联、到软件，你整个 AI factory 的一个 token output 是多少。

所以他未来在思考的方向是：现在你造任何一个数据中心，你思考的就是必须要匹配比如说1个吉瓦或者1.5个吉瓦的电力。但是他想未来通过软件去解决什么问题——因为现在电是一个稀缺资源，毕竟电网这一块的电要跟上的话没有那么快，电在整个数据中心里面还是一个稀缺资源。但是电网的使用率只有60%不到，那剩下的百分之三四十的电，在电网需求没有那么高的时候，有没有可能分配到数据中心这一块？也就是说，你数据中心必须要适应未来电的供给——它是一个 dynamic、一个变动的过程。

所以在整个市场试图思考"有没有一个环节能够拿出来一个产品去和英伟达竞争"的时候，老黄在看的是：我整个 AI factory、整个工厂，除了里边所有硬件的配套设施之外，包括我的软件——软件本身怎么去辅助所有的硬件、怎么去处理未来电这一块可能不是持续性的供给。他那个思考永远是比别人领先一步的——别人还在思考单一的节点能不能打败英伟达，但英伟达在思考的是我整个 AI 工厂的输出能不能领先于全球。

那么回到 GPU 和 CPU。你去看 Vera Rubin（今年年底推出的新品）的时候，其实 GPU 比 CPU 的话已经到了2比1的比例；如果从封装这个角度去看，已经是1比1的比例。所以老黄完全看到了未来 AI agent 落地之后对于 CPU 这一块的需求、对于 CPU 和 GPU 配比的需求，所以为什么 Vera Rubin 的设计就是2比1的设计。

但是他的一个观点是：未来 agent talk to agent，agent 下面可能还有一个 subagent，agent 和 subagent 之间的交流是通过 CPU 去决定的，但是你给任务给到了这个 subagent、给到了这个分的智能体身上之后，这个智能体本身还是需要思考的，那这个思考本身还是会需要 GPU。所以第一点，他其实已经看到了未来对于 CPU 的需求，所以在整个架构的设计里边，这个配比本身已经是2比1；另外一点，如果真的未来到了一个 agent 的时代，agent to agent，那你最后一个 subagent，信息传递过去的确是需要 CPU，但是这个 subagent 去思考的话，还是会回到 GPU 这一块。

所以这么一个我认为非常有 vision 的领导者，这个 vision 不单是体现在带领整个行业的中上下游一起往前走——这是第一点，已经非常不容易了；第二点是它能够看到未来 AI 应用这一块的落地。就单就这两点：你能看到需求、看到落地，第二你能带着行业往前走，难道他出来的产品本身、在产品设计这一块，你觉得它还能够落后他的对手吗？其实我认为是不会的——他已经看到这个未来，他知道未来这个痛点在哪，他一定是为了解决这个痛点这个方向去走。

所以我觉得英伟达还是那一个英伟达，它的护城河还是英伟达的护城河，包括产品的迭代这一块，其他公司基本上也是难以企及。而且最终还加上英伟达现在的估值还是在一个非常合理的阶段。所以英伟达无论是从基本面、从护城河、从估值，我认为现在如果你要去选一个标的去投的话，像英伟达、包括像 Broadcom 这类的公司，现在仍然可以去投。

不过，前面我和大家进行了一个坏消息和好消息——从好消息这个角度去看，现在的估值仍然没有非常高，那即使站在当下，我认为英伟达、Broadcom 这类的公司仍然可以去投。

沃尔玛财报

然后我们来看一下沃尔玛的财报，稍微快速带过一下。

首先未来3年 EPS 的预期其实并没有下降，如果和过去三年去做对比的话，反而还是出现了上升——未来三年是11、13和8，过去三年是6、3和5。当然我说了未来的盈利是有风险在那边的。

那从 P/E 这个角度去看，确实沃尔玛的 P/E 一直是在一个高位。沃尔玛、Costco 这两家公司 P/E 就是在一个高位。如果从 price to cash flow 这个角度去看的话，就还好，相对会合理很多了。但是沃尔玛像 Costco，它对于其他的一些零售销售公司来说，它一直就有一个溢价在那边。

看沃尔玛这类的公司，我更多的是从他们的财报去看整个宏观、去看整个消费者。从消费者这个角度去看，其实基本上是符合了银行的财报：高收入人群这一块，他说 "high income consumer spend confidently"，高收入这一块的人群消费现在还是非常有信心，想花钱、花起钱来就非常自信；然后低收入人群这一块，现在仍然非常谨慎。

这样的信号、这样的观察结果，我们经过去两年基本上都是这样的情况。因为股市走得好，所以高收入人群有资产在那边，所以消费起来就非常自信。然后低收入人群这一块，再加上油价在一个高位，你会发现这是从2022年以来第一次看到他们每一次加油、平均加油的体量在1加仑以下了——你每一次加油的体量越小，就代表你越来越谨慎，你期待油价会下跌，所以每一次的加油都不加满。

但是你把这两个人群全部加在一起的话，整体来说的消费还是保持在一个相对比较健康的位置。我们在看宏观经济的时候，不可能挑一个好的现象出来、或者挑一个坏的现象出来，就把它去代表所有——好的也得看、坏的也得看，综合起来去看整个宏观环境。

最后是沃尔玛的估值这一块。前面我说了它的 P/E 一直是在一个相对比较高的位置。今年12月份还记不记得价值股做得非常好的时候，沃尔玛45倍的 P/E，当时有人拿沃尔玛和亚马逊出来做对比——亚马逊20多倍、沃尔玛40多倍，那凭什么？亚马逊的增速那么高。那现在你会发现在过去这一段时间，3月份、4月份以来亚马逊走得非常好、也破了新高了，然后沃尔玛出现回调了。

所以一家公司在估值在高位的时候，我们就会发现：不是说你不可以持有，但是你要持有的话，你必须要有一个期待——接下去估值有可能会进行调整，那即使它基本面还是非常好，但是不妨碍它估值进行调整。像今天周四沃尔玛发布完财报之后，出现了7%的下跌，我认为合理——因为 P/E 在一个相对高位，你出来一个没有非常好的财报，市场有一些担心，进行一定程度的杀估值就是非常合理。那么即使杀完之后，周四收盘估值在40.2倍，还是在过去一个相对高位。

Intuit 财报

最后是 Intuit，一家软件公司，我稍微聊一下，因为我今天特地去听了一下他们的财报。

YTD（年初至今）将近要腰斩了，下跌了50%多了。然后这一次发布完财报之后——周三的财报，发布完财报之后周四下跌了20%，基本上一直都比较低迷。

其实我前段时间有和大家分享过软件公司，每家软件公司都不一样了，接下去你要去看它的营收增速有没有放缓、有没有加速，它的 margin 有没有受到伤害。正常来说，如果 AI 是利好这家软件公司的话，营收要加速、margin 可能保持在位置，那就是 AI 非常明显地利好这些软件公司。

那我们就从结果去看，包括现在市场的预期去看：Intuit 这家公司，它过去三年营收这一块的增速是30、3、6（编者注：原文如此），未来3年13、13、22，就稍微有一些放缓，但基本上还是保持在13%的频率，其实营收增速并没有放缓。所以这并不是一个坏的信号，这就是一个正常的信号，有没有 AI 它都是一样。

然后是它 EPS 的增速：22、8和19，未来三年17、14和13，稍微出现一些程度的放缓。但是你去看它的 PE，现在拉过去四个季度这个 PE 是13倍。你讲一下，它的 EPS 增速也没有非常明显的放缓，也就是价格腰斩，那就代表它的估值从原来的26倍下降到现在的13倍了——估值腰斩，完全是我们叫做 rerate，对于这些公司的估值完全是杀估值的这种行为。

那你去看 forward P/E，看他们2027财年，11.38倍，这个估值已经非常便宜。当然，某种程度，如果你拿22年的 Meta 去做对比的话，那可能可以去到更低——22年的 Meta 才8倍、9倍，当市场真的情绪面低到非常低的位置的时候，估值确实可以低到非常低。但是22年 Meta 出现的是它 EPS 增速的放缓也非常低，它 EPS 也下跌了60%到75%，EPS 下跌的幅度也非常快、出现了负增长。但是 Intuit 这一类的公司现在 EPS 增速基本上和以前保持一样，没有非常明显的放缓，就单纯的是一个杀估值——这个杀估值杀的是未来的不确定性。但是这个未来的不确定性能不能实现、未来这个负面消息能不能实现，说实话现在没有人知道。

从 margin 这个角度去看，operating margin，24年是39%。你看其实他们从20年到24年 margin 一直是在上升的，从34.7% 上升到了39.1%；25年40.2%，26财年（马上还有一个季度就结束了）是41.04%。所以基本上它的毛利率在过去三年、过去7年基本上是一直保持在一个缓慢上升的过程之中，包括市场对于未来三年 margin 的预期也是在42%左右。营收增速还是可以保持在12%至13%左右，margin 可以保持在42%，也就是代表 AI 其实对于市场的预期、对他们未来的营收和盈利没有任何影响。

下跌的可能理由

我去听了一下他们的财报会，如果要挑出一些下跌的理由的话：第一点，你可以挑——他们最大体量的那一块业务在报税这一块，特别是低收入人群，可能受到了一部分的影响。

然后另外一点，我看彭博都写出来这个新闻了，因为他们要裁员17%，所以出现了下跌——可能管理层本身对于自己内部的运营效率不是非常满意。但是通常来说，这些大科技裁员的话，股价不是应该上涨吗？所以裁员本身完全不应该是一个理由。只是你倒过去推的话，因为已经看到了下跌，所以说"因为他们裁员导致下跌"，但实际上对于大多数公司来说，裁员应该是一件好事，并不是件坏事。所以基本面的话，你要去找理由的话，可能就是报税这一块的业务受到了一定程度的影响。

Intuit 与 Adobe 的异同

某种程度，我认为他们和 Adobe 有一定的相似处。相似的点在哪？就是低端的这些客户、原本低端的这些客户完全受到了影响，甚至已经离开了、不用这些软件；Adobe 也是一样的，但是高端的用户他的 ARPU 反而是出现了上升——高端用户愿意花更多的钱，低端用户是完全损失。这是这两家公司之间的相似处。

但是这两家公司一个不是非常相似的点是在：报税这一块的市场，我认为它是有非常大局限性的——它是有一个 TAM 在那边，这个市场并不会扩大，你只是抢占别人的一些市场份额，比如说像抢占 HR 这一类的市场份额，它是可以从其他客户那边抢一些营收过来。但是像 Adobe 这一类的软件公司来说，Adobe 更多的是偏向于是个工具，而这个工具本身它是有一定护城河的。

当然，我不是说 Intuit 完全没有护城河，它有比如说监管那些护城河，包括 accountability，它有一部分的护城河在那边。但 Adobe 的话，因为现在 Adobe 确实低端人群没有人用了，高端人群这一块愿意花更多的钱、在设计这一块愿意花更多的钱；但是 Adobe 因为现在有了大模型的分发之后，免费使用的人群数量增速非常快——在这一部分免费使用的人群身上，有可能未来 Adobe 是可以去变现的。也就是它的确损失了一部分低端人群，但是通过大模型这一块的分发之后，反而未来会有更多的设计者会出现。所以我认为这可能是这两家公司之间的一些差别——有相似点，然后也有一些差距，因为毕竟每家软件公司都不一样。

Adobe 在6月份要发布财报了，反正看嘛。我是给 Adobe 两个季度的财报去验证我的思考是不是正确的：如果不正确的话，那就离场；如果正确的话，那非常好，证明未来如果有机会判断完全正确、他一步一步走出来、能够验证自己的话，那未来有没有加仓这个可能性也是有的；但是如果判断错误的话，那就离场了。

对 Intuit 的担心

讲回到 Intuit 这家公司来说，我个人是有一些担心的。我的担心点是在：我们往未来去看的话，AI agent，我个人的期待是未来每个人都会有自己的 AI personal assistant、都会有自己的个人智能体。那像报税这一类的事情，每一年只做一次，虽然你需要那个准确度非常高、且有一个 responsibility、有一个责任在那边，报税不能瞎报、不能乱报，但未来个人的 finance 全部交给智能体去处理，我认为是一个非常自然的过程。

这个智能体本身了解我所有的消费、了解我所有的账单、了解我所有的收入。我只要把我的账单、我所有消费的记录、包括我的收入全部交给 AI、交给我这个个人的智能体、个人助手的智能体，然后我个人助手的智能体帮我去找到匹配的报税的智能体，把这个任务交给他，然后这个报税的智能体帮我去完成这件事情——我认为是一定会发展的方向。

那这个报税的智能体，到底是 Intuit 去做、还是其他的一些 AI native 公司去做，我并不是非常清晰。说实话，从我个人的角度，我是愿意去接受的——只是现在可能还没有这个 AI 所存在，我也没有对这个 AI 有一定的舒适度。如果未来有这个 AI 的存在、有一定的舒适度，我绝对愿意去接受。甚至于我认为这个 AI 它能够提供的价值——因为现在你想，有很多税务的问题你其实已经去问 AI 了，只是 AI 可能并不是100%的时间都一定能够给你准确的答案，而在报税里边一定是有一些比较主观的看法、并不是一个客观的视角，那么所以这时候才有了我们叫做 assist system 的东西，就是辅助报税：你个人也好、AI 智能体也好，会给你一些建议和意见等等，这时候就体现了你这个产品本身的价值。

但是我说，从我个人的感受和体验来说，我是愿意把这一部分工作全部交给 AI 去做的。当然这个 AI 现在本身似乎还不存在，但是我认为未来一定会有这样一个 AI 智能体存在的——因为你使用的频率其实并不高，你一年就使用一次。所以我自己是愿意去尝试的。那未来是不是会以这种形式存在、未来谁能够承接到这个市场，我可能看得没有那么清晰。

结尾：Alpha Picks 量化选股

OK，那今天讲了那么多，最后要和大家分享的是 Seeking Alpha 下面的一个量化选股策略，叫做 Alpha Picks。

这边是标普500 YTD 的走势，8.3%；Alpha Picks 这个量化选股功能32.5%，还是不错的对吧。那我个人到现在为止，小幅跑赢他们的量化选股，所以我经营的投资组合也是不错的。当然他们那个投资组合，在4月份、3月份到5月份，基本上每两周他们会做一些标的上的调整，他们选的这些标的、过去选的这5个标的，其实走势还是不错的，基本上5个标的里面4个标的都跑赢了标普。

所以大家如果想去抄作业的话，现在 Seeking Alpha 有一个10%的优惠活动：Alpha Picks 量化选股功能有10%的优惠，然后他们还有一个 Premium 的订阅，也是10%的优惠，加上7天的免费试用。如果大家喜欢 combo，就 Premium 加上 Alpha Picks 的话，也有一个更加优惠的折扣。三个链接大家都可以在置顶的留言里面找到。

好，那今天就期内小视频，我们下期见。

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一、本期最反常识的观察：主持人把"数据源本身"变成了坏消息
绝大多数财经博主的"坏消息"都指向市场——某个收益率破位、某个财报暴雷。但这期视频里，那个被郑重其事提醒"千万不要错过"的坏消息，竟然是**他自己引用的彭博终端数据错了**。这是一个极罕见的、把镜头对准自己工具的时刻。
这里藏着一个被忽略的系统性真相：**当一把尺子可以被悄悄重新校准，它丈量出的"贵"与"便宜"就失去了意义**。纳斯达克综合指数的 forward P/E 从"30倍"被修正到"25–26倍"，差距不是市场变了，而是计算方法变了。他随后果断弃用综合指数、改用成分股仅100只的 NDX——这本质上是科学仪器里的"信噪比"决策：3000多个标的的指数，估值口径越容易被噪音和方法论变动污染。这其实是 Goodhart 定律的一个变体——**一个指标一旦被广泛当作锚，它就开始变得不可靠**。一个愿意公开承认"我上次给你们的数据是错的"的分析者，比一个永远正确的分析者更值得信任，这件事本身也是个反直觉的洞见。
## 二、跨领域连接：今晚整期视频，其实在反复讲同一个"分叉"故事
表面上这是四个互不相干的话题——

item.title: [Latent Space] AI Agents Need Computers: 74% MoM Growth, 850K/Day Runs, & New Agent Cloud — Ivan Burazin, Daytona
item.dc:creator: Latent Space
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、最值得玩味的信号：不是"好评"，而是"被人追着要"

大多数创业者用正面反馈来验证 PMF——用户说"我喜欢"。Ivan 的判断标准恰恰相反：真正的拐点不是用户在 demo 里夸你，而是第二天没发 API key，每一个人都打电话回来追问"我的 access 在哪"。这是一个反直觉的观察——"我们想要 access"这句礼貌话本身毫无信息量（人人都会说），有信息量的是用户愿意承担社交成本去催你。这其实和医学诊断里的"疼痛迁移测试"是一回事：病人说哪里疼不可靠，但当你按一个地方、他的身体不受控制地躲开，那才是真信号。Daytona 的 PMF 不是被"听"出来的，是被"追"出来的。

二、隐藏的真相：Daytona 根本不是一家"算力公司"，而是一家再保险公司

这是整集里被轻描淡写、却最颠覆的事实——平均利用率 15%，峰值 90%。如果你把 Daytona 当成"卖 CPU 的"，这个数字是灾难；但如果你换个领域看，它立刻合理：这正是保险公司、电网调峰电厂、航空公司超售系统的财务结构。客户买的从来不是 CPU，而是"峰值那一刻一定有容量"的承诺。所以他要把 RL 客户锁进 commit、要做跨地域的负载搬运——这些不是算力优化，这是精算和风险池。一个跨领域连接因此浮现：Cloudflare 能把利用率做高，是因为人类流量"follow the sun"、波动温和、可预测；而 agent 的 RL 负载是 10 个数量级的方波尖峰,可预测性趋近于零。换句话说，agent 经济正在把整个云基础设施行业，从"公用事业模型"推回"保险模型"。谁能定价不确定性，谁就赢。

三、最意外的发现：负载曲线的形状，暴露了一个 agent 的"出身"

Ivan 顺口提到两类负载：background agent（Cognition、Lovable、Harvey）的使用曲线"follow the sun"——中午高、午夜低、周末低；而 RL/eval 负载是不分昼夜的方波。停下来想想这意味着什么:那些模仿人类知识工作者训练出来的 agent，连人类的昼夜节律和周末都继承了。它们本不需要睡觉，却在午夜变懒——因为它们的"父母"（被模仿的人类用户）会在午夜变懒。而纯机器原生的 RL 训练负载没有这种节律。于是负载曲线变成了一种"指纹"：看一个 AI 的算力波形，就能判断它离人类有多近。这是一个跨领域连接——生物学里有"个体发育重演系统发育"，而这里是"算力波形重演训练来源"。沿着这条线推下去:未来 agent 越机器原生、越摆脱人类模仿，整个 compute 市场的可预测性就越差，保险模型的风险就越大。

四、被忽略的连接：那个"把整个 codebase 存成 JSON 扔进 S3"的客户

Ivan 当成趣闻讲的细节，其实是全集最深的一刀。一个客户放弃 git 的 diff 机制，每次把整个代码库整体快照、连差异都不算——因为快、因为便宜、因为不用动脑。这不是偷懒，这是 agent 原生的世界观：和 LLM 本身的工作方式一模一样——大模型改代码也从不"打补丁"，它重新生成整段。人类发明 git diff，是因为人类的认知带宽昂贵、必须用"聪明的增量"省力；而 agent 的算力便宜、聪明（约束推理）反而贵。于是一个惊人的推论：git 的整个增量哲学，是为认知稀缺的物种设计的。当协作主体变成算力充裕的 agent，"暴力全量"会系统性地战胜"优雅增量"。 GitHub 之所以会被新东西替代，根本原因不是它慢，而是它的世界观属于另一个物种。

五、一个埋着的悖论，留给你

Ivan 猛烈批评市场给"转售 token 的 SaaS"贴溢价是错的——利润结构变差了却被当成"再加速"。但请注意:Daytona 自己就是在转售 CPU。他的辩护是"消费式 API 收入是真实收入"。这里有一个值得你警惕的认知盲区——他给别人的标准（margin 变差就该被打折）和给自己的标准（消费式增长是真加速），用的是两套尺子。也许他对，也许"卖底层算力"和"卖套壳 agent"确有本质差异;但更可能的真相是:在一场所有人都在重定价的浪潮里，每个人都能为自己找到"我这次不一样"的理由——这恰恰是泡沫的定义。他预言的"冷水澡"，未必只浇在别人头上。

📝 转录文本

Cold Open

I've never experienced this — that people literally call you if you do not give them access. Like, they want access right now. And so it's like, okay, they don't want this. The thing that they want doesn't seem to exist, or they have not found it, and they really, really want what what we want. And then when we understood that, we're onto something.

And then when you think about the size of the market — like, the market for every single agent that will exist ever in the future is just like, what is that market? How big is that?

Sponsor Message

Before we get into today's episode, I just have a small message for listeners. Thank you. We would not be able to bring you the AI engineering, science, and entertainment content that you so clearly want if you didn't choose to also click in and tune into our content.

We've been approached by sponsors on an almost daily basis. But fortunately, enough of you actually subscribe to us to keep all this sustainable without ads. And we want to keep it that way.

But I just have one favor to ask all of you. The single most powerful, completely free thing you can do is to click that subscribe button. The Origin Story: Code Anywhere to Daytona

Sure. Like, really way back — me and my co-founder have been together — I've said this multiple times — it's like, we were married, and divorced, and married. Some people actually asked me, is my co-founder my partner? Like, they thought it literally — it's not literally, but we have done multiple companies together.

And, to your point, we had this shift where we went from Code Anywhere to the conference, called Shift, and then back to Daytona.

We originally started stacking servers, doing like virtualization in the early 2000s, and, you know, routers, and doing basically all these things at a foundational level. And that was a services company, which we sold to focus on what my co-founder actually invented — which was the very first browser-based IDE.

I say "the first" — before us was actually Heroku. They did it for a very, very short time, until they became Heroku. But outside of them, we were the only one, and it was called Cloud9 —

There was Cloud9 —

There was Cloud9 that came out slightly after us. There was Replit, which came out when we stopped doing it — Replit came out, and they have been successful since then, which is great. There was Nitrous.IO. There was quite a few that existed in time. But it was, like, too early.

But the interesting part is that, at that point in time — because there was no VS Code, for those that still remember — there was no VS Code, there was no Kubernetes, and Docker had just started when we — I'm not sure if it was even public at that point in time. And so we had to build everything into the whole stack ourselves. And that was the key learning that we brought into, and that we've been using in Daytona today.

But, you know, three years ago we started something similar with Daytona — which is not what we are today, but it was automating dev environments for human engineers — basically the underlining stack of Code Anywhere. And then we did a hard pivot last January to sandboxes. And so here we are.

What Is Daytona Today

So, what is Daytona today? Let's get, like, a crisp description. I'm wearing a shirt —

You're wearing a shirt? Yes.

Exactly. But we're going to probably have to change that, because it's also a subset of what we do. Unfortunately — we really love this. "Run code" is super simple, people interpret it different ways. I think we've given out five, six thousand of these shirts. People wear them with pride, because it doesn't really market about us, it markets to the person itself. So I think we did a really good job on that one.

But it's also a subset of what we do, because people, when they think about "run AI code," they just think about these small — let's call it isolates, code-execution boxes — that, you know, you send some code, you get an output.

Whereas what Daytona is today is essentially composable computers for AI agents. The market calls them sandboxes, which [is] misleading —

All these things —

Yeah, exactly. Because it can be misleading, because people usually think about sandboxes as a demo or a test environment, versus a production-grade environment.

But what Daytona does — if you think of the laptop that you have in front of you, or the computer that's over there, or, you know, my wife is an architect, so she has, like, a Windows with 3D graphics card inside to do 3D rendering — like, computers are different compositions of computers. And our belief is strongly that agents today and going forward will need all these different compositions of computers to do different types of tasks. And so we offer that, basically, through an API.

The insight that made us do this pivot is the quarter before that. So, end of 2024, when we had — basically, we did a demo with — I think we discussed this as well — Devin was not public. You actually gave me access to Devin at that time. So, Devin —

I did, yeah. I don't think I was supposed to.

Yeah, exactly. So it doesn't matter.

I gave, like, three friends access.

Yeah. Or it was a call, and you showed it to me — it doesn't matter. But OpenDevin was available, which [is] now called OpenHands. And so we're like, oh, this seems to be a thing. This is not public, let's take our [tool] for human automation of dev environments, and take OpenDevin, and launch that as a SaaS.

And we did that. Not very many people signed up and used it, but a lot of people reached out that were building agents, and they were like, "Hey, my agent needs a compute sandbox, runtime, whatever you want to call it" — I forgot what it was called at that point. And then we were like, oh, amazing — this is a new market, here is our infrastructure, here's our product, and go.

And what we found really, really fast soon was that people did not like what we had built. It didn't work.

And I remember talking to people at the beginning, when we're doing this — you know, the sandbox we're building for agents — people were like, "Oh, why is it different? It's the same thing. We have, like, EC2, we have VMs, we have all these things."

But we saw that everyone we gave it to — was like 20, 30 people — they all said, "No, like, this is not what we need. This breaks."

And basically, me and my co-founder, not knowing a lot about — because we're infra people, we're not AI people — so I basically took it upon myself to, like, watch every single podcast that exists, including all of these and all that, and sort of get up to date, read all the blogs, get [to] understand what's going on.

Do you want to shout out who else was useful? Just in case people are also looking.

So, generally — there's a few podcasts, different segments, different types. So there's you guys; No Priors; BG2 [Bill Gurley] was great while — yeah, while it was around. There's a few. 20VC is interesting from a different dynamic, and some are different dynamics. But —

But we're not really about the compute market.

It was also already — sorry —

I guess you're looking at the agent infra market.

I was looking at the agent market, in the AI market in general, and sort of understanding who are the players, what the perception, and how that goes. And, like, obviously you complement this with, like, going to conferences, going to events, going to meetups, reading white papers — like, doing all the things that you have to do to understand what's happening.

And so when we figured — when we sort of had an idea of what we had to build — literally over New Year's Eve, literally on New Year's Eve, I half vibe-coded the first MVP — first minimal viable product — of what Daytona is today. And I went to sleep at, like, 3:00 a.m. or something like that. I just put my, like, baby daughter and wife to sleep, and, you know, happy New Year's, and go back, just doing this.

And I sent it to my co-founder, my CTO, and he saw it in the morning, [and] he's like, "This is absolute garbage. Do not show this to anybody at all. But the idea is good."

And so he took two weeks, and he did it — [it didn't] look like that, not even — it was way worse, but it was a very simplistic view of what it should be. Like, it worked, but it was not ideal. And so he went — we went, found the hole, which is his job as CTO — to go, and he came back with this version.

We then called all the people that had said, like, "this is garbage," you know, a quarter ago. And we set up these calls, and we just demoed it to everyone. And all the calls went long — every single one. They were 15-minute calls, and they all went, like, 25, 30 minutes or whatnot. And everyone said, "We need — we want access."

There was no login, just an API key, 'cause this was just a beta or an alpha. And they said, "Oh, we want access." And we're like, "Sure, yeah, okay, thank you very much."

But after, like, the next day — if we did not send it, every single one — like, every call that we did, everyone came back: "Where is my IP?" Like, everyone wanted it. We're like, "Shit, like, this is it."

Like, I've never felt so — one, the understanding, to your point, was like: most people thought it was the same infrastructure for humans and agents. We understood, a quarter ago, it's not — we just didn't know what was the right primitive. And then when we came, and we gave it to these people — I've never experienced [this]. I've done multiple companies in my life. I've never experienced this, that people literally call you if you do not give them access. Like, they want access right now.

And so it's like, okay, they don't want this — the thing that they want doesn't seem to exist, or they have not found it, and they really, really want what we want.

And then when we understood that, we're onto something. And then when you think about the size of the market — like, the market for human engineers and enterprise is a very large market, so think GitLab or whatnot. But the market for every single agent that will exist ever in the future is just like, what is that market? How big is that?

And we're like, we are all in on this. And so that is where we made sort of the cut between the old product and the new one.

Building From First Principles

But it wasn't composable at the time.

So it was very — it was basically just a Linux box that you could change — that you could define number of CPUs, disk, and RAM. Like, that is what you could do. But you couldn't have multiple operating systems, you couldn't resize it on the fly, you couldn't add a GPU, you couldn't do all [those] things. It just — just the first sort of variation of that.

And was it bare metal from the start?

It was bare metal from the start. And so, the interesting thing that we thought about right away — so our —

Which — you know, give people the background — what is the normal path?

Yeah. So basically, most providers run this on top of VMs. And also — Firecracker. Yeah. They run a Firecracker on a VM.

And we also have Firecracker — we have multiple isolation layers, and we can do that. But the common way to do it is that the state of the machine, or the hard disk, is not part of the sandbox itself. And the other thing is, they're not meant to last forever. So most of them are preemptable — like, they're just a time that they can live.

And so our thought, when we're going into this, [was]: agents will be like humans, in the sense of — you don't want your laptop to be shut down until you're done with work. Like, you want to close the lid and open the lid, it's the same state. So agents would want that, like, pause and come back. They want those two things.

But also, agents really, really want speed. So when we thought about, like — we need something insanely fast. How to make it fast, how to make it long-running and stateful? And so those two things — it's like combining a Lambda and an EC2. Those two things together.

And so we didn't have an idea how others did it, because we didn't know too much that there was a market around this. It was more like, okay, this is what we need, what they need.

And we looked at Kubernetes — it wasn't good enough for that. We looked at Nomad — it didn't enable that. And so, our history in rewriting our own scheduler at Code Anywhere is basically what my CTO came up with. Like, he's like, "Oh, the learnings from there" — he brought it.

And the funny thing is, our third co-founder, when he saw it, he's like, "Dude, what is this? This is, like, 2008. Like, we went back in time." And he's like, "Exactly."

And so the reason why Daytona is, like, super, super fast — and you see this on benchmarks — is: we essentially run on bare metal, we have our own scheduler, we use the underlining disk, CPU, and RAM of the underlining machine — which means your IOPS are insanely fast, because there's no network between an EBS or something like that.

But also, the snapshot — the point-in-time, the templates — are also preloaded on the bare-metal machines. So when you fire off a sandbox from a template or a snapshot, you're essentially directed to the bare-metal machine where that snapshot is, based on that NVMe drive. And then it literally just turns on that machine, and it's local — there's no network latency, anything on there.

And so that is sort of the specificities — that, when we're thinking from first principles what a computer would look like for an agent, that is what we came up with, and that's what we created.

Benchmarks

Yeah. I should — maybe — I don't know if you endorse this, but someone does Compute SDK. You guys do very well on there, with, like, the TTI. I guess — is this a relevant benchmark for you guys?

I don't know. And it changes every day. So today, ARC — never heard of it.

Yeah, ARC —

So it is — but you are, you know, at least a third of the next tier of performance, and then there's a lot of other, better-known names that are very slow to sell.

Yeah. We've been the number one by far for a long time. And now there's different definitions also of sandboxes, different isolation patterns, different other things. So ArchE runs it literally on the S3 — the data. So it's a very different [thing], and they spin up a container for that. So the definition of a sandbox is something that we all need to get along with. But yeah, we're insanely fast on getting these things up and running.

And so you can see, even there, that it's a 0.101. So, like —

Close enough.

Yeah. I mean, what else do you need, right?

So, the benchmarks itself — I don't think the benchmarks equate to market ownership, or revenue, or anything like that. And I've seen this with multiple benchmarks, not just in sandboxes, but in general — benchmarks around —

It's table stakes.

Exactly. But it doesn't [equate] — like, you definitely have to be up there, and you have to be competing, so that people know that, "oh, this is definitely one of the top." Because this is only one dimension of what customers look for. There's other things, like: how many can you spin up consecutively? There's a feature set, there's support — there's all different things that people look at. But you definitely have to be there on the benchmarks.

Metrics and Scale

How many [do] people spin up consecutively?

So we have — concurrently, I guess, is the concurrency [metric]. So there's three metrics that we look at.

One is, like, time to spin up one. And so our time to spin up one is 60 milliseconds, with network latency. So: request, spin up, reply — 60 [ms], the whole thing, 60 milliseconds. That is one.

But if you want to spin up 50,000 at once, we are now at about 75 seconds. So it takes about 75 seconds to spin up, concurrently, 50,000. Some others — there's public data around this — take 2,000 seconds, which is 30 minutes. Like, there's different variations of that.

And then there is — so it is speed of one, speed of multiple, and then: how many can you consistently have up and running? And so we basically have, right now, no limit to how much we can add, because we basically own our own metal.

But the biggest customer of ours does, like, about 850,000 every single day — is sort of where they're just shy of a million every single day that they're running. We do have a request for half a million concurrent — which is literally half a million CPUs somewhere running. So that's an interesting [one].

And they pay by, like, vCPU-seconds.

Yeah.

The other thing is, I guess, the sleeping and the resuming, 'cause it's all the stateful resumption of all these things. What kind of workload are people putting through this? Like — do we measure by gigabytes in memory, gigabytes in storage — what are the costly ones out of all these features?

The most expensive thing [is] CPU. So [first CPU], the second, then it's RAM, then it's disk. We actually don't charge —

Which is snapshotting, right?

No, the snapshot is part of it. But basically, the size of your hard disk of your machine — so, do you have 10 gigabytes, you have 20, do you have 50, do you have whatever — and then the transference of that. Right now, currently, we don't charge for network at all.

Yeah, you got to fix that.

Yeah. It is very much a larger and larger part of our bill. So we're working around that part there. Obviously, that is the least expensive — so, the hard disk is the least expensive. So it's basically CPU, RAM for us, network (because we don't charge the customer), and then hard disk is how it's spun up.

Workload Types: Background Agents vs. RL and Evals

But there's also different types of workloads. So we basically split it up into two types of workloads in Daytona. One is what we call background agents, or long-running agents.

Okay.

And the other is basically RLs and evals, which I put sort of together. And so they have very different patterns of usage.

And if you look at the usage of a background [agent] — and I'll just name names of companies, not specifically — so, like, OpenHands —

Yeah.

So, like, a background agent — a Cognition, a Lovable, like all these things — a Harvey — these are all long-running background agents. And so if you look at their usage patterns, their usage patterns are similar to human — which is like, follow the sun. Basically, the usage pattern of that is, like, noon is probably the highest, and the midnight is the lowest, and then weekends are low, where you know, [versus] weekday.

So, actually — but that helps you to distribute your load through all time, you know.

The Spiky Workload Problem

Yeah. So the interesting thing is, like — we have those kinds of loads. But if you look at the researcher loads, they're quite different. So what they are is, like — if you give them concurrency of 10,000, or 50,000, or 100,000 CPUs, whatever it may be — when they fire off a run, it just [goes] 100%, and then just runs, runs, runs, and it stops. The usage pattern is squares, basically, right? And it's also not follow-the-sun, because people will fire it off at midnight before they go to sleep, but then wake up [and] — so it's very unpredictable, you don't know where that is.

So the shapes of the usage are quite different than we have had before. And also, what's interesting is — when it's sort of a follow-the-sun, even if you have a high-growth company, you can sort of predict your usage patterns and have enough capacity for that, because it sort of grows in a way you can project.

When you have companies doing sort of, like, evals and RL, they're super spiky. So they're going to come in — it's like, "we're going to use nothing," then, "can we have 100,000?", right? And then go back down, and then 100,000, and go back down. So it's very, very different.

So, do you want to lock them into commits so that —

Yeah, we do. So we have to lock them into some sort of commits to have that capacity, because we have to have, basically — we have to have the capacity for peak.

Yeah.

Right. And so, right now, Daytona's mean utilization is 15%. Fifteen.

Oh my God.

So it's very low — because it's very spiky. But it's very spiky [and yet] we get up to 90%.

Yeah.

So we have these things. And so what we're looking at right now as a company is, similar to Cloudflare, where you can, like, geo-move things around. But that works really well for, basically, the background agent — where [it's] follow-the-sun. But this — it's a very different shape. Obviously, with scale you figure these things out. But that's an interesting new problem that we have as a compute provider in the agent space.

The Compute Conference

And when we were doing the conference recently — and so we talked to, like, Nikita from Neon, and [I should bring it up] — [Parag] from Parallel and whatnot — everyone has the same problem, whereas, like, usage is super spiky. And this is something that has not happened before — that you have these types of, like — it was always [that] the amplitudes were not this high. So it's quite [an] interesting use case and problem to solve.

Yeah. I don't know if we're going to bring this up again, but let's just talk about the conference. You had, like, a thousand-something people at the Warriors game — at the, sorry, where is it, what's the —

Chase Center.

Chase Center. I went — it was very impressive. Obviously, you know how to throw a conference. What did you learn? You pulled together all these impressive names — and what were you looking for?

My thesis behind the Compute Conference was: let's bring together people that are building infrastructure for AI agents. Because when I think of what we're building, it is — the agent is the primary user. What are the ergonomics and usage patterns of agents?

And what I found — this was a theory, it wasn't proven — is that we all have these problems, as I touched on. And, as I was talking on stage, it was like: we all have the same underlining infra problems — which is this spiky workload, unpredictable workloads that we've never had before in human compute or human infrastructure.

And it's, again — it's the same when I was talking to Parag, or when I was talking to Lin, Nikita. Lin especially — I was talking to her the other day as well. It is a very interesting type of problem to solve.

[I'll] touch on Cloudflare, because there's a lot of talk about that recently — is how they solve that. Which is: they have a bunch of geos, and basically, as users work in different places, and depending on your tier, they can move you around the geos. And so that's how they get to the higher utilization. But you can sort of predict these. And it's — if it's something — you'll rarely get a spike that is 10 orders of magnitude. Like, you'll get a spike like, let's say one of your customers has an exponential curve — what is that? I mean, I'm using cloud as an example — 10%, 20%, whatever. I don't have this data, I'm just assessing — it's surely not 10x, right? It's surely not something there.

And so, how do you go out and solve this problem? And we're all solving this in different ways.

So she also has the same thing.

So, yeah. I know specifically that, like, Neon had that issue as well — like, how are we solving these spiky loads and things like that. 'Cause we talked about [it]. And so the interesting thing for me to actually internalize was: yes, everyone that's building for agents-first is going through this, and we're all solving similar problems.

Whereas Lin maybe has to care a bit more, because obviously she's doing GPU inference. And — for listeners, we did an episode with her one and a half years ago. And you have to care, but like —
