item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、比特币最大的悖论：它是做空美联储的资产，却靠美联储的"恐慌反射"而活

整集节目里最被低估的一句话，是 Noelle 对 2023 年银行业危机的复盘。当时所有人欢呼"比特币上涨是因为人们终于看清了银行系统的腐败与脆弱"，而她冷冷地纠正：不，比特币上涨是因为人们预期央行会出手放水。

请把这句话和她后面讲的 BLIS 交易（Big, Large and Lasting Stimulus）并排放在一起看，你会得到一个令人不安的结论：比特币诞生的叙事是"对抗美联储的法币滥发"，但在真实的资金流里，比特币其实是做多美联储那只随时会按下去的恐慌按钮。它不是法币的反义词，它是法币救助机制的杠杆化衍生品。换句话说——这是本期最反直觉的观察——加密世界一边在意识形态上诅咒央行,一边在仓位上押注央行永远不会让系统真正崩溃。一个真正"去中心化、抗审查、对抗印钞"的资产，它的最佳买点恰恰是"印钞机即将启动"的时刻。比特币的信徒和美联储的救市派，本质上是同一笔交易的两端。

二、"通行费"是贯穿整集的隐藏母题——从霍尔木兹海峡到 Hyperliquid 的 90% 分成

注意一个文字上的巧合：节目里中国油轮要驶出霍尔木兹海峡，需要向伊朗交一笔 "fee / toll"（通行费）；而 Ram 评价 Hyperliquid 时也用了完全相同的词——Hyperliquid 拿走 90% 的收入，是它多年苦功"exact 的那笔 toll"。两处用的是同一个英文单词，绝非偶然。

这揭示了一个跨领域的结构真相：地缘政治的咽喉要道经济学，和加密市场的基础设施经济学，是同一套逻辑。 谁卡在"必经之路"的节点上，谁就收税。伊朗卡的是物理海峡；Hyperliquid 卡的是链上交易场所；而 Coinbase 这次最精明的动作，恰恰是把自己安插成 USDC 的"抵押品管理者"——一个新的咽喉节点。它表面上是在和竞争对手"对齐合作"，实质上是花钱买下一个未来的收费站。

更妙的跨域连接在于：沃什想要的"减少点阵图、减少前瞻指引"，以及 SEC 想要的"减少财报披露频次"，同样是一种收费站的建立。当信息从"被免费喂养"变回"稀缺品"，谁能解读信息谁就重新拥有了护城河。 节目里 Noelle 半开玩笑地问"这是不是意味着分析师终于得真正干活了"——这句玩笑底下是严肃的产业重组：过去十年央行的"信息透明化"实际上摧毁了卖方分析的价值，而沃什正在悄悄把这个行业的定价权还回去。霍尔木兹、Hyperliquid、美联储——三个看似无关的场景，都在做同一件事：在必经之路上重新设卡收费。

三、美联储真正的产品不是利率，而是"信任"——它和它鄙视的加密 KOL 卖的是同一样东西

Noelle 反复强调一句话：鲍威尔的首要工作是"管理信任"（manage trust），美联储 most of what it does is 管理信任。她甚至毫不留情地拆解了"鲍威尔人格崇拜"——那个"你愿意一起去喝棉花糖拿铁的爷爷"形象。

这里藏着一个会让人愣一下的观察：美联储的核心产品，和这期节目广告时段里那些"KOL activations"、那些靠人格魅力带货的加密影响者，是同一种东西——被精心管理的、半准社交化（parasocial）的信任。 区别只在于美联储管理的是对一个机构的信任，KOL 管理的是对一个个人的信任。鲍威尔的 FOMC 发布会，本质上是一档运营了七年的、收视极高的"信任内容节目"。这也正是为什么 Steve 顺口说"SNL 第 52 季应该演一集 FOMC 会议"会显得如此自然——FOMC 早已经是一档有固定角色、固定剧本、固定观众的连续剧了。

由此引出本集最深的一处张力：当一个机构的主要资产是"信任"，它就再也无法承认自己错了。 这正是 Noelle 解释为什么美联储不能把通胀目标从 2% 改成 3% 的原因——"三是新的二"人人心知肚明，但一旦官方承认够不到 2%，被摧毁的不是某个数字，而是机构信任本身。于是美联储被锁进了一个荒诞的处境：它必须假装追求一个所有人都知道它达不到的目标。这和加密世界的某些项目方何其相似——叙事一旦立起来，就成了不能拆的承重墙。Noelle 留下的真正问题不是"独立性好不好"，而是那个更锋利的反问：当"独立"意味着"无需对任何决策负责、可以无视传票"，独立性还是优点吗？ 一个把信任当作产品来经营的机构，最终会发现"维护信任"和"承认真相"是直接冲突的——而这，恰恰也是整个加密行业还没想明白的那道题。

📝 转录文本

Powell Is Out, Warsh Is In: What It Means for Crypto

Bits + Bips — The Interview

【冷开场片段：Coinbase 与 Hyperliquid 的"对齐"】

So I was wondering what it said to you that Coinbase is, you know, through this gesture, aligning more closely with Hyperliquid. And if that, you know, kind of move there to increase its position of stake type even shed any further light on the stablecoin deal.

Yeah, Laura, I was thinking the same thing. It feels like in many ways, Hyperliquid and Coinbase are both going after this sort of everything-exchange situation — you know, spot trading, perpetuals trading, equities, prediction markets. And it's true, like, you know, they have to acquire a significant amount of HYPE and stake it to be able to offer this, you know, AQA. And so this shows significant alignment between the two businesses, even though there's so much competition.

To me, what this shows is that Coinbase is very certain in its growth as a primarily US-based compliant institution, whereas Hyperliquid, I think they see as more of this outside-of-US-jurisdiction, no-KYC platform — which I think Coinbase can't really compete as much in. But they can leverage the usage of Hyperliquid globally to grow distribution of USDC, which I think ultimately in the long term will positively affect their bottom line.

We're at a critical point. And so you're spot on in that, for the longest time, I would always say: Econ 101, Bitcoin has all the attributes of a successful currency. But the one thing it's been missing is a clear set of legal and regulatory guidelines. So if we have clarity in the mix, that's the puzzle piece. Like, that's the last piece to unlock all of the attributes of a successful currency.

Ram, let me actually throw this ball to you on the market side of things. Does this make you more or less bullish on Coinbase, on Circle in terms of the stocks, and on the deployment of stablecoins versus banks overall?

It's a win for all of them. It's an especially big win for Hyperliquid. For Coinbase and Circle, they were able to neutralize an emerging competitor. Very strategic move to get USDC involved — and with Coinbase essentially this collateral manager, installing themselves at a very key point in this new infrastructure.

For Hyperliquid, it's a major win — like 90% of the revenue here. So that's the toll that they exact for having accomplished a tremendous amount over the last several years. We talked about Hyperliquid, I think, three or four weeks ago. This is one of the assets that you wanna own this cycle. And it was very prophylactic of Coinbase to get ahead of that, because Hyperliquid is emerging as a decentralized trading venue. Obviously, Circle generates a material amount of recurring revenue, net interest income. So it's a win for everybody, but it's especially a big win for Hyperliquid.

I agree with you on distribution, but I think the other end of the barbell is energy. He who has access to unlimited energy and effectively cheap compute probably gets a win — and I think it's pretty fair to say that Elon has an edge there. I know that we were talking about this morning, the physics, the science of getting your energy pretty much for free in space ain't easy. But if anyone can do it, it's the guy who has the — I don't want to say the M word, but he has a very, very robust capability across the globe in getting things into outer space. So I think that's Elon's unfair advantage at the very back of the stack.

【广告时段】

A 3% top-off on my entire portfolio? Uh, yes. Who is saying no to that? That's free money. I don't care what's in your portfolio. That's a yes. If you've been looking for an excuse to consolidate your assets, this is it. Until May 31st, Coinbase is giving a straight 3% Bitcoin boost on any crypto or cash deposits all month. Coinbase One is the ultimate membership to make the most of your money, which, as you know, is how I optimize my finances. Zero trading fees on thousands of crypto assets, 3.5% APY on USDC, boosted staking and lending rewards, and up to 4% Bitcoin back with the Coinbase One card. Plus, you can still claim 20% off the first year of Coinbase One annual plans, a $50 Bitcoin bonus when you spend $100 on a new Coinbase One card in the first 30 days through May 31st. Plus, your share of 5 Bitcoin if you out-predict pro basketball coach Lethal Shooter, and a chance at a private shooting session with him. It's the perfect time to centralize your assets and maximize your earnings. Get your 3% boost at Coinbase.com/unchained. Visit Coinbase.com/unchained — that's Coinbase.com/unchained — to claim your Bitcoin bonus. No purchase necessary. See rules and other ways to enter. Terms apply to other offers. Futures swaps via Coinbase Financial Markets. Risk of 100% loss. Payouts event-based. Not investment in — not available in Nevada. Coinbase One Card is offered through Coinbase Inc. and Cardless Inc. Cards issued by First Electronic Bank. Bitcoin-back rates are based on cardholders' assets on Coinbase.

Step into a new era of wealth. Discover Nexo, the premier digital wealth platform. Manage your crypto portfolio with confidence and control. Receive interest on your digital assets. Borrow against them without selling. Trade a wide range of cryptocurrencies, all in one platform. Now available in the U.S. with 30 days of exclusive privileges for new clients. Experience Wealth Club Premier. Access enhanced interest rates, reduced borrowing costs, and crypto cashback on swaps. Get started today at nexo.com/unchained.

Multichain Advisors is an emerging technology growth firm that has helped create over $50 billion in enterprise value for more than 80 clients like Pith, MoonPay Commerce, and Wormhole. They've worked with some of the largest and most impactful companies in the space. They're the partner you want when you're navigating markets and trying to break out from the noise. They help navigate TGEs, go-to-market, BD and partnerships, capital markets advisory, PR, media placements, KOL activations, and more — driving execution from launch to scale. Their results are measurable. To learn more and start building real traction today, visit multichainadv.com.

Bitcoin changed how money works. Citraya changes how Bitcoin scales. Citraya uses Bitcoin as both the settlement and data availability layer. As Bitcoin's application layer, Citraya enables the first trust-minimized BTC on a fully programmable platform and a native stablecoin for Bitcoin, CTUSD. Citraya offers Bitcoin capital markets with lending, privacy, payments, Bitcoin yield, trading, and predictions. Citraya expands Bitcoin's utility without sacrificing its security. Citraya mainnet is live. Get started at citraya.xyz/unchained.

Etherfi is giving Unchained listeners 15% cash back on rideshares, groceries, and restaurants right now, which honestly is kind of wild for a card like this. On top of that, I'm getting 3% cash back on every single transaction using my actual crypto. No conversion fees, no nonsense. My bank never once did that. And it goes beyond just spending. You can borrow against your holdings at 4% or less, which is super useful if you don't want to sell your assets. You can also earn on all major assets, up to 8% APY, just by holding. And moving money is just easy. No hidden transfer fees, no friction. It just works globally. If you want to check it out, go to ether.fi.com.

【冷开场片段（重复）：Coinbase 与 Hyperliquid】

Coinbase also said that it significantly increased its position of stake type, and that was interesting to me because I view Hyperliquid in some ways as a competitor to Coinbase. So I was wondering what it said to you that Coinbase is, you know, through this gesture, aligning more closely with Hyperliquid — and if that, you know, kind of move there to increase its position of stake type even shed any further light on the stablecoin deal?

Yeah, Laura, I was thinking the same thing. It feels like in many ways, Hyperliquid and Coinbase are both going after this sort of everything-exchange — spot trading, perpetuals trading, equities, prediction markets. And it's true, they have to acquire a significant amount of HYPE and stake it to be able to offer this, you know, AQA. And so this shows significant alignment between the two businesses, even though there's so much competition.

To me, what this shows is that Coinbase is very certain in its growth as a US-based — primarily US-based — compliant institution, whereas Hyperliquid, I think they see as more of this outside-of-US-jurisdiction, no-KYC platform, which I think Coinbase can't really compete as much in. But they can leverage the usage of Hyperliquid globally to grow distribution of USDC. But I think ultimately in the long term, will positively affect their bottom line.

We're at a critical point. And so you're spot on in that, for the longest time, I would always say: Econ 101, Bitcoin has all the attributes of a successful currency. But the one thing it's been missing is a clear set of legal and regulatory guidelines. So if we have clarity in the mix, that's the puzzle piece. Like, that's the last piece to unlock all of the attributes of a successful currency.

Ram, let me actually throw this ball to you on the market side of things. Does this make you more or less bullish on Coinbase, on Circle in terms of the stocks, and on the deployment of stablecoins versus banks overall?

It's a win for all of them. It's an especially big win for Hyperliquid. For Coinbase and Circle, they were able to neutralize an emerging competitor. Very strategic move to get USDC involved — and with Coinbase essentially this collateral manager, installing themselves at a very key point in this new infrastructure. For Hyperliquid, it's a major win — like 90% of the revenue here. So that's the toll that they exact for having accomplished a tremendous amount over the last several years. We talked about Hyperliquid, I think, three or four weeks ago. This is one of the assets that you want to own this cycle. And it was very —

【节目正式开场与嘉宾介绍】

Hi everyone. Welcome to another episode of Bits and Bips the Interview, the show where we explore crypto and macro colliding one basis point at a time. Once again, my name is Steve Ehrlich. I'm the head of research at SharpLink, and I'm also your host. And we've got a terrific show for you today.

Lots going on in the macro world. Stocks and bonds are going in entirely different directions. Crypto is getting caught in the wake. We're going to have a new Fed chair tomorrow, and much, much more.

But as always, before we begin, just a little bit of housekeeping. One: nothing that you'll hear on this show should be considered investment or financial advice. For full disclosures, please see unchainedcrypto.com/bitsandbips. And then second, let's take a brief pause so we can hear from some of the sponsors who make this show possible.

If you've been loving Bits and Bips, don't forget that the show is transitioning to its own feeds on X, YouTube, and your favorite podcast player. If you're not already subscribed to Bits and Bips on its own channels, go there now and hit that subscribe button so you can keep up with our twice-weekly live streams and Macro-Meets-Crypto breakdowns. Bits and Bips will only be on the Unchained feed for a few more weeks, so subscribe today to be ready for launch. You can get all the links at unchainedcrypto.com/bitsandbips.

All right, welcome back. So let me bring in my guest. She's someone that will be very familiar to a lot of longtime listeners: Noelle Acheson — formerly at Genesis, I believe head of research at CoinDesk, and currently the author of a very widely read and influential newsletter, Crypto Is Macro Now. So welcome, Noelle.

Noelle: Hi, Steve. So good to be here today.

Steve: How are you doing today?

Noelle: I am good. I'm recovering from being about 95 degrees Fahrenheit in Philadelphia — a little summer weather in May.

Steve: Yeah, I hear you. I gather you're going to have to get used to that.

Noelle: Yeah.

【股债背离：两个不同的鼓点】

Steve: But I think like a lot of people watching and listening, I'm also curious to try to figure out what's going on in the markets today. And that's why you're actually the perfect guest to have on. Because as I said in my opening, stocks continue to do well — I know — but there are some warning signs. I mean, Nvidia announced another very big quarter, but the response was muted. But there's a lot of fear permeating, especially through the bond market — I know that's something you've followed very closely. And then on top of all that, we got our first inflation print post the Iran war really beginning. No one quite knows exactly what's going to happen there.

And Jerome Powell is enjoying, I guess — taping this on Thursday — his last day as Fed chair, although he's going to be staying on the board to continue voting, I guess, for the foreseeable future. And again, crypto is getting caught up in all of this. Bitcoin had been up to about 82,000, 83,000 in recent weeks. ETH was, I believe, in the 2400s. That's all since retreated.

So I want to kind of get into all of it. So let's really kind of just start right now, Noelle, with my first question. How are you reading especially some of the fear that is pushing through bond markets, pushing up yields — the 10, the 30-year? To me, these would seem to be worrying signs. But for the most part, the market is kind of taking it in stride.

Noelle: You're right. They are definitely worrying signs. What's more, they're worrying signs at a global level. You look at global bond yields — they are climbing. That's global tightening. That is not good for markets.

But stock markets move to a different drummer. This isn't new. They always have moved to a different drummer. What is new is the divergence — the scale of the divergence. It's astonishing. You probably remember back in the day, everyone was excited about the 60-40 portfolio because, in theory, you'd have bonds and stocks moving in separate directions. Well, you know, we're seeing that, but not quite at this scale.

The stock market is being driven — we can talk more about this later — by intrinsic and temporary factors: excitement about AI mainly. Look at the chip sector performance. Whereas the bond market looks at the macro outlook, what's ahead. The bond market traditionally has been known as — and I'm going to use big air quotes here — the "smart money," because they do focus on nothing but macro data and narratives and trends, whereas the stock market can get swept up, as we know, in some of the hype cycles that we seem to be diving into with greater frequency.

So we've got the stock market following the hype cycle, which may have some foundations, may not — we can get into that as well. And we've got the bond market following the macro indicators, which are not great right now. So that's why: two very different drummers, not painting the same story. But they don't need to.

【通胀：缓慢移动、且早在霍尔木兹危机之前就开始抬头】

Steve: Yeah. Well, let's talk about some of those macro indicators. I mean, the inflation print is something that I know people are paying attention to. I think that the producer price index is another one that is starting to creep up. But what else are you seeing? And how are you interpreting a couple of those sort of inflationary signs — given the fact that, I don't want to use the word "transitory," but theoretically, if the strait reopens, if there is some sort of deal for Iran, energy markets are going to go back to at least immediately back to where they were before February 28th, when the attack happened. But things should calm down a little bit now.

Noelle: Things should come down, certainly in terms of the oil price, but that's not necessarily going to bring inflation down right away, for two reasons.

One: inflation moves slowly. I mean, we've seen an uptick in the core index, which is what the Fed focuses on, but it's been small — because oil prices do affect everything, but it takes time to filter through.

And we are also going to see increased volatility in the expectations. And this is interesting, especially in the American, the U.S. economy: the impact of gas prices on inflation expectations. You fill up your car at the pump, you see the tick, tick, tick of the numbers going up, and that event — that feels like it's draining out of your bank account almost. So with gas prices climbing, even though they don't impact the core inflation, investors or savers, consumers feel that inflation is going higher. That's going to influence their expectations, which influences their behavior, which influences actual inflation.

So we could have an end to the Hormuz crisis tomorrow, and one, it will take quite a while for energy prices to resettle, and two, it'll take even longer for that resettling to filter through to the inflation indices and the expectations. So in other words, this trend of inflation is not going to be over anytime soon, no matter what happens in Hormuz — because it actually also isn't new. Inflation was building well before the Hormuz crisis started.

Steve: Can you double-click on that a little bit? Because, I know — you're based in Spain, you're European, obviously I'm American. Inflation — the Fed has not — inflation had been going down, especially since like the COVID inflationary boom occurred and the Fed was raising rates to kind of ratchet it down a little bit. But it didn't get close to the 2% target, but it had been coming down. So what do you mean when you say it had been creeping up even before this happened?

Noelle: I'm going to push back on the assumption that it had been coming down. It actually hadn't. If you look at the graph since '24, it's been flat. The core CPI has been hovering between 2.6 and 3. It really hasn't been coming down.

And even just a year ago, year and a half ago, many of us were saying, "Okay, this is it. Inflation is done. The disinflation is done, and we're going to be steady for a while and then start to take up again." Why are expectations of continued uptick in inflation — which we have seen, but since even before the Hormuz strike, through the PPI, through import prices, through export prices as well — why? Because of the deglobalization trend that even predates Trump's administration. This started during the Biden administration. So that's a longer-term trend. Inflation will be heading up because of that. And Trump is just accelerating and turbocharging that.

We had the tariffs flip-flopping. We don't really know what's going on with the refunds there, but prices were going up as a result of tariffs. And then the Hormuz crisis just lit a match underneath all of that. But seriously, if you look back, if you look at the charts, you see that, no, inflation hadn't been coming down for a while.

Steve: Yeah, no, I actually — I think you're right. I mean, I remember even some discussions about whether or not the 2% number actually had to be adjusted for, like, the kind of like reset, a neutral — sort of like a neutral Fed target rate, et cetera. So I think you're —

Noelle: Three is the new two, right?

Steve: Three is the new two.

Noelle: And this is something that many have been arguing — even many Fed officials have suggested — but they can't change the expectations because of the underlying and perennial problem of credibility, of trust in the institution. A large part of what the Fed does is manage trust. And if suddenly, "Hey, we can't reach two, so we're going to change our target," well, that's going to impact the trust in the Fed's ability to reach the target that they set.

Steve: Gotcha. Okay. Thank you for that. And we're going to get a lot more into the idea of Fed and trust in probably about 10 minutes or so.

【"BLIS 交易"：永久性的看跌期权已嵌入系统】

Steve: But I do want to kind of press you a little bit on sort of the whole, like, immovable force versus unstoppable — or immovable — you know what I mean — the whole idea of stocks versus bonds. Because in your newsletter this week, you pointed out a really interesting op-ed that was written by — I think it was a former deputy managing director of the IMF, Gita Gopinath — about sort of the BLIS trade, and kind of how it's perhaps a corollary, or even like a more sustainable version of sort of the taco trade. And it sort of fits into the same family as the Fed put.

And a really interesting book that I read a couple months ago about sort of, like, the rise of carry — the idea that there's always going to be a backstop, and how that got turbocharged, especially during COVID, when, understandably, central banks all around the world went into excessive accommodation in order to support economies that were grinding to a halt.

Can you please kind of explain — like, what do you think is going to give first? And I'd love for you to just also explain sort of this BLIS trade and whether or not that's going to be a determining factor.

Noelle: Well, the BLIS trade, for those who aren't familiar with it — it stems from a really interesting opinion piece published in the Financial Times a couple of weeks ago by, as you said, Steve, a former IMF chief economist and deputy managing director, Gita Gopinath, who's now a Harvard professor. And it has to be read through the lens of someone that comes from the IMF.

But she makes the excellent point that the market assumption that there will be a put, if you will, or a safety net, is not to do with the taco trade anymore. That's part of it, for sure — and Trump certainly has given the market many things to hang the taco expectation on, many things we want him to walk back. But her point is that this goes beyond that. The taco trade is temporary. It's limited to Trump's term.

But the BLIS trade — which is the acronym for Big, Large and Lasting Stimulus or Support — is structural. Her point is that there is no administration now that will not spend to get people out of difficulties, whatever those difficulties may be — be it a market crash, be it a banking crisis, be it high energy prices. We saw this in 2020, we saw this in 2022 with the energy prices. We're seeing this again, especially here in Europe anyway, with the Hormuz crisis. Governments are not going to be voted out of office because they are not spending to save people from whatever difficulty they may be in.

And this is irrelevant — it's irrelevant which political party you're talking about. It's actually also irrelevant whether you're talking about a democracy or not, because we've seen coups across — especially across the middle of the equator economies.

Now, this is relevant for the outlook for currency debasement, obviously. Where is the funding for the stimulus going to come from? Well, it's going to come from somewhere. They're going to simply make it happen through one of the many tools in their box. Longer term, this does add moral hazard, if that's the right term. It does add froth to the speculative side of the market. It's one of the reasons, I think, why we're just seeing so much risk-on appetite in this very uncertain environment, as you pointed out earlier. But it's structural. The put is now part of the system. That, of course, will add a whole different layer of fragility.

【AI 股票：被忽视的脆弱性】

Steve: Yeah, and I'm really curious to see, like, when or if something might come to a head — because every time anyone has been trying to kind of predict the doom loop ends up missing out, because the market always recovers, often with like a K or a V or some type of letter that shows divergent returns.

And one area I'm really focused on, I'd like to ask you about before we kind of move on to the next segment, is: what do you think all this means in particular for the AI stocks? I mean, OpenAI, I think, is expected to file for a confidential IPO perhaps as soon as tomorrow. Anthropic is on the hook likely later this year. I mean, these companies need to raise hundreds of billions of dollars in funding to build out all this infrastructure, buy chips from the likes of Nvidia. Actually, I think Anthropic — it was reported they made an operating profit this quarter, which is pretty impressive. But OpenAI loses a ton of money. And they're relying on all this debt to build out for the future. But they're also the ones — like, this whole segment, this is the segment that's driving stock markets higher. How do you think about that particular segment and its broader implications for the market?

Noelle: I follow the reports that insist that the earnings multiples — the PE, forward PE — are actually very reasonable right now. And what drives me mad is that everyone's assuming that the earnings are going to be met or exceeded. And while that has historically been the case, we can't assume that will always continue. Because, again, what is the basis of these earnings expectations? In many cases, it's the company guidance. In many cases, it's just simply extrapolating demand. We're assuming a ton of demand for chips and for AI infrastructure that may or may not appear.

Again, it may. I'm not going to claim to be an AI expert here, but we've seen before, Steve, many times, that technological innovations have their hype period in which the expectations are out of proportion to the reality, and they do eventually suffer a correction. Maybe this is going to be the very first exception in history — possibly. But it's rash to bet everything you have on that. And right now, everything the market has is betting on that. And that's an underlying fragility, which I think is being very overlooked.

You mentioned earlier Nvidia's earnings were good and the stock sold off. This has actually happened every single time Nvidia reports, for the past, I think, eight quarters. And every single time everyone's like, "Okay, this is over, the whole AI play is over." But no — what it is, is a sell-the-news event. This has become fairly normal. It's related to expectations: expectations before the report, run-up after the report, "Okay, they were good, we're done, we're out," and people sell. So I wouldn't read anything into it yet.

But you're still right — eventually you think something has to tip. And I will just throw out there that historically, market tops have been tipped by very large IPOs.

Steve: Yeah, that is a good point to pay attention to. And Nvidia, as you mentioned, is a really kind of funny case, because I think I read that there had been analyst expectations, I think, for either 14 or 15 quarters in a row. But analyst expectations are, I guess, theoretically should be rooted in reality, whereas the Twitter hype machine can just speculate wildly. And those are the ones that are the momentum traders.

So it is kind of funny — I found it interesting that Jensen Huang, Nvidia CEO, and the company, they pointed out how there's so much concern about, like, a degree of incestuousness between all these different AI companies, the chip manufacturers, the consumers, et cetera, that — I think they tried to point out that, like, half of their clients are not, like, the big hyperscalers, to maybe assuage concerns about too much concentration in a few big companies. But it is really interesting to see how that's going to play out.

Noelle: The concentration of clients in these companies — and these clients are no doubt also themselves dealing with higher input costs and increasing cost of debt. Are we certain the health of these clients is going to continue to be strong enough to maintain the earnings forecast that these hyperscalers and the chip manufacturers are selling to?

Steve: Okay, great.

Noelle: But I've been wrong. I've been wrong before. I've been expecting a market correction for a while now, and I've been wrong on the timing. So, to be taken with a big grain of salt.

Steve: Yeah. Well, I've never been wrong ever in anything I've written. So I don't know — if that feels like — just kidding.

Okay. With that, let's take a very quick break so we can hear from some sponsors who make the show possible.

【告别鲍威尔：感情归感情，但不能放他过关】

Steve: All right, welcome back. So again, I said Noelle is the perfect guest to have on this week. And a big reason why is because tomorrow we're going to have a new Fed chair.

So, Noelle, I want to give you the chance to either eulogize or sort of provide a bit of a valedictory speech to Jerome Powell — or maybe a little bit of both. He's been Fed chair for pretty much most of crypto's era of maturation, for lack of a better term. And given how Bitcoin and crypto were really designed to sort of be a counterweight to everything that happens at the Fed, would really love to hear your thoughts on kind of what his tenure meant for this industry.

Noelle: It's easy to get caught up in the personality cult around Jerome Powell. He certainly seems like the kind of grandfather everyone wished they had — the kind of guy you happily go out for a marshmallow latte with. But we really can't forget that he was also behind the debanking of many crypto firms. He was behind the closure of Silvergate and what happened in March 2023. He was behind a lot of really bad things that happened to the reputation of the United States banking regulators over the years. And he also really got the inflation story wrong.

So while I do confess to feeling a certain fondness for the man — because I have watched the FOMC press briefings, and I think he's done very well in the communication of the Fed's objectives and its inner workings — we have to remember that there were many things he either knew about and supported that ended up hurting not just crypto, but also the reputation of banking in the United States generally; or he was unaware — and that's also something to hold against him.

And this is even before we give the credibility of the case against him by the Department of Justice a second thought. And the main objection was: he's just not responsive, not even answering any of the subpoenas, which does also hint at a certain arrogance and an unwillingness to cooperate. Even if you disagree with the premise behind these White House moves, you sort of have to pretend to go along with them for the sake of precedent and protocol.

So, again — mixed. He had a lot to deal with. I mean, there's also the repo crisis. There was the pandemic. There was the inflation thing.

Steve: I forgot he was there during the 2019 repo crisis.

Noelle: Exactly. So he certainly had a lot to deal with. But in my book — again, affection, but does not get a free pass.

Steve: Yeah, I think that's probably how I would put it too. I'm an institutionalist. I mean, people who have listened to me on the show before know that I used to work for the U.S. government, the U.S. military — so I have a strong belief in sort of the objectivity, the non-partisanship of key institutions in the government. And his insistence on maintaining Fed independence, to me, is admirable, because he's clearly facing a lot of pressure.

And it's not even just, in my opinion, directly coming from the White House. But I think it also comes from the fact that a lot of Congress, especially in recent years, has sort of abdicated their responsibility to enact monetary — oh, I'm sorry, not monetary — to enact fiscal policy. And that's led to the Fed having to sort of pick up the baton there. And I know that's something that Kevin Warsh is actually going to try and push back on, because he doesn't think — he wants to reduce the Fed balance sheet and he wants it to kind of refocus strictly on monetary policy.

So I understand that. But again, to me, like — if the first line in his obituary as Fed chair, I guess, is that he sort of stood for independence, his second has to be: he missed inflation, as you mentioned. I mean, we all got sick of hearing the word "transitory" in 2021, 2022. And I have to admit, at that time, there was a certain logic to it. I mean, COVID seemed to be a one-off event, and it was definitely a long-tail event that people hadn't expected. And if markets reopened, theoretically it should come back down. But that's clearly not what happened. There were the deglobalization factors you mentioned, and just ways that supply chains had changed after that. And he missed it. And that led to record inflation highs in decades. It had to come back down. And that led to the banking crisis that you mentioned, because a few of those banks got caught on the wrong end of it with their treasuries. And that led to another unprecedented bailout. So it's hard to reconcile those two things. But — go ahead.

Noelle: And it wasn't even necessarily — I mean, he has a reputation for having defended independence, but perhaps that's worth picking at a bit. He certainly came out swinging after the subpoena was served, and that was startling, and good for him, and everything necessary, in my opinion — especially when his job, as he himself has said, often is messaging and cultivating trust in the institution. I think he did a good job there.

But we do have to remember that there wasn't a lot of independent thinking when it came to shutting down crypto-related banking. That was influenced by politics. That was not showing much independence there. And we also have to ask ourselves: to what extent does independence mean you are not accountable for any decisions? To what extent does independence mean you can simply ignore subpoenas? And so, again, there's some pushback to be had on what independence even means, and whether this Fed actually did exhibit that level of independence. And then that opens up the door to a fascinating conversation of: what do we mean by bank independence? When is it a disadvantage rather than an advantage?

Steve: Yeah, I guess for me, it means he's much more like Paul Volcker and a lot less like Arthur Burns. But as you mentioned, there's different definitions of independence. So that makes a lot of sense.

【沃什接任：他想要的，市场未必给得了】

Steve: Let's turn to his successor. I mean, Kevin Warsh. As I mentioned, he's gone through a bit of an evolution in his own thought on whether or not to be dovish or hawkish. One thing he made clear during his confirmation hearings is that he wants the Fed to move away from fiscal policy — which I believe he sort of characterized as being more of picking winners and losers — whereas monetary policy is a bit more democratic in terms of affecting the whole economy. And that's where he thinks the Fed should sit.

He also has plans to try to create new ways of measuring inflation, so the Fed can, I think, be a bit more accurate and more predictive in how it exercises monetary policy. And as you said, Noelle, Jerome Powell was front and center and gave a lot of forward guidance. Kevin Warsh doesn't want to do that. What are your expectations?

Noelle: I think that he can say he wants a lower balance sheet. The markets aren't going to let him have one. It's as simple as that. They're not going to let him have one.

And independence — sure. But the bond market is the boss here. And it's relevant for price stability. The Federal Reserve cannot let a disorder take hold in the Treasury market. It just can't do that, because that would affect the dollar, which would affect price stability, et cetera, et cetera, et cetera. So that is something that we can all wish for. I wish I were a professional piano player. Not going to happen.

In terms of forward guidance, I would not be at all surprised to see fewer FOMC press conferences, fewer dot plots. And there's a big debate to be had about whether this is going to be good or bad. It sort of ties in with what the SEC is doing — talking about also reducing the number of reporting periods of the year. Will the market be okay with less information, or will this add volatility? Does that mean analysts actually have to start doing their job, rather than being fed the information quite so frequently? I don't know.

This is a very, very big change. We get so used to a certain cadence, so used to being spoon-fed certain pieces of data, that when that's taken away, is that going to disrupt markets enough that he's going to have to put them back on the table? Or will it be a refreshing change, reduce costs, as well as reintroduce some original thinking that will, in the end, be healthy? I wouldn't be surprised to see him try that. Whether the markets will let him succeed or not, I don't know.

So I think that's pretty much all he's going to be able to do. He's certainly not going to be able to change the inflation measure, because it's not really up to him. He can influence what people might look at, but we're going to make our own decisions over what we think is important. And he's certainly not going to be able to lower rates.

Steve: Yeah. And speaking on that last part — being able to lower rates — I'm sure you saw yesterday, I guess it was yesterday, minutes were released, I believe, from the Fed's April meeting last month. One thing that, I guess, was revealed was that there was a lot more acceptance of hawkish views than what came out in the final vote, which was really just to kind of hold, hold rate steady. And that speaks directly to what he's going to be walking into, which is a higher inflationary environment, but a president that wants lower rates, believes that productivity coming from AI might put a bit of a lid on inflation. And then, to add to all this, Kevin Warsh is going to have to prove his, quote-unquote, "independence" from the president. So what are your expectations for the next couple of Fed meetings?

Noelle: I think the number one thing to look at here is what Trump says. Right now, he has publicly said that Kevin Warsh is going to do what he's going to do — he has full faith in him, good man, et cetera, et cetera — which, again, is quite astonishing given the environment that, you've said, Kevin Warsh is walking into.

We know Kevin Warsh isn't going to be able to lower rates — one, because he's just one vote, and pretty much nobody else is going to be voting with him on this. And two, Trump can't bash the person he chose, because so soon after choosing him. So there's a bit of a respite there.

I think one thing the market is getting wrong is expectations of a rate hike. I've been saying "no cuts" for a long time now, and it's reassuring to see that consensus take hold. Now there's a consensus for a hike this year. I think that's going too far in the other direction. He's not going to be able to lower rates, but nobody's going to — have the courage, or not enough are going to have the courage, to vote for a rate hike when there is still so much uncertainty. We don't know if this is transitory or not. We don't know what the other inflationary factors at work are going to do.

So there's going to be no hikes — so that'll keep Trump happy. There aren't going to be any cuts either — that may not keep Trump happy, but I think he'll be quiet, and that'll just simply give the Fed some breathing room. It'll give Warsh some breathing room to start to work on his relationships. Because in the end, it does come down to how much the FOMC committee trusts the chair, whether they'll go along with what he suggests or not. That'll influence monetary policy going forward.

Steve: Yeah, I'm actually a little reassured in a way. I know — until dissents started happening last year, it had been, what, decades before we had dissents? I'm actually more reassured that there are dissents at this point, because — you have so many different people in that room with different vantage points, and they monitor different parts of the country economically. There should be differences of opinion.

Noelle: Yeah.

Steve: I'm actually — yeah, I mean, move away from their groupthink. I'm actually looking forward — perhaps season 52 of SNL will have an FOMC meeting. There's enough characters there that people recognize. I think there really could be one.

Noelle: Yeah, that's actually brilliant. And here's another twist: what if the next dissent comes from the chair himself?

Steve: Yeah, that would certainly be something. I don't think that's ever happened before — I'm not sure, my history is weak — but that would be, again, kind of mind-blowing.

Noelle: I doubt — again, I doubt he'd do that, because his job, his number one job now, is to get the people to trust — get the FOMC members to trust him.

Steve: Yeah, I would say at least for his very first meeting, it would be very unlikely. But who knows after that.

【加密市场：比特币卡在区间里】

Steve: All right. So we talked a lot about macro. Let's talk about crypto. I mean, Bitcoin and Ether in the market — they kind of got caught up, especially over last weekend, when news came out on Sunday about potentially renewed strikes in Iran. It was put on hold, I guess, on Tuesday when President Trump mentioned that a few Gulf countries asked for a little more time to negotiate. But crypto hasn't really recovered yet. I know there was, I guess, some encouraging news that a few Chinese tankers made it out of the strait. Reporting seems to suggest that they paid some sort of fee or toll to Iran to do that. I don't know if that's going to be a model moving forward, but crypto seems stuck once again.

And it seems to be caught between: is it going to again be like a high-beta risk asset, or — in a world of higher inflation, et cetera — is the debasement trade going to come around, and will Bitcoin and other crypto, will it be able to take advantage? Or is it going to lose to gold again? How do you see especially the blue-chip crypto assets behaving right now?

Noelle: Possibly all of the above, to be honest. I mean, right now, I don't see any catalyst to push it out of its current range — at least not any positive catalyst.

There's always the danger of a stock market rout, which will take the major crypto assets down with it, even though correlations have become somewhat unrelated right now — uncorrelated. Law of nature: gravity will do its thing. However, will there be a stock market rout? Again, this is up in the air. We just don't know.

And the debasement trade continues. I mean, Bitcoin is a debasement hedge. And when people are worried about currency debasement, that tends to be often when Bitcoin does better. Back in the banking crisis in '23, Bitcoin jumped, and everyone was like, "Oh, it's because people realize the banking system is corrupt and fragile." And I was like, "No, actually, this is because people expect the central bank to step in with stimulus." And that's what Bitcoin tends to react to. If things go really, really badly in markets, and there are signs of the stimulus — the BLIS trade that we were talking about before — then yes, that could well boost crypto out of its slumber.

But when it comes to just risk-on appetite, which we are swimming in at the moment — Bitcoin's not doing very much in that respect, and it's because there's so much else at the moment for the risk-forward investors. You've got an endless number of AI-related plays. You've got prediction markets if you really want some fun stuff. There's just so much else to choose from.

And this is one of the disadvantages of Bitcoin becoming a macro asset — and this is something I've been focusing on for quite a while. It's a good thing, because Bitcoin does have increasingly a position, a role, a place in macro portfolios. But the downside is, it's now one among many assets. And other investors are going to choose the higher-volatility ones, which right now is not Bitcoin.

So, in sum: no catalyst to push it out of its range at the moment — until it does emerge from its range, and the momentum will kick in.

【Clarity Act：比特币不缺监管清晰度，缺的是 ETH】

Steve: Yeah. I mean, I guess the one catalyst that could be somewhere in the future is the Clarity Act. We haven't had a chance to talk about it, but I'm curious if — it's been discussed at nauseam everywhere, but I'm curious if you have any just quick thoughts on it, either the nature of the bill itself or the likelihood of anything being signed into law.

Noelle: I think we will get the Clarity Act this year — she says, hopefully. I mean, I wouldn't put it at a high level of confidence, but I just really think we will. That might be wishful thinking.

I don't think it's going to affect Bitcoin that much. I mean, let's face it, Bitcoin does not lack regulatory clarity. What does is ETH. We could see ETH benefit. And if ETH goes up, Bitcoin will go up also, because they often tend to trade in tandem. But overall — investors perhaps be more comfortable with Bitcoin because of Clarity? Maybe. But Bitcoin doesn't lack regulatory clarity.

Steve: Yeah. I mean, one thing that we're paying attention to — I put out a couple of threads on this — is, I mean, you're right: Bitcoin already has the clarity. ETH, for all intents and purposes, also has that clarity. There's even been guidance from the SEC pointing out how a lot of staking is not a security — our staking activities is not sort of the offering of securities — which is at odds with what Gary Gensler's SEC had thought.

It could be an unlock for DeFi, just because it sort of gives a little more certainty, especially for some of the TradFi firms to participate in. Whereas, like, DAOs aren't treated as a general partnership, which could be a big liability in certain cases. And hopefully there's going to be a better test to sort of determine when someone like kind of falls under FinCEN versus not, and has to comply with AML regulations and things like that. Like, that could be an unlock from Clarity.

But you're right. I mean, Bitcoin, ETH, even like XRP and Solana, et cetera — I mean, even if there hasn't been formal guidance speaking to those being commodities, there's enough judicial rulings. And if nothing else, there's the SEC blessing — what is it — '33 Act ETFs, which are by the very definition commodities, being like wrapping commodities into an ETF wrapper. I think that gives it that clarity. I hope I got that right. Sometimes I mix up the '33-'40 Act funds. Who can blame me on that one? It's seriously very complex.

Noelle: I have a question for you, actually, if I may. Do you think that Clarity passage is priced in at all? Another way to ask this is: do you think that if it falls through, there would be a sharp drop in crypto prices? Or do we just not care anymore?

Steve: It's hard to tell. And maybe it depends on the asset itself. I tend to think that it's not, just because crypto has been in such a big malaise for the last several months. I mean, it's really been coming down ever since President Trump was inaugurated again in January of 2021 [sic].

If there is some real momentum suggesting that we're going to see passage in the next few weeks — it really has to happen by early summer, or I don't think it's going to happen at all. We could see another kickstart, but I don't think that necessarily means Bitcoin is going to go to 140,000, ETH is going to go automatically to 5,000, and so on and so forth. There's going to be a longer tail. But I don't think it's completely priced in — and probably just because people still understand how many hurdles still have to be overcome in a short period of time for it to happen.

I mean, the Senate bills have to be reconciled, and it has to be reconciled with the House bill. At some point, there's going to need to be ethics language that is palatable to the White House. And all that has to get done and signed into law. I think they're targeting July 4th, which is really six weeks away, maybe. So I guess — I was thinking a little bit real-time as you asked me that question, but I think that's kind of where my head's at right now.

Noelle: Yeah, and the devil's in the details. I mean, one thing is the law; the next is the rulemaking that then gets layered on top of that. But one thing I've been wondering about is: if it doesn't — if it falls through — it's not the end of the world for crypto, because the SEC is on board, most of the financial regulators are on board, and they can just rulemake away until the end of President Trump's term. And if indeed a party opposed to crypto gets into the White House in '28 — well, perhaps by then crypto is just too big to unwind.

Steve: Yeah, you would hope. Or at least — I mean, crypto has proven itself to be such a powerful lobby, a powerful interest group, that I would imagine that a Democrat — yeah, like you said — even if it is a Democrat, or anyone, would not be as antagonistic as perhaps Gary Gensler's SEC was, just because there really was not much benefit that came from taking that particular posture.

【代币化与"创新豁免"：魔鬼藏在细节里】

Steve: Yeah. I wanted to ask you about one more quick thing, and then we have to start to wrap up here. But I wanted to get your sense of sort of this innovation exemption when it comes to tokenization, and kind of how that's going to interplay between these two markets.

Noelle: The innovation exemption — can [be a] devil in the details. One thing that I'm concerned about is the rumor that it's going to allow third-party issuance. In other words, anyone who wants to can issue a token wrapped around an equity that they actually have nothing to do with. Even possibly — I've heard some rumors that this would be without the company behind the stock's permission, which I think is absolutely nuts.

I mean, markets, for me, should be about capital formation. Derivatives enable capital formation by creating more liquid markets and giving some assurance to investors putting their money into certain stocks. But when you're creating a market that's just for derivative speculation — tokenized stocks, for instance — then that sort of subverts the ideals of markets, the inherent ideals of markets. And it's not necessarily favorable to crypto, given the reputation that crypto, to be fair, has of just pure speculation. "The only thing it's good for is speculation" — we've heard that many, many times. It's obviously not what you and I think, but we've heard it many, many times.

So that was a concern. However — devil in the details — it may, hopefully, not happen, in which case the tokenization innovation exemption is great news. Great news in that it encourages some experimentation. It's got to be careful — they're not going to be crazy, they're not going to let anyone come in, they're not going to let unlimited amounts be spent — but to encourage both entrepreneurs and market participants and innovators to start experimenting with this new type of marketplace that we know is going to be a big feature of markets five, ten years from now — I think that's a big step forward: the confidence that they're not going to get in trouble for trying new asset formats.

Steve: Yeah, absolutely.

【收尾：一个关注的指标，一个逆向观点】

Steve: Just last question. I'd love to have you answer one of two things. One: what's one chart, one indicator, that you're really paying attention to over the next few weeks or months? Or: what is one contrarian opinion that you'd like to share?

Noelle: Hmm. What I love about the questions you ask is that you don't hold punches. You do go for the overlooked stories. So congratulations on that.

In the indicator — I'm going to go with inflation. Inflation kills societies. And if we don't keep an eye on it, then there could be some very bad scenarios going ahead. Inflation will move the bond market. Inflation will dictate monetary policy. Inflation could also have a very big impact on fiscal policy at a global level. So, inflation. And inflation is something you can't hide from. You can massage the indices, as the incoming chair has said he wants to do — it's not going to help. You can't hide inflation. So, that.

And the contrarian indicator — I'm going to go with something that I think people are ignoring, and that is: we're so excited about the S&P 500 reaching all-time highs, we're overlooking the widening gap between the S&P 500 and the equal-weight index. There's the market-cap-weight index that we all follow — that's reaching all-time highs after all-time highs. And there's the equal-weight, which isn't. And the gap is widening. The last time the gap was widening at this rate was in 1999.

Steve: Hmm. For the dot-com bust, I guess.

Noelle: Anything that gets top-heavy — we know there's a law of physics — anything that gets top-heavy ends up tipping over.

Steve: Yeah, absolutely.

【节目结束】

Steve: All right. Well, Noelle, we'll have to have you back on again. Thank you so much for joining us. Thank you, everybody, for watching and listening.

That concludes this week's episode of Bits and Bips the Interview — but don't go anywhere. Laura is going to be right back on Unchained. She's going to speak with Dio Casares on how pre-IPO pricing is moving on-chain. SpaceX perps just launched on Hyperliquid, Anthropic and Open — I'm sorry — and then Anthropic and OpenAI shares are there too. And Nasdaq Private Market is teaming up with Polymarket. So stick around.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一、比特币最大的悖论：它是做空美联储的资产，却靠美联储的"恐慌反射"而活
整集节目里最被低估的一句话，是 Noelle 对 2023 年银行业危机的复盘。当时所有人欢呼"比特币上涨是因为人们终于看清了银行系统的腐败与脆弱"，而她冷冷地纠正：**不，比特币上涨是因为人们预期央行会出手放水。**
请把这句话和她后面讲的 BLIS 交易（Big, Large and Lasting Stimulus）并排放在一起看，你会得到一个令人不安的结论：比特币诞生的叙事是"对抗美联储的法币滥发"，但在真实的资金流里，比特币其实是**做多美联储那只随时会按下去的恐慌按钮**。它不是法币的反义词，它是法币救助机制的杠杆化衍生品。换句话说——这是本期最反直觉的观察——加密世界一边在意识形态上诅咒央行,一边在仓位上押注央行永远不会让系统真正崩溃。一个真正"去中心化、抗审查、对抗印钞"的资产，它的最佳买点恰恰是"印钞机即将启动"的时刻。比特币的信徒和美联储的救市派，本质上是同一笔交易的两端。
## 二、"通行费"是贯穿整集的隐藏母题——从霍尔木兹海峡到 Hyperliquid 的 90% 分成
注

item.title: [Business of Sport] Should Southampton have been Kicked Out of the Playoffs?
item.dc:creator: Business of Sport
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、这不是史上最大罚单——这是一份被没收的"期权"

南安普顿 CEO Phil Parsons 说这是"英格兰俱乐部史上最大处罚"，主持人轻轻一句话就把它拆穿了：如果南安普顿本就会输掉决赛，那么这个"史上最大处罚"实际成本为零。这里藏着一个出人意料的真相——这个处罚的大小是不确定的，它取决于一场永远不会发生的比赛的结果。

用金融的语言说：南安普顿失去的从来不是 2 亿英镑奖金，而是一份看涨期权——一张"约 50% 概率 × 2 亿英镑"的入场券。媒体说"踢出最富有的比赛"，仿佛奖金会原封不动躺进银行账户，但主持人点破：这笔钱会立刻"从另一头流出去"，变成工资和转会费。所以这是一份"薛定谔的罚单"：在判决落下的那一刻，它同时是史上最大、也可能是史上最小，只有那场不存在的决赛才能让它坍缩成确定值。Parsons 要求 EFL 在量刑前先做财务影响评估——主持人反驳得很准：那等于"对英冠球队从轻、对英乙球队从重"，因为损失的绝对值不同。这恰恰说明，惩罚一旦与"赌注大小"挂钩，规则就不再是规则，而变成了一张可以讨价还价的价目表。

二、最反常的细节：间谍战根本没用，他却坚持不懈

这是整段对话里最容易被忽略、却最值得玩味的事实：南安普顿赛季中早就派过"间谍"——去牛津，结果 2-1 输了；去伊普斯维奇，2-2。这套谍报战从未带来过好结果，主教练却一次次坚持。

如果间谍战是一个理性的"获取战术优势"行为，那么连续失败之后理应被放弃。它没有被放弃——这说明它的真实功能不是战术性的，而是心理性的。请注意一个跨领域的连接点：对话里反复强调，对手主教练的周四训练课是"魔法发生的地方"，是他"整周做的最重要的一件事"、是他缓解赛前焦虑、获得掌控感的仪式。南安普顿主教练想偷走的，与其说是对方的战术细节，不如说是对方的"确定感"。这让间谍行为更接近赌徒的迷信仪式、运动员赛前固定的穿袜顺序——一种在不确定性面前管理焦虑的强迫性动作。它之所以"前所未有地处心积虑"（换上 Eastleigh 球衣伪装、研究共用训练场地），恰恰因为它不是冷静的成本收益计算，而是一种强迫性的安全感采购。真正高明的间谍会在失败后收手；一个停不下来的人，暴露的是恐惧，不是算计。

三、为什么"最具体的那条规则"是承重墙——以及"中立专家"自己也是当事人

主持人坚持"规则手册神圣不可侵犯"，并做了一个关键区分：EFL 不是监管者，而是赛事组织者。这是一个被低估的洞见。监管者追求"正义与比例原则"，而赛事组织者追求的是机制设计中的激励相容——惩罚的任务不是"配得上罪行"，而是让违规变成一个严格劣势策略。这正是核威慑、博弈论里"可信威胁"的逻辑：罚款 30 万、扣 2 分，对 2 亿英镑的诱惑而言不构成威慑，反而是一张"值得一试"的报价单。

而这里有一个递归式的"啊哈时刻"：第 127 条是 EFL 所有规则里最具体、最不可能被误解的一条（72 小时内不得观察对手训练）。主持人的逻辑是——如果连这条最清晰的规则被打破都只换来"手腕上轻轻一拍"，那么书里所有更模糊的条款都自动贬值了。换句话说，第 127 条是整本规则手册的承重墙：抽掉标记最清楚的那块砖，整栋楼才会真正动摇。

最后一个让人意外的角度——请留意这位"中立专家"自己的身份。他在节目中坦白，自己正作为 Walton & Hersham 的董事，对前任教练（现在约维尔）"挖角"自家球员提起申诉。也就是说，这位反复强调"规则神圣、必须严惩、必须杀鸡儆猴"的评论者，本人此刻正是一桩同类案件中的原告。这并不削弱他的论证，但它揭示了一个系统性真相：在足球这个封闭生态里，几乎没有真正的"局外人"，每个发声者都同时是潜在的当事人——他的"规则原教旨主义"既是专业判断，也是利益结构的自然产物。而这也呼应了对话里那个最锋利的观察：为决策买单的，永远是离决策最远的人——不是董事会（"董事会绝无可能知情"），而是球员（薪资随升降级剧烈浮动）和球迷（不可退的酒店、孩子第一次去温布利的梦想）。这是企业丑闻里反复上演的委托-代理悲剧：责任在理论上集中于顶端，冲击波却总是打在边缘。

📝 转录文本

Should Southampton Have Been Kicked Out of the Playoffs?

Business of Sport — Bonus Episode

开场：一期临时加更

Bonus episode alert, Charlie. Ding, ding, ding.

And a first. A first for us. I'm not — who is it a bonus for? Is it a bonus for listeners, or is a bonus for me to see my fellow Charlie and his burgeoning Norwegian ski jumper ginger beard, which is, um, emerging at the moment?

Yeah. Well, it's a bonus for all of us, isn't it? Um, I know I said a little bit — more pronounced on a video, so I may need to get that sorted out. But, uh, it's quite nice doing a show that isn't under bright lights, so that I'm not looking the most pale that I could look, uh, against someone of your nice tan complexion.

Anyway, why are we doing this? Well, we're doing this obviously because of Spygate and the, um, recent developments in the case which, Charlie, when we recorded our show this week on Tuesday, was very much underdeveloped from the information that we have now. And we didn't really, uh, feel capable of talking about this at length without that information, knowing that it was going to release on Friday. So this provides the perfect opportunity to take a look at what's been happening over the last few days, 'cause I don't know about you, but my phone has been going off, and the amount of people going "I can't wait to hear your thoughts on Spygate" has made me think we have to do this.

Yeah, absolutely. It's been a seriously hot topic of conversation amongst — like, a lot of my friends are, like you, really Premier League fans, um, who only really pay attention to what's happening in the Premier League. And I guess probably where the crossover here is, this relates to the playoff final, which determines who goes into the Premier League. But nonetheless, it is still fundamentally an EFL matter. And what's been interesting to me is I've had loads of friends of mine who are EPL club fans getting in touch, um, with their views on this particular thing. I have to say, in most cases I don't really, um, agree with them, but, um, there we are.

Look, and I want to get into that. I want to get into why you don't agree with them.

案件回顾：Spygate 到底发生了什么

But first, there were a few things that were confusing me. And it's worth, just for a minute or so probably, explaining to anyone that hasn't been fully aware what's been happening, because this is probably one of the biggest stories in the EFL's history.

The two teams in one of the Championship playoff finals — Southampton and Middlesbrough — were caught up in this when Southampton sent a data analyst intern to go and record a training session, or multiple training sessions if you believe some of the WhatsApp messages, of Middlesbrough before — I think it was the first leg, wasn't it? It was the first leg of the playoff semi-final.

Anyway, this guy was caught on camera filming, and, uh, if you want to go and see quite an entertaining cat-and-mouse chase as he made his way through the golf club, putting on a different disguise in the changing room and shooting out the back to the local town — Sky Sports have done quite a good, uh, reenactment of this.

Either way, this is an illegal act in the articles of the EFL. It has been — I think it's regulation 127 — which was put in after Leeds United were caught doing a similar thing in 2019, uh, where they sent someone to go and have a look at a Derby County training session in a January of a standard, uh, season, and they were fined £200,000 for that at the time, and then this regulation was put in place. So since then, this has now become a specific offense against the rules of the league.

Anyway, the Championship playoff semi-finals went on. Southampton, unfortunately for the EFL, beat Middlesbrough in those, and were due to play the playoff final on Saturday. And, in quite an incredible turn of events, at the independent hearing on Tuesday — which was taking a look at the case and deciding how to punish Southampton, who have not denied the allegations — um, the commission kicked Southampton out of the playoff final and reinstated Middlesbrough.

And even better, Charlie — I think the other reason we wanted to do this today in particular — last night we got the confirmation that the appeal Southampton had lodged against that decision has been rejected by the EFL, meaning Middlesbrough will play Hull City on Saturday in the playoff final.

听证会的当事方：Middlesbrough 该被纳入吗？

Couple of things I was confused about. I'd love your thoughts on, uh, when it came down to that hearing. The EFL and Southampton were the two parties involved in it — so Southampton were the defendant and the EFL were the prosecutor — but Middlesbrough were very upset that they weren't involved in it. They thought that they had a right, based on the infringements taking place against them. That confused me, firstly just because I don't understand why the team that have this act against them can't then be a part of that, and most importantly couldn't have appealed had the decision gone against them — which I think is key, because Southampton could have appealed. So that's a first point. I was just interested on your thoughts on that.

Well, that's totally basic — just following of the normal justice procedure. So, for instance, you know, if you just take it out of football, go into normal life: um, I'm walking down the street, some guy comes along, he nicks my phone. Um, I report it to the police, the police interview me, the police take all the evidence that I've got to give, and then they put it to the Crown Prosecution Service, and the Crown Prosecution Service decides whether the prosecution — then conducts the prosecution. I'm not then invited back in to give a further opinion on the matter. I mean, I might be interviewed by a barrister as a witness — maybe, could be — but yeah, I don't get the chance to sort of say "Oh, I don't think you did a very good job there, I think you need to go back and ask the same questions again," or whatever. It's not the way it works.

So it's absolutely clear. Um, as somebody who's been multiple times an EFL club director: if you have a complaint against another club, you file that complaint, you give your evidence to support that complaint, and the EFL in this case then takes that on.

And I can tell you, I'm involved in one of these right at the moment, with a non-league club that I'm a director of, Walton & Hersham. We are, um, complaining about our former manager, who's now at Yeovil Town, um, tapping up our players while they were still under contract at Walton & Hersham. Um, and these things are often quite hard to prove. In this case, it's actually a photograph of him coming to our end-of-season celebration party uninvited and then speaking to the players in question. But, you know, however open-and-shut we might feel the case is, the bottom line is we have to present the evidence that we have to the Football Association in this case. And the Football Association then has to decide, first of all, whether to charge Yeovil Town, and then how to do so and go about it. And that's just the way it goes.

This is totally and utterly normal. This is how it should be. So first of all, you know, I'm sure that Middlesbrough aren't complaining today, right? But in as much as they ever had a leg to stand on, it was a wooden leg — that they didn't have a leg to stand on. No, they should not have been involved beyond the initial complaint.

处罚本身：到底有多重？

Should we evaluate what the outcome of the actual decision is, because it is absolutely massive? So, the punishment laid out to Southampton — of being kicked out of the playoff final — means that they'll miss out on the chance to get to the Premier League, which, as we know, is, for three years really, over £200 million worth of TV money. They've also been given a four-point deduction next season. So, a significant punishment. What do you think about the punishment that's been laid out?

So, um, I'm hearing words in the media from people who should know better, like "unprecedented." Well — I, a youngster like you might not know the history, um, but those of us who've been around a lot longer certainly do. This is not the first time the EFL has kicked a club out of the playoff final. In fact, the last time they did it, they did it after a club had won the playoff final.

Right. So I'm going to come on to Southampton's words on this, uh, in a moment, but Southampton basically are being denied the opportunity — 50/50 opportunity — to access the Premier League.

When Swindon Town were found guilty of financial infringements in 1990, they had already won the playoff final against Sunderland and they were effectively already promoted, and then they were not just stripped of that, they were relegated to the division below that. Now, as it happens, on appeal, on an independent appeal, they got reinstated back up into, um, what's now the Championship. It wasn't called that at the time — it was division two, because we were still pre-Premier League, um, days. But it's worth bearing in mind that their chairman got sent to prison. Right, their chairman got sent to prison. Lombardi was up in court. You know, the whole thing was absolutely mega.

So when people talk about this being unprecedented — it's not. There was a bigger case that happened 30 years ago, which was more dramatic than this, and it all adds up to the same thing.

规则手册的神圣性：EFL 是组织者，不是监管者

Which is that when you enter a competition, you do so based on the rule book. And once you sign a document saying "I accept the rule book, I have entered this competition and I accept the rule book" — once you've accepted the rule book, that's it. From that moment on, if you break the rule book, then effectively you delegate the judgment as to what your punishment might be to the competition organizers.

People often speak of the EFL as being a regulator. It's not. It's a competition organizer. That's what it is. And to make that competition work, all the participants in the competition have to accept that that rule book is absolutely sacrosanct.

Now, I've heard from a few friends who aren't professionally involved in the industry — and, you know, even people who are sort of quite hot football fans: "Yeah, but is this really such a big offense?" Absolutely, yes, it is a massive offense.

为什么是大罪：第 127 条与 72 小时规则

Which is why the EFL changed their rule book after the Leeds United affair, 'cause they found out, to their horror, that barring a rather broad commitment to act — this is EFL regulation 3.4, which requires clubs to act towards each other in good faith, which is a rather sort of vague catch-all thing — that's what they did Leeds United under. But that's not specific enough to really land on a club with full force. And that's why they created EFL regulation 127, specifically, 'cause they found out and were then advised by their legal advisers: "Look, your rule book currently isn't specific enough. If you want to stop that behavior, you have to specifically state that that behavior is not on."

So let's just read out what that rule says. EFL regulation 127 prohibits any club from observing or attempting to observe another club's training session within 72 hours of a scheduled match between those two clubs. It could not be clearer. It could not be clearer.

Now, the reason for this 72-hour rule is because of the rhythm and, uh, sort of the way in which a club's training schedule will work. So typically, if you have a match on Saturday or Sunday, then match day minus two — or sometimes match day minus three, hence the 72 hours, etc. — is what the coach will call his tactical training session. So that is the training session when the entire intended starting eleven will line up, and either those first-team squad members who are not playing, or the club's under-21 team, will then be put into the formation that that manager expects the opposition to use, and they will then walk through the whole tactical plan for that particular match.

Somebody said to me this week, "Yeah, but surely Saints know what sort of system Boro play." Yeah, but that's just a generalization — the system. It's the tweaks. It's the little moments and nuances within the system, where they've spotted something about how, in this case, how Southampton play. And the Middlesbrough manager saying, "Right, within my formation, I now want you to position yourself five yards further right, 'cause I want to block off that inverted run that comes off that wing." "Hang on a second — I've spotted that when one of their center backs steps out, quite often the other one doesn't step out with him. That offers a chance for a run in behind. So let's rehearse that." That's what happens in this training session, and that is what was the intent in recording that training session: to try and find out the special little plans that Boro were working on to try and help them against Southampton.

So Southampton were deliberately cheating, and there's no other way around it.

蓄意性：周四训练课才是"魔法发生的地方"

You've used the word "deliberate," right? And that, to me, is where everyone who comes at it going "Is it that bad an offense?" just falls down. This was so premeditated. It was so planned, and strategically planned to the extent where the day they decided to do it was specifically the day you've told me that sometimes even directors weren't allowed to come and view training sessions. People directly working for the clubs weren't allowed to be there.

Absolutely. 100%, Charlie. I would be told — and this is not by one manager, this is by pretty much every single manager that I've ever worked with — in various of our commercial agreements there'll be, you know, "Well, in return for your sponsorship, you can come and watch six training sessions a year, with certain guests," and all this type of stuff. And I'd always speak to the football department, and the football department tell me, "That's absolutely fine, Charlie, but not on Thursdays." Right? Never on Thursdays. That training session is sacrosanct. Even people inside the club are not allowed to come and watch that particular training session. That is where the magic happens. That's where the manager makes his biggest single difference.

If you were to say to a manager, "Of all the things that you do in the entire week, what is the single most important thing you do?" — he will tell you the Thursday training session. Almost without fail. That's where the real work is done.

Sunderland 的故事：规则就是规则，即便是无心之失

So, deliberate in nature. And, you know, as you've told me before — I know you nearly fell foul of it at Sunderland — people get kicked out, teams get kicked out of competitions, for relatively, should we say, innocent mistakes, where they field an ineligible player in a cup competition and he plays for the final five minutes of a game, and then they're kicked out.

This happens every year, Charlie. So it's a fun story, because it ended up being quite dramatic in a way. So, this is going back a few years. I was at Sunderland, um, and we were playing in the EFL Trophy. And it's probably fair to say that, in the group stages of the EFL Trophy, it's not the most important match that you're going to play in any season. It's quite often a match where various young players are given opportunities — maybe some players who aren't currently getting first-team action, or whatever it might be. And that might include some players who've been brought to the club on loan relatively recently, because these group matches happen often in September and October, just after the end of the transfer window. So quite often what you'll find is a player comes in on loan, isn't getting much match time, and then the manager says, "I'm going to give him a few minutes in the EFL Trophy next week."

Okay. So we're playing — I think it was away at Rochdale — in the Pizza Trophy, 'cause it was sponsored by Papa John's, I think, at the time. And the team sheet was submitted. I wasn't there, and I had a call from our head of communications, our head PR guy, Oscar Chamberlain, said, "I've just got the team sheet. Um, I'm pretty sure that one of our players on that team sheet is cup-tied. I think he played this competition — he played one match, or a few minutes in this competition, in August or something, for his home club," or whatever it might be.

So anyway, [I] said, "Right, go and get the — go and get [it] before it gets in front of the referee, 'cause the moment your team is declared, the referee then — that is your team." So [Oscar was] scurrying down to try [to fix it] now, and we rescued the situation. I say "we" — I had nothing to do with it. Oscar rescued the situation. And the long and the short of it is that, you know, we ended up going all the way. We ended up — now, if he hadn't spotted that...

If he had — you won the trophy.

We would have forfeited that match. We would not have got through to the next stage, and we wouldn't have ended up at Wembley.

Right? This is normal. This is normal. When you sign up to a rule book, you fully accept that there are consequences that come with breaking the rule book. And that would have been a totally unintentional, you know, minor infringement, with no intent to try and gain an unfair advantage.

如果不严惩，下一步会怎样？

Because what you have to think about, Charlie — I'm just going to sort of finish on this thought and then hand back over to you — is: if breaking rules, and particularly intentionally breaking rules, is not subject to severe punishment, what happens next? Where does that go?

So let's just say, for instance, that the punishment for this rule breach — which is spying on clubs' training games — is actually a £300,000 fine, £400,000 fine, maybe even a two-point deduction in the following season, whatever it might be. What question mark does that put in other clubs' heads?

Yeah — "might be worth it."

Charlie, you know, that's why everyone says "throw the rule book at them."

And I'm not saying in this case, but — you know, that whole phrase comes about from people saying, when there's an injustice or a breaking of a rule like this, you have to make an example. And you mentioned their points difference and financial [penalties] — like, if they didn't kick them out, the two other options could have been those two things. Now, if they'd done a financial penalty of £300,000 and Southampton get promoted, you know, that's a drop in the water of the £200 million windfall they're going to get from Premier League revenue over the next three years just from the media. Secondly, a points deduction — they would have, you know, the Premier League and the EFL — that would have been a separate regulatory discussion, because the Premier League don't necessarily enforce the points deductions that an EFL would impose. So they'd have to have an agreement there. Moving into a different competition, you know, there wasn't a clean punishment other than a complete breach of that rule. Fully intentional, absolutely deliberate.

不是第一次：牛津与伊普斯维奇

And, Charlie, most importantly — not the first time they've done it this season. They sent a spy to Oxford United before the game earlier in the season, uh, to watch the training sessions. Now, Oxford really showed them how that was done, beating them 2-1 in the game. So that didn't exactly work well. And then in April, I think they did the same to Ipswich. Um, and that game ended 2-2. So it's quite interesting that he persevered with this strategy — Tonda Eckert, I'm saying here, which is the manager of Southampton — persevered with this strategy of sending analysts to go and look at training sessions, when it didn't actually throw up great results for them.

The premeditation there — I saw that, you know, in the Ipswich game, I think it involved the data analyst dressing in an Eastleigh kit as a disguise, because Eastleigh play in [a] similar ground — I think maybe even the same complex as where Ipswich trained. So you just start thinking about the amount of thought, right, that has gone into this. And his defense was "I didn't know this wasn't allowed," reportedly — that was the defense that was given. So it's the ignorance card, which, as we know, is not a defense. Now, that's unfortunate if you didn't know that, but you can't use that as a defense here.

It's absolutely not a defense at all.

Phil Parsons 的声明与"史上最大罚单"之争

Um, so I just want to read out what Phil Parsons, the chief executive of Southampton, has said — and, by the way, somebody who, you know, I absolutely respect, and I think he's actually a very good CEO.

Agreed.

So Phil's said: "We are sorry to the clubs involved, and most of all to the Southampton fans." That's fair enough — you know, that's correct. Because, yes, absolutely, the clubs have been wronged — the other clubs, that is — but it's fair also to point out that, you know, the Southampton fans have been spending a lot of money traveling home and away supporting that club, and effectively all of their hopes and dreams [have] been dashed on, effectively, something which they would have had no part in and wouldn't have supported.

He then goes on to say, more interestingly: "We believe the financial consequence of yesterday's ruling makes it, by a very considerable distance, the largest penalty ever imposed on [an] English club."

Now, I totally understand why he's trying to make that point. You have to try and, you know — as in poker, you have to try and do the best you can with the hand you're dealt. But it is — not to put too fine a point on it — first of all, I'd like to point out: they have not been promoted, as Swindon Town had been all those years ago. They are in the final, and therefore the punishment that's been given them might either be the largest or maybe the smallest. Because if they had actually lost the game this weekend and been kicked out [of] the playoffs, then they're not really giving anything up, really, in financial terms. Okay, there's a small points deduction next season — but then we've had teams getting handed points deductions this season for tiny infractions of spending rules. I mean — like, in West Brom's case, [a] tiny, tiny infraction.

So really, I don't think it's up to the EFL to assess the financial consequence of punishments it hands out.

£110 million 的真相：球员才是受影响最大的人

Because let's just think about this financial consequence as well. There's a lot of lazy thinking around what this means financially — winning the playoff final: "Oh, you get £110 million quid, it's the richest game in football."
